question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 38169/22-27, to detail (i) whether the water was potable prior to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust accepting handover of the building; (ii) any tests which were carried to demonstrate this; and (iii) the dates on which they occurred.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 38169/22-27, to detail any other healthcare buildings he is aware of in (i) Northern Ireland; (ii) the rest of the UK; and (iii) further afield, not to have drinking water acceptable for use prior to occupation.
To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 38169/22-27, to detail any issues and conditions that have arisen to render the water in the new Maternity Hospital in Belfast non-potable.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 38169/22-27, to detail on what basis the water has been deemed not-potable, including the relevant tests which have indicated this and the dates on which they occurred.

To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work of (i) Hydrop; (ii) Andersen Caledonia; and (iii) Porton Down, in independently reviewing the decision by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to accept handover of the new Maternity Hospital in Belfast.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 28057/22-27, to detail (i) the rationale as to why Hydrop are no longer involved in the independent review into the water systems in the new Maternity Hospital in Belfast; (ii) when this decision was taken; (iii) any decision- making process used; (iv) anyone who provided advice on the matter; and (v) anyone who was involved in taking the decision.

To ask the Minister of Health whether any (i) assessment has been made of the effectiveness and reliability of the critical power supply at the new Maternity Hospital; and (ii) issues have emerged or been raised, in the last six months.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any funding for (i) additional bed capacity; and (ii) site redevelopment at Craigavon Area Hospital, (a) provided by his Department in 2025-26; (b) planned for 2026-27; and (c) planned for subsequent years.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 28058/22-27, to detail any reason why the review by independent experts or publication of its report would need to be suspended until installation and validation of the remediated water systems is complete.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the proportion of the adult population who were prescribed an antidepressant, in each of the last ten years.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the levels of (i) hydrogen peroxide; (ii) chlorine dioxide; (iii) heavy metals; and (iv) microplastics, in the water supply at the new Regional Maternity Hospital in Belfast (a) as reported by D & F Engineering Services in November 2025; and (b) at each occasion subsequently that the water has been tested.

To ask the Minister of Health whether the same specific strain of Pseudomonas has been detected in the new Regional Maternity Hospital in Belfast in (i) February 2023; (ii) April 2024, and to detail the most recent occasion it was detected.

To ask the Minister of Health whether acceptance of handover of the new Regional Maternity Hospital by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust in March 2024 was full and final or whether at that point it still remained subject to some caveats, such as closeout of snags.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any insect infestations reported, in the last six months, at the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre in Belfast.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of individuals who have been interviewed for the post of Chair of Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, since the removal of the previous Chair in July 2025.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of times that flood water has had to be pumped out of Portadown Health Centre car park, in each of the last five years, including the estimated associated cost.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the process by which the new Chair of Belfast Health and Social Care Trust was selected.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of times that flood water has had to be pumped out of Portadown Health Centre car park, in the last 12 months, including the estimated associated cost.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail who has responsibility for the management of the car park at Portadown Health Centre.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any meetings at which officials from his Department discussed the potential to independently review the care arrangements of service users, in the last two years, with Health and Social Care Trust representatives.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the evidence base for reducing the level of support and intervention for the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Under the Department’s Accountability and Oversight arrangements, the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has demonstrated sustained progress in delivering the actions arising from the DCO and McBride/Hill reports’ recommendations. The behavioural and cultural issues and the working environment with the Trust’s cardiac surgery team have been part of these arrangements.  The Trust’s progress against the reports’ recommendations has been regularly discussed and reviewed through these arrangements, and a robust evidence base to inform decisionmaking has been provided. On the basis of improvements demonstrated by the Trust, the Department has determined that the level of support and intervention required by the Trust in relation to culture and governance can appropriately be reduced from Level 5 to Level 4 within the Support and Intervention Framework. It is acknowledged that improvements to culture will take time to develop and evidence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of behavioural and cultural issues and the working environment, within the cardiac surgery team at Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Under the Department’s Accountability and Oversight arrangements, the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has demonstrated sustained progress in delivering the actions arising from the DCO and McBride/Hill reports’ recommendations. The behavioural and cultural issues and the working environment with the Trust’s cardiac surgery team have been part of these arrangements.  The Trust’s progress against the reports’ recommendations has been regularly discussed and reviewed through these arrangements, and a robust evidence base to inform decisionmaking has been provided. On the basis of improvements demonstrated by the Trust, the Department has determined that the level of support and intervention required by the Trust in relation to culture and governance can appropriately be reduced from Level 5 to Level 4 within the Support and Intervention Framework. It is acknowledged that improvements to culture will take time to develop and evidence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the commissioning arrangements for Liaison Care’s involvement in reviewing individuals’ care needs; and (ii) whether there has been any regional element to its consideration and planning.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any options, short of legislative change, that could be available in the short term to ensure post-19 provision for those with complex needs.
answer_text: I fully recognise the importance of ensuring that young people with complex needs are appropriately supported as they move into adulthood, including those who do not fit neatly within a single diagnostic category. Support to meet assessed individual needs of young people with complex needs beyond school can be accessed through a range of established pathways, across a range of programmes of care, including autism. Work is ongoing to review and update the Adult Autism Care Pathway to help achieve consistency in diagnostic and support services across Trusts, in line with NICE guidelines.  The responses to the recent public consultation on the new Learning Disability Service Model are being analysed by my Department. This will inform the new Learning Disability Service Model and accompanying three-year Service Delivery Plan. Although the Model is focused on learning disability services, it includes actions aimed at improving consistency, coordination and planning across the system, including during key transition points.A Regional Transition Protocol for Children and Young People with a Learning Disability, including those with co-occurring Autism, has been developed. My Department is continuing to seek funding to facilitate a pilot of the protocol to support full regional implementation. Children’s and Adult Learning Disability Services across Health and Social Care Trusts remain committed to enhancing the Transition process.My Department will continue to work with HSC organisations and Executive colleagues to strengthen pathways and improve coordination of support for young people with complex needs.I also remain committed to reviewing the Chronically Sick & Disabled Persons (NI) Act 1978 and related legislation in the first year of the Service Delivery Plan for the new Learning Disability Service Model to assess the need for legislative change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Regional Lifespan Gender Service prescribes cross-sex hormones for young people under the age of 18.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on progress towards the creation of a managing authority for controlled schools.
To ask the Minister of Health whether the Regional Lifespan Gender Service will provide treatment for young people who have obtained cross-sex hormones online.
To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has made any assessment of the safety of treatment with cross-sex hormones.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s Gender Identity Service has paused the prescription of cross-sex hormone treatment for new patients under 18 to allow full consideration of the proposed clinical prescribing policy currently being consulted on  by NHS England. Proposed changes to such treatment will need to be carefully reviewed by clinicians to ensure patient safety. The Health and Social Care System in Northern Ireland remains committed to providing appropriate support and care to young people and their families during this period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to completely ban the use of cross-sex hormones, as NHS England have done.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s Gender Identity Service has currently paused the prescription of cross-sex hormone treatment for new patients under 18 to allow full consideration of the proposed clinical prescribing policy currently being consulted on by NHS England. Proposed changes to such treatment will need to be carefully reviewed by clinicians to ensure patient safety. The Health and Social Care System in Northern Ireland remains committed to providing appropriate support and care to young people and their families during this period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the policy of his Regional Lifespan Gender Service on cross-sex hormones for under 18s.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s Gender Identity Service has currently paused the prescription of cross-sex hormone treatment for new patients under 18 to allow full consideration of the proposed clinical prescribing policy currently being consulted on  by NHS England. Proposed changes to such treatment will need to be carefully reviewed by clinicians to ensure patient safety. The Health and Social Care System in Northern Ireland remains committed to providing appropriate support and care to young people and their families during this period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has made any assessment of the potential to include the functions of the Office of Public Guardian as envisaged by the Mental Capacity Act 2005, within the Office of Care and Protection.
answer_text: Part 7 of the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 already provides for the creation of an Office of Public Guardian for Northern Ireland, exercising the same functions as the Office of Public Guardian for England and Wales under the Mental Capacity Act 2005. These provisions, however, have not yet been commenced. My officials have been undertaking preliminary work to scope what would be required to allow for commencement, which will be dependent on the availability of future funding.The Public Guardian’s functions will include establishing and maintaining registers of lasting and enduring powers of attorney and orders appointing deputies, receiving reports from attorneys and deputies, and dealing with complaints about the way in which powers are being exercised by attorneys and deputies. The High Court’s Office of Care and Protection already exercises these functions in relation to enduring powers of attorney and orders appointing controllers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has made any assessment of the level of implementation of independent advocates, as outlined in the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.
answer_text: The Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 provides the statutory framework for decision-making on behalf of people aged 16 and over who lack capacity in relation to their care or treatment. It includes provision for Independent Mental Capacity Advocates (IMCAs) to support and represent individuals who lack capacity and have no appropriate person to do so in specified decisions.The IMCA provisions have not yet been commenced. Implementation of the Act to date has been partial, with provisions relating to research, money and valuables and aspects of deprivation of liberty now in operation.As part of wider implementation planning, officials have undertaken preliminary assessment work and are progressing further policy, operational and financial analysis. This includes consideration of service delivery models, workforce requirements, regulations and operational guidance, commissioning and cost considerations, and engagement with relevant stakeholders. Decisions on the timing of commencement will be taken in the context of the wider phased implementation of the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people who are held under a Deprivation of Liberty Order in accordance with the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.
answer_text: As of the end of February 2026, there were 3,536 individuals subject to a Trust Panel Authorisation (TPA) and 76 individuals subject to a Short-Term Detention Authorisation (STDA) under the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any (i) process; and (ii) timeline, to appoint a specialist to produce a report on water ingress at the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust have advised that a specialist independent Report was commissioned by BHSCT though the Directorate of Legal Services (DLS) to assess the causes for the water ingress to the building. In early February 2026 DLS informed the Trust that the water expert who had been commissioned for the report had withdrawn from the commission.  The Trust have asked DLS to urgently appoint a replacement.  This replacement is expected to be in place within the next 2-3 weeks.  DLS have not yet confirmed to the Trust the impact of this on the completion date for the Report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) assessments; (ii) treatments; and (iii) diagnostics, provided through the £85 million for red flag and time-critical assessments and treatments, in 2025-26.
answer_text: To help reduce the waiting times for assessment and treatment across a range of services, £85m in recurrent funding has been allocated to the HSC in 2025/26 to support waiting list activity. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail a breakdown of health and social care negligence costs, in each of the last five years, broken down by (i) Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) other health body.
answer_text: The following tables provide details of health and social care negligence costs in each of the last five years based on the costs incurred to the Department’s DEL resource budget.Table 1 – Health & Social Care TrustsHealth and Social Care negligence Expenditure - £’0002024-25 2023-24  2022-23  2021-22  2020-21 Belfast Trust19,98611,70410,34410,5576,947Northern Trust14,8527,3158,8484,2196,181South Eastern Trust 18,6366,05010,3357,1035,667Southern Trust7,31610,6568,4855,3494,782Western Trust 8,4575,7917,5494,3657,875NIAS3321381122Grand Total69,57941,51745,59931,60431,474Table 2 – Other Health BodiesHealth and Social Care negligence Expenditure - £’0002024-25 2023-24  2022-23  2021-22  2020-21 HSCB---3,560935SPPG*2,2561,547893--RQIA--322-Grand Total£2,256£1,547£896£3,582£935*Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) became part of the Department on April 1, 2022, following closure of HSCB.My Department continues to be mindful of the need to manage public money carefully, as we navigate the challenging financial environment we are facing. Clinical negligence payments relate to an area which is largely driven by legal process and where financial impacts are hard to predict.There are several factors which have contributed to the increase in the value of settled claims in recent years.The reactivation of medical negligence claims following the Covid pandemic. The introduction of the amended Discount Rate in March 2021 which has led to an increase in the value of claims.The increase to the Guidelines for the Assessment of General Damages in Personal Injury Cases in Northern Ireland, Sixth Edition (The Green Book) which saw increases of in and around 25% to most injury brackets and in some cases 100% – 150% increases.Progress of claims relating to Neurology negligence. Settlement of a number of longstanding and complex high value claims involving Periodical Payments Orders and the cumulative effect of yearly payments.Rising costs of ongoing care in high value claims.Cases are also reaching resolution quicker and increasing numbers of claims settled in each year.It should also be noted that a single high value claim can have a significant effect on any given year.  Specifically, there were 12 very high value claims resolved in 2024/25 each at a value over £1M in damages.

answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust have advised that they commissioned an Independent Water Review in relation to concerns at the New Maternity Hospital in 2025.  They advised that this Report commented on the levels of chemical dosing based upon available records.

answer_text: The new senior executive pay structure was agreed in 2025, to be effective from 1st April 2023.  Over the period 1st April 2023 to 31st December 2025, there were 115 individuals paid on senior executive terms & conditions for all or part of this 33-month period. Please note that 115 is not the number of senior executive posts as there have been several movements, joiners and leavers during this period.  Future movements in the senior executive cohort cannot be predicted with certainty, and therefore the Department cannot provide a projected figure for 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any support he has received from Executive colleagues to ensure he meets his house building targets.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any actions to be taken; and (ii) the associated costs, which would be required in order for Northern Ireland to be considered for inclusion in the PATHWAYS research programme.
answer_text: For Northern Ireland to be considered for inclusion in the PATHWAYS research programme, necessary actions would need to be taken that would involve the research and clinical service. Northern Ireland would be required to complete a separate application for funding and ethical and regulatory approval as these are not covered by the existing set of approvals for NHS England.  In addition, the clinical service would need to be part of the National Multidisciplinary Team review as this is the pathway for eligibility.  In her recent assessment of the Regional Gender Identity Service in NI, Baroness Cass highlighted a number of actions that would require implementation to enable NI to participate in PATHWAYS. However, currently I have suspended Northern Ireland’s participation in the clinical trial pending the outcome of a judicial review in England. If the PATHWAYS clinical trial proceeds in the future, following the conclusion of the judicial review, I will consult Executive colleagues before reconsidering Northern Ireland’s participation.  The welfare of young people remains my priority.NHS England has commissioned and funded the study as part of a wider research programme in partnership with the National Institute for Health and Care Research (NIHR). The research and treatment costs for Northern Ireland participants would be borne by my Department if Northern Ireland’s participation in the clinical trial was to be resumed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any financial support her Department provides to companies to help support their apprenticeship programmes.
answer_text: My Department’s ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeship (HLA) programmes are demand-led, with employers establishing apprenticeship opportunities in line with business need. The Department funds the directed, off-the-job training element of an apprenticeship, investing over £48 million annually across both programmes. This funding ensures that employers are not financially liable for the delivery of apprenticeship training, making the recruitment of apprentices a more feasible and attractive option.To further support employers, an incentive payment is available upon the successful achievement of a Level 2 or Level 3 apprenticeship framework. This payment ranges from £670 to £2,008 depending on the occupational area and is designed to assist with the broader costs of supporting and mentoring apprentices. Employers in the North are also exempt from paying Class 1 National Insurance contributions for apprentices under the age of 25, subject to certain conditions. Further support is available through the Construction Industry Training Board Northern Ireland (CITB NI), which offers Employer Apprentice Grants for construction-related trades.Finally, the Department’s Apprenticeship Action Plan recognises the need to encourage more employers to recruit apprentices and includes deliverables such as piloting Apprenticeship Navigators to support SMEs employing their first apprentices.

answer_text: Data in relation to unallocated Looked After Children cases is reported by all 5 Health and Social Care Trusts to my Department and reported below as regional figures as of the 30th of September 2025. At May 2025, this team was managing 243 children and young people who did not have an allocated social worker.  The Trust advised that statutory visits were being undertaken on a six-weekly basis for children within the LAC 5 team.Children and young people aligned to the LAC 5 rotational team (243 at May 2025) were not included within the Trust's reported unallocated case figures.By July 2025, the Trust reported 328 unallocated cases, of which 252 were being "managed" through the LAC 5 rotational team.  By September 2025, Belfast HSCT reported the highest number of unallocated cases in the region.While my Department recognised that the establishment of the LAC 5 rotational team provides a level of service to children in care, this arrangement should be considered a business continuity mitigation, rather than a substitute for allocated social work practice. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of (i) the quality and outcomes of (a) cardiac surgery; and (b) cardiology services, in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, in the last ten years; and (ii) any concerns raised over the performance of clinicians.
answer_text: Quality outcomes in UK adult cardiac surgery are benchmarked nationally through risk-adjusted mortality and complication data collected by the National Adult Cardiac Surgery Audit (NACSA) enabling each unit’s performance to be benchmarked against expected outcomes and peer centres across the UK. The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust submits cardiac surgery data to NACSA, which is part of the National Institute for Cardiovascular Outcomes Research (NICOR). A review of the validation reports dating back to 2015 for the Belfast Trust, produced by NICOR, concludes that the nationally reported outcomes for both the cardiac surgery unit and individual surgeons are as expected given the risk profile of patients receiving cardiac operations in the Trust, and are consistent with peers in other UK cardiac surgery units.With regards to cardiology, the Belfast Trust delivers both district general hospital level cardiology care as well as tertiary care services. Therefore, the range of cardiology services provided are broad and highly specialised. These services include advanced cardiac imaging such as cardiac MRI and CT, as well as nuclear cardiology to provide detailed heart assessments. Interventional cardiology services, such as coronary angioplasty (stenting), Transcatheter aortic valve implantation, and structural heart interventions, are also available to treat a variety of heart conditions. Additionally, electrophysiology services provide pacemaker and cardiac device implantation, as well as ablation for arrhythmias. The Trust also offers comprehensive heart failure management both in hospital and community-based. For patients with adult congenital heart disease, specialised care and interventions are provided. Furthermore, cardiac rehabilitation programmes play a pivotal role in helping patient recover and improve their cardiovascular health following cardiac events. The Royal Victoria Hospital also functions as one of the two regional Primary PCI centres, providing 24/7 emergency percutaneous coronary intervention for acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction (heart attack). Given the breadth of the cardiology services provided, there is no one central repository for measuring quality and outcomes data. However, the Trust continually monitors its cardiology service quality and patient outcomes through established governance systems. This includes morbidity and mortality meetings, whereby clinical cases are reviewed and discussed including any unexpected complications or deaths. As part of benchmarking cardiology service quality and patient outcomes, the Trust also submits data from numerous cardiology service areas to recognised regional and national quality frameworks, including NICOR, the National Congenital Heart Disease Audit, National Cardiac Benchmarking Collaborative, the British Cardiovascular Society and the British Heart Rhythm Society. Belfast Trust have advised me that within cardiac surgery and cardiology services combined, at present

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount spent on the development of a Mental Health Outcomes Framework, in the last five years.
answer_text: My Department recognises the importance of having robust data systems in place to ensure that clinical data is accessible, usable and can act as a strategic asset for decision-making, policy development and the improvement of clinical outcomes across mental health services.Action 34 of the Mental Health Strategy commits to the development of a Regional Mental Health Outcomes Framework, developed in collaboration with service users, carers and professionals, to underpin and drive service development and delivery.Considerable work has been progressed to develop the Regional Outcomes Framework, informed by engagement with a wide range of stakeholders including Health and Social Care Trusts, statisticians, academia and service users, in line with the Strategy’s principles of co-production and co-design.The Framework was finalised in October 2022 and implementation is ongoing, aligned to wider Mental Health Strategy delivery and the regional encompass digital health and care record programme.Due to the nature of invoicing and programme delivery arrangements, it is not possible to fully disaggregate expenditure relating specifically to the Mental Health Outcomes Framework from broader Mental Health Strategy implementation activity. The costs provided therefore represent an estimate based on identifiable work associated with framework development.The estimated spend on development of the Mental Health Outcomes Framework over the last five years, including proportions of staff costs and commissioned research, is £88,874.75. The estimated expenditure has been calculated using best available financial information, including proportional staff input and commissioned outputs attributable to MHOF development activity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will introduce a bespoke plan to tackle cancer waiting times in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I fully acknowledge that cancer waiting times in Northern Ireland are unacceptable, and improving them is a central priority for my Department. While the Member asks whether I will introduce a bespoke plan, I want to be clear that a comprehensive and operational plan already exists. The Elective Care Framework (ECF) Implementation and Funding Plan is the primary and detailed mechanism through which we are addressing red flag cancer waits, backed by a ring-fenced £85 million for red flag, cancer and time-critical services in 2025/26. This funding is being used to expand core capacity across diagnostics, endoscopy and elective specialties, particularly within urology, breast, dermatology, ENT, plastics and gynaecology services—all of which contribute directly to performance against the 62-day cancer standard. Through the ECF, significant investment is being directed into endoscopy, alongside multi-year expansion of CT, MRI and non-obstetric ultrasound (NOUS). These actions target the early-pathway bottlenecks that most affect timely diagnosis. Short-term stabilisation measures, including additional in-house clinics and independent sector support, are also being used to ensure patients are seen as early as possible. The Cancer Strategy provides longer-term direction, and some actions overlap—such as stabilising oncology and haematology, protecting Cancer Nurse Specialist posts and strengthening phlebotomy. However, it is important to stress that the Strategy is not the primary delivery vehicle for improving cancer waiting times; the ECF is. Despite the pressures, it is worth noting that once diagnosed, 88% of patients receive their first definitive treatment within 31 days, and the vast majority of patients receive an excellent standard of care. My Department will continue to drive forward ECF implementation to secure sustainable and measurable improvements for patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of children with with acquired brain injuries, enrolled in 2024-2025; and (ii) how this data was collected.
answer_text: There were 111 pupils enrolled in the 2024/25 academic year recorded as having an acquired brain injury.  The department receives this information as part of the annual school census return that each school must complete each October.  The annual school census collects information from all voluntary and private pre-schools, nursery, primary, post primary and special schools.   This information is recorded by primary, post primary and special schools in the medical conditions section of the Schools Information Management System (SIMS). Within this there is a dropdown list of medical conditions that schools may choose from. Nursery schools and voluntary and private preschool centres record the same information through an online form, which also provides access to this standardised list of conditions.Schools should normally only record a pupil’s medical diagnosis or physical condition on the medical register when evidence has been provided by the parent; or forwarded to the school by the Education Authority (EA) (where it may be included in the statement); or directly from a Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) practitioner who will have parental consent to share the information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 38582/22-27, to detail any steps taken to ensure the A1 Junctions Phase 2 project can proceed.
answer_text: The A1 Junctions Phase 2 project is a critical road safety infrastructure scheme and I am fully committed to its delivery.Phase 2 will deliver enhanced safety measures along a 25km stretch of the A1 route between Loughbrickland and Hillsborough, costing in the region of £120 - £130m and will include the closing up of all gaps in the central median and the provision of a number of new grade separated junctions.I reaffirmed my predecessor’s commitment to the project and his ringfencing of associated budget through the decisions taken as part of my Department’s major projects prioritisation scheme.Whilst we await the Court of Appeal’s ruling on the A5 before the scheme can proceed, I have recently instructed officials to extend the restricted list for a period of 12 months in order to maintain Phase 2 as a viable scheme and ensure its delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to address the needs of children with acquired brain injuries.
answer_text: Each of the region’s HSC Trusts report that they do not have a formal, commissioned paediatric neurorehabilitation service. Children presenting with an acquired brain injury are allocated to a named Paediatrician who coordinates referrals to inpatient and community services. Individual Care Plans are formulated under the supervision of Consultant Paediatricians with input from the multi-disciplinary team predicated upon assessed individual need.Children and young people impacted by ABI are able to access Allied Health Professional (AHP) services via referral to core services. These include physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech and language therapy, and other relevant therapeutic supports.Children can access support through the Regional Acquired Brain Injury Consultation Service, which offers expert input at a regional level.The Regional Acquired Brain Injury Unit in Belfast is an adult inpatient facility, accepting individuals aged 16 years and over. The Unit delivers a multidisciplinary rehabilitation model, comprising specialised Consultants, Nursing staff, Physiotherapists, Occupational Therapists, Speech & Language Therapists and Dietitians.Family involvement is encouraged as part of the rehabilitation process. Patients also have access to an activity coordinator, providing therapeutic and recreational activities—such as art therapy, music and games—intended to promote wellbeing, enhance social interaction, support confidence-building, and contribute to overall quality of life.Where specialist intervention is required and not available locally, Trusts utilise Extra Contractual Referrals (ECRs) to specialist healthcare providers across the United Kingdom.My Department also funds a regional contract with the Child Brain Injury Trust for Family information/support for children with ABI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) total; and (ii) percentage spend, by Health and Social Care Trusts on staff costs, in each of the last ten years.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of cases of acquired brain injuries that have been diagnosed in (i) adults; and (ii) children, in the last six years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: I can advise the Member that comprehensive data on the number of acquired brain injury (ABI) diagnoses is not centrally held across Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts, and in most cases cannot be extracted from existing information systems without a labourintensive manual review. As a result, the ability of Trusts to quantify ABI diagnoses in either adults or children is significantly limited. The position across Trusts is set out below.Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT)The Belfast Trust has advised that it is unable to provide the requested information, as data on ABI diagnoses is not captured on their systems. Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT)The Northern Trust has advised that it does not hold figures relating to the number of children or adults diagnosed with an acquired brain injury. Support for children and young people with ABI is delivered through a range of services based on assessed need, however diagnostic counts are not recorded in a form that can be retrieved. South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT)The South Eastern Trust has provided the only available numerical information, however, these figures relate to referrals to the Community Brain Injury Team for adults, not to confirmed diagnoses.Adult referrals (Community Brain Injury Team):2020/21: 1062021/22: 1142022/23: 1162023/24: 1212024/25: 1192025/26 (to end January 2026): 99 In addition, the Trust reports that as of November 2025, approximately 91 adults with a known ABI were on the caseload of its Community Physical Disability Teams, and that a manual review suggests that around 325 adults with ABI have been known to their service over the past six years. These figures again relate to service involvement, not diagnoses. No diagnostic data is available for children within the Trust.Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT)The Southern Trust has advised that it is unable to provide the number of adults or children diagnosed with an acquired brain injury, as this information is not centrally held and producing it would require a manual review of records, removing clinical staff from essential duties. Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT)The Western Trust similarly reports that it does not hold figures for either adults or children newly diagnosed with an acquired brain injury. The Trust notes that there is no specific ICD10 code for ABI, and therefore it can only identify admissions coded under traumatic brain injury (S06.2, S06.3, S06.8, S06.9) or hypoxic brain injury (G93.1; P21.9 for newborns). These codes do not allow identification of when a patient was first diagnosed, nor do they capture all forms of ABI. There is no comprehensive dataset maintained across the region’s Health and Social Care Trusts that records acquired brain injury diagnoses for adults or children over the past six years. Only the South Eastern Trust could provide related referral activity for adults, which does not equate to diagnostic data. As such, it is not p

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on (i) the amount that has been spent on the Waiting list Reimbursement Scheme, since its reinstatement in June 2025; and (ii) the number of people who have availed of it.
To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the number of cases that have been added to the backlog due to the ongoing industrial action by the Criminal Bar Association.
To ask the Minister of Health whether abiraterone will be made available for prostrate cancer patients.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. This link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventions. In practice this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also routinely available in Northern Ireland.Where NICE has recommended the use of abiraterone (Zytiga®) in the treatment of prostate cancer, this medicine is available to eligible patients in Northern Ireland, in line with the current NICE guidelines. Further information on this treatment is available at the following links:https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta259https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta387https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta951https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta1110

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions taken to identify patients most at risk due to long waits for cancer treatment, to prioritise them for diagnosis and treatment.
answer_text: My Department recognises that prolonged waits within cancer pathways can increase clinical risk and remains focused on ensuring that patients with the greatest clinical need are identified and prioritised appropriately.In Northern Ireland, the most significant pressures within cancer pathways arise at the start of a patient’s pathway and at the diagnostic stage, rather than in the delivery of treatment. Once a cancer diagnosis is confirmed, patients are generally able to access treatment within clinically appropriate timeframes. As a result, a key focus of risk identification and prioritisation activity is on patients waiting for diagnostic tests and confirmation of diagnosis.Health and Social Care Trusts operate established clinical prioritisation and escalation arrangements, led by clinicians and multidisciplinary teams, to identify patients at greatest risk while awaiting diagnostics or treatment. These arrangements include:•	Clinical triage of referrals, including urgent suspected cancer referrals, using recognised red flag /urgency criteria and supported by consultant oversight.•	Ongoing clinical review of diagnostic waiting lists, with the ability to re-prioritise patients where symptoms worsen, new information becomes available, or there is concern about disease progression.•	Use of cancer multidisciplinary team (MDT) meetings to review complex cases and to expedite progression through diagnostic pathways where increased risk is identified.My department continues to work closely with Trusts to identify key bottlenecks in cancer pathways and to ensure these are prioritised through dedicated service improvement plans and associated investment requests. At a regional level, we are also addressing diagnostic capacity pressures through targeted investment in diagnostic services and waiting list initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail a breakdown of any spending of the £85 million funding for red flag cancer referrals to date.
answer_text: On 30 May 2025, I announced the publication of an Implementation and Funding Plan for my Department’s Elective Care Framework (ECF) which detailed the actions needed to start to address the demand capacity gap, reform services and reduce the current backlog of patients waiting.It set out a breakdown of how the waiting list funding from the Programme for Government in this year’s budget was to be invested across three areas; namely the expansion of capacity to reduce the red flag/time critical capacity gap; increasing core routine capacity; and non-recurrent funding to start tackling the backlog of patients waiting.£85 million had been earmarked for the first of these, expansion of red flag/time critical capacity, to include cancer referrals. The breakdown in allocation of this can be found in the ECF Implementation and Funding Plan as described above, available at: www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/elective-care-framework-restart-recovery-and-redesign My Department continues to work with Trusts to ensure that recurrent capacity is operational as quickly as possible. Developing new services, however, are subject to the normal governance arrangements pertaining to financial management and recruitment processes. While funding was only confirmed in year, good progress has been made with additional services now in place for urology, endoscopy, diagnostic imaging, ophthalmology and vascular services. The funding has also allowed the appointment of new consultant dermatology nurses, Macmillian nurses and Systemic Anti-Cancer Therapy (SACT) capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any action his Department is taking to ensure capital budgets are spent and not recycled year after year.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the School Enhancement Programme.
answer_text: The second call to the School Enhancement Programme was closed to applications in February 2017.  Four separate tranches of projects have been announced to proceed in planning, with the final announcement made on 5 May 2020.  A total of 72 projects have been announced across the four tranches, with their status summarised as follows:Construction has been completed on 1 school.Construction is ongoing for 4 schools.Construction procurement is underway for 5 schools.There are 14 schools at RIBA Stage 4 (Technical Design).There are 9 schools at RIBA Stage 3 (Detailed Design)There are 8 schools at RIBA Stage 2 (Scheme Design)There are 28 schools at RIBA Stage 1 (Technical Feasibility).There are 3 schools at RIBA Stage 0 (Scoping).I have made funding available to progress SEP projects through the design stages to pre-tender.  A decision to release projects to tender will be taken on a case-by-case basis, dependent on budget availability at that time.I will continue to make the case for additional capital funding for education through the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any projects which are now being held back as a result of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme appeal.
answer_text: The A5WTC High Court judgment, which went against my Department, was hugely disappointing, not just for me, but for all the families and campaigners such as Enough is Enough who have been extremely diligent and steadfast in their commitment to ensuring that the road is built.My Department lodged an appeal against the judgment and the Department for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs and Friends of The Earth were granted permission to formally intervene in the appeal. The case was heard in part by the Appeal Court on 9 and 10 December. The Appeal Court then referred the matter of the Climate Change Act back to the High Court where the case has had an initial hearing with a further one scheduled for 25 February.  Following an outcome from this, the Court of Appeal will then reconvene.The outcome of this appeal will have far-reaching implications for all major infrastructure projects pursued by departments across the North and to safeguard my Department’s limited resources, I am carefully considering how to advance other infrastructure schemes in line with the outcome of the A5 judgment.To preserve the progress already made, I have ensured these schemes can remain live until the final judgment is delivered through the extension of the tender validity periods for both the A4 Enniskillen Bypass and the Lagan and Pedestrian Cycle Bridge. My officials are also preparing options for my consideration to protect the A1 Junctions Phase 2 project.My priority remains the delivery of these vital infrastructure projects, and I am committed to protecting the progress of these schemes as much as possible while the legal process continues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration that has been given to collecting waiting times for GP appointments, as is in England.
answer_text: My Department does not currently collect this specific data. My Department is, however, aware of developments in England and continues to work closely with GMS contractors and wider system partners to address the challenges and pressures affecting patient access to General Practice. As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients.  Decisions relating to the day to day operation of the GP practices, including the use of technology, telephony systems and appointment arrangements, are determined locally by practices, taking account of their available workforce and patient population. My Department has issued guidance to practices relating to the management of patient demand and optimising workflow to maximise efficiency, and consideration of how technology can be best used to improve experience for both patients and practices.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any information his Department holds in relation to waiting times for GP appointments.
answer_text: The Health & Personal Social Services (General Medical Services Contracts) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004 does not place a contractual requirement on GP Practices to record or report waiting times for GP appointments. My Department does not hold data on waiting times for GP appointments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amounts paid by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to the contractor for the new Regional Maternity Hospital for adjustments or variations to the works information, including the (i) date; and (ii) reason.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether only a partial replacement of the pipework in the new Maternity Hospital in Belfast is necessary.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust undertook a multi-disciplinary review relating to the water systems within the New Maternity Hospital.  The multi-disciplinary team reviewed the extant information and recommended to Trust Board to proceed with Option 2 (a new neo-natal domestic water system and localised remediation throughout the rest of the building).   Following ratification by the Trust Board, I sought a review of the multi-disciplinary team’s recommendation.  This review, which completed on 11 September 2025 did not raise any concerns regarding the process or the recommendation from Belfast Trust.The Trust’s Responsible Persons for water met in January 2025 to review the extant position regarding the remediation works including information received within the above reports. They agreed that none of the latest information received would necessitate a review of the decision to proceed with Option 2. Taking account of the above opinions, I am satisfied that only a partial replacement of the pipework in the new Maternity Hospital is necessary

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) each payment made by the Belfast Health and Social care Trust to the contractor for the new Children’s Hospital for adjustments or variations to the works, broken down by (a) date; (b) reason; and (c) cost; and (ii) similar details for any other payment requests from the contractor for adjustments or variations which remain outstanding.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust conducted a Public Interest Test in relation to this query, although this was not a requirement for an AQW. Following this assessment, they have concluded that they were unable to release the requested information, as it is considered commercially sensitive.I can advise, however, that the total of payments made by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to the contractor for the new Children’s Hospital for adjustments or variations to the works up to 10 January 2026 is £1,401,187.34. The adjustments and variations included inter alia, changes in relation to piling, retaining walls, quantities remeasurement, human resources, asbestos clearance, temporary services, NIE supply and drainage amendments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any precautions that have been taken to protect cleaners and other staff who need to access the building, from any water defects in the new Maternity Hospital in Belfast.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has advised that the new Maternity Hospital is a safe environment for staff to work. As a precaution, since handover, staff have been advised not to drink mains water in the building, as is consistent with the commissioning of a new hospital building. Only once all tests meet the required standards and the Responsible Engineer confirms the water is considered wholesome should the water be used for drinking.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the water anywhere in the new Maternity Hospital in Belfast is potable.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust have undertaken several water sampling tests on the water systems within the new Maternity Hospital in Belfast.  Since pseudomonas aeruginosa was first discovered within the building the Trust has advised that the water is not to be considered potable.The Trust have advised that the new Maternity Hospital is a safe environment for staff to work.  As a precaution, since handover, staff have been advised not to drink mains water in the building, as is consistent with the commissioning of a new hospital building.Only once all tests meet the required standards and the Responsible Engineer confirms the water is considered wholesome should the water be used for drinking.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the main findings of the independent assessment in late 2025, into water quality in the new Maternity Hospital in Belfast.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust have advised as follows:‘Belfast Health and Social Care Trust continues to work with our consultant design team, DoH Health Estates and our water specialist on the delivery of the remediation works for the new Maternity Hospital.The Trust have received a number of assessments and reports related to the water systems and water quality within the new Maternity Hospital in the latter part of 2025.  This has included the following reports: The Minister of Health’s Independent Report by Water Hygiene Centre dated 11/09/2025 Report by D & F Engineering Services Ltd dated 17/11/2025 Report by HYDROP E.C.S. LTD dated 24/12/2025 The Trust’s Responsible Persons for water met in January 2025 to review the extant position regarding the remediation works including information received within the above reports. They agreed that none of the latest information received would necessitate a review of the decision to proceed with “Option 2”.  This option involves the installation of a new domestic water system to the neo-natal unit, with localised remediation through the remainder of the domestic water systems in the building. As previously reported, there remains a risk that in the event these works are not successful the Trust may need to reconsider the decision to proceed with Option 2.’

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) number of; and (ii) percentage of, patients that were receiving care in escalation or corridor areas at each Emergency Department, in each week from the start of November 2025.
answer_text: My Department does not currently hold data in a format that would allow me to report the number or percentage of Emergency Department (ED) patients receiving care in escalation or corridor areas.I recognise the importance of understanding the use of escalation and corridor spaces as part of quality and safety monitoring. My Department is working closely with the EPIC Team and Health and Social Care Trusts to develop a standardised data collection process that will capture this information consistently across all EDs. This work will form part of a broader programme to enhance transparency and support evidence-based decision-making.EDs operate in a highly dynamic environment where escalation spaces are used flexibly to maintain patient safety during periods of exceptional demand. These areas are not routinely recorded as a distinct category in existing datasets, which focus primarily on overall attendances, waiting times, and clinical outcomes.While specific data on escalation areas is not currently available, my Department requires all Trusts to adhere to strict clinical governance standards when using non-designated spaces for patient care. This includes:Risk assessments and infection prevention measures for any escalation area;Senior clinical oversight to ensure patient safety and dignity; andContinuous monitoring of patient flow and escalation triggers as part of Trust surge plans.I am actively engaging with representatives of the Royal College of Emergency Medicine (RCEM) following their recent survey on care in EDs.  I regard this as an important piece of work and I am engaging directly with RCEM on their findings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail an estimate of the ambulance road hours lost each week, as a result of delays at Emergency Departments, from the start of November 2025.
answer_text: Table 1 below provides the regional weekly ambulance road hours lost due to emergency department (ED) delays from the start of November 2025, and the corresponding average and longest handover times recorded.Table 1Ambulance Handover DelaysDateLost HoursAverage Handover TimeMax Handover Time27 Oct 2025 - 02 Nov 20252,29201:15:1315:29:2703 Nov 2025 - 09 Nov 20252,86101:30:3714:49:3010 Nov 2025 - 16 Nov 20252,14001:09:4812:06:5917 Nov 2025 - 23 Nov 20252,06901:08:4613:56:3924 Nov 2025 - 30 Nov 20252,70301:33:1317:07:4101 Dec 2025 - 07 Dec 20253,41901:59:4516:28:3008 Dec 2025 - 14 Dec 20252,95001:40:5216:43:1515 Dec 2025 - 21 Dec 20252,63401:28:0019:04:0622 Dec 2025 - 28 Dec 20251,57800:56:5609:28:1929 Dec 2025 - 04 Jan 20263,23501:49:3717:09:5805 Jan 2026 - 11 Jan 20263,63702:12:5419:44:07My Department acknowledges the significant pressures facing EDs and the impact on the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service’s (NIAS) ability to offload ambulances promptly. A regional workshop was held in August 2025, supported by the Permanent Secretary and Chief Medical and Nursing Officers, and was attended by the Trusts and NIAS.  Based on best practice from the London Ambulance Service and King George Hospital shared at the workshop, agreement was reached to aim to eliminate ambulance handover delays exceeding two hours by 1 December 2025. New regional ambulance handover guidance is being developed to support this. Trusts are also working to prevent deterioration in shorter delay thresholds (15, 30, and 60 minutes), with a longer-term objective of achieving the 15-minute standard.To support improved flow and reduce handover delays, my Department has repurposed £10 million to initiatives such as expanding Hospital at Home, increasing shortstay and intermediate care beds, enhancing frailty and multidisciplinary assessment at the front door, expanding Same Day Emergency Care, and strengthening early review and discharge co-ordination. These actions sit within each Trust’s Unscheduled Care Improvement Plan and are overseen by the Regional Coordination Centre. All hospitals remain able to activate additional surge beds when required.In addition, funding has been provided to NIAS to enhance the use of ‘Hear and Treat’ and ‘See and Treat’ pathways, which allows patients to receive appropriate care without being transported to hospital, where this is clinically safe to do so.Through a sustained, systemwide focus on reducing ambulance handover delays, the region achieved improved performance despite significant winter pressures. In December, the average handover delay reduced to 1 hour 20 minutes, down from 1 hour 39 minutes in December 2024. Additionally, handover delays exceeding two hours fell to 1,949 delays, compared with 2,170 delays in December 2024.These measures collectively strengthen the system’s capacity to improve ambulance handover performance, improve patient flow, and ensure safer, timelier care across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of Assembly Questions for Written Answer to his Department for which answers remained outstanding at 4 January 2026 from (i) myself; and (ii) other Members, for (a) the 2025-26 session; and (b) 2024-25 session and earlier.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he has taken to increase the number of psychiatry inpatient beds available.
answer_text: All Trusts are experiencing significant pressures as a consequence of increased demands upon services, including an increased number of new referrals, complexity, acuity of people presenting to services and increased workforce pressures, as a result of increased vacancy levels.There are 393 commissioned acute inpatient mental health assessment and treatment beds for the region. My officials have been monitoring and reviewing capacity and demand via the Regional Bed Capacity Network, as well as daily huddles with regional bed coordinators and engagement with individual Trusts on specific emerging issues. This engagement has been central to understanding local issues, variances across the region and problem solving with regard to bed flow. In recognition of the challenges faced, and given the current budgetary constraints, my Department has supported and endorsed the decision by the Western Health and Social Care Trust to re-purpose Rathview House nursing home/ covid centre, to acute bed stock, creating an additional 6 acute mental health beds.Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, in response to over occupancy in the Adult Mental Health Inpatient Centre (AMHIC), took proactive action to introduce the Psychiatric Assessment and Treatment Hub (PATH). My Department has met with the Trust to understand the impact of this service, learning and potential scalability. The positive outcomes of PATH so far are recognised. My Department has subsequently received a business case from the Belfast Trust for longer term funding. This business case is subject to due process, and has to be considered within the wider context of current financial constraint and priorities.A regional protocol for managing circumstances where discharges from mental health beds have been delayed; is currently in the process of being developed, to promote regional consistency, and assist with improving bed flow and availability.

Working in partnership with the Royal

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of Emergency Department waits in excess of twelve hours, in each of the last six weeks.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the average; and (ii) the longest, ambulance handover time, for each day in the last four weeks.
answer_text: The table below sets out the average and longest ambulance handover times recorded for each day in the last four weeks:Ambulance Handover DelaysDateAvg Handover TimeMax Handover Time08/12/202502:37:0016:43:1509/12/202501:55:1710:39:3110/12/202502:03:3214:00:1111/12/202501:34:4208:52:1712/12/202501:19:0706:35:1113/12/202501:08:4206:30:5414/12/202501:20:0411:48:3015/12/202501:45:4710:56:1716/12/202501:40:0714:43:3717/12/202501:36:4116:01:0218/12/202501:24:5719:04:0619/12/202501:19:4811:22:1520/12/202501:10:2208:51:5121/12/202501:21:3309:04:0622/12/202501:18:1408:11:4523/12/202500:54:0006:50:0024/12/202500:41:4302:41:1125/12/202500:42:2703:02:3826/12/202500:49:3006:57:3727/12/202500:55:3205:19:3628/12/202501:15:5309:28:1929/12/202501:41:0216:31:5330/12/202501:36:5607:12:5531/12/202501:29:3109:58:0101/01/202601:52:0917:09:5802/01/202601:57:2713:36:0503/01/202602:03:2711:31:1104/01/202601:58:3610:36:26Grand Total01:27:4319:04:06It should be acknowledged that there was an early and significant flu season with cases starting to increase in November and peaking in the first week of December. Flu numbers remained high throughout December with episode numbers higher than 2024/25. Seasonal peaks in respiratory illness will inevitably place additional pressure on hospitals.Vaccination rates, especially for those over the age of 65 (72.4%) and in care homes (79.3%) were good. Additional support has also been provided to care homes by Trusts in the administration of antivirals to residents where flu outbreaks occurred. This intervention is aimed at reducing admissions from care homes.Ambulance handover delays are influenced by system-wide pressures, particularly during periods of high demand. The recent flu surge contributed to increased hospital admissions and reduced bed availability, which in turn affected the ability to offload patients promptly at emergency departments (EDs).Reducing ambulance handover delays is a key priority for my Department, as prolonged delays impact the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service’s (NIAS) capacity to respond to calls in the community. To address this, my Department and the Trusts have implemented a range of measures, including:New ambulance handover guidance is being developed with Trusts and NIAS, aiming to eliminate delays exceeding two hours and progressively reduce shorter thresholds;Targeted investment to improve patient flow, including additional short-stay and intermediate care beds, expansion of Same Day Emergency Care pathways, and enhanced discharge coordination;Hospital at Home services and community-based care initiatives to reduce admissions and free up capacity in acute hospitals; and,An integrated clinical hub within NIAS to provide clinical triage and alternative care pathways, reducing unnecessary conveyances to EDs.These actions are part of a wider Unscheduled Care Improvement Plan designed to improve system flow and ensure timely access to emergency care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail an estimate of the ambulance road hours lost each day in the last four weeks as a result of delays at Emergency Departments.
answer_text: Table 1 below sets out the estimated ambulance road hours lost each day in the last four weeks due to delays at emergency departments (EDs).  This equates to a total of 10,319 hours lost, approximately 16% of operational capacity.Table 1Ambulance Road Hours Lost 2025DateLost  Total10,319Table 2 below sets out the estimated ambulance road hours lost each day in the same period in 2024/2025 due to delays at emergency departments (EDs).Table 2Ambulance Road Hours Lost 2024DateLost  Total13,273As shown in Table 1, an estimated 10,319 ambulance road hours were lost over the most recent fourweek period, with daily figures ranging from 133 to 659 hours. In contrast, Table 2 shows 13,273 hours lost during the same period in 2024/25. This represents a reduction of around 2,950 hours from the previous year.My Department recognises the significant pressures faced by EDs, and is working closely with Health and Social Care Trusts to implement a range of actions aimed at reducing these delays and improving system flow. A key focus of this is to reduce the need to convey patients to hospital by expanding ambulatory pathways that provide safe alternatives to ED attendance. Funding has been allocated to support the growth of Hospital at Home services and Same Day Emergency Care, enabling more patients to be assessed and treated either in the community or on the same day without admission.In addition, support has been provided to the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) to enhance the use of ‘Hear and Treat’ and ‘See and Treat’ pathways, which allow patients to receive appropriate care without being transported to hospital. These measures are designed to relieve pressure on EDs, improve patient flow, and ensure that patient safety remains the highest priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any assessment that has been made of the effectiveness of the Overarching Winter Preparedness Plan 2025/26.
answer_text: When we published the Department’s Overarching Winter Plan alongside the Trusts’ local plans in October, I stressed that the system and the public need to work together and all play our part. This will help to make sure that HSC services are ready and available for those who need them most. Improving effectiveness includes measures such as getting vaccinated, taking steps to stay well and co-operating with the hospital discharge processes.The 2025/26 Winter Plan builds on last year’s foundations, focusing on expanding urgent care alternatives such as Phone First, minor injury units, and urgent care centres. These initiatives are embedded within each Trust’s Plans. However, while these plans can help mitigate winter pressures, they cannot prevent them. In terms of mitigation, in advance of Christmas, all Trusts focused on increased discharges to maximise bed capacity, putting hospital sites in an improved position compared to the same period in 2024.While Emergency Department (ED) attendances and ambulance arrivals were on some days higher than forecast, we have seen improvement in comparison with 2024.  There have been reductions in both the number of patients in ED awaiting admission and the number of patients waiting over 12 hours once admitted.While Flu rates peaked earlier in 2025, I am encouraged that related admissions had begun to decrease over the festive period in all age groups. We must however remain vigilant and continue to take all the steps we can to reduce the transmission of infectious diseases. As Minister of Health, I remain committed to doing everything I can and us all working together to ensure we achieve a stable reformed and sustainable health service for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the percentage of time that Emergency Departments did not have a resuscitation cubicle available, in the last year.
answer_text: My Department does not hold data in a format that would allow me to report the percentage of time that resuscitation cubicles were unavailable across emergency departments (EDs) during the last year.ED resuscitation areas operate in a highly dynamic and clinically driven environment. Cubicles are allocated based on patient acuity and immediate clinical need, rather than fixed occupancy metrics. When a critically ill or injured patient arrives, they are treated in the resuscitation area without delay. Once stabilised and clinically appropriate, patients are moved promptly to other areas within the ED or admitted to a ward, freeing the cubicle for the next patient.Operationally, resuscitation cubicles are not static resources; their availability fluctuates minute by minute depending on patient flow, treatment complexity, and discharge/admission processes. EDs also employ flexible escalation measures, such as:Repurposing adjacent clinical spaces for resuscitation care during surge periods; andDeploying additional staff and equipment to maintain safe care when demand exceeds designated resuscitation capacity.Because of these dynamic practices, my Department does not routinely record or monitor resuscitation cubicle availability as a percentage of time. Capturing this metric would require real-time occupancy tracking systems, which are not currently in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) average; (ii) longest, and (iii) 95th percentile, duration of waiting time to receive cervical screening results, in 2025.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of; and (ii) percentage of, Emergency Department patients receiving care in escalation or corridor areas, in the last year; and (ii) how does this compare with the last five years.
answer_text: EDs operate in a highly dynamic environment where escalation spaces are used flexibly to maintain patient safety during periods of exceptional demand. These areas are not routinely recorded as a distinct category in existing datasets, which focus primarily on overall attendances, waiting times, and clinical outcomes.I recognise the importance of understanding the use of escalation and corridor spaces as part of quality and safety monitoring. My Department is working closely with the EPIC Team and Health and Social Care Trusts to develop a standardised data collection process that will capture this information consistently across all EDs. This work will form part of a broader programme to enhance transparency and support evidence-based decision-making.My Department requires all Trusts to adhere to strict clinical governance standards when using non-designated spaces for patient care. This includes:Risk assessments and infection prevention measures for any escalation area;Senior clinical oversight to ensure patient safety and dignity; andContinuous monitoring of patient flow and escalation triggers as part of Trust surge plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the additional welfare costs following patients spending time on waiting lists.
answer_text: It is not possible to quantify the additional welfare costs which are incurred as a result of patients spending time on waiting lists. These costs will be subject to a range of factors, including clinical condition and personal circumstances. Welfare related benefits are a matter for the Department of Communities.The revised Elective Care Framework (ECF), which was published on 24 May 2025, set out a five-year roadmap to reduce waiting times, including how earmarked funding would be used for waiting list activity. As you are aware, only £50m of this funding represented new money. Even with these financial pressures, there has been significant progress since March 2025 with the number of patients waiting over four years for an outpatient assessment reduced by 35% and the number of patients waiting for an inpatient or day case procedure reduced by 47%. Diagnostic waits have also reduced, with endoscopy waits down by 62%.In parallel, the Department is progressing work on the development of a Support While Waiting policy, which aims to improve the support available to individuals while they remain on waiting lists, including better information, signposting and consideration of wider impacts such as employment and wellbeing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the basis on which the Southern Health and Social Care Trust provided assurance to the Public Health Agency in January 2020 that the performance of cervical screeners was within an acceptable range.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) advise that correspondence issued from the Trust to the Public Health Agency (PHA) on 16 January 2020 which provided comments and feedback on a PHA Quality Assurance (QA) visit from 2019. The final QA report was subsequently issued by the PHA in February 2020.The SHSCT has advised that in the letter of the 16 January 2020 from the Trust to the PHA, the Trust advised PHA that screener performance was within an acceptable range at that time. The response in the letter also says that the monitoring systems in place were robust and that the Trust monitored all screeners in accordance with laboratory protocol. The SHSCT further advises that the letter of 16 January 2020 also notes that the issue of identifying underperformance and achievable standards for individuals within the screening programme should be discussed at the Regional Laboratory QA group, as a matter of urgency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a contractor has been appointed to replace pipework at the new Maternity Hospital in Belfast.
answer_text: A Design Team has been appointed to take forward the agreed scope of remedial works.  A Contractor will be appointed on completion of this initial phase of design works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions taken by the Southern Health and Social Care Trust when the Public Health Agency recommended the removal of an individual from cervical screening in 2019.
answer_text: The Southern Trust advise that following a Quality Assurance (QA) visit from the PHA on 20 September 2019, the PHA, within its final report (issued in February 2020), did not recommend the removal of an individual from screening but instead recommended that the Trust consider this staff members role within screening.  In the subsequent action plan, in response to this, the Southern Trust responded that the performance indicators at that time were within acceptable limits. The Trust also requested a regional discussion on screener sensitivity indicators. Some of the cervical screening-related reports, published to date, detail that there was evidence of underperformance over many years of a small number of cytology staff and that the management of this underperformance was inadequate. The Southern Trust has accepted that the actions taken by the Trust at that time were not sufficiently robust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the additional costs of medication prescription following patients spending time on waiting lists.
answer_text: Firstly, it is important to acknowledge that the current waiting times are lengthy for patients across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform Health and Social Care (HSC) delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.In terms of additional costs of medications prescribed to patients while they are on waiting lists, my Department does not routinely collect this information and therefore no specific assessment of such costs is available. Treatment needs are highly individualised and waiting lists cover a wide range of specialties and conditions meaning such costs cannot be meaningfully aggregated.However, my Department published Valuing Medicines – A Strategy for the Sustainable Use of Medicines in Northern Ireland in September 2025 which acknowledges that patients on waiting lists often rely heavily on prescription medication to manage their conditions. Cost-effective practice is already well established within the HSC, with the Medicines Optimisation Regional Efficiency (MORE) programme currently generating annual savings in the region of £20 million. Further change is essential to maintain progress and respond to growing challenges, including those associated with waiting lists.The Valuing Medicines Strategy also sets out a high-level framework to support the more effective and sustainable use of medicines across the HSC system, which would include patients taking prescribed medication to manage their conditions while on waiting lists. This includes supporting patient involvement in decisions about their medicines, promoting preventive care, and improving access to non-medical therapies that can be used in place of, or alongside, prescribed medicines.
