question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail an estimate of the percentage of independent care home providers who are paying staff the real Living Wage.
answer_text: My Department does not hold data on the percentage of independent social care providers paying staff the Real Living Wage (RLW),  the percentage of independent care home providers who are paying staff the real Living Wage, the percentage of social care staff employed in the independent sector who are in receipt of the real Living Wage or the percentage of independent care home staff in receipt of the real Living Wage.		My Department’s Fair Work Forum developed an evidence-based case for improving pay, terms and conditions of the social care workforce. To inform their work, the Forum carried out a survey with both registered social care workers (SCWs) in the independent sector and employers in the independent care sector. The results of the surveys undertaken are contained within the Justification Report for the introduction of the Real Living Wage. I intend to publish the Justification Report for introduction of the Real Living Wage on my Department’s website in due course which will allow Members the opportunity to consider the survey results within the context of the full report. 		Evidence gathered through the survey carried out by my Department’s Fair Work Forum indicates that, while some staff report pay at or above the RLW, many employers report that they do not pay RLW. The survey found that 54% of employee respondents reported being paid equal to or greater than the RLW (based on the 2023 RLW rate of £12.00/hour), whereas most employers (73% of domiciliary care respondents and 88% of wider social care respondents) reported that they do not pay the RLW. These findings should be interpreted with caution due to limited response rates.I remain committed to implementing the RLW for independent sector care workers and this will be prioritised when planning for the 2026/27 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how he plans to fund the implementation of the Adult Protection Bill.
answer_text: The Adult Protection Bill business case was approved on the condition that the Bill will not be implemented until such time as funds are available.  This enabled the Bill to be introduced so that it can undergo scrutiny in the Assembly and be ready for implementation as soon as possible. Officials are developing an implementation plan based on a phased approach which would be subject to funding becoming available. The Adult Safeguarding Transformation Board will determine the order in which elements of the Bill should be implemented, taking into account available funding alongside policy and other considerations, including views from the Committee for Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 34350/22-27, whether he will place copies of the initial interim report and other iterations of the McBride-Hill Report in the Assembly library.
answer_text: The McBride/Hill report is publicly available on the Department’s website. No copies of the initial interim report and other iterations will be placed in the Assembly’s Library.

answer_text: The total number of samples tested for primary HPV within the Belfast Trust for calendar month of October 2025 was 1,308.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount of funding that (i) his Department; and (ii) each of its arm's-length bodies, has provided to fund trade union officials and activities, in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail his latest estimate of the cost of remedial work that is required at the Belfast Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust have advised that the estimated cost of remedial work required at the Belfast Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre is £7.62M.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to increase mental health bed numbers, in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: It is not possible to quantify the additional welfare costs which are incurred as a result of patients spending time on waiting lists. These costs will be subject to a range of factors, including clinical condition and personal circumstances. Welfare related benefits are a matter for the Department of Communities.The revised Elective Care Framework (ECF), which was published on 24 May 2025, set out a five-year roadmap to reduce waiting times, including how earmarked funding would be used for waiting list activity. As you are aware, only £50m of this funding represented new money. Even with these financial pressures, there has been significant progress since March 2025 with the number of patients waiting over four years for an outpatient assessment reduced by 35% and the number of patients waiting for an inpatient or day case procedure reduced by 47%. Diagnostic waits have also reduced, with endoscopy waits down by 62%.In parallel, the Department is progressing work on the development of a Support While Waiting policy, which aims to improve the support available to individuals while they remain on waiting lists, including better information, signposting and consideration of wider impacts such as employment and wellbeing.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, given their departmental responsibilities in respect of international relations, whether they were consulted by the Minister for the Economy prior to her departmental instruction not to engage in the UK Government's trade talks with Israel.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when it is intended to decant patients from the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre to the Knockbracken site; and (iI) the estimated to cost to make the alternative building fit for purpose to accommodate the patients temporarily.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust have advised that the date for the initial temporary decant of patients from the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre to the alternative building located on the Knockbracken site is forecast to be January 2027.The Trust have also advised that the estimated cost of making the alternative building fit for purpose is £2.28M.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any penalty clauses there are for adjustments in the construction of the new Belfast Children’s Hospital.
answer_text: The construction contract for the new Belfast Children’s Hospital does not contain any such clauses as penalty clauses are not permissible in construction contracts. It does, however, include clauses governing adjustments/variations to the Works Information which may result in a change to the tendered total of the Prices and/or the Completion Date.  These clauses are as outlined in Section 6 entitled Compensation events of the NEC3 Engineering and Construction Contract Option B.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any actions he is taking to get more people into work in Upper Bann.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail how he has allocated the £215 million earmarked by the Executive for waiting list activity this year, per the Programme for Government.
answer_text: The Elective Care Framework (ECF) Implementation and Funding Plan, which was published in May 2025, detailed how the £215 million earmarked funding outlined in the Programme for Government (PfG) would be used for waiting list activity. This initially included £85m to build core HSC red flag and time critical capacity, £80m to build routine capacity and £50m to help reduce the current backlog of long waiters. The Plan is available at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2025-05/doh-elective-care-framework-funding-plan-may-2025.PDFAs you are aware only £50m of this represents new money and in line with the flexibility provided for this funding, £73.5m has subsequently been redirected to reduce the wider deficit, though this figure may change depending on the timelines of new staff taking up posts. Even with these financial pressures, there has been significant progress this year already with the number of patients waiting over four years reduced by 29% and the number of patients waiting for an inpatient or day case procedure reduced by 41%. Additionally, over 120,000 additional outpatient, diagnostic and inpatient/daycase procedures have been delivered between April 2025 and September 2025, exceeding the PfG target of 70,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any representations he has made to the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs on behalf of farmers and pet owners in relation to the veterinary medicine deadline of 1 January 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the performance of senior management of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust in relation to the handover of the new maternity hospital at the Royal Victoria Hospital.
answer_text: Projects located on acute hospital sites are inherently complex with each being unique and bespoke with complex building services installations and systems having to be co-ordinated and commissioned. In short there is substantial risk associated with projects of this type. In relation to the handover of the new Maternity Hospital whilst I am extremely frustrated with the situation I am satisfied that given the information provided to date it would appear that the senior management team of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust acted appropriately. With the benefit of hindsight, all parties involved might consider that alternative actions could have been taken. I have tasked all involved with the handover process to ensure that any lessons learnt from this situation are applied moving forward. Examples of improvements already implemented because of lessons learnt from the maternity hospital handover process include a weekly meeting between the Trust Director of Strategic Development and senior departmental officials to ensure that the new maternity and children’s hospitals are progressing and the re-integration of Health Estates to my Department. The project structures for the Children’s and Maternity Hospital projects are currently being enhanced through the creation of Project Safety Groups. This structure is in alignment with emerging NHS guidance for construction projects. I can confirm that the Project Safety Group for water systems on both these projects are in place with regular meetings taking place. It is my assessment that these additional measures are appropriate at this time however this will remain under continual review, and I will take further action where this becomes necessary. I can advise that the remediation project of the domestic water system at the maternity Hospital is now underway and I can assure you that the Trust and officials are acutely aware of my desire for this to be expedited. I confirm that I have tasked the Trust to explore phased introduction of patients and services to the new maternity building at the earliest opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department holds or administers Crown Indemnity for Health and Social Care Trust consultants, including Dr. Michael Watt.
answer_text: My Department holds Crown Indemnity for Health and Social Care medical and dental staff employed across our HSC covering negligence (acts or omissions) of HSC staff in the course of their employment. Crown Indemnity does not extend for claims arising from a consultant's private practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the estimate of any funding by (i) Belfast Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) his Department, towards the legal defence or representation in cases relating to neurology recall patients.
answer_text: Total fees paid to counsel representing Belfast Health and Social Care Trust in Neurology negligence cases concluded to date is approximately £260,000. My Department has not funded any part of the legal defence.For the avoidance of doubt, neither Belfast Health and Social Care Trust nor my Department has made any contribution towards the defence costs of former consultant neurologist Michael Watt in respect of Neurology Negligence cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of cases relating to neurology recall patients has his Department been a Notice Party in court proceedings.
answer_text: My Department was a Notice Party in a Judicial Review application https://www.judiciaryni.uk/judicial-decisions/2023-nikb-46. This case was a challenge to the voluntary removal of Dr Watt from the medical register. There have been no further records of any cases relating to neurology recall patients in which the Department has been a Notice Party in court proceedings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the reasons why his Department was a Notice Party to judicial review proceedings in O’Neill and McHugh v MPTS 2023 NIKB 46; and (ii) whether his Department has funded or authorised any part of the legal defence or representation arising from neurology recall cases.
answer_text: The issuance of proceedings against any party is a matter for the plaintiffs, my Department has no control in this regard. My Department has not funded any part of the legal defence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how he regulates surgical and non-surgical cosmetic treatments; (ii) the legislative and regulatory framework under which these operate; and (iii) any actions he has taken to update the existing framework.
answer_text: Surgical Cosmetic TreatmentsThe HSC does not routinely commission surgical procedures for purely cosmetic reasons.  Such surgeries may be offered however, for example, as a result of an emergency or via elective care where significant physical or psychological health reasons exist, and may include plastic and reconstructive surgery services. In terms of regulation and ensuring the quality of such services, these are subject to all extant requirements on HSC Trust providers to ensure safe and effective care. This includes for example: •	The Health and Personal Social Services (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 imposes a Statutory Duty of Quality on HSC Trusts for the quality of care they provide or commission. It is therefore primarily for HSC Trusts in the first instance to have in place adequate and effective clinical governance, risk management, quality and patient safety systems, and to be assured that these are operating robustly in practice, in order to identify, address and act on quality concerns and drive improvements. •	The day-to-day arrangements to provide quality care is the responsibility of the provider, namely the Trust. Trusts employ professional staff in specific clinical governance roles with an emphasis on quality and safety of care. These include the Medical Director, Director of Nursing, Acute Specialty Clinical Directors and Leads, and Infection Control lead.  The Trust’s Board, made up of Executive and Non-Executive Directors, has an overarching responsibility for clinical and corporate governance - this includes patient safety.•	In terms of clinical practice, the practice of for example an individual doctor or nurse sit within various, layered and integrated systems of governance aimed at ensuring safe and effective care. In relation to healthcare professionals, individual practice will be governed by effective professional governance arrangements within Trusts complying with the regulatory standards as set by the relevant regulatory body (for example, GMC, NMC, HCPC requirements and professional standards, supervision etc), and this sits within wider organisational governance (for example, performance and HR policies). There are also organisational governance and escalation systems in place for identifying and addressing concerns in relation to non-clinical teams and individuals.Commissioners are responsible for the safety and quality of services they commission on behalf of their population. My Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) is the commissioner for health and social care services in Northern Ireland - noting that a Trust may also be commissioner of services, as well as a provider. SPPG has in place arrangements for monitoring and improving the quality of health and social care services. This takes the form of a range of interventions and approaches including regular performance and service improvement checkpoint meetings, the use of external organisations to

This was a joint venture between Acute Services and Adult Learning Disability Teams. This room is specifically for individuals with a diagnosed learning disability and/or sensory processing difficulties and/or autism. The room is situated in a quiet area in the Emergency Department and is equipped with sensory equipment for adults and children that need a space to relax before or after hospital appointments.The Carrick 2 Ward, at Holywell Hospital, has a dedicated multi - sensory room which provides a safe space to support individuals with learning disabilities, autism and/or sensory processing needs who may present with behaviours that challenge whilst in the care of the Carrick 2 Ward. The Unit has plans to continue to develop outside space with sensory equipment. There are also plans to develop a sensory room in Hollybank short break unit. Each Adult Day Care Centre within NHSCT is equipped with a dedicated sensory room designed to support adults with a Learning Disability. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how a failsafe role has been put into operation by the new regional cervical screening laboratory.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) advise that the failsafe function has been centralised within the Regional Cervical Cytology service and addresses all actions required for all Trusts.  The direct referral and failsafe procedures are modelled on the NHS England Cervical Screening professional guidance documents https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/cervical-screening-cytology-reporting-failsafe/cervical-screening-failsafe-guidance The Regional Cervical Cytology service is responsible for:Transferring test results and recommendations for management to the call and recall system using standard result and action codes.  This is an automated process where results are fed from the Regional NIPIMS laboratory information management system directly to the Public Health Agency (PHA) Call / Recall office using agreed and standardised reporting codes.Notifying the sample taker and GP (or responsible clinician) of test results and recommendations for management.  This is completed by an automated electronic transfer of all results to the sample taker or GP practice or responsible clinician via the Regional NIPIMS laboratory information management system to the electronic GP systems or to the regional encompass system. Operating a direct referral system for participants who need a referral to colposcopy.  This will include daily communication with each colposcopy clinic.  This is completed by the service via the Direct Referral policy, all cases with a positive outcome, and are therefore suspended from the screening programme, will be notified daily from the regional Failsafe Office to responsible parties in all Trusts.  Daily communication with patient ID and outcomes allows for Trust Colposcopy service to make appointments for women via encompass.The Trust also runs an urgent direct referral system, which will urgently refer women with the most significant positive outcomes to the Trust’s Colposcopy service and will also directly notify the women’s GP, due to the rapid nature of this assessmentInforming GPs and responsible clinicians about participants who require referral for colposcopy.  This is undertaken via the electronic transfer of the report via the Regional NIPIMS laboratory information management system to the electronic GP systems or encompass, and there is an additional rapid manual process for urgent directly referring cases. Making sure that histology results are collated with cytology results.  The Trust is working with the PHA to fully automate this process - at present this is a manual process to locate and assign all follow-up histopathology results with the initiation cytology result.  This activity is completed by the use of a Regional computer system hosted by PHA called CYRES.  This software is also used for other failsafe activities and records.  The regional service also follows the guidance below associated with failsafe. Keep a record of all test results which are subject to laboratory failsafe - NIPIMS, CYRES,

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) number of; and (ii) percentage of, patients who had cervical screening test results authorised (a) within two weeks; (b) within two to four weeks; (c) within four to six weeks; (d) within six to eight weeks; (e) within eight to ten weeks; (f) within ten to twelve weeks; and (g) over twelve weeks, in each of the last twelve months.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) number of; and (ii) percentage of, participants, in each of the last 12 months, who have received letters from the regional cervical screening service within 14 days, as per the Northern Ireland Cervical Screening Programme minimum standard.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, responsibility for informing women of their cervical smear test results remains with the GP. Therefore, no participants will have received letters from the regional cervical screening service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) average; and (ii) longest, waiting time for cervical screening test results to be authorised, in each of the last 12 months.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the date on which it was decided to introduce HPV primary screening; and (ii) a timeline of the necessary actions subsequently taken in advance of its introduction.
answer_text: A Ministerial policy decision was made in March 2022. In September 2023 a digital lead was appointed to investigate a solution for introducing primary HPV (pHPV) testing into the screening pathway.  During October 2023 this involved an evaluation of the problem and development of an agreed technical solution with key stakeholders.  In November 2023 technical implementation and testing of the solution with all key stakeholders took place.  In early December 2023 testing was completed successfully, and primary pHPV testing was introduced into the Northern Ireland Cervical Screening Programme on 11 December 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of cervical screening samples were (i) processed; and (ii) reported in Northern Ireland, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Over 195,000 current recipients of Universal Credit in Northern Ireland will be eligible for automatic passporting to Help with Health Costs from 1 December 2025. The underlying threshold eligibility criteria for the Help with Health Costs have not changed, rather, some individuals who may have previously accessed support through the Low-Income Scheme will now be automatically passported. As a result, it is not possible to accurately estimate the number of individuals who will benefit from the reinstatement of automatic passporting, or to provide a reliable assessment of the overall impact on cost or service delivery.The Department of Health does not have access to data on Universal Credit recipients so therefore we are not in a position to provide any more detailed breakdown.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the extent to which cervical screeners in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, found to be underperforming from 2008 onwards, were operating as the checkers for other screeners.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) has advised that two screeners in the SHSCT, found to be underperforming from 2008 onwards, were carrying out checking duties as part of their work.All cervical cytology slides deemed negative or inadequate by the primary screener should have a rapid review by another screener. All abnormal slides at primary screening will have a full rescreen by another screener (checker).  As part of quality control processes, some cytology slides deemed negative or inadequate will also undergo a full rescreen by a checker.   The checker role relates to a laboratory process control measure and therefore is a relatively small proportion of a screener's workload.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of occasions that the NHS Cervical Screening Programme Gynaecological Cytopathology External Quality Assessment found the performance of a screener in Northern Ireland to be outside the acceptable range, since 2008, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Gynaecological Cytopathology External Quality Assessment (referred to as EQA) scheme has been in place since 2015. The scheme centres on the assessment of slide sets by all staff who report cervical cytology on behalf of the Cervical Screening Programme (CSP).  The table below sets out the number of screeners whose performance was outside of acceptable range in EQA since 2015.HSC TrustNumber of screeners YearNorthern Trust12016/2017Belfast Trust12018/2019Southern Trust0N/AWestern Trust0N/AAny performance notification was managed in accordance with national guidance and internal governance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) adverse incidents; (ii) near misses; and (iii) learning events, within the cervical screening programme reported by Health and Social Care Trusts to the Public Health Agency, in each quarter since 2008.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) advise that correspondence was issued to all service providers in July 2014 to advise that a new protocol was introduced from April 2014, requiring quarterly reporting of any learning incidents related to cervical screening. Prior to April 2014, there was no formal reporting of learning events. The PHA protocol states that the definition of a learning event is wide but can relate to potential clinical risk to the eligible population, risk to the service, adverse media attention and risk of litigation.  It must be noted that many learning events identified by screening services are isolated events and not systemic throughout the programme or posing a serious risk to the eligible population.  Learning events can apply to any part of the screening pathway. The number of learning events reported to the PHA, by HSC Trusts, for each quarter from 2012/13 is provided below. These learning events include colposcopy, laboratory, IT and admin related events. above data is captured from the events reported by Trusts directly to PHA. The Regional SAI process requires Trusts to report serious adverse incidents to the SPPG (not PHA). The PHA has advised that it does not categorise learning events so it cannot report on ‘near misses’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any support available to mainstream schools to accommodate children with special educational needs.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) provides extensive support to dedicated units within mainstream schools for children with special educational needs (SEN) through link support, universal programmes, targeted support and specialist support, as well as providing professional learning and development opportunities to aid schools in supporting pupils displaying behaviours of concern.Comprehensive funding support packages are provided by EA.  Approximate allocations range from £18k for Nursery and Primary schools to £46k for Post Primary schools.  Up to two additional SEN points are available for teaching staff which is covered by the EA central budget, as are Specialist Provision Classroom Assistants costs.Each newly set-up class receives a £3k lump sum to purchase resources alongside an initial £20k furniture and equipment budget associated with any set up works required, and annual allowance of £3k.A new enhanced support package is available to schools for the 2026/27 academic year.  This includes a Whole School Learning & Development offering where 20% of teaching staff in mainstream, and up to 5 Classroom Assistants, can avail of specific SEN training, for up to 5 days release between April and June 2026.  SENCO Release of 1 month for stabilising Specialist Provisions to embed processes plus a further 1 day per month per class is included.  Administrative Support of £10k will be provided to each school covering additional administrative tasks associated with the creation and maintenance of specialist provisions. Furthermore, 5 Principals who have implemented Specialist Provisions will be released as Principal Champions to provide support to new schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average bed occupancy levels at each mental health inpatient facility, in each month from and including April 2025.
answer_text: Adult acute mental health in-patient bed pressures have been an ongoing concern. Over the past number of years, bed occupancy levels on adult mental health wards have persistently exceeded the recommended level of 85% from the Royal College of Psychiatry. Demand for services consistently surpasses bed capacity.  The data shown below, taken from the Regional Adult Mental Health Bed Dashboard, held by SPPG, outlines the regional monthly average bed occupancy level.April 2025May 2025June 2025July 2025August 2025September2025October 2025106.9%106.9%104.6%104.5%104.2%103.4%103.3%Trusts do have systems in place for collating data in regards to occupancy levels within their mental health facilities. However, transition to the digital health care system, Encompass, has brought changes and new ways of collating information, which staff require time to familiarise with. As a result, some Trusts are currently going through a process of validation and quality assurance exercises on data being collated. Until this process is complete, there may be gaps in the data that Trusts are able to supply for reporting purposes, which is reflected in the individual responses from Trusts.Belfast Health and Social Care Trust ResponseIn addition to the Acute Inpatient Mental Health Centre (AMHIC), the Belfast Trust is host to the regional medium secure unit, Shannon Ward, and locked rehabilitation, Clare Ward. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount of its capital allocation for 2025-26 has his Department sought to have converted to resource spend.
answer_text: The Health & Social Care sector is facing severe financial pressures in the current financial year, which are expected to continue over the medium term.  In response, there will be a need to consider a wide range of measures to secure financial sustainability.The Department of Health to date has not made formal arrangements to seek a switch between its capital and revenue budget in the 2025-26 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the dates on which each version of the McBride-Hill Report was received by his Department; and (ii) the subsequent engagement and actions which occurred before the final document was arrived at and published.
answer_text: Mr McBride and Dr Hill provided an initial draft of their report relating to Belfast Trust culture, governance and leadership to the Minister on 3 October which was discussed at a meeting on 7 October. It was stressed that Mr McBride had scheduled final staff interviews during week commencing 7 October and updates/changes to the report would be considered following completion of these interviews. The next iteration was received on 10 October and following receipt of Departmental comments on 16 October, and on Mr McBride’s return from a week’s leave, the final report was shared with the Minister on 20 October and publicly shared on 22 October.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the IT costs for (i) his Department; and (ii) each of its arm's-length bodies, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: See below for the costs associated to IT systems in the last 5 years, broken down by ALB. The breakdown excludes revenue elements for services such as managed print and mobile phones and focuses solely on systems-related costs. Additionally, all salaries both revenue and capitalised have been excluded, as have intangible assets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail a breakdown of all potential additional costs estimated to arise from the Adult Protection Bill, during the first ten years of its operation.
answer_text: The draft Adult Protection Bill will cost approximately £12 million in year one and £120.8 million over the first ten years of implementation.  I have attached a breakdown of the costs over the 10-year period.The business case for the Bill was approved in March 2025 on the basis that the Bill would be introduced but implementation delayed until budget was available. A key factor in determining if additional costs will be incurred is the length of delay in introducing different elements of the Bill.  For example, the biggest cost in implementing the Bill is around the recruitment of Adult Protection Social Workers associated with implementing the new powers within the Bill. These costs were calculated for the period 2024-25 to 2033/34 (approx. £86 million) but would need to be revised if there were significant delays in introducing this element of the Bill.The Adult Safeguarding Transformation Board will determine the order in which elements of the Bill should be implemented, taking into account available funding alongside policy and other considerations, including views from the Committee for Health.  Individual business cases will then be developed for each element to be introduced which will involve updating costs if required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any further consideration he has given to instigating a public or fully-independent inquiry into the issues with the new Maternity Hospital accepted for handover by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust in March 2024, including the performance and actions of senior management in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust and his Department.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of HPV tests carried out in (i) Belfast; and (ii) Gateshead, as part of contingency arrangements since the Belfast laboratory received accreditation.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) advise that a total of 30,832 samples have been tested from the date the laboratory received accreditation.As of 24 October 2025, a total of 11,015 samples have been tested at Gateshead.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of staff working in the Belfast laboratory fully trained to perform HPV testing.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) advise that the Regional Laboratory employs 10 full time equivalent posts associated with HPV testing over a range of grades, roles and responsibilities.  This is broken down as follows:4 Band 3 medical laboratory assistants,3 Band 4 biomedical support staff,2.5 Band 5 biomedical scientists, and0.5 Band 8a biomedical scientist lead.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the technical problems with equipment that's impacting on HPV testing in Belfast; (ii) when they were first detected; and (iii) what actions were taken.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) advise that at present the supplier is unable to provide a root cause analysis of the current issue encountered with the equipment. The suppliers are actively engaged in the isolation and repair of all identified issues.BHSCT have advised that the first issue was identified and reported to the supplier on the 15 August 2025. This was addressed at the time with the equipment returned to service. However, an additional range of issues led to the equipment being stood down on 5 September 2025.  BHSCT advise that when the first issue was identified the supplier was immediately contacted to undertake maintenance and repair on the 15 August 2025. The Trust has worked with the supplier from this date to try to rectify the issue and return the equipment to service.  As per Trust protocol, the issue was internally reported on the Trust’s Quality Management system and notification issued to laboratory management 18 August 2025. Due to the ongoing issues and lack of immediate resolution this was then escalated within the Trust to Senior Management and onwards to Public Health Agency on 29 August 2025.  The Trust’s contingency arrangement was enacted on 23 September 2025, due to total loss of capacity on 5 September 2025. This delay in enacting the contingency arrangement was due to anticipation that the issues with the analysers would be rectified sooner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) average; and (ii) longest, current waiting time for an adult autism assessment, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the percentage his Department has invested of the £1.2 billion his predecessor stated was required to implement the Mental Health Strategy.
answer_text: Existing departmental resources, wherever possible, have been used to support progress on the Strategy’s actions.The Northern Ireland Mental Health Strategy: A Review of the deliverability of the Strategy’s Actions 2026-2029, published on 9 October, reported that a total of £12.3 million has been utilised across 20 Mental Health Strategy actions up to the end of 2024/25. This represents just 16% of the funding identified as necessary (£76.93m) to deliver the Strategy over that period.  There has also been Capital spend of £4.7m from 2020/21 to 2023/24 for the Northern Trust Mental Health Inpatient Unit.Total Mental Health Strategy spend figures for 2025/26 will not be available until the end of the financial year. However, as of end September 2025 £5.9 million in recurrent funding has been allocated, and a further £157 000 of non-recurrent spend has been recorded.  While additional non-recurrent expenditure may be incurred this financial year, these amounts are expected to be immaterial, and final figures remain subject to confirmation. When considered alongside funding utilised to date, as detailed in the aforementioned report, the current allocation and spend position accounts for only 13% of the total estimated requirement (£138.42 million) for the full period from the Strategy’s publication to the conclusion of the 2025/26 financial year.

answer_text: No additional funding has been provided to my Department to support implementation of the Strategy. As a result, existing departmental resources have been used to support progress on the Strategy’s actions.The Northern Ireland Mental Health Strategy: A Review of the deliverability of the Strategy’s Actions 2026-2029, published on 9 October, reported that a total of £12.3 million has been utilised across 20 Mental Health Strategy actions up to the end of 2024/25. This represents just 16% of the funding identified as necessary (£76.93m) to deliver the Strategy over that period.  The breakdown by financial year is as follows:YearActual Expenditure (£)2022/231,529,2002023/244,630,0002024/256,146,000There has also been capital spend of £4.7m from 2020/21 to 2023/24 for the Northern Trust Mental Health Inpatient Unit.Total Mental Health Strategy spend figures for 2025/26 will not be available until the end of the financial year. However, as of end September 2025 £5.9 million in recurrent funding has been allocated, and a further £157 000 of non-recurrent spend has been recorded.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail on how many of the 35 actions of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 has implementation commenced.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any clinical advice he or his Department has received stating that an interim mother and baby unit could not be provided safely.
answer_text: The clinical advice I have received to date indicates that the current proposed model, delivered through integrated community and acute services, is the safest and most appropriate option available at present while work continues to establish a permanent Mother and Baby Unit in Northern Ireland.The provision of a temporary facility in Northern Ireland was considered prior to the development of a business case and it was deemed this was not feasible at the time, because none of the five Trusts could provide a suitable location.However, I have directed officials to revisit the feasibility of an interim specialist unit pending the delivery of the Regional Mother and Baby Unit. As part of these determinations, my officials will, amongst other things, consider the outworkings of the temporary provision at Exeter and South Wales as well as feedback from those involved in the delivery of these temporary units.  This will enable officials to identify whether it is possible to deliver similar provision in Northern Ireland. I remain committed to evidence-based decision-making and any option taken forward must provide a safe, appropriate environment for those women requiring inpatient care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of restricted procedures performed; and (ii) the associated costs, in each of the last twelve months, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Health whether the operation of the interim mother and baby units in South Wales and Exeter has impacted his Department’s consideration of the potential to provide a safe interim unit in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The clinical advice I have received to date indicates that the current proposed model, delivered through integrated community and acute services, is the safest and most appropriate option available at present while work continues to establish a permanent Mother and Baby Unit in Northern Ireland.The provision of a temporary facility in Northern Ireland was considered prior to the development of a business case and it was deemed this was not feasible at the time, because none of the five Trusts could provide a suitable location.However, I have directed officials to revisit the feasibility of an interim specialist unit pending the delivery of the Regional Mother and Baby Unit. As part of these determinations, my officials will, amongst other things, consider the outworkings of the temporary provision at Exeter and South Wales as well as feedback from those involved in the delivery of these temporary units.  This will enable officials to identify whether it is possible to deliver similar provision in Northern Ireland. I remain committed to evidence-based decision-making and any option taken forward must provide a safe, appropriate environment for those women requiring inpatient care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether it is the assessment of his Department that existing mental health provision is a clinically safer option for mothers and their babies than the establishment of an interim specialist unit.
answer_text: Improving perinatal mental health services remains a top priority for my Department. In the absence of a dedicated Mother and Baby Unit, we have significantly enhanced existing provision through the establishment of specialist community perinatal mental health teams, improved staff training, and strengthened care pathways and crisis support.I have, however, asked officials to revisit the feasibility of an interim specialist unit in Northern Ireland. While current clinical advice suggests this is not ideal, my officials are working at pace to identify whether there may be a workable short-term solution. I remain committed to evidence-based decision-making and any option taken forward must provide a safe, appropriate environment for those women requiring inpatient care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, since the commencement of the construction of the new Maternity Hospital in Belfast, to detail (i) each extension of time that was requested; (ii) the reason for the extension request; (iii) who made the request; (iv) how much time it added; and (v) the estimated cost of each extension.
answer_text: Extensions of Time (EoTs) are dealt with and assessed under the conditions of the construction contract under which there are aspects of commercial confidentiality. This response provides an overview and totals in relation to your request.Since the commencement of the construction of the New Maternity Hospital in Belfast, there have been sixteen EoT requests made under the contract.The reasons for the EoT requests included Change Control Orders (CCOs) and Project Manager’s Instructions (PMIs) regarding;Fire safety matters; Compliance with Building Control requests;Works on adjacent parts of the Royal Victoria Hospital site which had an impact on the Maternity Hospital site (e.g., underground ducts, flues); Covid-19 related site closure and delays; Amendments to internal layouts, ventilation and bedhead arrangements including those impacted by Medical Gas installations, internal ducting and cabling;Time taken to approve systems such as Nurse Call and Emergency Lighting systems and to confirm sitewide existing medical gas capacity;Delay in receipt of comments from the Approved Person (AP) in relation to medical gases;Delays to medical gas inspection and commissioning;Water-on delay; andDomestic water systems splashing remediation.Each EoT request was made by the contractor.The total time requested by the contractor under EoTs 1-16 inclusive amounted to 257.86 weeks. The total time awarded to the contractor amounted to 183.7 weeks.The estimated cost associated with the total of EoTs awarded is c. £17.4m.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount that his Department has contributed to (i) the School of Nursing and Midwifery at Queens University Belfast; (ii) its severance schemes; and (iii) individual severance packages, in each of the last ten years.

answer_text: As an autonomous institution with charitable status, Queen’s University Belfast (QUB) is responsible for its own workforce policies and procedures, including those related to arrangements such as severance packages.Consequently, my Department does not hold the information sought.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of missed appointments, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The below table details the number of missed outpatient appointments for each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust. Table 1: Outpatient face-to-face missed appointments by HSC Trust 2020/21 – 2024/25 Year BHSCT NHSCT SEHSCT SHSCT WHSCT Total 2020/21 24,554 5,515 9,364 5,638 9,685 54,756 2021/22 40,298 9,317 19,163 10,445 14,113 93,336 2022/23 46,638 10,404 20,548 12,974 17,045 107,609 2023/241 44,401 9,120 19,087 13,738 17,774 104,120 2024/252 44,761 9,837 16,714 13,158 17,044 101,514 Total 200,652 44,193 84,876 55,953 75,661 461,335 1Data from 9 November 2023 for the South Eastern Trust was sourced from encompass. 2Data for the South Eastern Trust, Belfast Trust from 6 June 2024 and Northern Trust 7 November 2024 was sourced from encompass. Data sourced from encompass is classed as "Official Statistics in Development" whilst undergoing evaluation. As a result, users should exercise some caution with this data. To help reduce the number of DNAs (Did Not Attend) and cancellations, minimise any inconvenience to patients, and reduce costs to the health service, Health and Social Care Trusts have implemented text messaging reminders for upcoming appointments. These reminders not only prompt patients to attend, but also provides an opportunity to cancel or reschedule in adequate time if they are unable to attend. This ensures that valuable appointment slots can be offered to other patients.Alongside this, work is ongoing to raise public awareness of the impact of missed appointments on the health service, highlighting how DNAs contribute to longer waiting times and reduced access to care for others. Together, these measures aim to improve attendance, support better use of resources, and enhance patient care.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail agency costs for (i) their Department; and (ii) each of its arm's-length bodies, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Agency costs for (i) the Executive Office; and (ii) each of its arm's-length bodies in each of the last five years are detailed in the table below:Year2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25TotalTEO£746,108£1,454,495£2,459,202	£1,905,595£1,878,870£8,444,270COSICA-£90,868£193,606	£58,264£125,739£468,477MLKDC£37,778£49,691£125,789£106,377£129,097£448,732SIB£52,164£138,658£201,876£88,363£172,341£653,402CRC£17,520£24,842£70,410£186,359£141,064£440,195CVSNI£259,670£169,083£71,878£92,072£40,213£632,916VSS£201,709£457,614£661,826£320,344£236,166£1,877,659NIJAC£115,063£109,858-£18,725£22,088£265,734ECNI£204,071£307,921£167,620£299,421£340,596£1,319,629Total£1,634,083£2,803,030£3,952,207£3,075,520£3,086,174In relation to TEO, the costs above include:- Temporary staff engaged through employment agencies;- Temporary postings via the Interchange Scheme from partner organisations with relevant skilled staff. (Interchange is an NICS HR administered scheme designed to promote and encourage the interchanging of people, information, ideas and good practice between the public, private, voluntary and social economy sectors);- NI Statistical Research Agency staff (DoF employees) - Seconded staff from NI Courts and Tribunal Service to the Victims’ Payments Redress Board.Within the NICS, agency staff are engaged under a central Agency Workers Protocol that was procured by CPD and is managed by NICS HR.  Rates are set through competitive public procurement in line with NICS regulations.  Departments and arm’s-length bodies use this contract when business needs arise, mainly due to vacancies or work priorities.  Roles match substantive posts and require job descriptions. NICS HR is working to reduce reliance on agency staff through permanent recruitment and workforce planning.  A number of recruitment competitions are in progress, including large-scale campaigns for key grades such as Grade 6, Grade 7, Administrative Officer (AO) and Executive Officer 2 (EO2). Departments are encouraged to prioritise permanent appointments over temporary solutions wherever possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration has been given to commissioning a community-based ear wax removal service within primary care audiology pathways.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) when the Adult Audiology Task and Finish Group is expected to report on its recommendations; and (ii) whether this will include an assessment of the viability of a mixed model of provision, including community-based audiology providers.
answer_text: In recognition of the need to improve waiting times for audiology across the HSC, officials in my Department have established a Task and Finish Group. This group will determine the current funded capacity and demand for audiology services, along with opportunities to improve existing care pathways.The Task and Finish Group is expected to complete its first phase later this autumn, after which it will seek to address any capacity gaps identified. My Department will consider all feasible options to address any capacity gap in the service. This includes the option of providing a mixed model of care, whereby audiology services are also delivered in both the primary and community sectors. As with the development of any new service, introducing a mixed model of care will require additional funding, which is not yet available. Although Trusts currently have a number of audiologist vacancies, withdrawing Trust resources from the HSC to deliver community audiology services would undermine existing HSC service provision. As you will be aware Trust audiology services are a vital component of the overall ENT service.Several discussions have taken place with Primary and Community Audiology Providers, including recent engagement with the National Community Hearing Association (NCHA), which has agreed to develop a proposal for a small pilot. This pilot will demonstrate how a primary or community model would interact with the wider service and outline the likely challenges and benefits of such a model.However, it should be noted that if new funding is identified for primary and community activity and procurement, any change would likely require a lead-in time. The HSC would need to undertake a comprehensive procurement exercise to provide the service which would take several months. Officials continue to work at pace towards a viable solution within the resources currently available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients who have travelled to Great Britain for CAR-T therapy, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Below are the number of patients from NI who have been approved for CAR-T therapy in the last five years.  To note, after approval, some of these patients may not have been treated for medical reasons.  Release of information in respect of a very small volume of patients creates the potential for patients to be identified and would breach HSC confidentiality responsibilities.  The extensive media coverage of CAR-T in recent months has the potential to increase the risk of breach as the wider public may have an increased interest in the therapy. To protect patient confidentiality the number of patients approved for CAR-T have therefore been grouped as follows:Calendar YearNumber of Approvals2020/2021/2022282023/202441The larger volumes in the latter years’ category are consistent with the publication of additional NICE recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an estimate of the number of patients who could benefit from CAR-T therapy each year.
answer_text: Patients from Northern Ireland have access to CAR-T treatments as per circular “HSC(SQSD) 12-22 – NICE Technology Appraisals – Process for Endorsement, Implementation, Monitoring and Assurance in NI” which supports the timely introduction of effective new medicines.If CAR-T treatments are approved by NICE then patients in NI will be granted access to them on an equitable basis with other nations through the arrangements in place for referral to trusted centres in the wider NHS.  It is therefore not possible to provide an accurate assessment of the numbers of patients who may benefit from CAR-T Therapy going forward each year as future numbers will be highly dependent on an new National Institute for Clinical Excellence (NICE) positive appraisals and the number of patients eligible for these.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when CAR-T cell therapy be rolled out.
answer_text: Patients from Northern Ireland have access to CAR-T treatments as per circular “HSC(SQSD) 12-22 – NICE Technology Appraisals – Process for Endorsement, Implementation, Monitoring and Assurance in NI” which supports the timely introduction of effective new medicines.If CAR-T treatments are approved by NICE then patients in NI will be granted access to them on an equitable basis with other nations through the arrangements in place for referral to trusted centres in the wider NHS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the most recent workforce plan for anaesthetics was published.
answer_text: The Medical Workforce Planning Report for Anaesthetics and Intensive Care Medicine 2016 – 2026 was accepted by my Department in December 2016.  The Department is currently arranging to publish it and other historically accepted strategic workforce review reports on the Departmental website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail a timeframe for the publication of referral to treatment waiting times, as provided elsewhere in the UK.
answer_text: My Department fully supports the development of a referral to treatment target. This target will require patients to be tracked from when they are referred from their GP and onwards through all subsequent assessment diagnostic and treatment episodes. Such tracking will require a clear and succinct set of rules and well-defined definitions from referral to treatment. From a technical perspective, when the new regional encompass system is through its stabilisation stage next year, Northern Ireland would be better placed to initiate work to measure the full patient journey.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department’s assessment of the impact of anaesthetic workforce shortages on paediatric anaesthesia.
answer_text: Additional paediatric anaesthetists and intensivists are required in Northern Ireland to help reduce waiting lists, expand theatre capacity, pre-assessment clinics and chronic pain services for children.  To expand theatre capacity would also require additional anaesthetic theatre nurses and other relevant surgeons (paediatric surgeons, gastroenterologists, ENT, ophthalmology consultants etc).There are currently eleven specialist paediatric anaesthetists in the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC) who are skilled in working on the most complex and smallest children.  Elective Care Funding will enable a further two anaesthetists to be employed in RBHSC.  This will increase anaesthetic capacity.  While this additional funding will help address some of the anaesthetic service gap it will not address it all.District General Hospital (DGH) employ general adult anaesthetists who deal with the general population.  However, these anaesthetists can train to receive specific skills to work with children.  These clinicians provide anaesthetic capacity for less complex and older/larger children.  This enables DGHs to carry out less complex procedures locally while RBHSC focuses on the most complex and regional cases.It is essential that the region works collaboratively to ensure that children and young people receive the best care in the most appropriate location.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of healthcare-associated infections, in each of the last five years, broken down by (i) Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) facility.
answer_text: Healthcare associated infections (HCAIs) are infections that develop while receiving medical care in hospitals, care homes or other health facilities. These can include blood stream infections and Clostridioides difficile infections. More information about HCAIs is available at https://www.publichealth.hscni.net/directorate-public-health/health-protection/healthcare-associated-infections Trusts are required to report to cases of Methicillin-sensitive and Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA and MRSA) blood stream infections, Gram-negative blood stream infections and Clostridioides difficile infections to PHA. PHA follows a statistical disclosure small cell policy to avoid revealing information about individual patients. This means that breakdown of information into smaller units (e.g. by hospital) requires suppression of small numbers that could pose a risk to privacy of individuals.  Information has been supplied by Trust only, as breakdowns by hospital are not usable after application of statistical disclosure control.Healthcare-associated (HCA) bloodstream infections Health and Social Care Trusts report positive blood culture infections with the following organisms to PHA: Gram negative bloodstream infections (GNBs) - Escherichia coli (E. coli), Klebsiella oxytoca, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Klebsiella sp and Pseudomonas aeruginosa.Gram positive bloodstream infections - Methicillin-sensitive and Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA and MRSA).Toxin-positive culture results for Clostridioides difficile are also reported.Healthcare associated (HCA) infections are those with a specimen date greater than or equal to two days post admission to a hospital facility.Table 1 outlines the total number (healthcare-associated and non-healthcare-associated) of MSSA, MRSA, GNB bloodstream and Clostridioides difficile infections reported to the Public Health Agency during the period January 2020- September 2025.Table 1:  Total episodes of MSSA, MRSA, GNB blood stream and Clostridioides difficile infections, by Health and Social Care Trust, Northern Ireland, 2020-2025OrganismHealth and Social Care Trust202020212022202320242025Methicillin Susceptible Staphylococcus Aureus (MSSA)Belfast130168177171207108Northern616476877360South Eastern73946411010577Southern606268616368Western454657446134 Grand Total369434442473509347OrganismHealth and Social Care Trust202020212022202320242025Methicillin Resistant Staphylococcus Aureus (MRSA)Belfast19131321168Northern128111286South Eastern77= 5913= 5Southern= 5= 510= 5= 5= 5Western798= 5= 5= 5 Grand Total---504522OrganismHealth and Social Care Trust202020212022202320242025Gram Negative Bloodstream Infections (GNBs)                     Includes E.coli, Pseudomonas aerguginosa and Klebsiella spp.Belfast595602623615614422Northern404415416508424324South Eastern302316322374367299Southern276313311332367342Western243264287285268159 Grand Total182019101959211420401546OrganismHealth and Social Care Trust202020212022

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has given further consideration to an independent review or inquiry into the new Maternity Hospital and other recent major capital projects within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline over what time period the Doctor and Dentist Case Review mechanism has operated, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health whether the Doctor and Dentist Case Review mechanism is still in operation in the health and social care sector.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the processes applied by Health and Social Care Trusts when individuals on waiting lists for either outpatient appointment or admission in paediatrics and paediatric surgery reach the upper age limit for that service.
answer_text: The approach to paediatric care varies across Trusts and can depend on individual circumstances. Generally, in paediatric medicine, when a child is referred to a consultant paediatrician before their 16th birthday, their first appointment will be offered by a paediatrician—even if the appointment date falls after they turn 16. However, if a referral is made after the child’s 16th birthday, some Trusts will still offer a paediatric appointment, while others will transfer the referral to adult services. In some cases, the decision is based on the nature of the patient’s condition.Paediatric surgery follows a similar pattern. Some Trusts begin the transition process as early as age 13, while others wait until age 16. Regardless of the transition age, the focus remains on ensuring that the child’s care is not adversely affected during the move to adult services.For both elective and non-elective admissions to inpatient paediatric wards, children are typically admitted up to their 16th birthday. However, Trusts may continue to manage patients with severe, complex, or life-limiting conditions within paediatric services—particularly when the patient has a long-standing relationship with the paediatric team or when specialist expertise lies within paediatrics (e.g. certain neuro-disability or cancer diagnoses).As part of transformation funding in 2019, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency provided additional investment, protocols, and support to ensure Trusts are aware of their responsibilities and can deliver age-appropriate care for children and young people across Northern Ireland.Although funding was made available to assist with Age Appropriate Care and all Trusts now treat children up to the age of 16, local Trust nuances exist in relation to transitions but with the new CHP Programme Board and Steering Group in place now the service will be seeking to introduce regional consistency across Trusts across a range of issues, including this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the costs resulting from a Health and Social Care Trust postponing an outpatient appointment (i) day case; and (ii) admission.
answer_text: Outpatient appointments and scheduled treatments can be postponed for a variety of reasons including the date no longer suits the patient, the patient has already been admitted to hospital, or the consultant is no longer available. When a Trust is notified that a patient’s appointment or surgery date needs to be postponed, they will normally schedule another patient or alternatively offer the clinic capacity or theatre list to another consultant and therefore no financial loss may occur. As such, it would be inappropriate and misleading to use a unit cost to extrapolate the total cost for missed appointments. However, as an indication, the Northern Ireland average marginal unit costs of attendances are below:Marginal Outpatient Cost: £220Day Case: £988Inpatient Procedures: £6,202
