question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when (i) the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) his Department, were formally advised that BAM had withdrawn from the Children’s Hospital project.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust and my Department were formally advised that BAM had withdrawn from the New Children’s Hospital Project on 18th March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department has delivered for Upper Bann since he took office in February 2024.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the events which occurred between BAM withdrawing from the Children’s Hospital project and Graham Construction signing the contract.
answer_text: The following actions took place between the 18 March 2025 when BAM formally withdrew from the project and the 20 March 2025 when John Graham Construction Limited signed the construction contract:18 March 2025 – John Graham Construction Limited were issued with the original Pre-Qualification Questionnaire 1 document for completion by them to allow the Department of Health and the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to evaluate same to ensure that John Graham Construction Limited still met all the PQQ 1 requirements in their own right.19 March 2025 – John Graham Construction Limited submitted a completed PQQ1 with appropriate appendices.19 March 2025 – John Graham Construction Limited PQQ1 submission issued to an evaluation panel consisting of Health Estates Programme Director, Health Estates Project Manager and the BHSCT Director of Strategic Development.19 March 2025 – The evaluation panel each individually assessed the submission and confirmed that John Graham Construction Limited met the requirements of the PQQ1.  Health Estates Director ran Constructionline Report which confirmed that John Graham Construction Limited had a constructionline notation value in excess of £30M.19 March 2025 – Contract Award Letter issued by Health Estates on behalf of Belfast Health & Social Care Trust confirming that John Graham Construction Limited have been evaluated against the criteria contained in the PQQ1 and their request to restructure the Economic Operator Team had been accepted.20 March 2025 – John Graham Construction Limited signed the contract.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) whether he continues to consider additional bed capacity at Craigavon Area Hospital as described in his Department’s Draft Budget 2025-26 Equality Impact Assessment, as a contractual commitment; and (ii) the amount he will allocate to this in 2025-26.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust continues to develop the long-term plans for the site redevelopment programme at Craigavon Area Hospital (CAH), and additional bed capacity is being considered as part of this.  Due to the scale of the programme the project will be broken down into phases, and the Trust is currently refining the preferred phased redevelopment approach at CAH. The substantial projected cost of the programme means that affordability will be a key issue, in the context of the challenging financial environment and the scale of demand for other capital investment projects in the Health and Social Care sector.Following the announcement of the outcome of the UK wide Spending Review 2025 in June, the Department of Finance commissioned a Budget exercise that will determine the capital budgets for Northern Ireland Department’s for the years 2026-27 to 2029-30. My ability to commit to funding capital projects, including the CAH redevelopment project, will depend on my Department’s capital allocation from the Budget process. The draft Budget outcome is not expected to be confirmed until later this year and will inform the development of the Department of Health (DoH) capital investment plan which will set out the projects to be taken forward over the next ten years. The projects included under the Contractually Committed sub-heading in the DoH Draft Budget 2025-26 Equality Impact Assessment included both fully committed projects and those that had made less progress through the approvals process which are classified as being partially committed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what actions were taken by (i) the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) his Department, to ensure Graham Construction being confirmed as contractor after BAM withdrew from the Children’s Hospital project, would meet all procurement, legal and best practice requirements.
answer_text: The following actions took place between the 18th March 2025 when BAM formally withdrew from the project and the 20th March 2025 when John Graham Construction Limited signed the construction contract:18th March 2025 – John Graham Construction Limited were issued with the original Pre-Qualification Questionnaire 1 document for completion by them to allow the Department of Health and the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to evaluate same to ensure that John Graham Construction Limited still met all the PQQ1 requirements in their own right.19th March 2025 – John Graham Construction Limited submitted a completed PQQ1 with appropriate appendices.19th March 2025 – John Graham Construction Limited PQQ1 submission issued to an evaluation panel consisting of Health Estates Programme Director, Health Estates Project Manager and the BHSCT Director of Strategic Development.19th March 2025 – The evaluation panel each individually assessed the submission and confirmed that John Graham Construction Limited met the requirements of the PQQ1.  Health Estates Director ran Constructionline Report which confirmed that John Graham Construction Limited had a constructionline notation value in excess of £30M.19th March 2025 – Contract Award Letter issued by Health Estates on behalf of Belfast Health & Social Care Trust confirming that John Graham Construction Limited have been evaluated against the criteria contained in the PQQ1 and their request to restructure the Economic Operator Team had been accepted.20th March 2025 – John Graham Construction Limited signed the contract. BAM’s withdrawal had no bearing on legal and best practice requirements which had already been incorporated into the contract documentation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 22331/22-27, to detail why he stated in a press release on 19 February 2025 that he tasked Departmental officials to identify the key lessons from the new Maternity Hospital project and ensure that they were applied ahead of the contract signing for the new Children's Hospital.
answer_text: AQWs 17609/22-27, 20711/22-27, 22331/22-27 were in relation to potential water system changes required to be implemented on the New Children’s Hospital project.  As per AQW 22331/22-27 response published 14th February 2025 the review of the design of the water systems within the New Children’s Hospital and development of proposed amendments will not occur prior to the signing of the contract.My statement referenced on BBC website article published 19th February 2025 was in relation to improvements that I was seeking to the management of the new Children’s Hospital which I wanted to be applied prior to contract signing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when a letter of intent was issued regarding Graham BAM Healthcare Partnership building the new Children’s Hospital.
answer_text: A letter of intent to award contract for the construction of the New Children’s Hospital to Graham Bam Healthcare Partnership was issued on the 5th February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the procurement process undertaken for the construction of the new Children’s Hospital in Belfast.
answer_text: A procurement strategy for the New Children’s Hospital (NCH) was prepared by CPD Health Projects (now DoH Health Estates) and approved by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT).  Following consideration of various options, the most appropriate procurement route was determined to be the Restricted Procedure with contract award being assessed on the basis of cost alone.The pre-qualification stage established a shortlist of 5 suitably experienced contractors with the capability to deliver a project of the size and complexity of the NCH using objective criteria.One of the suitably experienced contractors shortlisted, withdrew from the procurement process on 1 December 2020.Tender documentation was issued to the remaining four suitably experienced shortlisted contractors in May 2021 with the tender return date being set as 28 November 2021 15:00hrs. Following requests from a number of tenderers the tender period was ultimately extended until 5th April 2022 15:00hrs.During the tender period three further contractors withdrew from the process for a variety of reasons at differing times ranging between 25 May 2021 and 5 April 2022.The tender submission period ended on 5 April 2022 15:00hrs with one tender received from the Graham BAM Healthcare Partnership (GBHP).It is the assertion of the CoPE, CPD Health Projects (now Health Estates), that the tender received from GBHP was a competitive tender in that the tender received was submitted in advance (5 April 2022 12:02hrs) of the late withdrawal of the other remaining tenderer (5 April 2022 14:45hrs).The tender received has been benchmarked against a range of healthcare schemes at various stages across the United Kingdom and Ireland. Whilst the cost per m2 of the new children’s hospital is 12.29% higher than the average of the benchmark schemes, it is the view of CPD Health Projects (now Health Estates) that the tender received represents value for money given the plethora of factors impacting on the construction industry at present including but not limited to:Constantly evolving impact of BREXIT and COVID 19 pandemic;Worldwide raw material shortages caused by supply/demand imbalance;Fuel and energy cost increases exacerbated by the situation in Eastern Europe;Labour rate increases resulting from supply/demand imbalance as a result of ‘pull’ of Dublin and UK mainland construction projects along with inflation driven upward wage pressure;National insurance rate increase; andRed diesel fuel levy.The impact of these inflationary factors will not have been reflected in the majority of the benchmark projects given that the base dates pre date the time period when the inflationary factors occurred.It would be the view of CPD Health Projects (now Health Estates) that the factors listed could be in excess of the 12.29% difference between the benchmark cost per m2 and the cost per m2 of the New Children’s Hospital.In summary CPD Health Projects (now Health Estates), on behalf of the BHSCT, can confirm th

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when Graham Construction signed the contract to build the new Children’s Hospital.
answer_text: John Graham Construction Limited signed the contract to build the new Children’s Hospital on 20th March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the circumstances in which Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service would move from the initial detailing of fire safety concerns to an organisation, to the invoking of formal proceedings.
answer_text: The circumstances in which NIFRS will move from detailing fire safety concerns to issuing an Enforcement Notice, are inherent to the fire safety auditing process. When an NIFRS fire safety inspecting officer completes an audit of a premises a total score is produced that determines; the overall compliance level of the premises and the corresponding action that NIFRS will take – Informal or Formal action. Fire safety audit outcomes are categorised as per Table 1 – Audit Outcome Categories.Table 1 – Audit Outcome categoriesAction OutcomeCompliance LevelDescriptionScoreInformal ActionCompliance Level 1Broadly Compliant(0-25)Informal ActionCompliance Level 2Notice of Deficiencies(26-35)Informal ActionCompliance Level 3Action Plan(36-45)Formal ActionCompliance Level 4Enforcement Notice(46-55)Formal ActionCompliance Level 5Enforcement Notice - Fast Track(>55)An enforcement notice may be considered immediately following a fire safety audit where the outcome is enforcement, or when an action plan and up to two extensions of time have expired, and it is in the public interest to proceed with a view to prosecution.Where the outcome for the fire safety audit is enforcement, an inspecting officer would firstly refer to the NIFRS Enforcement Management Model to verify or modify the outcome. If it does not require adjustment, it is at that point that the inspecting officer would then discuss the notice with a NIFRS senior fire safety inspecting officer to explain why an Enforcement Notice, in their opinion, is necessary. If the senior officer agrees, the Enforcement Notice would be drafted to be served.Before serving an Enforcement Notice, NIFRS is required to consult with the Building Control, the local council Environmental Health, the Health & Safety Executive if used as a place of work, and any other person whose consent to any alteration would be required by any statutory provision. The consultation period lasts 14 days after which the Enforcement Notice will be served on the appropriate person.Where a premises is deemed to be, in the inspecting officer’s opinion, so serious a risk to relevant persons in the event of fire, the use of the premises shall be prohibited.  A Prohibition Notice will be served on the premises by an inspecting officer, authorised by NIFRS senior management (Principal Officer).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the extent of fire safety concerns from Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service about a premises which would necessitate the (i) Health and Safety Executive; or (ii) another health and safety enforcing authority being informed of the concerns.
answer_text: If an NIFRS fire safety inspecting officer was conducting an audit of a premises whereby it scored - ‘Compliance Level 4’ or above, then the inspecting officer has identified sufficient fire safety deficiencies to require formal action to be taken. As per Article 37 (3) of the Fire Services (NI), Order, 2006, before serving an Enforcement Notice, NIFRS is required to consult with Building Control, the local council Environmental Health, the Health & Safety Executive if used as a place of work, and any other person whose consent to any alteration would be required by any statutory provision. The consultation period lasts 14 days after which the Enforcement Notice will be served on the appropriate person.Separate to the audit process described above, if NIFRS inspecting officers identify issues relating to Process Fire Precautions or, matters that are the concern of another enforcing authority, they will inform the relevant body by phone or in writing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the circumstances in which fire safety inspectors having raised concerns with an organisation move to engaging with the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service senior management about invoking a potential enforcement notice.
answer_text: If a NIFRS fire safety inspecting officer was conducting an audit of a premises whereby it scored - ‘Compliance Level 4’ or above, then the inspecting officer has identified sufficient fire safety deficiencies to require formal action to be taken, an Enforcement Notice.  An inspecting officer would firstly refer to the NIFRS Enforcement Management Model to verify or modify the outcome. If it does not require adjustment, it is at that point that the inspecting officer would then discuss the notice with a NIFRS senior fire safety inspecting officer to explain why an Enforcement Notice, in their opinion, is necessary. If the senior fire safety inspecting officer agrees, the Enforcement Notice would be drafted, signed by the inspecting officer and served by the inspecting officer.Where a premises is deemed to be, in the inspecting officer’s opinion, so serious a risk to relevant persons in the event of fire, the use of the premises shall be prohibited.  A Prohibition Notice will be served on the premises by an inspecting officer, authorised by NIFRS senior management (Principal Officer).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the involvement of officials from Health Estates in providing professional advice on the design, construction and handover of the new Regional Maternity Hospital in Belfast.
answer_text: DoH Health Estates’ role in major capital projects is documented in ‘Health Estates Directorate Governance Framework for Engagement with Health and Social Care Trusts and ALBs’ which is available on the Department’s internet site. Health Estates Directorate operates as a Centre of Specialist Expertise (CoSE) and is the Centre of Procurement Expertise (CoPE) for capital infrastructure across the healthcare estate.In the period from April 2014 to April 2023, Health Estates’ function as CoPE was transferred to the Department of Finance where CPD - Health Projects provided this service on behalf of Department of Health and its arm’s length bodies (ALBs).  Within its function, Health Estates provides professional direction and guidance to enable the ALB to act as an ‘intelligent client’ in the delivery of its capital development programme, including procurement, cost management and project management of all projects above the ALB’s delegated limit of financial authority. The Project Execution Plan for each project sets out the roles and responsibilities of project parties and is used for all above delegated limit projects. A template PEP is appended to the Health Estates Directorate Governance Framework for Engagement with Health and Social Care Trusts and ALBs.In relation to the New Maternity Hospital, I can advise that officials from Health Estates provided the roles as set out in the above Governance Framework for the design, construction and handover of the project.  Project management responsibilities for the design, construction and handover stages were set out in the Project Execution Plan.Project Management roles included: Investment Decision Maker – BHSCT BoardSenior Responsible Owner (SRO) (formerly Project Owner) -BHSCT Director of FinanceProject Steering Group- Chaired by the SROProject Board – Chaired by BHSCT Project DirectorProject Team – Project Board plus the members of the Design Team and the Construction Team.Project Director – BHSCT Co-Director of Capital Planning and RedevelopmentProject Manager (managing the design team) - Health Estates/ CPD Health ProjectsAssistant Project Director - BHSCTUser Groups - BHSCTCommissioning Groups – BHSCTAt handover, Health Estates managed the Design Team who in turn managed the construction contract and certified that construction completion was achieved.		Following project completion in March 2024, the management of the facility became the responsibility of BHSCT.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of farm businesses that would be subject to the requirement of providing a phosphorous balance based on 170 kg rather than the suggested 150 kg per hectare per year of livestock manure production contained within the consultation on the Nutrients Action Programme for 2026 – 2029.
answer_text: The consultation on the Nutrients Action Programme for 2026-2029 proposed that more intensively stocked farms, with annual livestock manure nitrogen production at or above 150kg nitrogen per hectare per year, will be subject to specific measures. One of the measures is a requirement for farms to operate within a phosphorus balance limit.Based on a threshold of annual livestock manure nitrogen production at or above 170kg nitrogen per hectare per year, approximately 2985 farm businesses would be subject to the phosphorus balance requirement.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for a breakdown of the (i) number; and (ii) associated costs, of industrial tribunal payments by her (a) Department; and (b) each of its arms-length bodies, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: A breakdown of the (i) number; and (ii) associated costs, of industrial tribunal payments by (a) my Department; and (b) its arm’s length bodies, in each of the last ten financial years is outlined in the table below:FinancialYearCore Department and AgenciesArm’s Length BodiesNumber of casesCost£kNumber of casesCost£k2024-254148122712023-24188036932022-23005692021-22*104312020-21451*32019-203725472018-196581356292017-18*5582082016-1700142,8792015-16*816274	Note:‘*’ means a figure of less than 3 has been treated under rules of disclosure.		Due to the confidential nature of industrial tribunal payments, the amount spent may not always be recorded in a way that makes it possible to identify all the relevant costs. Further detailed analysis would be needed to potentially identify the relevant information. However that could only be obtained at disproportionate cost and there is no certainty that the necessary details would be available.The ten year period extends beyond the Department’s normal retention policy. As such, whilst the Department has endeavoured to answer the question in full, the response may not be complete in all instances.Some cases are classed as a group action where the claims of multiple claimants making broadly the same allegation are consolidated. Each separate group action is reported in the table as one case rather than reporting the number of claimants.The information for arm’s length bodies relates to cases within the Police Service of Northern Ireland, Northern Ireland Policing Board and Probation Board for Northern Ireland. These details have been consolidated, as disclosing the data for each arm’s length body could potentially lead to the identification of individuals due to the low number of cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount of chemical nitrogen fertiliser imported into Northern Ireland in (i)1995; and (ii) 2024.
answer_text: The total nitrogen content of artificial fertilisers purchased by the Northern Ireland agriculture sector was (i) 127,477 tonnes in 1995; and (ii) 70,123 tonnes in 2024 which is a decrease of 45 per cent.  Fertiliser purchases are influenced by various factors including market and weather conditions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) current; and (ii) former, employees of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust currently engaging in constructive dismissal proceedings.
answer_text: As at the 10 September 2025 there were no current employees and eight former employees of Belfast Health and Social Care Trust engaging in constructive dismissal proceedings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline how the responsibilities of the PSNI differ from those of police forces in (i) England; and (ii) Wales.
answer_text: While the PSNI undertakes the same core policing functions as forces in England and Wales, its responsibilities differ because of Northern Ireland’s unique political, security and accountability context.  The Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) is a unitary police service responsible for the entire region. It is overseen by the Northern Ireland Policing Board, which includes representatives from the Northern Ireland Assembly and independent members. The Board is responsible for appointing senior officers, setting priorities, and monitoring performance.In England and Wales Policing is decentralized across 43 territorial police forces. Each force is accountable to an elected Police and Crime Commissioner who sets the force's priorities and budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he can provide a date for when the new maternity hospital at the Royal Victoria Hospital will be open and operational.
answer_text: At present, I am unable to provide a definitive date for when the new maternity hospital at the Royal Victoria Hospital will be open and operational. The primary factor contributing to the delay is the ongoing remediation of the domestic water system due to contamination with Pseudomonas Aeruginosa. The Department was advised by Belfast Trust, who confirmed that a multi-disciplinary team has completed a review of the remediation options available to address the water safety issues within the new Maternity Hospital.  The team comprised experts in microbiology, neo-natal care, midwifery, nursing, and estates.  The team reviewed three options which had been developed by the Trust, working with expert consultants. Following the conclusion of this comprehensive review by the multi-disciplinary team, the Trust has recommended the installation of a new domestic water system for the neo-natal unit, alongside localised remediation in other departments, as the most effective and proportionate solution. I have requested an independent expert review of the Trust’s decision making.  I have appointed an independent reviewer whose report is due with me later this week.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the British Pregnancy Advisory Services’ online advice to women from Northern Ireland on accessing abortion pills from overseas, given its role in the provision of abortion services in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The British Pregnancy Advisory Service (BPAS) is an independent charity which provides the main referral point into Health and Social Care (HSC) abortion services in Northern Ireland. Its website correctly advises that while Early Medical Abortion (EMA) medication is safe, even when taken outside a clinical setting, neither BPAS nor HSC Trusts are able to provide abortion pills in the post under the provisions of the Abortion (Northern Ireland) (No 2) Regulations 2020. It also contains information about external sources of EMA medication, for home use, which it considers to be reliable and genuine. My Department does not encourage the use of unregulated sources of advice or treatment for any aspect of healthcare, and has provided clear advice on how to access abortion advice and care in Northern Ireland through the NI Direct and HSC Trust websites and other literature. My Department has asked BPAS to ensure that any information included on its website is compliant with the legal framework for abortion in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether his Department has carried out an assessment of the provision of abortion pills from international providers; (ii) whether such provision is legal in Northern Ireland; and (iii) for his assessment of whether abortion statistics for Northern Ireland account for such abortions.
answer_text: My Department has not carried out an assessment of the provision of abortion medications from international providers.  Such provision is not provided for under the Abortion (Northern Ireland) (No 2) Regulations 2020 (the 2020 Regulations) and therefore does not form part of the commissioned abortion services in Northern Ireland.In Northern Ireland, abortion outside the parameters of the 2020 Regulations is unlawful and may be a criminal offence. The Regulations apply only in Northern Ireland. However, a woman in Northern Ireland cannot be prosecuted for an offence under the 2020 Regulations. The annual abortion statistics report on activity carried out within Health and Social care under the 2020 Regulations and are derived from the information provided, as required, under regulation 10 of the 2020 Regulations.  International providers of abortion medications are not governed by the 2020 Regulations, therefore any provision through these means occurs outwith the Health and Social Care system and will not be included in statistics published by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the methodology used in selecting the limit of 150kg per hectare, per year, of livestock manure production, including imports as a metric for introducing the requirement for a phosphorus balance, contained within the consultation on the Nutrients Action Programme for 2026–2029.
answer_text: My Department proposes to introduce a Farm Phosphorus Balance limit for more intensively stocked farms which are defined as those with annual livestock manure nitrogen production at and above 150kg Nitrogen/hectare/year. These limits will also apply to any farms that are producing less than 150kg Nitrogen/hectare/year but subsequently import manure to exceed this level.  The purpose of the phosphorus balance limit is to reduce surplus phosphorus and limit the buildup of excess soil phosphorus levels on farms.There are approximately 3500 farms in this 150 kg Nitrogen/hectare/year category. In total, these farms account for 17% of NI farms yet produce some 50% of total manure nitrogen. They also represent a significant proportion of surplus phosphorus production owing to their greater reliance on concentrate feed inputs. Therefore, implementing the phosphorus balance limit on these more intensively stocked farms will have the greatest environmental impact while minimising the number of farms affected. By adopting this approach, the regulatory requirement on a much greater number of lower stocked extensive farms will not be increased.The proposal that the phosphorus balance limit will apply to other farms which import manure and then exceed 150kg Nitrogen/hectare/year is to reduce the risk of lower intensity farms importing large quantities of manure which could result in a build-up of excess soil phosphorus levels.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when it was first discovered that (i) black mould was forming on plasterboard; (ii) the roof edge needed to be sealed, at the new Belfast Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre, as well as (a) who was informed and (b) what actions were taken.
answer_text: Black mould was first discovered/reported by the BHSCT Estates team on 6th February 2018/13th February 2018, prior to the building being handed over to BHSCT on 23rd June 2019. After a preliminary investigation it was determined a known leak had caused this. The source of the leak and mould were rectified by the Contractor. Dampness was further reported in January 2020 during the defects liability period and an investigation by the design team resulted in review and replacement within all affected areas by the Contractor. BHSCT Estates team expressed concerns as to multiple structural co-ordination issues, gaps in walls and roof edges not being jointed/sealed in, January/February 2018. Roof leaks were formally reported in September 2019, with the design team instructing the Contractor to carry out the remedial works, which were completed. BHSCT Estates team informed the project delivery team through a Site Survey Tracker as per the agreed contractual route. The project delivery team included representatives from the Trust Capital Redevelopment team, the CPD Health Projects team and the externally appointed design team. As per Trust governance processes on receipt of information detailing an issue this was added to the snag/defect schedule.  The snag/defect schedule was reviewed by the project delivery team at monthly meetings throughout the defects liability period.  Actions were taken to investigate and, where appropriate, remediate the issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any concerns raised about the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre building prior to its handover being accepted by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, including who raised the concerns and any actions taken.
answer_text: As is the norm for all capital construction projects a snagging and defects list for the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre was developed in conjunction with DoH Health Estates (formerly CPD-Health Projects), Trust Estates, Trust Service Leads, Trust Capital Planning and Redevelopment Team, Contractor and Design Team prior to hand over in June 2019.  All the stakeholders were allowed to add items of concern to the list for rectification.All stakeholders were updated on progress of works to address any concerns relating to snags and defects at monthly update meetings.  The snagging and defects list was a live document that was regularly updated throughout the one year defects period post-handover with issues being raised and remedied on an ongoing basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the involvement of officials from Health Estates in providing professional advice to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust on the development of (i) the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre, including handover; (ii) the Energy Centre project at the Royal Hospital site; and (iii) the new Regional Maternity Hospital in Belfast, including handover.
answer_text: DoH Health Estates role in major capital projects is documented in ‘Health Estates Directorate Governance Framework for Engagement with Health and Social Care Trusts and ALBs’ which is available on the Department’s internet site. Health Estates Directorate operates as a Centre of Specialist Expertise (CoSE) and is the Centre of Procurement Expertise (CoPE) for capital infrastructure across the healthcare estate.In the period from April 2014 to April 2023, Health Estates’ function as CoPE was transferred to the Department of Finance where CPD - Health Projects provided this service on behalf of Department of Health and its arm’s length bodies (ALBs).  Within its function, Health Estates provides professional direction and guidance to enable the ALB to act as an ‘intelligent client’ in the delivery of its capital development programme, including procurement, cost management and project management of all projects above the ALB’s delegated limit of financial authority. The Project Execution Plan for each project sets out the roles and responsibilities of project parties and is used for all above delegated limit projects. A template PEP is appended to the Health Estates Directorate Governance Framework for Engagement with Health and Social Care Trusts and ALBs.In relation to the specific projects you refer to, I can advise that Health Estates provided the roles as set out in the above Governance Framework and project management functions were delivered as follows:the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre, including handover; Project management responsibilities for the design and construction stages of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre project were set out in the Project Execution Plan.Project management roles included: Investment Decision Maker – BHSCT BoardSenior Responsible Owner (SRO) (formerly Project Owner) - BHSCT Director of FinanceProject Steering Group - Chaired by the SROProject Board – Chaired by BHSCT Project DirectorProject Team – Project Board plus the members of the Design Team and the Construction Team.Project Director – BHSCT Co-Director of Capital Planning and RedevelopmentProject Manager (managing the design team) - Health Estates/ CPD Health ProjectsAssistant Project Director - BHSCTUser Groups - BHSCTCommissioning Groups – BHSCTAt handover, Health Estates managed the Design Team who in turn managed the construction contract and certified that construction completion was achieved.		Following project completion in 2019, the management of the facility became the responsibility of BHSCT.the Energy Centre project at the Royal Hospital site; Project management responsibilities for the design and construction stages of the BHSCT Energy Centre project at the Royal Hospital site were set out in the Project Execution Plan.Project Management roles included: Investment Decision Maker – BHSCT BoardSenior Responsible Owner (SRO) (formerly Project Owner) -BHSCT Director of FinanceProject Steerin

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the composition of the multi-professional group referenced by Belfast Health and Social Care Trust senior management while giving evidence to the Committee for Health on 12 June 2025, which advised on the preferred way forward for remediation work at the new Maternity Hospital; (ii) how each member was appointed to the group; and (iii) the specific expertise each brought to taking such a decision on an issue of this nature.
answer_text: The multidisciplinary team which advised on the preferred way forward for water system remediation work at the new Maternity Hospital   included the Director of Nursing and Midwifery, the Director of Child Health and NISTAR, Maternity, Dental, Gynaecology, and Sexual Health, the Medical Director, and the Director of Strategic Development. The group also had senior representation from Microbiology and Infection Control, Estates, Maternity and Neonatology, the Commissioning Team, and members of the Maternity Hospital Project Team.  All members are employees of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.Members of the multidisciplinary team were appointed based on their leadership roles, clinical expertise, and strategic relevance to the scope of the project. Selection was guided by the need for comprehensive representation across all key domains involved in the decision including maternity, neonatal, associated healthcare services and the project delivery team. The members of the multidisciplinary team bring together a diverse range of expertise including senior oversight in nursing, medicine, child health, maternity, neonatology, infection control, estates, commissioning, and project development to ensure comprehensive planning, governance, and service integration for the Maternity Hospital Project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an estimate of the annual costs across the health and social care system of (i) patients cancelling or not attending appointments; and (ii) Health and Social Care Trusts cancelling the appointment.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland average marginal cost for a new outpatient appointment in 2024/25 was £220, £988 for a day case and £6,202 for an elective inpatient. Costs quoted above are Northern Ireland average unit costs from the 2023/24 Trust Cost Submissions. For day cases and inpatients, the averages relate to surgical specialties, and it should be noted that the actual cost will vary widely based on specialty and complexity.The total number of patients who failed to attend an outpatient appointment in 2024/25 was approximately 150,000. The cost associated with this lost capacity was approximately £33 million. The total number of patients who failed to attend an inpatient or day case over the same period was approximately 9,300. The cost associated with this lost capacity was approximately £19 million. Calculating the total cost of missed attendances depends on a variety of factors as fixed costs associated with activity still apply. Hospital cancellations cannot be used as direct indicators of lost capacity, as they do not accurately reflect real-time capacity loss. Clinics and/or patients are often cancelled in advance due to a range of factors including staff availability or changes to clinic scheduling, rather than patients being cancelled on day of the clinic. As such, these cancellations do not translate into measurable costs or lost clinical output as patients are generally rebooked into other appointments.In order to reduce the number of DNAs/cancellations, minimise any inconvenience to patients, and reduce the costs associated with patient communication, Health and Social Care Trusts have implemented text messaging functionality to support referral acknowledgements, patient validation, and text message reminders to notify patients of their upcoming appointments. This approach aims to provide a timely and improved method of communication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 25176/22-27, for his assessment of the disparity in the numbers of adults assessed for autism in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust compared with other areas.
answer_text: The disparity in the number of adults assessed for autism in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust over the past three years is due to the absence of a dedicated adult autism assessment and diagnostic service. Instead, assessments have been delivered by clinicians seconded from existing services such as Clinical Psychology and Occupational Therapy within adult mental health teams.Between 2021 and 2023, significant pressures on these teams led to reduced clinic capacity, with only one assessment clinic held per month. In May 2024, resources were reconfigured, and an additional staff member was trained and released to support the service. This allowed the number of clinics to increase from one to four per month. However, demand continues to exceed capacity, resulting in long and growing waiting times.We acknowledge that the demand for adult autism diagnostic assessments is outpacing the capacity of services and that there is significant variation in provision across the region. This is unacceptable and we are committed working with Health and Social Care Trusts and the wider system to find sustainable solutions and develop, coordinate and drive forward delivery of regional work to improve timeliness to diagnosis, reduce variation and better meet population needs now and in the future. My Department recently appointed a project lead for Adult Autism to take forward this work. On 2nd July, we held a regional workshop focusing on care pathways for autistic adults without a co-occurring learning disability. This workshop brought Trusts together with autistic adults to reflect on where we are currently, why it is important we get this pathway right and consider collectively what we can do within existing resources to improve timeliness to diagnosis and meet support needs. An initial action plan was co-developed during the workshop and a task and finish group will be convened to take this work forward over upcoming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an estimate of the costs resulting from a patient failing to attend an outpatient appointment (i) day case; and (ii) admission.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland average marginal cost for a new outpatient appointment in 2024/25 was £220, £988 for a day case and £6,202 for an elective inpatient. Costs quoted above are Northern Ireland average unit costs from the 2023/24 Trust Cost Submissions. For day cases and inpatients, the averages relate to surgical specialties, and it should be noted that the actual cost will vary widely based on specialty and complexity.The total number of patients who failed to attend an outpatient appointment in 2024/25 was approximately 150,000. The cost associated with this lost capacity was approximately £33 million. The total number of patients who failed to attend an inpatient or day case over the same period was approximately 9,300. The cost associated with this lost capacity was approximately £19 million. Calculating the total cost of missed attendances depends on a variety of factors as fixed costs associated with activity still apply. Hospital cancellations cannot be used as direct indicators of lost capacity, as they do not accurately reflect real-time capacity loss. Clinics and/or patients are often cancelled in advance due to a range of factors including staff availability or changes to clinic scheduling, rather than patients being cancelled on day of the clinic. As such, these cancellations do not translate into measurable costs or lost clinical output as patients are generally rebooked into other appointments.In order to reduce the number of DNAs/cancellations, minimise any inconvenience to patients, and reduce the costs associated with patient communication, Health and Social Care Trusts have implemented text messaging functionality to support referral acknowledgements, patient validation, and text message reminders to notify patients of their upcoming appointments. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what consideration took place regarding retaining, until the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust achieved HPV accreditation, one or both of the existing accredited test providers at (i) the Northern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) have advised that whilst the Northern and Western Trust laboratories had been providing primary HPV (pHPV) testing on behalf of the region prior to the commencement of the new regional laboratory, this testing was being provided using non-recurrent funding and so delivery was reliant on temporary staffing.Given that a decision had been made in July 2024 to move testing to the regional laboratory at Belfast, temporary staff were actively looking for new roles so no sustainable pHPV testing capacity was in place at either laboratory.In addition, the laboratory regional transition coincided with the go live of a cellular pathology module within the new Laboratory Information Management System (LIMs).  This module went live in Belfast on 21st October.  Part of this included a new system for reporting cervical screening results.  Any ongoing provision of pHPV testing in the Northern and Western laboratories would have required both laboratories to adopt the new information system to include system testing, staff training and adjustments to workflows and procedures.It should be noted that the Belfast Laboratory has successfully secured an extension to its UKAS accreditation to include pHPV testing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the overall monthly costs, including staffing costs, for flushing the water systems at the new Royal Maternity Hospital, Belfast, in line with the recommended regime.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients currently considered to be medically fit to leave hospital but are having their discharge delayed, broken down by (i) hospital; and (ii) the reason for the delay.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail how the total number of patients considered medically fit to leave hospital but had their discharge delayed, has varied over the last 12 months.
answer_text: In July 2025 I had reported that the number of complex delays were within the range of 490-550 per day, with the following caveats:There will always be a percentage of delays which are within the complex delay target time of 48 hours.The number of delays will vary across the week with delays increasing over weekends and holiday periods.To date, complex delays across February ranged from 531 to 626, with the majority of days the delays being between 531 and 570. There is increased pressure in our hospitals over the winter months with increased rates of admission particularly for older people which will translate to increased numbers of delayed discharges over the winter months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how he is meeting the duty placed on his Department in the Health and Social Care (Reform) Act (Northern Ireland) 2009, to provide social care placements that would alleviate delayed hospital discharges, given the number of places provided by the independent sector remains inadequate.
answer_text: There are 468 registered care homes in Northern Ireland, providing a total of 15,836 beds. Nursing home beds account for two thirds of these beds, with one third accounting for residential care homes. Independent sector care homes account for 93% of the total care home beds registered in Northern Ireland, with statutory care home beds representing the remaining 7%. (Census of Bed Availability in Registered Care Homes in Northern Ireland on 27 September 2023 (Dec 2023)).HSC Trusts advise that there are no plans at present to increase bed provision within the statutory sector. They have advised however that when independent sector providers develop proposals relating to the increase of care home bed capacity they engage with them and consider in line with contractual requirements and tariff.  In order to explore increasing independent care home bed capacity the HSC Trusts have published market engagement notices on “Find a Tender”. These Market engagement notices explore increasing of bed stock, and have particularly focused on dementia care beds in recent years. My Department acknowledges the need to increase bed availability and to make better use of existing vacant capacity across the region. In the event that essential care is not available at the point of discharge from hospital, individuals may be discharged temporarily to a care home to await a package of care. This further impacts on care home bed availability for those that require it longer term.  This type of shortfall is driven in large part by workforce capacity issues in the Homecare sector across Northern Ireland.   My Department are taking various steps through the Social Care Collaborative Forum (SCCF) to provide vision, along with strategic advice and guidance on social care via workstreams to address delays.  Workstream one is focused on maximising capacity in care homes and home care, as outlined below;Trusted AssessorThe underlying principle of the Trusted Assessor model is to promote safe and timely discharges from hospitals when individuals are transitioning to care homes.  Waiting for the pre-admission assessment of an individual by a care home is reported as one cause of delay in hospital discharges.  In recognition of this issue, the then Permanent Secretary wrote to Trusts’ Chief Executives in December 2022, outlining changes to the arrangements for discharge from hospitals to care homes, with the aim of preventing delayed discharges. Adult social care providers were advised that they could adopt and use assessments carried out while people are still in hospital, as long as the assessment was made by a suitably qualified or suitably trained person.  Funding was provided to Trusts to undertake a pilot.  To date 4 Trusted Assessors are in place.  Data demonstrates significant progress in the coordination and management of how individuals transition from hospital to care homes and importantly the relationship between Trusts and the care home sector. In order for this model t

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when fire safety issues at the Royal Victoria Hospital were first detected; (ii) a timeline of how and when those concerns were communicated through the governance structures of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to senior management and the Board, including the actions taken.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the vesting orders on the land along the A5 Western Transport Corridor required (i) Ministerial Direction; and (ii) sign-off from the Permanent Secretary.
answer_text: The vesting orders on land along the A5 Western Transport Corridor were made on the basis of a Ministerial decision, but did not require a Ministerial Direction.  The Permanent Secretary was aware of and was content with decisions in relation to the making of the vesting orders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline at what stage after an organisation has been advised by the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service of fire safety concerns would enforcement action be (i) considered; and (ii) initiated.
answer_text: In determining the most appropriate action to take, NIFRS considers the circumstances of the breach.NIFRS will seek compliance with the law by educating and informing, thus offering the dutyholder information and advice, both verbally and/or in writing. This will include an explanation as to why any specified work is necessary and a period for completion. Legal requirements will be clearly distinguished from best practice or non-statutory fire safety advice.Before formal enforcement action is taken, (unless immediate action is required to reduce the risk to life or to prevent evidence from being destroyed), NIFRS will provide the person responsible with an opportunity to discuss the circumstances of the case and, if possible, resolve points of difference.If proceeding with formal enforcement action, NIFRS can act immediately, if there is an immediate serious risk to relevant persons or following a 14-day consultation period with relevant agencies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to outline in what circumstances would another health and safety enforcing authority be informed of Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service fire safety concerns about a particular premises; and (ii) to detail on what date was the Health and Safety Executive advised of Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service concerns regarding fire safety at the Royal Victoria Hospital.
answer_text: Before serving an Enforcement Notice, Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS) is required to consult with the district council, the Health & Safety Executive if used as a place of work, and any other person whose consent to any alteration would be required by any statutory provision.Before formal enforcement action is taken, (unless immediate action is required to reduce the risk to life or to prevent evidence from being destroyed), inspectors will provide the person responsible with an opportunity to discuss the circumstances of the case and, if possible, resolve points of difference.The Health and Safety Executive has not been advised of NIFRS concerns regarding fire safety at the Royal Victora Hospital. NIFRS fire safety inspectors are currently engaging with the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust regarding the Royal Victoria Hospital and no formal action has been taken as yet. If formal enforcement action is initiated, consultation with other agencies will occur and the Health and Safety Executive will be informed as part of this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any occasions when enforcement action over fire safety concerns has been pursued by the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service against (i) his Department; and (ii) its arm's-length bodies, in the last ten years.
answer_text: Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service has not taken enforcement action against the Department of Health or its arm’s length bodies in the last ten years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration has been given to relocating patients (i) in other areas of the Royal Victoria Hospital; and (ii) in other hospital sites, following fire safety concerns raised by the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service.
answer_text: I have been advised by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust that they will ensure patients are provided with treatment and care in a safe environment.   Should there be a need to relocate patients from certain ward areas, the Trust will look for appropriate alternative space across the Royal Victoria Hospital campus and its other acute campuses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact of taking action to respond to fire safety concerns raised by Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service will have on the numbers of (i) acute admissions; and (ii) elective activity, at the Royal Victoria Hospital.
answer_text: The Belfast Health & Social Care Trust has provided the following information in relation to the impact of taking action to respond to fire safety concerns raised by the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service on acute admissions and elective activity at the Royal Victoria Hospital:It will have an adverse impact on ambulance off load times,  emergency department decision to admit waits for an inpatient bed, particularly overnight, and the Trust’s ability to accept regional patients from other Hospitals.The closure of the 10 unfunded, undesignated beds will have no direct impact on elective activity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service has raised safety concerns about the Royal Victoria Hospital other than regarding access to fire exits.
answer_text: My officials have been advised by the Belfast Health & Social Care Trust Estates that they are not aware of any other safety concerns raised by Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service regarding the Royal Victoria Hospital building.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any dates in the last five years when the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service have reported fire safety concerns following inspections of facilities of (i) his Department; and (ii) its arm's-length bodies, including actions which were taken in response.
answer_text: (i) Nil return. There have been no fire safety concerns raised by the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS) in the last five years regarding facilities in the Department of Health.(ii) The Department’s arm’s-length bodies have responded as follows:Western Health & Social Care Trust (WHSCT)The WHSCT has had no enforcement notices from the Northern Ireland Fire & Rescue Service (NIFRS) in relation to fire safety for the 5-year period since 1/4/2020.  The WHSCT continues to work closely with our partners in NIFRS and welcomes both the scheduled and unscheduled fire safety audits that NIFRS undertake periodically in terms of external and independent assurance as an integral part of our governance framework.  The NIFRS does provide feedback to the Trust from these audits and these can include some best practice recommendations that are then taken forward by the relevant service(s)/ward(s)/department(s) as part of their ongoing fire risk assessment and action planning processes. Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT)In the last 5 years NIFRS have inspected 22 properties in the SHSCT estate:19 of these inspections resulted in a ‘broadly compliant’ outcome with no significant fire safety issues.3 of these inspections resulted in ‘Notification of Deficiencies’ being issued to the SHSCT. Of these, 1 has been closed out with the required actions completed in line with NIFRS Action Plan and 2 are ongoing.South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT)In the last 5 years NIFRS have issued the following to the SEHSCT following a fire safety inspection of Trust facilities:7 Trust owned properties have been issued with Notification of Deficiencies.1 non-Trust owned property was issued with Notification of Deficiencies.1 non-Trust owned property was issued with an Action Plan.Actions were taken by the SEHSCT to address the above in line with the Trust Fire Safety Policy and governance arrangements.Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT)The NHSCT advised that there were 72 advisory items identified in inspections undertaken by NIFRS in the past five years. At the time of writing, 32 have been completed and 40 are ongoing.Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT)The BHSCT has advised that it works closely with NIFRS on all fire related matters.Concerns had previously been raised by NIFRS regarding the combustible cladding on NI Cancer Centre [20th February 2023].  The cladding has now been remediated with a non-combustible type.Generally NIFRS audits of Trust premises have resulted in a ‘broadly compliant’ letter with some minor notifications of deficiencies which have all been addressed.Northern Ireland Fire & Rescue Service (NIFRS) NIFRS have an annual fire risk assessment process in place to inspect all NIFRS premises under the Fire Service Order 2006. Over the last 5 years no improvement or enforcement notices (formal action) has been served on NIFRS in regards to fire safety concerns across their premises.Where minor

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to outline the information on which the Chairman of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust based his assessment to the Committee for Health on 12 June 2025 that the best advice he had at that stage regarding the new Maternity Hospital was that Option 2 was the safest option; and (ii) for his assessment of this.
answer_text: The Chairman of the Belfast Trust, along with the wider Trust Board received a detailed report on 5 June 2025 from the multi-disciplinary review team established by the Trust to review the remediation options available for the water systems in the new Maternity Hospital and to make recommendation on the preferred option.  The review team comprised a broad range of expertise including microbiology, neo-natal consultants, midwifery , nursing, estates and other professionals.  The recommendation to Trust Board identified Option 2 as the preferred option.  This option involves the installation of a new domestic water system to the neo-natal unit, with localised remediation to other departments.  The report also provided an estimated programme for the remedial works under option 2, and estimated costs for the same. Following careful consideration and review by Trust Board, the option recommended by the multi-disciplinary review team was accepted by Trust Board on 5 June 2025.  The assessment provided by the Chairman of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to the Committee for Health on 12 June 2025 was based on the recommendation by the multi-disciplinary review team.I have requested an independent review of the Trust’s recommendation.  Until this review has been completed I am not in a position to provide an assessment of the Trust’s recommendation. The Trust has provided a report to me outlining the route forward for the new Maternity Hospital, including a programme of remedial works and costs for the same.  I have announced an independent review of the Trust’s decision.  Following this review the relevant papers will be shared with the Health Committee.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when (i) the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust; (ii) the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust Board; (iii) his Department; and (iv) he, was first informed of Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service concerns over fire safety at the Royal Victoria Hospital.
answer_text: I can advise as follows in relation to NIFRS concerns over fire safety and the Royal Victoria Hospital.(i)	The BHSCT was notified by NIFRS on 4 June 2025; (ii)	The BHSCT Board was notified on 3 July 2025; (iii)	This Department was notified on 11 June 2025; and (iv)	I was first informed on 13 June 2025.The Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS) conducted a routine audit of the Royal Victoria Hospital (RVH) building on 4 June 2025.  This audit was attended by members of Belfast Trust’s Fire Safety Team and service group representatives at ward level. Following the audit, NIFRS arranged a follow-up meeting with the Trust on 11 June to discuss the audit findings.  An update on the outcome of this meeting was provided to senior managers in the Trust who notified the Department of Health in a telephone conversation on 11 June 2025. Subsequently an early alert was raised with the Department of Health on 12 June regarding the issues raised in the audit. In response to ongoing concerns, particularly regarding obstructed Means of Escape in certain ward areas, NIFRS scheduled a further on-site meeting on 17 June.  This meeting was attended by Trust senior managers and service group managers. A follow-up on-site meeting was arranged for 25 June to continue addressing the identified issues. The NIFRS audit occurred after Belfast Trust’s June Trust Board and was therefore notified to Trust Board on 3 July 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the challenges faced by community pharmacy owners when paying medicine wholesale invoices and any associated risks to the supply chain.
answer_text: My Department has held regular engagements with pharmaceutical wholesalers since November 2023 to understand the issues raised by community pharmacy representatives regarding payment of medicine wholesale bills to ensure that the supply of medicines to the public is not affected. There has been some anecdotal evidence of pressures on the ability of some community pharmacies to pay their bills on time, however this was not necessarily widespread across the network and the threat of cancelling Direct Debits raised by community pharmacy representatives previously does not appear to have materialised.Additionally, the most recent engagements between my Department and wholesalers were in January 2026, and whilst they stated there were pressures across the UK, the three full-line wholesalers did not provide evidence that NI community pharmacies were experiencing more difficulties paying their medicine bills compared to other regions of the UK.The supply of medicines to community pharmacies is made via commercial arrangements between pharmaceutical wholesalers and community pharmacies and the ability of contractors to pay their bills is partly linked to their remuneration, for which my Department has a statutory duty to pay fair and reasonable rates. To provide stability and certainty to the community pharmacy sector and ensure the continued supply of medicines to patients, my Department has invested significantly in recent years. Since April 2018, funding for community pharmacy has increased by almost £60m (57%). This is the highest level of funding to date and is comparable with the rest of the UK.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of BMA research into concerns about the practice of physician associates released on 03 April 2025; (ii) what actions he has taken as a result; and (iii) whether he has requested that any interim safety measures are put in place until the Leng Review reports.
answer_text: I am aware of the BMA’s research into the practice of Physician Associates and have noted the important issues and concerns raised.Officials in my Workforce Policy Directorate have commissioned a consultant from the HSC Leadership Centre to take forward an evaluation of the Physician Associate role across the HSC.  It is intended that this evaluation will be concluded by the end of June and will provide a firm evidence base and recommendations on the future of the Physician Associate role in Northern Ireland. This evaluation will also consider learning points emerging from the review of Physician Associate and Anaesthesia Associates roles currently underway in England.The General Medical Council has taken forward regulation of the Physician Associate profession across the UK, effective from December 2024. I consider this an important step in the development of this profession, as the GMC will set the standards that Physician Associates need to follow, and will oversee their continued adherence to these standards throughout their careers. The GMC have advised that, since regulation of PAs became operational in December 2024, approximately 47% of PAs have submitted an application for registration. The GMC has registered 1,658 PAs to date, and these numbers are expected to grow over the coming months.

answer_text: The information requested is included in the table below.Academic YearQUBUK (Other)RoIElsewhere (International Medical Graduates)TOTAL  2025/26*54235331152024/2546203501192023/2437194571172022/23462610381202021/227126491102020/214318643110*Recruitment for the 25-26 academic year remains active with a further   recruitment round scheduled for February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what actions he is taking to maximise the likelihood of GP trainees establishing long-term careers in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has implemented the following range of initiatives to build and support the GP workforce in Northern Ireland:Integrated Training Posts (ITPs) enable GP Trainees to undertake an additional six months of their programme within a GP Training practice, thereby providing extra time and support to develop the skills required to thrive as a GP in Northern Ireland once fully qualified.GP Fellowship Schemes support recently qualified GPs to transition from the training environment to independent practice, with opportunities for personal and professional development. The GP Mentor Scheme is a structured programme with formally trained and approved mentors trained by the Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency.  This programme is an important resource to enable GPs who have been working in general practice in Northern Ireland for some time, as well as those entering the workforce, to avail of support and mentorship from experienced, trained peers in a safe and positive environment.The GP Retainer Scheme provides stable work in a suitably supportive environment and an identified trained mentor for three years. It includes a mandatory funded Continuous Professional Development programme provided by NIMDTA to assist with the appraisal and revalidation processes.The GP Attract, Recruit, Retain Scheme supports recruitment of GPs in hard to recruit areas.  Since its establishment in January 2023, 95 GP practices have applied and received approval in principle for funding under the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the average length of time taken to bring a case relating to serious violence against women and girls to court in the last ten years.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No. 10 - AQO 2222/22-27			   ANSWER   Data systems held by the Department in relation to cases completed at court do not contain information on the gender of a victim, apart from where it is specified in the offence description, so it is not possible to directly answer the question posed.  In September 2024, the Executive launched a Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls.  This framework intends to bring together a whole of society and whole of government response to address this issue which impacts the lives of far too many women and girls across our society.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) on what date he became aware of the Northern Ireland Audit Office report Access to General Practice in Northern Ireland finding that the current contract does not provide the Department with the ability to effectively performance manage GP practices; and (ii) what actions he has taken as a result.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Audit Office report on Access to General Practice was published on 20 March 2024.  Whilst I was made aware of the report in my First Day Brief upon my appointment on 29 May 2024, the finding detailed in your question was not included in that brief.However I welcome the NIAO report and its findings, including the Report’s recognition that the issues impacting general practice are complex and will require a sustained long-term response.  The NIAO report acknowledges the funding and capacity constraints faced by my Department in tackling these issues.The Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group is responsible for providing a contract management function across all General Medical Services contractors. The SPPG works closely with Business Services Organisation Counter Fraud and Probity Services to provide comprehensive counter fraud processes through the prevention, detection and investigation of fraud, corruption and other possible financial irregularities in Health and Social Care.Services are investigated by the Probity Team at Practice Level though a visit process to identify and investigate any financial irregularities.  Practices are visited on a 3 yearly cycle and services to be investigated are agreed between the SPPG and BSO Counter Fraud and Probity Services on an annual basis.In 2024/25, my Department introduced a new approach to performance and assurance in General Practice, with the introduction of the Northern Ireland Contract Assurance Framework as part of that year’s contractual negotiations.The NICAF is a comprehensive governance assurance process to evidence/provide assurance on the delivery of GMS through the GMS Contract.  The components are:Northern Ireland Contract Assurance FrameworkAgreed processes for assessing contractors compliance against contractual requirements. An agreed escalation process for managing concerns to include an appeals process.As set out in the NICAF, any aspect of the Framework may be subject to verification checks. The SPPG reviews evidence submitted under the NICAF to determine the level of assurance/compliance against the requirements of the NICAF domains and indicators.A key aim of the NICAF is to streamline and enable a more consistent approach for practices, thereby identifying and dealing with issues early.Every Practice in Northern Ireland will receive a Contract and Governance Visit at least once in a three-year period.You will be aware that my Department has worked to introduce new measures in relation to access to GP services as part of the 2025/26 GMS contract. The access improvements include a requirement for Practices make some of their appointment slots pre-bookable, so that patients do not always have to call back another time when daily capacity is reached; making sure that Practices make the most of existing digital functionality to make ordering repeat prescriptions easier; requiring Practices to ensure their staff deal with patients appropriately when
