question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of grievances within (i) his Department; and (ii) each of its arm's-length bodies which (a) have been concluded in the last year; and (ii) remain ongoing.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how Care Quality Commission inspection and regulation of general practitioners compares with the position in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Health and Personal Social Services (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 established the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA), setting out its powers and duties. The Health and Social Care Act 2008 established the Care Quality Commission (CQC), setting out its powers and duties.Whilst the legislative framework under which the CQC was established permits them to monitor, inspect and regulate NHS GP practices and GP out-of-hours services the legislation under which the RQIA was established does not allow for RQIA to do so within Northern Ireland.  Quality and safety in general practice are monitored, by my Department’s Strategic Performance and Planning Group, through the Northern Ireland Contract Assurance Framework (NICAF), which forms part of the GMS contract and applies to all GP practices in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the finding in the March 2024 Northern Ireland Audit Office report on Access to General Practice that there are no formal standards or targets of performance in terms of access to services including, time taken to answer calls, triage turnaround, timely access to appointments or the number of appointments made available; and (ii) to list the actions he has taken as a result.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Audit Office report on Access to General Practice was published on 20 March 2024.  Whilst I was made aware of the report in my First Day Brief upon my appointment on 29 May 2024, the finding detailed in your question was not included in that brief.However I welcome the NIAO report and its findings, including the Report’s recognition that the issues impacting general practice are complex and will require a sustained long-term response.  The NIAO report acknowledges the funding and capacity constraints faced by my Department in tackling these issues.The Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group is responsible for providing a contract management function across all General Medical Services contractors. The SPPG works closely with Business Services Organisation Counter Fraud and Probity Services to provide comprehensive counter fraud processes through the prevention, detection and investigation of fraud, corruption and other possible financial irregularities in Health and Social Care.Services are investigated by the Probity Team at Practice Level though a visit process to identify and investigate any financial irregularities.  Practices are visited on a 3 yearly cycle and services to be investigated are agreed between the SPPG and BSO Counter Fraud and Probity Services on an annual basis.In 2024/25, my Department introduced a new approach to performance and assurance in General Practice, with the introduction of the Northern Ireland Contract Assurance Framework as part of that year’s contractual negotiations.The NICAF is a comprehensive governance assurance process to evidence/provide assurance on the delivery of GMS through the GMS Contract.  The components are:Northern Ireland Contract Assurance FrameworkAgreed processes for assessing contractors compliance against contractual requirements. An agreed escalation process for managing concerns to include an appeals process.As set out in the NICAF, any aspect of the Framework may be subject to verification checks. The SPPG reviews evidence submitted under the NICAF to determine the level of assurance/compliance against the requirements of the NICAF domains and indicators.A key aim of the NICAF is to streamline and enable a more consistent approach for practices, thereby identifying and dealing with issues early.Every Practice in Northern Ireland will receive a Contract and Governance Visit at least once in a three-year period.You will be aware that my Department has worked to introduce new measures in relation to access to GP services as part of the 2025/26 GMS contract. The access improvements include a requirement for Practices make some of their appointment slots pre-bookable, so that patients do not always have to call back another time when daily capacity is reached; making sure that Practices make the most of existing digital functionality to make ordering repeat prescriptions easier; requiring Practices to ensure their staff deal with patients appropriately when

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any contracts; and (ii) other arrangements, health organisations in Northern Ireland have with Children's Health Ireland.
answer_text: As part of the All Island Paediatric Cardiac Network, my Department has a Service Level Agreement in place for children and young people from Northern Ireland to access paediatric cardiac surgery and paediatric cardiac catheterisation services in Our Lady’s Hospital, Crumlin which is part of the Children’s Health Ireland Group (CHI).There may also be a small number of children each year who are referred to CHI by their consultant on the basis of their individual and exceptional clinical needs through the Extra Contractual Referral mechanism which is managed by the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group.The Belfast HSC Trust has a service level agreement in place for the provision of Paediatric Complex Pain Service. This agreement commenced in 2025/26 and includes Multi-Disciplinary Team assessment and the provision of treatments and therapies.  Additionally, CHI is currently supporting the paediatric gastroenterology service in Belfast HSC Trust through a virtual on-call arrangement.  CHI Gastroenterologists provide an out of hours telephone advice service on an ad hoc basis to consultant paediatricians in the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC).My Department does not hold information on any arrangement other health organisations in Northern Ireland have with CHI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether is aware of occasions of a new hospital opening when it was known to have Pseudomonas present.
answer_text: I am not aware of any occasions of a new hospital opening when it was known to have pseudomonas aeruginosa present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will place a copy of the report requested by Peter May into the senior leadership of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, which was compiled by Peter McBride and taken to the board, in the Assembly Library.
answer_text: A document was compiled following the Peter McBride 2023 listening exercise, however this was a presentation rather than a report. At the Health Committee Meeting on 12 June 2025 the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust confirmed its willingness to share this document with the Health Committee. The Trust have confirmed that they shared the presentation with the Health Committee on 13 June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether it would be appropriate for the new regional Maternity Hospital to become operational while Pseudomonas is present.
answer_text: I have asked the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to look at the feasibility of a phased opening of the new Maternity Hospital once the localised water remediation works are completed.  Any decision in relation to the operationalisation of the new regional maternity hospital will be taken by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust taking account of all pertinent information including results of regular testing to monitor rates of pseudomonas and other water borne pathogens in the building.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) when did (a) he; (b) his Department; and (c) the board of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, first become aware of difficulties within the cardiac surgery team; and (ii) what actions were taken.
answer_text: My officials briefed me on the difficulties within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust cardiac surgery team in early March 2025.Since then, in discussions with Trust officials, trades unions and others, I have received conflicting timelines regarding when these cultural and behavioural issues first arose. Consequently, I have asked my Permanent Secretary to undertake a rapid review of any pertinent files to establish if my Department was aware of these problems earlier than the first quarter of 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the date on which the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre was handed over to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust from the contractor.
answer_text: The Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre was handed over to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust on 17th June 2019.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the diversity, equality and inclusion-related activities of (a) his Department; and (b) its arm's-length bodies, in each of the last three years; and (ii) the associated costs for each organisation.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail when each consultant cardiac surgeon at Belfast Health and Social Care Trust completed (i) revalidation; (ii) annual appraisal; (iii) patient and colleague feedback exercises, including any concerns identified and actions taken as a result and by whom, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The information provided below relates to Belfast Health and Social Care Trust consultant cardiac surgeons. Given the small number of cardiac surgeons within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, in providing this response the Department must be mindful of its requirement to comply with the principles set out in the Data Protection Act 2018 and the UK General Data Protection Regulations (UK GDPR) 2018 as providing a breakdown in relation to each cardiac surgeon could on its own, or in conjunction with information already in the public domain, could potentially lead to the identification of those individuals. Therefore, a breakdown containing the following detail has been provided in the Department’s response to this Assembly Question: All revalidations have been completed in the last five years (if applicable) with revalidation due dates being scheduled. Appraisals (as applicable to staff in post during the relevant year) relating to 2020 were completed in 2021, 2022 and 2023, or are in progress; those relating to 2021 were completed in 2022 and 2023; those relating to 2022 were completed in 2023, 2024 and 2025; those relating to 2023 were completed in 2024 and 2025; and those relating to 2024 are either in progress, or are pending. Patient and colleague feedback exercises were completed between 2018 and 2025 with the majority of these being completed in 2022 and 2023. No concerns have been raised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the arrangements in place for the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust Board to monitor clinical risk and patient safety.
answer_text: All clinicians, managers and co-directors are responsible for managing and controlling all elements of risk to patients, service users and staff.  There is a clear line of accountability through to the Trust Board. The Trust Board has an approved Integrated Governance and Assurance Framework, a Risk Management Strategy and an established Assurance Committee. The Integrated Governance and Assurance Framework supports the Trust in identification of potential risks, the key controls through which these risks will be managed, as well as the sources of assurance as to the effectiveness of these controls. This Framework also sets out a system of delegation of responsibility at Trust Board, Executive Team and Directorate levels. Risk management is at the core of the Trust’s performance and assurance arrangements and the Assurance Committee, reporting directly to Trust Board in this key area. The Assurance Committee is responsible for ensuring that there is a robust system in place for identifying strategic risks and significant gaps in controls and assurance for consideration. The Assurance Committee regularly challenges or seeks verification of the quality of evidence, which is brought to it. This Committee, along with the Audit Committee scrutinises the effectiveness of the Risk Management Strategy. The Trust utilises Risk Registers as a means of documenting the risk profile and treatment plans for controlling and minimising risk. The Board Assurance Framework Risk Document provides Trust Board oversight for the effective and focused management of the strategic risks that arise in meeting Trust priorities. It is a high-level summary of risk to the delivery of key priorities, focusing on existing controls, assurances and confirming internal and external arrangements. In addition to the Board Assurance Framework Risk Document, risk registers are maintained at divisional, directorate and corporate levels to record all forms of risk including clinical, operational and financial risk.Collectively, the above systems and processes provide the Executive Team and Trust Board with assurance on the effective monitoring and oversight of clinical risk and patient safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the level of sampling that it is proposed will have to be carried out at the new regional Maternity Hospital building; and (ii) a breakdown of the specific sets of measures which will be enacted when different levels of Pseudomonas are detected.
answer_text: This response has been provided by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.Detail the level of sampling that it is proposed will have to be carried out at the new regional Maternity Hospital buildingWhen the remediation works commence on site, decisions about water sampling and actions taken in response to results arising will be taken by a dedicated Project Water Safety Group (PWSG) for the project. The PWSG will oversee all remedial activities for the water systems which may be considered necessary to safely begin clinical services in the New Maternity Hospital. This group will be established in line with current best practice recommendations, specifically NHS Estates Technical Bulletin (NETB) No.2024/3 https://www.england.nhs.uk/publication/nhs-estates-technical-bulletin-netb-no-2024-3 . Although the PWSG will ultimately make decisions about testing and response to results, the current expectation of the Trust Water Safety and Usage Group (WSUG) is that a comprehensive programme of water sampling will be undertaken during the remedial works and on their completion.  The WSUG also expect that all sampling will be aligned with the Trust’s extant protocols which specify robust methodologies for the collection of samples, their storage and processing at an accredited laboratory.Provide a breakdown of the specific sets of measures which will be enacted when different levels of Pseudomonas are detectedThe Belfast Health and Social Care Trust will establish a specific set of measures in advance of the remediation works commencing to ensure that all parties are aware of the procedures and methodologies to be employed during the design and installation phases to ensure where possible the safety of the water system.  In the event of bacteria being discovered within the new water system, the Project Water Safety Group will be responsible to assess each situation and to determine the specific sets of measures which will be enacted in response to any detection, taking cognisance of all relevant Health Technical Memorandums including HTM 04-01.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of grievances being pursued by staff in the Cardiac Surgery Unit in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: As of 11 June 2025, there are no grievances by staff in the Cardiac Surgery Unit being pursued by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what consideration has been given by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to establishing a trust board clinical governance committee.
answer_text: The existing Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s Integrated Governance and Assurance Framework Document sets out the Trust’s Board arrangements for integrated governance. The implementation of assurance process across all aspects of the Trust, including those relating to clinical governance, are via the Framework’s Assurance Committee and its relevant steering groups.  Currently, the Trust is reviewing the Framework Document and the focus of this review includes the robustness of existing clinical governance structures and processes. This review will be further enhanced by the support of an external governance expert as per the arrangements agreed between the Trust and the Department, following the Trust’s recent escalation to Level 5 under the Department’s Support and Intervention Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what consideration has been given by his Department and its arm's-length bodies to seeking to switch capital to revenue expenditure; and (ii) what actions have been taken.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 21212/22-27, in relation to the Project Safety Groups (PSG), to detail (i) the membership of each PSG; and (ii) the dates on which (a) it was established; (b) an Independent Subject Matter Expert was appointed by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to lead the PSG; (c) it agreed its terms of reference; and (iv) it has met.
answer_text: The subject matter expert for the Project Water Safety Group was appointed on 3 July 2025 with the group constituted and meetings having commenced on 9 July 2025.  This group has met on a weekly basis thereafter (12 times in total up to and including 24 September 2025).  Draft terms of reference were issued to the group at its initial meeting, and these were subsequently agreed on 23 July 2025. The Project Water Safety Group consists of the following:Independent Subject Matter Expert;  BHSCT Consultant Microbiologist;BHSCT Director of Strategic Development;BHSCT Assistant Project Director (Senior Project Manager);BHSCT  Project Lead (Project Manager Capital Redevelopment);BHSCT Head of Risk and Environment;BHSCT Head of Estates;BHSCT Technical Project Support Officer;DoH Health Estates Designated Responsible Officer; andMain Contractor Representative.Work is ongoing to appoint suitable subject matter experts to the other areas of work including medical gases, fire, High Voltage/Low Voltage (HV/LV) and ventilation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the date on which the report into the difficulties at the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s (BHSCT) Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre was provided to (i) the BHSCT; (ii) his Departmental officials; and (iii) himself.
answer_text: The date on which the report into difficulties at the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre was provided to Belfast Health and Social Care Trust was September 2024.   Department of Health officials were notified formally through an early alert from the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust on 17th September 2024 to advise of pin holing in pipework and the need for extensive remedial works to correct this.   I was advised on the same date.      Subsequently, investigative works undertaken in two patient rooms identified a number of issues, in addition to the pipework, that may prove to be key contributory factors in the deterioration. Further investigation is to be undertaken.   Belfast Trust is exploring the best solution to carrying out remediation work while continuing to provide the highest standards of care within the centre and keeping disruption levels to a minimum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 27420/22-27, to outline the reason for the delay between the post being approved and subsequently advertised.
answer_text: The period between approval of the Belfast Trust Director of Strategic Development post on 8 March 2024 and the advertisement of the post on 19 August 2024 consisted of several actions outlined below:10 June 2024 – Department of Health confirmed level/banding of post following evaluation of the role.1 July 2024 – Belfast Trust corporate detail to be included in recruitment pack agreed.11 July 2024 – Belfast Trust media plan and recruitment timetable agreed – request for Trust Board members to be identified as panel members issued.22 July 2024 – Not all panel members available for planned dates therefore alternative dates had to be agreed.9 August 2024 – Alternative dates agreed.19 August 2024 – Post advertised by Belfast Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) whether ongoing flushing of the water system at the new regional Maternity Hospital will be required to continue over the full lifetime of the new building; and (ii) the level at which this will have to occur.
answer_text: I have been advised by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust that they are not yet able to determine whether ongoing flushing will be required over the full lifetime of the new building.  The current flushing regime is required because the facility is unoccupied.  Once occupied any flushing regime, if deemed necessary, will likely be at a reduced level as an occupied building will have natural/normal flushing occurring.   The flushing regime will be determined, monitored and amended where necessary by a dedicated Project Water Safety Group (PWSG) for the building.  This group and the water management regime will be maintained by the Trust until the PWSG have received the necessary assurances of the safety and viability of the water system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 19628/22-27, to outline (i) why Hydrop are no longer assisting the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust; (ii) when the decision was taken; (iii) what the decision making process was; (iv) who provided advice on the matter; and (v) who was involved in taking the decision.
answer_text: Hydrop continues to provide specialist support to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust in relation to water safety measures within the new Maternity Hospital.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the benefits of a separate water system for the neonatal unit at the new regional Maternity Hospital compared with a single system for the entire building.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust places critical importance upon water safety in clinical environments, particularly in neonatal care settings where patients are among the most vulnerable.   Following a comprehensive independent review of the water systems at the new Regional Maternity Hospital, the Trust has carefully considered the merits of a separate water system for the neonatal unit as compared to a single integrated system for the entire facility. The Trust Multi-Disciplinary Team assessed this proposal alongside other options and determined collectively that this was the most appropriate course of action.    I have been advised by the Trust that their approach is informed by the latest guidance for water safety within Health Technical Memorandum 04-01: Safe water in healthcare premises.  This guidance which was updated in August 2024 recommends enhanced water safety measures within augmented care areas due to the higher levels of risk associated with immunosuppressed patients. Included within the recommendations of HTM 04-01 is the use of separate and smaller water systems within augmented care areas.  Smaller water systems have the benefit of being easier to control and can minimise the potential for stagnation compared to larger reticulated systems.  In the event of contamination or system failure, a separate system allows for quicker isolation and remediation, minimising disruption to critical services and reducing potential risks to patients.Caveated by the fact that I am not a water expert my assessment of the above benefits suggests to me that the decision to install a separate water system for the neonatal unit at the new Maternity Hospital is a sensible, pragmatic approach noting that whilst risks will be mitigated and minimised by this approach there are no zero risk solutions available.Given the timescale and cost involved in installing a separate water system for the neonatal unit I have appointed an independent reviewer to demonstrate to my Department that the Trust has undertaken a robust assessment and analysis of all relevant information in reaching their decision for the remediation of the water system at the New Maternity Hospital.  I have also asked the Trust to explore the possibility of a phased opening of the building.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the extent of the additional risk presented by water-borne pathogens, such as Pseudomonas, from replacing contaminated pipework in the neonatal unit only, and not the remainder of the new regional Maternity Hospital.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has provided a report to me outlining the route forward for the new Maternity Hospital, which details plans to proceed with works to separate the water system to Neo-Natal, as an augmented care area, in addition to localised remedial works elsewhere in the building.   I announced an independent review of the Trust’s decision which has recently been completed.  This independent review has concluded that the Trust has demonstrated an acceptable level of governance in the management of this issue and that the selected option, when considering all factors presented, is the correct approach. This option provides the most potential flexibility for project delivery, less impact on other services whilst focussing on the susceptibility of the service users.   The decision to replace the pipework in the neonatal unit only, while undertaking targeted remediation in other areas of the new Regional Maternity Hospital, reflects a proportionate and evidence-based approach to managing the risk posed by water-borne pathogens such as Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Three options were considered by the Trusts multi-disciplinary team in assessing the best approach: Localised remediation works across the hospital; Targeted replacement in high-risk areas such as the neonatal unit with local remediation works in other areas; Full replacement of all domestic water pipework The Trust, supported by interim findings from external consultants, concluded that the full replacement was not warranted. Instead, the decision was made to prioritise the neonatal unit—where patients are most vulnerable—and implement a revised flushing regime and localised remediation elsewhere. This ensures, as far as reasonably practical, that the most clinically sensitive areas receive the highest level of protection, while still addressing the broader issue through enhanced monitoring and remediation. The Trust has also committed to ongoing testing and review, meaning the approach can be escalated if necessary. It is important to acknowledge that no option is entirely risk-free. Decisions must be made based on a careful assessment of relative risk, clinical need, and the practical implications of each course of action. Taking all of the above into account I am satisfied that the chosen course of action is appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 18963/22-27, to detail (i) when the final report on the water systems at the new regional Maternity Hospital was received by (a) the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust; and (b) his Department; (ii) what actions have been taken as a result of the final report; and (iii) how it has influenced decision making regarding how the new hospital might operate safely.
answer_text: (i)(a)	The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust received the interim report from independent experts in late November 2024. The final report is not anticipated until the full installation and validation of the remediated water systems is complete. However, the findings of the interim report have already informed the Trust’s response and were influential in the decision made regarding the necessary water remediation in the New Maternity Hospital.(i)(b) 	The Department of Health received a copy of the interim report from the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust on 26th November 2024.(ii)	As stated above the final report is not anticipated until the full installation and validation of the remediated water systems is complete. The Trust have advised that the following actions have been taken in response to the findings of the interim report and ongoing guidance from the Trust’s Subject Matter Expert on Water plus the Trust’s Water Safety and Usage Group:The Trust developed and instigated a remediation plan to address the contamination of the water systems, including the implementation of a comprehensive flushing and testing program.The Trust commissioned an independent review involving experts, including the UK Health Security Agency at Porton Down.The Trust took the decision to pause the clinical commissioning phase to ensure no risk to patients, especially neonates.The Trust worked to determine the best course for remediation and safe operation.(iii)	The Trust has advised that, as a result of recommendations from the interim report and continued guidance from experts, the Trust has taken the decision to delay the hospital’s opening by up to two years due to the need for remedial work, noting that they are making every attempt to streamline this.The Trust has made it clear that the hospital will not open until it is assured that the water systems pose no risk to babies, prioritising patient safety above all.  My Department notes that whilst risks will be mitigated and minimised there are no zero risk solutions available.Given the timescales outlined above I have appointed an independent reviewer to demonstrate to my Department that the Trust has undertaken a robust assessment and analysis of all relevant information in reaching their decision for the remediation of the water system at the New Maternity Hospital.  I have also asked the Trust to explore the possibility of a phased opening of the building.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what role he anticipates the Water Safety and Usage Group at the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust having in the decision making around (i) remedial actions to ensure the safety of the water system at the new regional Maternity Hospital; and (ii) in the facility becoming operational.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust have provided a submission to me outlining the route forward for the water remediation works at the new Maternity Hospital, which details plans to proceed with works to separate the water system to Neo-Natal, as an augmented care area, in addition to localised remedial works elsewhere in the building.   I have announced an independent review of the Trust’s decision which will report in due course.  The Trust will continue regular testing to monitor rates of pseudomonas and other water borne pathogens in the building and any further action will be based upon these results. The estimated programme provided by the Trust has indicated a period of 28 months until the remediation works have been completed and the building becomes fully operational. During the period when these remediation works are implemented on site, and after they are completed, a robust flushing, management and testing regime will be established for the building.  The exact details of how this is to be established and the management of this regime to ensure patient safety will be determined, monitored and amended where necessary by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust Water Safety Usage Group. The water management regime will be maintained until the Trust have received the necessary assurances of the safety and viability of the water system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the differences between the Health and Social care Trusts in the average number of hours per week offered within a care package.
answer_text: Each individual is assessed in their own right and in the context of their level of independence and social supports and networks in their system of care.Each Health and Social Care Trust apply the regional criteria for assessment of each individual’s needs.  A care and support plan is then developed with that person and their family/carers.  A package of care may be required for the individual to support them to remain as independent as possible and to remain living at home for as long as possible.With reference to your last enquiry, namely AQW 26069/22-27 in which you asked for the average package of care hours per Trust, I do note that for the last year we have full data, ie 2023 that there were some variances across the Trusts.  There are a number of factors which may account for this and my department has recently begun a piece of work to interrogate this data in more detail.  The outworkings of this will be taken forward through the work of the Social Care Collaborative Forum and I look forward to sharing this with you in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what further requirements the United Kingdom Accreditation Service required the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to meet before considering granting HPV accreditation.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) has advised that UKAS visited BHSCT in January 2025 and assessed the processes and procedures in place at BHSCT, as part of the Trust’s request for an extension to the scope of its cytology accreditation to include HPV testing.  At the time of the visit, UKAS requested additional information/evidence be provided to them addressing a small number of findings by the end of April 2025, to include:Changes to staffing to be clarified due to a retirement.A form to be document controlled on Quality Management System.Update to validation documentation to add further tests on manufacturer’s instructions.Add pipetting to training and competency list.Add more detail to environmental swabbing process in the Standard Operating Procedure.This evidence was submitted to UKAS by 30 April 2025. It is hoped that a decision on extending the scope of the accreditation will be made shortly but the timescale is entirely dependent on UKAS.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of HPV tests that were carried out within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust without United Kingdom Accreditation Service accreditation in each of the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration has been given to standardising the provision of care packages on a regional basis.
answer_text: My department has given a commitment to collate data and review how Trusts assess and commission packages of care to ensure a standardised approach going forward.  This work will be part of the 10-year Strategic plan for Social Care and will be taken forward by my department through the Social Care Collaborative Forum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the relationship and involvement of (i) his Department; (ii) its arm's-length bodies; and (iii) other organisations in Northern Ireland, with Children's Health Ireland, in areas including the provision of health care and research.
answer_text: As part of the All-Island Paediatric Cardiac Network, my Department has a Service Level Agreement in place for children from Northern Ireland to access paediatric cardiac surgery and paediatric cardiac catheterisation services in Our Lady’s Hospital, Crumlin which is part of the Children’s Health Ireland Group.A small number of children may be referred to Children’s Health Ireland by their consultant on the basis of their individual and exceptional clinical, including emergency, needs through the Extra Contractual Referral mechanism. The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) has a Service Level Agreement (SLA) in place for the provision of Paediatric Complex Pain Service. This agreement commenced in 2025/26 and includes Multi-Disciplinary Team assessment and the provision of treatments and therapies. Additionally, CHI currently supports the paediatric gastroenterology service in BHSCT through a virtual oncall service. CHI gastroenterology consultants provide an out of hours telephone advice service on an adhoc basis to consultant paediatricians in the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children. This Department does not hold information, on any relationship or involvement other organisations in Northern Ireland, outside of this Department or its ALBs may have with CHI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his latest estimate of the implementation costs of his Department’s Adult Protection Bill.
answer_text: The draft Adult Protection Bill will cost approximately £12 million in year one and £120.8 million over the first 10 years of implementation.  However, it is likely that actual spend will be less than anticipated in the first year, leading to a more gradual rise in the following years due to incremental recruitment. A further business case focussed on the detail of implementation will be developed in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the reformed Scottish dental payments model, particularly in terms of its potential applicability to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My officials have been closely monitoring the impact of the dental payment reform introduced in Scotland in November 2023. Unfortunately, the data required to fully analyse the suitability of adopting the same approach in Northern Ireland is not available.High level estimates of the cost to Northern Ireland of adoption of a similar model suggest that it would require a substantial increase to the dental budget, therefore any consideration must take into account the returns on such an investment in terms of increased access and patient and practitioner satisfaction.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what consideration has been given to enhancing the powers of the Regulation and Improvement Authority in relation to regulating dental activity; and (ii) what actions have been taken as a result.
answer_text: The Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) is the independent body responsible for regulating and inspecting the quality and availability of Health and Social Care (HSC) services in Northern Ireland. RQIA's inspections aim to identify best practice, to highlight gaps or shortfalls in services requiring improvement and to protect the public interest.There is no explicit level of activity required by the General Dental Services Regulations; there is only a requirement that registered Health Service patients must have their needs met within a reasonable timeframe. Without a measurable standard to compare, RQIA are unable to regulate this aspect of dental care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how much was allocated to private health care providers in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Expenditure on private healthcare providers in each of the last 10 years is set out in the table below. Private Healthcare Expenditure - £’m2015/162016/172017/182018/192019/202020/212021/222022/232023/242024/2529.432.816.824.224.227.259.074.863.668.0 This funding relates to the use of private sector providers to tackle waiting lists.We all agree our waiting times are wholly unacceptable and reducing waiting times and addressing the demand/capacity gap are the most pressing challenges facing the Health and Social Care Sector.The Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan describes a combination of measures to tackle our waiting lists including maximising the existing infrastructure, expansion in core capacity in a range of specialties and partnership working with the Independent Sector. To achieve the maximum impact, the system relies on use of the Independent Sector to deliver additional waiting list activity.  With the scale of the backlog of patients waiting for treatment, it is expected that this relationship with independent sector providers will be required for some time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will be increasing the number of available student physiotherapy places.
answer_text: I approved the commissioning of education and training  programmes for 2025/26 on 14 May 2025.  Despite the ongoing pressures on my Department’s budget, I managed to identify £160 million for investment across all professions, with a major focus on expanding student training places for future Allied Health Professions.  This has enabled my Department to commission 505 AHP pre-registration training places from Ulster University - the highest ever. 110 pre-registration Physiotherapy training places will be provided for 2025/26 representing an increase of 38% over the level two years ago.  Any potential further expansion of training places in the Physiotherapy and other pre-registration healthcare programmes commissioned by the Department will need to consider both affordability and competing priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service Community Resuscitation Strategy; and (ii) whether it is undertaking a review to inform a future successor strategy.
answer_text: The Community Resuscitation Strategy was launched in July 2014 by the then Minister for Health, Edwin Poots, with the aim to increase the survival rate for those who suffer an out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, to the highest level that can be achieved across Northern Ireland.  The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) is leading on the successful implementation of the Community Resuscitation Strategy and is funded to provide a supporting role in advising organisations, businesses, and individuals regarding siting of defibrillators and best practice in the use of defibrillators. Public engagement and awareness sessions on CPR and use of AEDs in communities, continue to increase in number year on year. Community resuscitation strategy groups within individual council areas, develop specific objectives and action plans to improve survivability from out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, with the aid of local resources.  NIAS monitor and review progress to ensure the anticipated benefits of the Strategy continue to be delivered. NIAS has worked with the British Heart Foundation to develop ‘The Circuit’ (https://www.thecircuit.uk/) which is a national online defibrillator network linking to every ambulance service in the UK.  NIAS can readily provide information on AEDs registered on ‘The Circuit’ to 999 callers, should they require access.There are currently 3,888 Automated External Defibrillators (AEDs) available to the public and registered through NIAS on ‘The Circuit’.  NIAS Community Resuscitation Team continue to be involved in activities within communities which will strengthen the chain of survival, and NIAS have installed 35 publicly accessible defibrillators outside NIAS premises across the province.There are currently 426 Community First Responder (CFR) volunteers and 24 schemes in NI.  NI also has approximately 2,500 members of the public who have a minimum of Basic Life Support training and have registered to be a GoodSAM responder. Since members of the public went live on GoodSAM in February 2023 over 297 people have accepted a GoodSAM alert to a cardiac arrest.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what analysis his Department has carried out on General Dental Services statistics in relation to the growing shift away from NHS dentistry.
answer_text: My Department continuously monitors and analyses various aspects of dental services to monitor General Dental Service provision, with the Business Services Organisation (BSO) carrying out analysis of the day-to-day operational aspects of General Dental Services including registration data. Additionally, the BSO publishes regular statistical analyses, along with quarterly updates.The annual General Dental Statistics for 2024/25 are due for publication at the end of June. However, the most recent quarterly update shows a reduction in both registered patients and the number of practices providing Health Service care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients awaiting day case inpatient admission in plastic surgery, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The numbers of patients waiting for admission for inpatient or day case treatment in the Plastic Surgery and Paediatric Plastic Surgery specialties, by Health and Social Care Trust, are published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Northern Ireland Inpatient and Day Case Waiting Time Statistics’ bulletin. https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/inpatient-waiting-timesThe most recent figures are included in the associated ‘Statistics on Inpatient & Day Case Waiting Times by HSC Trust March 2025’ Excel file on the Department’s website:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-waiting-time-statistics-inpatient-and-day-case-waiting-times-march-2025

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the dates during 2025 when he or his officials have met with General Practitioner representatives to seek to agree new contract arrangements.
answer_text: My officials met with the Northern Ireland General Practitioners Committee (NIGPC) on the dates listed below as part of the formal negotiation process for the 2025/26 General Medical Services Contract.  14 August 202418 September 2024 Discussion on all agreed agenda items was not completed on 18 September, as NIGPC representatives left the meeting.  No further meetings took place until December 2024 as NIGPC withdrew from formal negotiations during this period.11 December 202415 January 2025 19 February 202512 March 2025In addition to the meetings noted above, my officials also engaged with NIGPC as follows:5 March 2025 - Teams Call with Chair and NIGPC representatives.27 March 2025 - Teams Call with Chair and NIGPC representatives. I met with the Chair of the NIGPC and other NIGPC representatives on 29 April 2025 to set out my Department’s final offer on the 2025/26 Contract.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 20710/22-27, to outline (i) when the decision was taken to create the post of Director of Strategic Capital Development; and (ii) on what date the post was advertised.
answer_text: Belfast Trust Executive Team agreed the decision to establish a new Director of Strategic Development post on 19th February 2024.  The Permanent Secretary approved the post on 8th March 2024, and it was subsequently advertised by the Trust on 19th August 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 26373/22-27, to provide a breakdown of the £18.324 million of grants provided by his Department in 2023-24.
answer_text: Just over £9 million of these monies was spent on ten projects which were successful in securing a proportion of the €62m available through the Health Strand of the INTERREG VA (IVA) Programme (2014-2020).  It is detailed as 4a Health and Social Care in the table below.  A key aim of each of the ten projects approved for IVA funding was to promote social inclusion, reduce health inequalities and combat poverty and discrimination through partnership working and collaboration.  The ten projects covered areas as diverse as: Acute Services; Mental Health; E-Health; Life and Health Sciences; Health Promotion; Healthy Ageing; and Healthcare Intervention Trials.Funding from the Department was channelled through the Special EU Programme Body (SEUPB) the Managing Authority for INTERREG VA which in turn allocated funding directly to the projects.The table below details the full breakdown of the £18.324 million of grants provided by me Department in 2023-24.Breakdown of Grants paid in 2023-24Grant Description£mPhysical and Sensory Disability0.576Grants to Voluntary Bodies3.515Contribution to MHRA (National Institute of Biological Standards and Control)0.350Medical Libraries*1.227Degree on Social Work2.937Training Support Costs0.161Social Care Institute of Excellence0.110Open University Degree in Social Work0.1944a Health and Social Care9.050Other0.204Total18.324*The Healthcare Library of Northern Ireland is a library and information service operated by Queen’s University Belfast (QUB) and commissioned by the Department of Health. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any support she will make available to businesses in relation to changes and advancement of Artificial Intelligence technology.
To ask the Minister of Health whether disposal policies permit charitable organisations to access medical equipment no longer utilised by Health and Social Care Trusts and other health bodies.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trust have waste and asset management policies and procedures in place so that assets no longer required are disposed of in a safe and appropriate manner in accordance with relevant legislation and best practice guidance. Assets may be deemed no longer required if surplus to requirements, obsolete, beyond economic repair or at the end of their useful life. Assets that remain serviceable may be transferred to other bodies, including charities, subject to appropriate procedures being completed.

answer_text: To clarify, the total spend on Research and Development by my Department in 2023-24 was £43 thousand not £43 million.  This was for research to support the Northern Ireland Cancer Strategy

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the relationship between the Pharmaceutical Society of Northern Ireland and the General Pharmaceutical Council.
answer_text: The General Pharmaceutical Council is the regulator for pharmacies, pharmacists and pharmacy technicians in Great Britain. The Pharmaceutical Society Northern Ireland is the regulator for pharmacies, pharmacists and trainees in Northern Ireland. They are independent organisations but work together, within the limits of their respective legislation, to ensure consistent regulation of the pharmacy profession across the UK. This cooperation is formalised in a Memorandum of Understanding covering issues such as fitness to practice, education and training, and Continuing Professional Development for dual registered pharmacists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the additional elements of work required, beyond water safety issues, before the new Maternity Hospital in Belfast can become operational.
answer_text: There are a number of other elements of work, not related to the water issues, which the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust need to undertake prior to the opening of the new Maternity Hospital. The Trust are satisfied that these works will be implemented prior to opening and that they will not impact on the timescales for the hospital becoming operational. These works include medical gas pipe replacement in the neo-natal area, installation of an isolated and uninterruptible power supply system and clinical commissioning of the building.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department has taken following the Supreme Court judgement in For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers.
answer_text: The Department has written to the Equality Commission Northern Ireland (ECNI) and has received confirmation that ECNI is currently developing formal written guidance on this issue, anticipated to be published in June 2025. Once published, the guidance will be taken into consideration by the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 24270/22-27, to detail any (i) savings; and (ii) operational efficiencies that have been achieved in other bodies as the result of the Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency assuming responsibility as the Single Lead Employer for Resident Doctors and Dentists in training.
answer_text: My Department is currently carrying out an evaluation of the Single Lead Employer function provided by NIMDTA for resident doctors in training.  While this evaluation has not yet concluded, initial findings are that this initiative has had a positive impact on the experience of resident doctors as they progress through their training.  It therefore supports the overall objective of increasing the recruitment and retention of doctors in HSC which, among many benefits, contributes to reducing expenditure on medical locums.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the extent of the increase in pharmacists’ annual registration fee with the Pharmaceutical Society of Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Society, and not the Department, is responsible for explaining the reasonableness of its proposed fees’ increase.  I have made it clear to the Society, when taking forward the regulations to give effect to a change in fees, that I expect them to be able to demonstrate how they have had regard to the impact on registrants; not least given the considerable financial pressures facing this sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average length of stay of admissions in each of the last ten years, broken down by hospital.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the recommended level of hospital bed occupancy.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland there is no formal bed occupancy rate set by way of a performance target or standard, nor is there a universally accepted benchmark for bed occupancy rates. However, the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) suggested in 2018 a “pragmatic maximum” of 90% whilst the 2023/24 NHS England operational planning guidance recommended that bed occupancy should not exceed 92%.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the weekend discharge rates in each of the last ten years, broken down by hospital.
answer_text: My department does not have data covering the entire ten-year span requested. The data we have provided spans a three-year period from April 2022 to March 2025. Table 1 shows the number of complex discharges that were delayed by more than the 48-hour target and number of non-complex discharges (simple discharges) that were delayed for more than the 4-hour target for each year in the last three financial years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.  With the ongoing implementation of the Encompass electronic patient record system by the Belfast, Northern and South Eastern Trusts, we are in a transition phase towards fully digital records. This transition involves extensive validation of data, which has resulted in gaps in the data available. The data for the Ulster Hospital for the years 2023/24 and 2024/25 is incomplete for the aforementioned reason. The percentage for complex discharges for the year 2024/25 is derived from data spanning April to October 2024, and similarly, the percentage for simple discharges for 2023/24 is based on data from April to October 2023. Unfortunately, there is no data available for Ulster site simple discharges for the year 2024/25.Table 1: Weekend Discharge Rates April 2022 - March 2025Complex DischargeSimple DischargeTrustHospital22/2323/2424/2522/2323/2424/25Belfast Mater27.0%27.0%21.1%47.4%35.7%25.4% RVH42.1%36.0%26.9%56.8%61.7%55.5%Northern Antrim57.9%57.5%43.5%53.3%52.7%50.7% Causeway48.3%39.3%43.3%54.8%47.2%44.2%South Eastern Ulster43.6%36.9%34.2%53.7%50.4%UnavailableSouthern Craigavon23.9%25.1%27.8%49.5%51.0%49.6% Daisy Hill11.3%16.2%20.1%36.4%34.9%27.2%Western Altnagelvin33.2%27.7%24.4%44.6%49.0%52.6% South West Acute19.8%15.1%8.3%35.1%27.3%19.1%Source: This data is sourced from Admissions & Discharges DWH via Business Objects/Encompass, which includes manual returns from Trusts for the Service Delivery Plan (Table 1).In response to the challenges Trusts are facing with weekend discharges, my department is actively collaborating with Trusts to decrease the number of delayed discharges, particularly for patients who are medically fit to be discharged. In 2024/25, Trusts were mandated to develop preparedness plans, which included specific measures aimed at enhancing patient flow through urgent and emergency care services and ensuring timely discharges.These initiatives encompass the deployment of Safer Patient Care bundles, the expansion of discharge lounges, and the facilitation of quicker access to community-based equipment. We are also focusing on improving complex discharge processes, which is a critical aspect of the Regional Control Centre’s service improvement agenda. Additionally, Trusts were encouraged to review and optimise their internal discharge processes and to implement seven-day working practices to support better discharge flow. A regional guidance document on best practices for discharge lounges was disseminated to all Trusts to enhance the processes for simple discharges.In 2025/

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many hours per week are currently provided in the average care package.
answer_text: Every year in September my Department carries out a survey over a one-week period in relation to domiciliary care, this is the CC7B and is available on the Department of Health website.  Due to the introduction of encompass, data was not available in September 2024 for South Eastern Trust (SET) or Belfast Trust (BT). The data from September 2023 for all five Trusts indicates that the average hours per week for a package of care were 12.7 hours. There has been some fluctuation in the average hours per week delivered between 2019 and 2023. The highest average hours of care were delivered in 2022 at 13.1 hours possibly as a consequence of Covid-19 and the desire for people to remain at home rather than go to a care home. Also due to isolation requirements during this period there was less family support available which resulted in more demand to supplement existing packages of care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration he has given to standardising the approach to the protection of care packages when an individual is hospitalised across Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: My department have provided direction previously that protection of care packages should be in place across all Trusts in Northern Ireland.  In the main, the Trusts will hold a package of care for up to 10-14 days which on some occasions may be extended if the person lives in what would be considered a ‘difficult to source’ area.  The holding of care packages does come with a significant cost to the Trusts, however this cost is balanced with the opportunity for more timely hospital discharges and a reduction in the time and resources spent on the sourcing of new packages of care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many hours per week did an average care package entail in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Officials in Information Analysis Directorate within my Department carry out a survey every September regarding domiciliary care.  This survey is referred to as the CC7B and can be found on the Department of Health website.  Below is data provided by that Directorate: HSC Trust20142015201620172018201920202021202220232024BHSCT6.68.398.89.29.610.811.61212.5N/ANHSCT10.910.89.910.510.811.312.212.312.310.510.3SEHSCT12.81314.213.613.814.415.616.216.515.9N/ASHSCT11.811.311.911.912.21214.213.212.712.812.8WHSCT11.111.511.411.510.611.111.51211.110.810.9NI10.41111.311.311.411.81313.213.112.7N/AData is not available for Belfast Trust and South Eastern Trust for 2024, this is due to their move over to the encompass system and we hope that this data will be available through encompass in the next few months.  Without the data for these two Trusts I am unable to provide a regional average for 2024.The average package of care has continued to increase over the past ten years.  The spike from 2020-2022 is likely down to Covid-19.   During covid-19, families were unable to see their loved ones as much and attendance at day centres was stopped due to concerns of spread of the virus.  Also due to the virus outbreaks in care homes short breaks were not an option.  This therefore had an impact on the demand for additionality to packages of care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the duty placed on his Department in the Health and Social Care (Reform) Act (Northern Ireland) 2009 to provide, or secure the provision of, health and social care, to detail any action he is taking to increase health and social care placements and alleviate delayed hospital discharges, given the inadequate number of independent sector places currently available.
answer_text: There are 468 registered care homes in Northern Ireland, providing a total of 15,836 beds. Nursing home beds make up approximately two thirds of these beds, with one third being within residential care homes. The greatest number of registered care homes are located within the geography of the Northern HSC Trust area. The number of registered care homes is significantly lower in Western HSC Trust, closely followed by Southern HSC Trust. HSC Trust representatives have advised that the majority of beds in their nursing and residential care homes are provided by the independent sector. Independent sector care homes account for 93% of the total care home beds registered in Northern Ireland. (Census of Bed Availability in Registered Care Homes in Northern Ireland on 27 September 2023 (Dec 2023).I acknowledge the impact that available care home placements have across all sectors including the acute sector. Across the region HSC Trusts advise that there are currently no plans to increase bed provision within the Statutory Sector. They have however advised when independent sector providers develop proposals relating to the increase of care home bed capacity they engage with them and consider in line with contractual requirements and tariff. In order to explore increasing independent care home bed capacity the HSC Trusts have published market engagement notices on “Find a Tender”. These Market engagement notices explore increasing of bed stock, and have particularly focused on dementia care beds in recent years. I acknowledge the need to increase bed availability and to make better use of existing vacant capacity across the region. Workstream 1 of the Social Care Collaborative Forum is taking forward work to progress a regional digital solution for bed capacity and availability. It is hoped that this digital solution will improve communication regarding location of available beds and provide an approach for data gathering that supports better identification, individual care planning and provision of services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the main findings of the Domiciliary Care Landscape Report by Innovation and Consultancy Services.
answer_text: The Innovation Lab within the Department of Finance provided my Department with a Home Care landscape report. The report included a review of literature and the current policy landscape together with a review of user research.The objective of the review was intended to inform the baseline understanding of the current domiciliary care model through a review of existing literature and the delivery landscape, and to explore the case for change, as part of the wider ongoing work to reform adult social care, and develop a new model of Home Care.The main findings can be summarised as follows:Financial sustainabilityThere are a number of financial consequences associated with increasing demand for services (with ageing demographic projections), alongside increasing operating costs. This places an increasing emphasis on difficulties around recruitment and retention resulting in a difficult picture with regards to financial sustainability.The expectation is a continued year on year increase in expenditure on Home Care services, which will continue to add significant pressure to the Health budget.Workforce sustainabilityThere is a need for an increase in the workforce in both the statutory and independent sector, with common themes currently presenting challenges around: Recruitment and retention remuneration; Variations in rates between providers; andExternal financial drivers, including minimum wage consequentials.The report also provided a literature review and highlighted gaps and areas for improvement in terms of capturing service user and provider insight and feedback, in order to enhance understanding of users when developing a future model for domiciliary care.This report is the first step in the process of reforming the current model of Home Care, and my Department is now considering options to make the service sustainable for  the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any plans he has for a wildlife intervention programme to prevent the spread of bovine TB.
answer_text: Rates of bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) in Northern Ireland remain much too high.  I have been clear since becoming Minister, that something must change, and this change must be based on science-led and evidence-based solutions capable of delivering real and meaningful reductions in bTB levels. This needs to happen urgently and can only be progressed with both stakeholders and government working collaboratively together. That is why I established the TB Partnership Steering Group earlier this year, a new body consisting of key stakeholders across farming, industry, veterinary and environmental organisations.  This Group has worked with my officials since January to develop a new delivery plan and I was pleased to endorse the Group’s agreed Bovine TB in Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication which was published on the 15th of April.The Blueprint is founded on the principle that eradicating bTB requires a unified, multisectoral approach.  It sets out work needed in almost 40 actions across three key themes- People, Cattle and Wildlife.  It builds upon the Chief Veterinary Officer (CVO)’s review of bTB in Northern Ireland, which was published in November 2024, which itself took account of the actions within the 2022 bTB Eradication Strategy. Internally, a new Bovine Tuberculosis Eradication Transformation Programme has also been established to deliver the actions set out in the Blueprint, some of which will need further public consultation; new or amending legislation; and additional resources before implementation. With respect to wildlife specifically, my officials continue to work on the issues and gaps identified by the judicial review ruling in October 2023, which quashed my predecessor’s decision to advance a non-selective cull of badgers in areas of high TB incidence.  This work will provide me with detailed advice on potential wildlife intervention options and involves looking at the latest scientific evidence; the potential legislative options relating to an intervention; and updating figures relating to how much these interventions could cost.  I anticipate having this advice before summer.  A full public consultation will also be needed before I can make a fully informed, science-led decision on wildlife intervention.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the proportion of healthcare professionals that are trained in suicide prevention.
answer_text: This information is not held centrally and suicide prevention training data held by HSC Trusts is not available as a proportion due to fluidity of staff movements following training.HSC Trusts have responsibility around staff training and how they collect that data. Suicide prevention and emotional health and wellbeing training will continue to be widely promoted and staff encouraged to undertake training.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the proportion of GPs that are trained in suicide prevention.
answer_text: My Department do not hold lists or information regarding GPs and their training. As independent contractors, and in order to provide the services that they are contracting for, GPs will be required to meet the required training and competency standards.All doctors wishing to become GPs undertake training covering a wide range of conditions including mental health.I have a number of initiatives which are designed to ensure that GPs and Practices can access training readily and at a time that is convenient.GPNI was established during the Covid-19 pandemic to ensure effective communication was maintained, with all those working within GP Primary Care. This service is commissioned from the Eastern GP Federation Support Unit and allocation for this service for 2024/25 is £50.5k. The GPNI website and webinars are now widely accepted as a key regional communications platform designed to improve dissemination of, and access to, information for Primary Care practitioners.Practice Based Learning (PBL) is a programme of learning sessions for GP’s and the wider General Practice staff. The aim of these PBL is to allow dedicated time to be set aside for educational sessions to contribute to the continued professional development of GP’s and their Practice Staff.Provision of PBL was previously developed by the then HSCB for all GP Practices, however from March 2020 SPPG commission the provision of PBL from the Federations for their member Practices. PBL is facilitated by local GP Federation Support Units and £178k has been allocated for PBL for the 24/25 year. They have provided a cost-effective programme of ongoing learning for their GPs, nurses and wider Practice staff.GPs are ultimately responsible for ensuring that they maintain their own continuous professional development (CPD) in order to keep their knowledge, skills and training up to date. This includes participation in annual appraisal and in Revalidation, the requirements of which are set by the General Medical Council (GMC). Every licensed doctor must revalidate to show that they are fit to practise and to maintain their licence to work in the UK.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his next steps in reforming home care provision.
answer_text: As you are aware, within workstream one of the Social Care Collaborative Forum (SCCF), my Department continue to work with other stakeholders on maximizing capacity in Home Care.  Some of the regional work going on at present includes:The Early Review Team - this model ensures that for any new or increased package of care, a review of the individual’s needs is completed between 1-8 weeks.  Any hours of the package that are no longer needed are recycled back into the service to take someone off the waiting list.  Trusts have received a small amount of recurrent funding for the early review team.  My Department is working with the Trusts currently on what it would take to scale up this model and cost of same.Roll out of a digital system for home care - this houses schedules for home care services within Trust-led home care.  This digital system provides oversight of the capacity on specific schedules, therefore optimising the number of hours that can be delivered within existing staffing levels.  In addition, it can identify those areas at full capacity and where staff recruitment needs to be focused.  Three out of the five Health and Social Care Trusts have implemented this system and are at varying stages in the process.  The other two Trusts are aiming to roll this out this year.My Department has also established a Project Steering Group to look at the demand, capacity and complexity of the work that home care staff do. Work is ongoing to understand better, how the Trusts commission home care services, what the actual cost of statutory and independent sector home care is.  We have also completed a survey with a number of people who have had to wait on their package of care and the impact this has had on them.  The reports on these pieces of work are being developed currently.Looking to the future my department, want to review the domiciliary care contract and evaluate best practice in current models of home care with a view to developing an improved regional model for home care in the community.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Southern Health and Social Care Trust proposal to transfer Craigavon Area Hospital surgical pre-operative assessment to the St Luke’s site in Armagh, including any potential risks resulting from this.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust has a dedicated pre-operative team, committed to providing the best possible service for their patients. Significant efforts are being made to increase elective theatre activity regionally and improve waiting times for patients, which consequently has an impact on demand for pre-operative assessment. There are approximately 104 elective main theatre sessions each week in the Southern Trust which the team must have patients assessed and ready for.  Pre-operative assessment was previously offered from both Daisy Hill Hospital and Craigavon Area Hospital sites. The pre-op team were dispersed from their then outpatient footprint as a result of pressure for clinical space during the height of the covid-19 pandemic. In recent years, the service has been disrupted on a number of occasions, having to move location, in response to wider demands on physical space. Despite working at full capacity, this is not a productive environment for staff and regrettably not meeting demand, resulting in delays for patients in getting to theatre. The Trust’s accommodation group has recognised the need to find a more suitable location for this important service. Accommodation across Trust sites is a major challenge, particularly at busy acute hospitals. With rising demand for services, it has been a priority where possible to relocate those services, that are not dependent on a hospital setting, to other suitable sites where patients will also benefit from improved access to parking.  Over recent months they have been exploring options to improve working conditions for staff, efficiency of their pre-operative process and access to the service for patients. They have engaged with staff, Trade Union and other stakeholders throughout this process and are committed to providing a service that meets the needs of patients living throughout the entire Southern Trust area.Suitable accommodation has been identified on the St Luke’s hospital site in a purpose facility with adequate, accessible parking for both patients and staff.  The service will remain the same in this new location and have the same access to anaesthetic colleagues as currently. The team will benefit professionally from being located centrally together, through improved communications, efficiency and safety and as a result minimising any potential risks and being able to offer overall more holistic care to their patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of people currently awaiting a full or partial care package; and (ii) how the current figure compares to each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The number of people currently awaiting a full or partial care package in Northern Ireland at the end of February 2025 was 3,330My department put a formal process in place to monitor those waiting on packages of care from September 2022 and therefore we do not hold data for the past 10 years.  As a comparison to position at (i) above, at the end of February 2024 there were 3,915 and at end of February 2023 there were 4,569 waiting on full and partial packages of care.  This shows a reduction of 1, 239 people waiting on packages of care at the end of February 2024 compared to end of February 2023.Please note there are some caveats with these figures:Figures provided have been sourced from Trusts’ manual returnsFigures provided are management information collected for operational purposes, and are not official statisticsBHSCT - activity excludes Family and Childcare and Mental Health

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the recommended level of bed occupancy in inpatient mental health facilities.
answer_text: The recommended bed occupancy for acute inpatient mental health facilities should average 85%. The 85% safe occupancy baseline is in keeping with The Royal College of Psychiatry’s ten standards for adult in-patient mental healthcare, which states bed utilisation is at its most efficient when bed occupancy is at 85%. (Do the right thing: how to judge a good ward. Ten standards for adult in-patient mental healthcare, 2011).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale for Craigavon Area Hospital surgical pre-operative assessment transferring to the St Luke’s site in Armagh.
answer_text: Despite working at full capacity, it has been long acknowledged by the pre-operative team that the service is not meeting demand. Their current accommodation within the Craigavon Area Hospital site is spread across various departments, making communication and efficiency very difficult within any given session. Co-locating the team together in one building will decrease the amount of time colleagues spend walking between rooms to communicate and share information and ultimately enhance their overall working conditions. The St Luke’s site has more accessible and free parking facilities and very pleasant outdoor surroundings and walkways should staff wish to use at breaktimes. Improved communication and efficiency of the team’s working practice will enhance the standard of service they can offer to their patients. It is anticipated that patients will be able to park at the St Luke’s site much more easily than Craigavon Area Hospital and appointments should be shorter.  There will be no change to the range of services that the team can provide from the planned new location in St Luke’s Hospital site, Armagh. The proposed relocation of the pre-operative assessment service aims to improve working conditions for staff and ensure a more patient centred service. There will be no change to service provision and no patient safety issues have been identified. It is anticipated that the new arrangements will support the team in offering a more professional and effective practice. The management team have very openly engaged with staff and Trade Union colleagues throughout this process.
