question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the tariffs recently announced by the President of the United States of America.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, further to power outages at Heathrow airport as a result of a fire, to outline whether she has undertaken a review of all critical infrastructure.
answer_text: My Department has Business Continuity Plans in place designed to ensure that the Department can continue to operate during a business interruption. Each critical business area and ALB has more detailed operational plans in place to ensure disruption to services and critical infrastructure is minimal during and following any incident.These include plans for our roads network, our reservoirs, our public transport, our ports and airports and our water and sewerage infrastructure.Assurance is also provided through the department’s Emergency Management Development Programme that these plans are reviewed and tested on a regular basis.My Department also has a Major Emergency Response Plan for managing the response to civil contingency incidents, which can have a major impact on our critical infrastructure. This plan is aligned with the Civil Contingencies Framework and is reviewed and tested regularly. Any activation, such as the response to Storm Éowyn, will automatically trigger a full internal review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of times an ambulance was unavailable to respond to a 999 call due to having been waiting at a hospital to hand over a patient during each month of (i) 2024; and (ii) 2025.
answer_text: My Department has confirmed that the specific data requested isn’t recorded, so we are unable to answer this Assembly question. However, the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) has a policy whereby all patients that require an ambulance response from NIAS will receive one. There is no circumstance in which NIAS would refuse a patient an ambulance. Nevertheless, NIAS acknowledges that the response times for patients can often be slower than they would strive for across the region.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether there are any plans to light up Parliament Buildings in recognition of the 80th Anniversary of VE Day on 8 May 2025.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update of any business engagements during the recent visit to Washington DC.
answer_text: The deputy First Minister held a number of meetings and engagements in Washington DC including: a meeting with the President of the United States,  meetings with Members of Congress; US Chamber of Commerce the State Department; the British Ambassador; the Irish Ambassador; the Taoiseach; students from Georgetown University; the Washington Forum; the Ireland Funds Gala; and a Queen's University Smithsonian Institution event.  The deputy First Minister also hosted a Breakfast for 250 guests, delivering a keynote speech and providing a unique networking opportunity for stakeholders that showcased NI as a great place to live, work, study and invest.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of cervical screening laboratory sites recommended to the HPV Board by the panel tasked with considering this issue.
answer_text: A laboratory stakeholder workshop was held on 27 October 2023 to undertake an option appraisal and risk scoring of 14 different options for delivery of the future HPV and cytology services for cervical screening within Northern Ireland. These options ranged from one laboratory delivering the full service, to 4 laboratories each delivering both HPV and cytology testing.The full option appraisal and risk scoring outputs from this workshop were shared with the Primary HPV Implementation Project Board at their meeting on 16 November to inform their discussion and decision making. There was no separate panel tasked with considering this issue.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the costs incurred to date in developing the new cervical screening lab in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) the estimated overall cost.
answer_text: The in-year (2024/25) cost of the new cervical screening laboratory at Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) is £410k. £190k of this amount was met through funding retractions from legacy Trusts, while the balance was met by the BHSCT.  The overall cost of the new cervical screening service model for primary HPV testing is £2.65m.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 21214/22-27, to detail the statistics broken down by individual screener.
answer_text: The Department does not hold this information. I have been advised by the Public Health Agency (PHA) that trend statistics for individual screeners cannot currently be provided as this has the potential to be indirectly personally identifiable due to working patterns and the small number of screeners involved in any single Trust. The PHA is undertaking further work to extract and collate screener performance data. This requires historical data to be extracted, validated and presented in a format that will not be personally identifiable, and which is meaningful in regard to benchmarking against national standard definitions. It should be noted that screener sensitivity data used in Northern Ireland prior to 2020/21 was calculated using a different definition and cannot be directly benchmarked against the quoted standard of 90% sensitivity for all abnormalities.  Once completed, consideration will be given to data protection requirements prior to any release of data as there is a risk that individual level data, even if anonymised, could be personally identifiable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he has taken to ensure private ambulances are appropriately regulated.
answer_text: Independent Ambulance Services (IAS) are utilised by the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) primarily to supplement its capacity to deliver routine Patient Care Services (PCS).  NIAS uses the support from IAS providers in the same way it utilises its own PCS crews i.e., for low-acuity, routine, non-emergency ambulance transport.  NIAS does not permit the use of blue lights and does not utilise IAS crews for emergency work, unless it is to support a NIAS crew at a cardiac arrest, or to respond to an emergency call which has been assessed by NIAS’s own clinicians as suitable for a non-emergency crew.Currently, governance and assurance is provided via NIAS, who undertake twice-yearly unannounced inspections of provider ambulances.  Quarterly governance meetings take place with all providers, which includes a plenary learning and update session, followed by a one-to-one meeting with each provider.I have asked my officials to undertake a review of the current non-emergency transport policy for HSC, and I anticipate that exploring the current regulatory position of private ambulance providers will form part of this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current percentage of health and social care spend allocated to general practice; and (ii) how this compares with other parts of the UK.
answer_text: The Department’s most recent Investment in General Practice report for the period 2019/20 to 2023/24 was published in October 2024. The report can be accessed at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/investment-general-practiceThe report shows that in 2023/24, the total investment in General Practice, including the reimbursement of drugs dispensed in General Practice, was £388million.  The Northern Ireland Audit Office report on Access to General Practice in Northern Ireland noted that in 2022/23, based on the £375m total investment (including reimbursement of drugs), the cost of General Medical Services under the GMS Contract was approximately 5.4% of overall health and social care spend.In 2023/24, based on the same methodology applied in the NIAO report, the total investment in general practice of £388m equates to 5.2% of the overall health and social care spend.Unlike other parts of the UK, Northern Ireland has an integrated health and social care system.  The overall health and social care budget in Northern Ireland therefore, includes funding for personal social services.In terms of health spend only, excluding spend on social care, in 2023/24, the total GMS spend as a percentage of revenue health spend in Northern Ireland (based on HMT National Statistics - Country and Regional Analysis), was 6.1%. These figures are for revenue spend only and do not include capital spend.It is important to note that in terms of General Medical Services, there are differences in the services delivered across the UK nations as well as in GMS contractual arrangements. This makes comparison of GP investment between other parts of the UK difficult.In light of this, UK-wide data on investment in General Practice, previously prepared and published by NHS Digital, was discontinued after 2018/19, as changes in contractual arrangements in each country has meant that payment categories are no longer directly comparable. The most recent published reports on Investment in General Practice in England and in Wales are for the 2022/23 financial year.  Information on investment in General Practice in Scotland has not been published since 2018/19.  Within the context of the caveats outlined above, the table below shows investment in General Practice (including reimbursement of drugs) in 2022/23 as a proportion of the health revenue spend for Northern Ireland, England and Wales.CountryInvestment in General Practice as proportion of Health Revenue Spend (2022/23)*Northern Ireland6.4%England9.0%Wales6.6%*Investment in General Practice based on the published Investment in General Practice Reports.  Health revenue spend as per HMT National Statistics - Country and Regional Analysis

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 22327/22-27, to detail the reason for the extent of the increase in the operating expenditure of the Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency in the last four years.
answer_text: The rise in operating expenditure costs in the Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency (NIMDTA) in the last four years is a result of NIMDTA assuming responsibility as the Single Lead Employer for Resident Doctors and Dentists in training in Northern Ireland. The first cohort of trainees transferred onto NIMDTA’s Payroll in August 2019, with the remaining groups transferring in a phased approach over the period up to December 2021. NIMDTA currently provides this function for over 2000 Resident Doctors and Dentists in training.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he will take to improve ambulance handover times in light of the March 2025 Northern Ireland Audit Office report Ambulance Handovers in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Audit Office report underlines the seriousness and scale of the pressures facing health and social care services and I note the recognition that the root cause of delayed ambulance handovers is the wider severe health and social care system pressures and hospital discharge delays. Patients are waiting too long in ambulances outside our emergency departments as a direct consequence of poor flow in our hospitals and social care constraints, increasing the risk of harm to patients and also impacting on NIAS’s capacity to respond to emergency calls in the community. While pressures manifest at the Emergency Department, flow through the system and getting those people medically fit for discharge back into the community remain significant issues, meaning that a whole system approach is required. Some progress has been made in delivering changes to services to meet the needs of patients and service users, such as the introduction of Urgent Care Centres and local Phone First services and the introduction of pathways for patients to access a range of services as alternatives to going to an Emergency Department, where this is appropriate.  It is important to reiterate that there is no quick fix and addressing the current situation will require sustained long-term effort, additional recurrent funding and support across the political spectrum. However, I remain determined to find better ways to work as a system to tackle these complex issues. I have asked my officials to look at initiatives used elsewhere and they are engaging with the London Ambulance Service, to understand the model they have implemented with Trusts in the London area which have resulted in improved ambulance handover times. I have also initiated a series of winter planning workshops to identify solutions to the system flow issues that we collectively face. I attended the first of these events on 4 March 2025 and was encouraged by the engagement and enthusiasm from HSC leaders across the system. I expect to see better outcomes in advance of next winter, including improvements to hospital handover times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any information he has on deaths or illness in relation to weight loss injections and weight loss products.
answer_text: The Department does not hold specific information on the number of deaths or illness associated with weight loss medicines, such as GLP-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1 RAs). The UK’s medicines regulator, the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA), carries out effective surveillance after marketing to ensure that adverse drug reactions (ADRs) relating to medicines are identified and that appropriate regulatory action is taken to protect public safety if needed. As with other medicines, GLP-1 RAs can cause ADRs. The specific known ADRs associated with particular GLP-1 RAs is outlined in the relevant Patient Information Leaflet (PIL) and Summary of Product Characteristics (SmPC) for individual products but may commonly include gastrointestinal adverse reactions such as nausea, diarrhoea, stomach pain, vomiting and constipation, as well as hypoglycaemia (low blood sugar). Other more serious, but less common, risks such as pancreatitis and gall bladder disorders have also been associated with GLP-1 RAs.Patients and professionals are encouraged to report any ADRs relating to GLP-1 RA medicines to the MHRA’s Yellow Card scheme. The MHRA publishes Interactive Drug Analysis Profiles (iDAP) which display an overview of all UK spontaneous suspected effects reported through the Yellow Card Scheme, including GLP-1 RAs. It is important to note however that reporters are asked to submit Yellow Card reports even if they only have a suspicion that the medicine may have caused the ADR, and so iDAPs should not be interpreted as a list of known side effects. It may be difficult to tell the difference between something that has occurred naturally and an ADR, and many factors have to be considered by the MHRA when assessing whether a medicine has caused a reported ADR. If a new side effect is identified, information is carefully considered in context of the overall side effect profile for the medicine, and how it compares with other medicines used to treat the same condition. The MHRA will take action, if necessary, to ensure that the medicine is used in a way that minimises risk, while maximising patient benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his initial estimate of the likely costs of additional remedial work required at the Belfast Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre, beyond the previously documented pipework problems.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has recently completed exploratory works in two bedrooms within the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre to investigate the damage caused by pipework leaks and water ingress in the building.  The Trust await a full report from the consultant recommending the extent of remediation work required along with the likely cost and programme implications.  Until such times as this report is received, I am not able to confirm the extent of remediation work required, nor the programme or cost implications of same.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the Framework Document detailing the roles and relationships of health and social care bodies was last (i) reviewed; and (ii) updated.

A further review is required to reflect the changes in the Health and Social Care Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 which gave effect to the closure of the Health and Social Care Board.  This will be taken forward when resources permit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of out-of-hours (OOH) GP shifts were unfilled at each OOH provider (i) in each of the last three years; and (ii) so far in 2025.
answer_text: Trust Area2022/232023/242024/25 (to date)SEHSCT43114227SHSCT1040449588BHSCT2520908268DUC1373211252WUC199213151040The information for this question is provided in financial years rather than calendar years. The issue of ensuring GP cover for the out of hours services has in recent years been particularly challenging. The clinical workforce can choose whether or not they wish to work in the GP OOHs service. The majority of clinical staff do so in addition to their daytime work, are retained by Providers on an ‘as and when required’ basis and the Providers are dependent on workforce availability in each area. Challenges in recruiting and retaining GPs include the fact that they are not contractually obliged to work in GP OOH; shifting attitudes in relation to achieving work life balance and the preparedness or willingness of doctors to cover out of hours sessions; and busier and more complex day time general practice reducing the inclination or ability of GPs to work additional out of hours sessions on top of their daytime practice.There has been a significant increase in the number of GPs and Advanced Nurse Practitioners (ANPs) working for the service over the past few years as well as the number taking contracted posts which has helped to improve fill rates.When viewing these figures it is important to remember the service’s multidisciplinary nature. OOHs providers do not require every advertised GP shift to be filled, and can still operate efficiently and meet the needs of clinically urgent patients with a smaller team. Unfilled shifts do not necessarily imply a lack of service provision.OOHs providers frequently deploy a mix of clinicians to ensure that urgent patient needs are met. Clinical cover can also be provided by Nurse Advisers, Advanced Nurse Practitioners (ANPs), Pharmacists, Mental Health Workers and Paramedics, who have been increasingly incorporated into the OOHs service over the past few years to improve clinical cover and increase resilience of the OOHs service.

answer_text: The rise in staff costs in the Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency (NIMDTA) between 2019 and 2024 is a result of NIMDTA assuming responsibility as the Single Lead Employer for Resident Doctors and Dentists in training in Northern Ireland. The first cohort of trainees transferred onto NIMDTA’s Payroll in August 2019, with the remaining groups transferring in a phased approach over the period up to December 2021.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 23004/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether patients who have been admitted with acute mental illness to the Belfast Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre are going to be required to be relocated to other sites.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has recently completed exploratory works in two bedrooms within the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre to investigate the damage caused by pipework leaks and water ingress in the building.  The Trust await a full report from the consultant recommending the extent of remediation work required along with the likely cost and programme implications.Apart from the two rooms where the exploratory works have been undertaken, the unit remains fully operational at present.  The Trust’s early assessment does not indicate that the unit will need to close while remedial works take place.  A partial decant may be necessary to facilitate the remediation works, however I have sought assurances on behalf of service users and their families that the Trust will ensure that care is provided in an alternative suitable facility should this be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will make a statement on the difficulties that have emerged with the new Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre, beyond the previously documented pipework problems.
answer_text: Having attended the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre along with the Chair and Deputy Chair of the Health Committee on 26th March 2025 and subsequently attending the Health Committee on 3rd April 2025 to discuss the issues associated with the Centre I can advise that I will not be making any further statement on this matter at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the structural problems in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s acute mental health unit, beyond the pipework issues that the Assembly has already been advised; and (ii) his estimate of the likely remedial costs.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has recently undertaken investigatory works within two bedrooms in the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre.  These works were undertaken to determine the extent of remediation works required to rectify the damage which has been caused by water ingress within the building.  The investigation works have only recently been completed and the Trust await a report which will detail the proposed remediation work and the anticipated costs and programme for the same.  Until such times as this report is received the Trust are not in a position to confirm the extent of remediation work, nor the programme or cost implications.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the effectiveness of Health and Social Care Trusts' winter preparedness plans for 2024-25.
answer_text: Winter pressures in 2024-25 have been challenging in a very difficult context, budgetary constraints, influenza infections leading to hospital admissions created challenges of infection control and staff sickness.Resolving winter pressure issues will take time, with the long-term solutions requiring sustained long-term effort, additional recurrent funding and support across the political spectrum.At this point in time, the effectiveness and overall impact of the measures included in the winter preparedness cannot fully be assessed. However, I intend to reflect on this during March, where I will be bringing together officials and leaders from across the system to make sure that we learn from this winter and do what we can, as early as possible, to build resilience to mitigate the pressures we will undoubtedly face again next winter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the introduction of (i) new cybersecurity services across health and social care; and (ii) digital services organisation in health and social care.
answer_text: The HSC Cyber Security Programme was established in 2018. It is a regional forum for Health & Social Care Organisations and NIFRS. Its purpose is to inform strategic direction, meet all challenges presented by Cyber Security within HSC, NIFRS and partner organisations, in a shared and coordinated forum, to disseminate learning and agree a collective approach for action of Cyber Security matters.The HSC Cyber Security Programme board, set the strategic direction of the HSC Cyber Security Programme, with each individual organisation held accountable for its implementation within their organisation.To date the programme has delivered a number of key strategic, technological and personal services across the HSC including:HSC Cyber StrategyHSC network security controlsTechnical layered security approach HSC Cyber Resourcing for Health TrustsAn agreed cyber incident response Vulnerability management identification and controlsStaff training and awarenessCyber Policy and StandardsDigital Health and Care NI (DHCNI) is part of the Department of Health and is established as the data and technology lead for the health and social care system in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what assessment he has made of the organisational governance within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The high-level governance arrangements for each Health and Social Care Trust in Northern Ireland are set out in the Governance Statements included in their annual accounts and are structured in a broadly similar way across the region – each Trust has a Board which has primary responsibility for oversight of Trust governance, several Board committees for detailed oversight , internal and external audit functions and is overseen by various regulators and the Department. The Belfast Trust has, in recent years, generally received a satisfactory annual assurance from Internal Audit and has provided formal assurances on internal control and governance. For context, the Belfast Trust has circa 20,000 whole time equivalent employees, delivering a diverse and complex range of services. The risks it faces across these service lines are diverse and complex. There have been several issues in relation to Estates and Capital Development functions within the Trust which have given me concern.In light of these issues I have asked my officials to work closely with the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to ensure that governance and oversight is improved in this area. Examples of improvements already implemented include a weekly meeting between the Trust Director of Strategic Development and senior departmental officials to ensure that the new maternity and children’s hospitals are progressing and the re-integration of Health Estates to my Department.Other improvements include submission of regular update reports on the maternity and children’s hospital to the Departmental Board and Health Committee to ensure that sufficient scrutiny and resource is provided to enable successful delivery of these crucial projects. It is my assessment that the organisational governance is appropriate however this will remain under continual review, and I will take further action where this becomes necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current number of children in care; and (ii) how that figure compares with each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Over the last ten years, the number of children in care has consistently increased, with figures published by my Department showing the following numbers of children in care: DateNo. of children in care31 March 20152,87531 March 20162,89031 March 20172,98331 March 20183,10931 March 20193,28131 March 20203,38331 March 20213,53031 March 20223.62431 March 20233,80131 March 20243,999Research has pointed to an enhanced awareness of child protection issues over recent years as one factor underpinning decisions to protect children who are potentially at risk by bringing them into care.  There is also an established link between increasing levels of child poverty and the numbers of children in care.  Children from the most deprived areas in Northern Ireland make up the highest proportion of children in care for 12 months or more (43% compared to just 5% of children from the least deprived areas). The report of the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services, published in June 2023, highlighted the impact of poverty and social deprivation on families, and was clear about the need for more support for families to help them care for their children.  My Department has established a programme of reform which is leading on the implementation of a number of the Review’s recommendations, including improving support for families and services for children with disabilities.  My Department publishes Official Statistics on the number of children in care in the annual report ‘Children’s Social Care Statistics for Northern Ireland’ (Children’s social care statistics for Northern Ireland 2023/24 | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk).  The next release of these Official Statistics for year ending 31 March 2025 is due in October.Additionally, my Department publishes monthly provisional figures on children in care (Northern Ireland children’s social care services data (provisional) | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration that has taken place in his Department on the introduction of (i) prescription charging; and (ii) domiciliary care charging.
answer_text: The three-year plan published by my Department in December 2024 includes a commitment to consult on potential charges for Prescriptions and Home Care, with the possible intent of raising additional funding for the Health Service. Any proposals for the introduction of these measures would require the approval of NI Executive Ministers, as this is a cross-cutting issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the percentage of the mental health budget that has been allocated to child and adolescent mental health in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The indicative budget allocation to Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) over the last 5 years is summarised below; alongside the allocation to CAMHS as a percentage of the planned mental health budget:Budget AllocationApr '20 - Mar '21Apr '21 - Mar '22Apr '22 - Mar '23Apr '23 - Mar '24Apr '24 - Mar '25CAMHS £m2327293132CAMHS % of Mental Health Budget7%8%8%8%8%The Mental Health Strategy acknowledged that CAMHS requires additional investment and Action 10 of MHS gives a commitment to increase funding to 10% of the Adult Mental Health budget. My Department will continue to work closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts and other key stakeholders to develop services that meet not only current but emerging needs of this population and work with executive colleagues to secure the necessary resources required to meet this Action.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the percentage of the overall health budget that has been allocated to mental health in each of the last five years.
answer_text: In each of the last 5 years, 7% of the overall health budget has been allocated to mental health: Budget AllocationApr '20 - Mar '21Apr '21 - Mar '22Apr '22 - Mar '23Apr '23 - Mar '24Apr '24 - Mar '25%%%%%Mental Health 7%7%7%7%7%It is clear that our mental health services require more funding. Within this context, the current Mental Health Strategy and its accompanying Funding Plan sets out what is required, including a commitment to increase the spend on Children and Adolescent Mental Health Services. While some progress has been achieved in delivering this commitment, in the absence of additional funding being allocated to my department for full implementation of the strategy, future funding decisions will necessarily reflect the continuing constraints facing the HSC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the proportion of consultations in general practice that have taken place face to face in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Data collection in relation to General Medical Services consultation numbers (General Practitioners and Practices Nurse) commenced in October 2020 to help with service planning and to help identify trends as the former Health and Social Care Board / General Practice transitioned through the pandemic.  From October 2021 to present, the data collected encompasses the whole practice team.  The data collected is a proxy of overall numbers across all General Practices and provides an overarching view of activity/trend information.    The following table provides the proportion of consultations in general practice that have taken place face to face from 2020/21 – 2023/24:   Financial YearMonths Total Number of ConsultationsPercentage of those that are face to face consultations2020/2021October 2020 – March 20213,296,381*(32%)2021/2022April 2021 – March 20228,463,825**(39%)2022/2023April 2022 – March 2023   9,401,964(45%)2023/2024April 2023 - March 2024  10,229,447(51%)* General Practitioners and Practices Nurse only**encompasses the whole practice team from October 2021

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the review of the Public Health Agency.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Adult Protection Bill.
answer_text: The draft Bill is largely complete, though discussions continue around one outstanding policy issue. However, it is not thought that the outstanding issue would impact introduction of the draft Bill as amendments could be made later, if necessary.My officials are currently engaging with Departmental Finance and Economists around the approval of the business case to support the draft Bill. Following approval of the business case, the draft Bill will be submitted to the Executive for their consideration in advance of introduction.It is intended that the draft Bill will be introduced to the Northern Ireland Assembly as soon as possible, following the completion of this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline how much of the £8.1 million funding, announced in February 2024 to repair the road network, has been spent in Upper Bann to repair potholes.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and this has had a significant impact on its ability to deliver essential services such as road maintenance. In February 2024 former Minister O’Dowd, allocated £8.1m of capital structural maintenance funding to improve the road network.  My Department does not manage the road network on a constituency basis, but I can confirm that £2.05 million was allocated to my Departments Southern Division.Southern Division covers 3 council areas.  This allocation breaks down to:£821k of capital funding to the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Council area;£820k of capital funding to the Newry, Mourne and Down Council area;and £411k of capital funding to the Ards and North Down Council areaI would also advise that pothole repairs are funded from a Resource allocation and as such none of this funding was used specifically to repair defects that met my Department’s Limited Service maintenance policy, other than defects that may have been repaired as part of the resurfacing works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why there was a twelve-week delay in cervical smear test results being sent to the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Laboratory provision for the NI Cervical Screening Programme moved to a single laboratory in Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, on 1st November 2024.  The time to receive a result is dependent on the result of the HPV test and whether additional cytology reporting is required. There are ongoing delays in the authorisation of completed test results to GPs as the process is currently manual. The majority of results awaiting authorisation and issue relate to HPV negative samples.  Testing of an electronic auto-authorisation system is underway and this process is expected to be completed in the next couple of weeks. This will clear the majority of results currently awaiting authorisation and will contribute to faster result times going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Data Institute.
answer_text: The case for the HSC Data Institute was set out in 2022 in the HSC Data Strategy. Discovery work was completed in September 2023, following significant engagement across DoH and HSC. Current focus is on strengthening existing data teams to support and work effectively across HSC/ DoH; reviewing governance structures to ensure best use of resources and support more effective decision making. A HSCDI business plan is being implemented with supporting work streams for; coding and standards, information governance, analytics and insights and research and innovation opportunities through the NI Trusted Research Environment (NITRE). Key achievements to date include: Primary Care Multi-Disciplinary Teams (PMT) programme data reporting, to support continued funding/extension of Programme, planning of HSC services, and identification of health incidence and associated management.Patient level costing project to deliver highly detailed analysis of patient costs across the Trusts.Integrated Care System (ICS) identifying data sources to ingest, analyse and create data visualisation dashboards for reporting on ICS indicators.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 20413/22-27, to detail any assessment he has made of how effective the measures have been in reducing delayed discharges.
answer_text: There are a number of monitored measures that continue to evidence positive gains made. My department however, are not in a position to map the direct correlation for each measurement, on every occasion for a number of reasons.  For example, when capacity is created in home care provision, it may go directly to an individual waiting in hospital, however on occasions the care will be directed to individuals waiting in the community or in a care home.  Nevertheless, in those circumstances those steps indirectly provide necessary flow through the community and hospital relating to discharge transitions.  My department remain confident that all measures taken are having a positive effect.Early Review TeamWhen this model was devised in October 2023, a number of measurements were agreed with Trusts and monthly returns provided to my department to determine the number of hours saved in the pilot period.  Between October 2023 and September 2024, a total of 2884 hours per week were recycled.  This equates to approximately 50 – 300 new home care packages.  Another return will be requested from Trusts to determine gains made between 01 October 2024 to 31 March 2025.Care Line LiveOne Trust that is at an advanced implementation phase with this initiative had recycled 4138 hours of home care provision by month 9.  These are hours that are utilised to provide home care for individuals delayed in hospital or in a care home unnecessarily.  This equates to 100 - 400 new individuals being able to avail of a home care package that otherwise may have been on a waiting list.Trusted Assessor Early measurements from this initiative evidences more timely discharges to care homes, reducing the number of days individuals are awaiting a pre-admission assessment for a care home. The Department will be putting further measures in place to capture the total bed day savings as a result of this initiative.Care Home Availability Digital AppThis initiative is saving professionals time by having access to data more efficiently, in turn saving that administrative time for professionals to undertake clinical interventions. This is only available on one Trust at present and is being rolled out regionally.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how much money will be invested in 2025 and 2026 for disability housing adaptation schemes.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any drug trials currently taking place in the United Kingdom relating to skeletal dysplasia; (ii) the stage these trials are at; (iii) the number of children from Northern Ireland with the condition that have been offered a place in these trials; (iv) the referral process; and (iv) why these trials were not available to all Northern Ireland children with the condition.
answer_text: The term “skeletal dysplasia” covers a broad range of conditions. This response relates to skeletal dysplasia, achondroplasia, osteochondrodysplasia, osteogenesis imperfecta and scoliosis. There are currently 8 studies taking place in the UK. These are:Losartan for older adolescents and adults with osteogenesis imperfecta - Be Part of Research - Trial Details - Losartan for older adolescents and adults with osteogenesis imperfectaStudy to Evaluate Efficacy and Safety of Romosozumab Compared With Bisphosphonates in Children and Adolescents With Osteogenesis Imperfecta - Be Part of Research - Trial Details - Study to Evaluate Efficacy and Safety of Romosozumab Compared With Bisphosphonates in Children and Adolescents With Osteogenesis ImperfectaBracing Adolescent Idiopathic Scoliosis Study (BASIS 2) - Be Part of Research - Trial Details - BASIS 2To Assess the Efficacy, Safety, and Tolerability of INCB000928 in Participants With Fibrodysplasia Ossificans Progressiva - Be Part of Research - Trial Details - To Assess the Efficacy, Safety, and Tolerability of INCB000928 in Participants With Fibrodysplasia Ossificans ProgressivaA Phase 3, Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled, Multicenter Study to Evaluate the Efficacy and Safety of Vosoritide in Children with Hypochondroplasia Vosoritide in Hypochondroplasia - Clinical Trials Registry - ICH GCPA Clinical Trial to Evaluate Efficacy and Safety of TransCon CNP Compared With Placebo in Infants (0 to <2 Years of Age) With Achondroplasia https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT06079398Study to Evaluate Efficacy and Safety of Romosozumab Compared With Bisphosphonates in Children and Adolescents With Osteogenesis Imperfecta https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05972551Extension Study of Infigratinib in Children with Achondroplasia (ACH) https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05145010All of these studies are open and recruiting at present.  The Department does not hold information about the number of children from Northern Ireland who may have been offered a place in these trials.  In order for children from Northern Ireland to take part in a trial, the relevant sponsor would have to open a recruiting site in Northern Ireland, or participants from Northern Ireland would have to be referred by agreement to another recruiting site elsewhere in the UK by a responsible medical consultant.   The Northern Ireland Clinical Research Network (NICRN) co-ordinate expressions of interest for clinical trials. The number of patients who can participate in a clinical trial is limited by the trial design as approved by an independent research ethics committee and the MHRA.  It is not possible for any trial to be available to all patients with a condition due to the limited numbers who will be recruited and the specific patient characteristics required by the study design.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current process to support and integrate children with skeletal dysplasia into the community and education settings.
answer_text: In supporting children with skeletal dysplasia, the role of the multidisciplinary team is crucial in supporting the Children or Young People (CYP) to develop so as they can engage in daily activities in as similar way as possible to their peers, to thrive and realise their full abilities.From as early a stage as possible, Allied Health Professionals (AHPs) such as sonographers and radiographers contribute to the diagnostic process and help to inform relevant treatment plans.Health Visitors through developmental reviews will ensure children are referred to the appropriate services for assessment of their needs and where appropriate are receiving additional support.Dependent upon their assessed needs, a child and their family will receive a range of treatment interventions to support their development and mastery of tasks, focusing on the child’s developing motor skill, respiration, speech, eating, drinking, swallowing, nutrition and independent skills. As the child continues to grow, development of their mobility is considered in respect of the possible need of custom-made prosthetics and the potential need to adaptation to their home and school environment in order to optimise the child’s overall development, independence and wellbeing.In order to support their learning and development within the educational setting CYP with skeletal dysplasia may require support from a number of AHPs, such as Physiotherapists, Occupational Therapists and Speech and Language Therapists. As appropriate and required, these professions will identify and provide any relevant treatment or service likely to be of benefit in addressing the Special Educational Needs (SEN) of the CYP.The ultimate aim of therapeutic supports and interventions delivered by AHPs is to enhance the child’s learning and school experiences, enabling them to reach their potential and fully access the educational curriculum. The therapeutic interventions provided to support CYP with SEN include directly working with the CYP in the school setting, providing tailored/specialist advice, programmes and specialised training for educational staff involved supporting the CYP to minimise the impact of their SEN. It also includes assessment and recommendation of necessary equipment and adaptations to meet the CYP’s needs in the school environment aimed at enhancing their independence, safety and overall management.Community Nursing Teams may also be involved in the care of children with skeletal dysplasia overseeing and reviewing their care plan and training staff within educational settings to support the children and young people in the school environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of children with skeletal dysplasia that have been referred to, or have had access to, (a) the Evelina specialist multidisciplinary clinic led by Dr Melita Irving at St Thomas’ Hospital, London; and (b) the specialist consultant Dr Paul Arundel based in Sheffield Children's Hospital; (ii) the referral process is; (iii) whether the referral process is the same across all Health and Social Care Trusts; and (iv) why access is not available to all children with the condition.
answer_text: 14 children with skeletal dysplasia have been recorded through the Extra Contractual Referral Processes as referrals to specialist centres in England including Evelina, GOSH, Alderhey and Stanmore Royal Orthopaedic Hospital.   Further breakdown of this information by centre given the very small number of patients could result in patient identification and breach confidentiality requirements.  Belfast Trust have established networked arrangements in place between Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children’s (RBHSC) endocrinology team and Sheffield Children’s Hospital.  This arrangement supports local access to this highly specialist service with Dr Arundel from Sheffield and the Belfast Team working jointly to deliver a bone clinic 3 times a year.  This arrangement offers local access to specialist care negating the need for an Extra Contractual Referral and travel to GB.In addition, and if required the specialist team in Belfast will refer or signpost families to support services to support children to live their lives.  The clinical management of children with this condition is always supported by a multidisciplinary approach, tailored to the child.  The spectrum of skeletal dysplasia is extremely broad, ranging from relatively minor conditions to severe life-limiting skeletal disorders and care is delivered responsively in line with the specific needs of the child. Extra Contractual Referrals must be completed by a secondary care consultant.  The need for referral is clinically determined by the referring consultant and all secondary care consultants have access to the ECR mechanism for this purpose.The majority of ECR referrals are via Belfast Trust as local secondary care clinicians usually refer complex patients to the regional specialist for assessment/ongoing care, to include, if appropriate, onward referral to a specialist NHS provider.The Department is not aware of any reason why the ECR mechanism would not be accessible to this cohort of children as the need for referral is clinically determined.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of additional families that expressed an interest between 1 July 2024 and 31 December 2024 in having their deceased family member’s clinical records reviewed in the Neurology Deceased Patients Review
answer_text: Within the period from 3 July to 31 December 2024 a further nine families of deceased patients have come forward to the RQIA to explore involvement in the Neurology Deceased Patients Review (DPR).  My Department and the RQIA will consider the outcome of the engagement with families who have come forward and will provide clarification on the next steps, including the numbers of deceased patients within the DPR Phase Three, as soon as practicable.

answer_text: The full business case approved amount for the New Children’s Hospital project is £671,282,501.85.  Department officials and Trust staff have reviewed the budget required and are content that the project can be successfully delivered for this amount.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the specialist training offered to medical professionals in relation to the treatment of patients with skeletal dysplasia and the associated health conditions.
answer_text: Skeletal dysplasia, as one of a group of conditions that occasionally require specialist orthopaedic care, is included in the curriculum for both the Clinical Genetics and the Trauma & Orthopaedics specialty training programmes. The Paediatric curriculum for trainees specialising in Endocrinology identifies skeletal dysplasia as a condition to manage. Paediatric trainees also have the opportunity during their Neonatology and general/community Paediatrics rotations to observe the diagnosis of skeletal dysplasia and the management of children with skeletal dysplasia in inpatient and outpatient settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the clinical pathway after diagnosis of skeletal dysplasia in each Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of children with skeletal dysplasia, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: My Department has advised this information is not available on the basis of a primary diagnosis search and partial data could be erroneous.  It is therefore not possible to give an overall breakdown for skeletal dysplasia by HSC Trust. The range of conditions within skeletal dysplasia include ahrondroplasia and osetogenesis imperfecta and can also manifest in other genetic conditions which affect other body systems such as Marfan's disease, storage diseases (e.g. Hurler's disease) and neurofibromatosis. There are other forms of skeletal dysplasia that are also quite mild with minimal impact and may be diagnosed incidentally on x-rays taken for other reasons. Each of these conditions is quite rare but because there are so many (400+) heritable disorders of bone there is a significant number of children involved.  Given this very broad range of conditions, the identification of patients under ‘skeletal dysplasia’ may be of limited value as the needs of the individuals are very different, as are the services that they need to access.

answer_text: There are no periods during 2024 when there have not been any doctors working throughout all of the out-of-hours GP services.

To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the construction costs for the new Children’s Hospital at the Royal Victoria Hospital, in light of the Northern Ireland Investment Strategy stating that this will be greater than £1 billion.
answer_text: The construction cost for the new Children’s Hospital at the Royal Victoria Hospital is £389,000,000.00.The Northern Ireland Investment Strategy Report is incorrect in stating that the construction cost is greater than £1 billion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how many people are diagnosed with dementia in Northern Ireland; and (ii) whether an assessment has been made on how this will change over the next two decades.
answer_text: Approximately 15,500 people currently have a dementia diagnosis in Northern Ireland.  It is estimated that there are currently over 25,000 people living with a form of dementia in Northern Ireland, this number is projected to rise to 43,000 by 2040. Northern Ireland is projected to have the largest increase in the number of people living with dementia across the UK by 2040. This is because the size of the older population in Northern Ireland is expected to increase much faster than the rest of the UK.Given the predicted increase in prevalence of dementia, coupled with the current diagnostic rate, it is estimated that by 2040 there will be 26,500 people diagnosed with a dementia in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his estimated annual cost to the health service as a result of obesity.
answer_text: The financial costs related to the harms caused by overweight and obesity are substantial. A report published by the British Heart Foundation in 2023 estimated the annual expenditure by the Department of Health on obesity to be £100m, while the overall societal cost of obesity in Northern Ireland is estimated at £500m per year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the introduction of the electronic transfer of prescriptions.
answer_text: My Departments Strategic Planning and Performance Group leads on the ePharmacy programme which includes a project to replace the current written prescription system with an electronic prescribing system. Whilst there is policy support for this programme my Department continues to operate under significant budgetary and financial constraints including in our Capital budget and timelines have not yet been confirmed.  I remain hopeful that the implementation of this project will be able to proceed once capital funding has been prioritised for this programme.There are four projects within the ePharmacy programme. 		Go Pharmacy NI (to support clinical services delivery in Community Services);		Electronic Transmission of Prescriptions;		Electronic Prescribing Eligibility System (EPES) replacement; and		NI Drug Tariff Masterfile.A business case is currently in development for capital funding, however due to pressures on the departmental capital budget, this is not likely to be prioritised until 2026/27 at the earliest.

answer_text: My Department recognises the ongoing challenges facing General Practice and the impact that increasing demand and workload pressures are having on GPs.Despite the pressures on General Practice and the number of practices that have handed back their contracts, with the support of GP Federations and Trusts in taking on contracts, my Department has been able to ensure that in recent years, only one of these contract hand-backs has resulted in practice closure. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his estimated annual cost to the health service as a result of diabetes.
answer_text: Diabetes affects nearly 115,000 people in Northern Ireland, with the number continuing to grow.When launched in 2016, the Diabetes Strategic Framework stated an average of 10 people were newly diagnosed with diabetes every day.  It was estimated that in the United Kingdom, 10% of the Health and Social Care budget was spent on the treatment of diabetes and its complications. This was equivalent to about £1 million daily in the context of Northern Ireland and was projected to rise to 17% of the total budget by 2035.Diabetes UK estimated the annual diabetes care cost in Northern Ireland is circa £291millon. This estimation is broken down through diagnosis, management and complications costing arising from living with diabetes, with projected costs raising to £524.1 million in Northern Ireland in 2035.However, many of the complications are preventable through early intervention and improved management of the lifelong condition.In 2024, an updated cost of illness analysis, commissioned by Diabetes UK and carried out by York Health Economics Consortium at the University of York, estimated that type 1, 2 and gestational diabetes together cost the UK almost £14 billion in 2021/22.The Northern Ireland Diabetes Network was established to deliver against the Diabetes Strategic Framework.  Over the last three years, in partnership with stakeholders, the Network has developed a Regional Integrated Pathway for Diabetes.  This will standardise access and provide equity to support improved treatment and care, with effective use of all available resources, to improve outcomes for all People Living with Diabetes across Northern Ireland.In addition, Public Health Agency invests in a number of programmes that align to diabetes prevention and continues to promote activities that will encourage healthy lifestyles.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 20712/22-27, to detail the number of times the two-hour backstop, introduced in February 2023 to reduce long waits outside Emergency Departments, was breached on each day in (i) December 2024; and (ii) January 2025.
answer_text: The total number of times the ambulance handover two-hour backstop was breached on each day in December 2024 and January 2025 is provided in Table 1.  Ambulances arriving at emergency departments should handover the patient within 15 minutes of arrival.Table 1 – Number of times the ambulance handover 2-hour backstop was breached each day in December 2024 and January 2025December 2024Total Ambulance Handover waits >2 hrsJanuary 2025Total Ambulance Handover waits >2  Department continues to work with Trusts and the Northern Ireland Ambulance Services (NIAS) to reduce ambulance handover delays.  These delays are directly impacted by overcrowding in emergency departments and, therefore, not solely within the control of NIAS, but which impact on NIAS’s capacity to respond to emergency calls.  All Trusts have developed improvement plans aimed at improving flow through emergency departments, including actions aimed at improving discharge delays.To reduce conveyances to emergency departments, my Department has provided funding to NIAS’s Integrated Care Hub which triages and prioritises calls waiting for an ambulance response, and provides clinical advice to direct suitable patients to the most appropriate services for their clinical needs.  This includes referrals to community-based Hospital at Home services and pathways for falls, diabetes and epilepsy.The Hub streamlines ambulance responses and contributes to reducing conveyances to emergency departments, thereby alleviating ambulance handover delays across the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his estimate of the annual costs of dementia; and (ii) whether an assessment has been made on how this will change over the next two decades.
answer_text: Our costing information does not capture health or social care spend per capita for individuals with a diagnosis of dementia.The London School of Economics and Political Science in November 2019 conducted an assessment into the total costs of dementia care across the 4 Nations of the UK. The total costs of dementia in the UK in 2019 amounted to £34.7 billion at 2015 prices, with Northern Ireland accounting for £0.8 billion.  This is the total cost associated with supporting older people living with dementia rather than the extra costs attributable specifically to dementia itself.  This is expected to triple by 2040.It is estimated that there are currently over 25,000 people living with a form of dementia in Northern Ireland, this number is projected to rise to 43,000 by 2040. Northern Ireland is projected to have the largest increase in the number of people living with dementia across the UK by 2040. This is because the size of the older population in Northern Ireland is expected to increase much faster than the rest of the UK.  Given the predicted increase in prevalence of dementia, coupled with the current diagnostic rate, it is estimated that by 2040 there will be 26,500 people diagnosed with a dementia in Northern Ireland.

answer_text: Administration CostsTable 1 below details the breakdown of administration costs in the Department and Arm’s Length Bodies.  These include the management costs when relevant.Allocations to the Trusts and Northern Ireland Blood Transfusion Service all fall under commissioning and therefore are not classified as administration costs. Trusts and NIBTS are therefore excluded from Table 1.The Business Services Organisation (BSO) carry out the administration functions of the Trusts through a Service Level Agreement.  These administration costs are therefore included in BSO administration costs.NIFRS do not split operation costs between administration and management costs therefore included in administration costs in above Table are the staff costs for non-uniformed employees.Table 1 – Administration CostsOrganisation2019-20 £’0002020-21 £’0002021-22 £’0002022-23 £0002023-24 £000Department *29,86736,09542,90396,305108,849ALBs:Health and Social Care Board*56,69660,56065,505--NI Practice & Education Council for Nursing & Midwifery1,2771,4251,4291,5051,423Northern Ireland Medical & Dental Training Agency2,0913,2363,1483,3274,106Business Services Organisation (BSO)   86,05296,452108,631122,853126,666The Children’s Court Guardian Agency Northern Ireland4,0584,3324,4094,6794,733Patient & Client Council1,3211,4491,6031,8151,800Public Health Agency22,02729,59236,85532,10034,315The Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority6,5307,0137,1607,4147,494Northern Ireland Social Care Council1,9872,1742,5692,6772,552NI Fire and Rescue Service9,65311,01411,06111,57512,927*From April 2022 the Health and Social Care Board was closed and brought into the Department of Health as the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG).Management CostsTable 2 details the management costs of the Department and ALBs.
