question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 20711/22-27, whether the review of the design and any proposed amendments will occur prior to the signing of a contract for the new Children’s Hospital.
answer_text: The review of the design of the water systems within the New Children’s Hospital and development of proposed amendments will not occur prior to the signing of the contract.All parties to the contract are aware that amendments will be required but these cannot be determined and implemented until the lessons learnt from the Maternity Hospital water system issues are established.   Any amendments required will be valued in accordance with the contract conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions that were requested of the contractor by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust during the defects liability period for new regional Maternity Hospital.
answer_text: Actions requested of the contractor during the defects liability period relate to the resolution of defects.  The contractor continues to work with the Trust and the consultant design team to resolve outstanding defects on the new Maternity building.  This work has been ongoing since handover of the building, in accordance with normal contractual procedures.  Particular attention has been given to scheduling any repairs of defects to ensure that they do not impact on extant water safety issues within the building.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions taken at the new regional Maternity Hospital by the contractor during the defects liability period.
answer_text: The contractor continues to work with the Trust and the consultant design team to resolve outstanding defects on the new Maternity building.   This work has been ongoing since handover of the building, in accordance with normal contractual procedures.  Particular attention has been given to scheduling any repairs of defects to ensure that they do not impact on extant water safety issues within the building.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount paid to Legionella specialists Hydrop in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) report that there have been no payments to Hydrop from 2020/21 to 2023/24 inclusive, Payments totalling £40.2k (excluding VAT) have been made to Hydrops by BHSCT between during 2024/25.The remaining four Health and Social Care Trusts and NIAS report that there have been no payments to Legionella specialists Hydrop in any of the last five financial years (2020/21 to date).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the domestic water system in (i) the neonatal unit; (ii) the maternity theatres; and (iii) other specific areas in the new regional Maternity Hospital can be separated off from the overall system of the wider hospital.
answer_text: I can confirm that the domestic water system in the neonatal unit, the maternity theatres and other specific areas in the new regional cannot currently be separated from the overall system of the wider hospital. It would be possible to undertake alterations to the system in the future to facilitate this separation.  Before such alterations could take place the extent of work required, the cost and time involved, and the potential impact on clinical areas of the hospital, would need to be determined.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an estimate of the amount of water that has been required thus far to flush the water system of the new regional Maternity Hospital; and (ii) to detail the associated cost.
answer_text: I refer the member to my response to AQW 21210/22-27

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the specific strains of Pseudomonas Aeruginosa detected in the new regional Maternity Hospital.
answer_text: All Pseudomonas aeruginosa isolates from water in the new maternity hospital have been characterised using a molecular typing method called "Multi-Locus Variable number tandem repeat Analysis" (MLVA)" in a UKAS / ISO 15189 accredited service provided by Belfast Trust. With only two exceptions, 300+ P. aeruginosa isolates from water sampling in the new hospital in 2023 and 2024 were indistinguishable using MLVA, with the same 4,5,12,5,5 profile. Further analysis of a subset of these isolates using genome sequencing confirmed all were closely related, belonging to the same (ST253) sequence type. For clarity I would note that a set of samples were taken in the building in January 2025.  Type testing from isolate from this round of sampling is not yet available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when (i) he; and (ii) his officials were first made aware of concerns about medical gas pipework in the new regional Maternity Hospital.
answer_text: I was first made aware of concerns about medical gas pipework in the new regional maternity hospital on the morning of 26 November 2024.My officials had been aware of this issue since October 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an estimate of the volume of water required per month for the flushing regime proposed for the new regional Maternity Hospital; and (ii) to detail the associated cost.
answer_text: During the construction of the new maternity hospital, the contractor reported high levels of pseudomonas aeruginosa (PSA) contamination within the domestic water systems. Following this the contractor carried out works on the water systems.    The contractor continued to test the water and provided a set of samples immediately prior to handover. The results of these samples were reviewed, and they met contractual obligations. The building was handed over on 29 March 2024.   Following handover, the Trust put the water systems within the building into ‘operational mode’ and ran a period of ‘steady state’. During this period, circa mid-May 2024 the Trust took sampling from all water outlets within the building, and PSA was detected in a significant number of outlets.  The flushing regime volumes of water since handover per month are contained in the following table:MonthVolume of Water Used (m3)Cost April 2024668£2,474.27May 2024352£1,303.81June 2024413£1,529.75July 2024520£1,926.08August 2024407£1,507.53September 2024401£1,485.30October 2024475£1,759.40November 2024429£1,589.02December 2024685£2,537.24January 20251,065£3,944.76February 20252,496£9,245.18March 20251,588£5,881.95April 20251,585£6,282.94Total11,084m3£41,467.24Based on the flushing regime of recent months (Jan 25 – April 25) post-handover it is estimated that the monthly volume required to flush until such times that the maternity hospital is occupied will be circa 1,680m3.  The associated NI Water charge (25/26 charge per m3 equals £3.96) for the supply of this water volume per month is circa £6,660.00.The average flushing consumption per month (April 24 to April 25) equates to 853m3. The increased volume per month of 1,600m3 has resulted from the following changes to the flushing protocol over the period:Addressing a number of “dead legs” in the system (sections of pipework where water was not flowing regularly), which have now been added to the flushing list; A number of contaminated outlets have also been removed with temporary “flushing tails” installed in their place.  These are typically lengths of copper pipe in lieu of taps, which discharge directly into basins and showers.  These flushing tails use more water than taps when the building is being flushed; andIntroduction of routine cleaning procedures which include significant usage and flushing of all taps and showers in the building to mimic normal usage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any progress made in increasing the availability of meaningful day activity for those with a learning disability.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts are responsible for the delivery of meaningful day activity for those with a learning disability, aligned to assessed needs. Meaningful day activity can be provided within buildings based Day Care setting or via community based Day Opportunities. Day services have been expanding regionally with a focus on transitioning out of buildings based Day Care, to community based Day Opportunities. This offers a more diverse range of services in local communicates such as social farming, further education and supported employment. The majority of these opportunities are available through the community and voluntary sector. Recent examples include the expansion of the Day Opportunities Programme in the Southern Trust into Armagh and Craigavon. The Trust has also developed new opportunities in the form of a coffee cart on their Bannvale site. The Northern Trust have also recently expanded community based Day Opportunities through a new performing arts service and a new social farming contract.Health and Social Care Trusts continue to engage with the community and voluntary sector, service users and carers to develop meaningful community based day activities.Health and Social Care Trusts are also actively reviewing pathways and processes to support better access to community based opportunities. This in turn increases availability within buildings based services for those that are assessed as requiring this type of service. My Department is developing a Learning Disability Service Model for Northern Ireland. Within this model there is a focus on meaningful day activity within the community, to ensure that individual needs can be met through a diverse range of services. Prioritising availability of buildings based day services for those with complex needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any progress made on the delayed discharge of individuals with a learning disability.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) continue to focus on and progress the safe discharge of adults with a learning disability, considered medically fit and delayed in hospital.All HSCTs currently have a small number of individuals delayed in hospitals. Planning from a multi-disciplinary, multi-agency and Independent Advocacy perspective is ongoing in all cases to facilitate appropriate discharge arrangements. HSCTs can however experience challenges securing appropriate community service provision, particularly for individuals with complex needs requiring bespoke arrangements. My Department continues to monitor the discharge of adults with a learning disability. I recognise challenges do exist in securing and developing suitable placements and services for adults with complex needs and in sustaining these placements post discharge within the community, particularly in the context of available funding. Workforce challenges, linked to the above, also impact upon the timely discharge of people to the community.Work is ongoing within my Department to finalise the Learning Disability Service Model. Throughout 2023/24, a body of work has been undertaken in respect of this. This included a thematic review of published research and clinical excellence guidelines, consideration of data to better understand population need and service capacity plus feedback from people supported, families/carers, professionals and providers. This work and feedback informed a draft Learning Disability Service Model which sets out the Department of Health’s vision regarding home, transitions, health and wellbeing, citizenship, families and carers (including short breaks) and mental health and behavioural support for people with a learning disability. All of the above will assist in ensuring people with learning disability are discharged into the community with the services and support they need.  A Public Consultation exercise on the model will take place in the coming months, pending its finalisation.My Department is also leading on the reform of Adult Social Care through the Social Care Collaborative Forum. The ‘Commissioning and Contracting’ workstream and the ‘Maximising Capacity’ workstream will support best use of current provision and expansion of placements within care home settings from a residential and nursing care perspective. I anticipate this will further assist with hospital discharges and the provision of respite/short breaks to support individuals and their carers post discharge. My Department remains committed to working with HSCTs and other agencies to facilitate the safe and effective discharge of adults with a learning disability and to sustain individuals in their community placements post discharge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any progress made in ensuring the availability of appropriate accommodation for those with a learning disability.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the provision of health and social care services in accordance with population and individual assessed need.  Whilst the provision of accommodation is not considered the specific remit of HSCTS, Trusts do provide care placements or services where adults with a learning disability reside. Such care placements may be within residential, nursing home, supported living or bespoke/single occupancy settings. HSCTS utilise statutory provision and work with independent sector providers to meet the needs of adults with a learning disability.HSCTs also work with other agencies such as the Northern Ireland Housing Executive to enable adults with learning disabilities to access accommodation, suitable to their needs. HSCTs continue to actively engage with independent providers to extend existing services or develop new services/range of accommodation types to meet the population needs of adults with learning disabilities. It is acknowledged that there are challenges in securing and developing suitable placements or accommodation for adults with complex needs and in sustaining individuals within their local community placements, particularly within a restricted funding or commissioning envelope.My Department is currently leading on the reform of Adult Social Care through the Social Care Collaborative Forum. The Commissioning and Contracting workstream and the Maximising Capacity workstream will support best use of current provision and expansion of placement provision within care home settings from a residential and nursing care perspective. My Department is also working from a strategic perspective to support adults with learning disabilities in line with assessed need to live in their own homes in local communities. Throughout 2023/24, a significant body of work has been progressed to inform the development of a service model for adults with a diagnosed learning disability who have been assessed as needing additional health and social care support. This work included thematic review of published research and clinical excellence guidelines, consideration of data to better understand population need and service capacity, alongside feedback from people supported, families/carers, professionals and providers. This work and feedback informed a Draft Learning Disability Model which sets out the Department of Health’s vision regarding home, transitions, health & wellbeing, meaningful lives & citizenship, families & carers (including short breaks), and mental health & behavioural support. My Department is working to finalise, ‘The Learning Disability Service Model’, with Public Consultation on the model anticipated in coming months.My Department remains committed to working with HSCTS and other agencies to ensure adults with learning disabilities have access to accommodation and services, in line with assessed individual and population need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any progress he has achieved in enhancing short break provision for those with a learning disability.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the provision of short breaks in accordance with population and individual assessed need. HSCTs have procedures in place to optimise capacity within the resources available to them. My Department has made significant progress in enhancing short break provision for adults with learning disability, by investing £26.75 million recurrently in short breaks. This an increase of £2.5million from the previous year. Most noticeably there has been a 48% increase in short break provision within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.Work is also ongoing within my Department to finalise the Learning Disability Service Model. This work sets out my Department’s vision regarding a future model for learning disability services, including improved access to short breaks and greater support for families and carers. Public Consultation is anticipated in coming months, pending finalisation of the model.My Department is also leading on the reform of Adult Social Care through the Social Care Collaborative Forum. The ‘Commissioning and Contracting’ workstream and the ‘Maximising Capacity’ workstream will support best use of current provision and expansion of placement provision within care home settings from a residential and nursing care perspective, this will assist with the provision of short breaks to support individuals and their carers. My Department remains committed to working with HSCTS and other agencies to offer short break provision to adults with a learning disability and to offer support to their families/carers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department’s approach to the implementation of National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidelines.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Guidelines are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. While all NICE guidance is considered to be best practice in Northern Ireland, my Department has published circulars on four specific types of NICE guidance including: NICE Technology Appraisals (TA) NICE Clinical Guidelines (CG)NICE Interventional Procedures Guidance (IPG) NICE Public Health Guidance (PH). Each of these circulars sets out the roles and responsibilities for each HSC organisation in regard to the process for endorsement, implementation, monitoring and assurance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any progress made on implementing National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guideline 47 on attention deficit hyperactivity disorder.
answer_text: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) Guideline 87 covers recognising, diagnosing and managing attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) in children, young people and adults. It aims to improve recognition and diagnosis, as well as the quality of care and support for people with ADHD.This guideline was endorsed for implementation in May 2018, with HSC Trusts working towards implementing the guidance from that date. Trusts are expected to implement the guidance as far as possible within existing resources, as there is currently no dedicated funding source to support the implementation of NICE Guidelines in Northern Ireland and any resources required are considered against other competing priorities. In this context, further work is required to achieve full implementation of NG87 across all Trusts.The Department of Health has recently commissioned research on ADHD, the findings of which will help inform options for a future commissioned ADHD service, subject to future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any conversations he has had with the Minister of Finance for additional money to offset the rise in employers' National Insurance contributions for the voluntary and community sector.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail when a Regional Mental Health Service will be operational in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Establishment of a Regional Mental Health Service for Northern Ireland is an ambitious and complex project bringing together leadership from across a range of organisations and sectors to work collectively in developing a regionally consistent, integrated system of mental health care to our population.To support this project, a five-year implementation plan has been codesigned with representatives from primary and secondary care, the voluntary and community sector and people with lived experience. This plan is publicly available on:RMHS Publications - DOH/HSCNI Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) and includes details on key actions and timescales for delivery. Work has commenced on delivery of year one actions within the plan and progress to date has focussed on developing arrangements for enhancing the voice of services users in the early development of the new service, including the appointment of a Regional Service User Consultant to lead this work. In parallel, work has also commenced to put in place the enabling organisational arrangements, including establishment of a Regional Collaborative Board who will lead the development and delivery of the new service.  Subject to continuing progress and securing the necessary additional funding, it is anticipated that the new service will be fully operational by 2029.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the progress made in reducing the reliance on inpatient services for those with a learning disability.
answer_text: It is a priority for me to reduce reliance on inpatient services for adults with Learning Disabilities. I want to ensure that inpatient resource is available when it is needed and where possible, that individuals receive their care and treatment within their own home, if in accordance with assessed need. It is important that people are supported to live in their community through preventative care, such as provision of adequate short break services and robust day services, to meet their assessed need.  Enhancements to short break services, Day Opportunity and community service provision will be considered as part of the draft Learning Disability Service Model. Public Consultation is anticipated in coming months, pending finalisation of the draft model.Where adults with learning disabilities experience mental health issues and become unwell, it is envisaged individuals should have access to a stepped model of care, within the community; enabling them to access increased support at a point of crisis. This stepped model approach would provide options to prevent hospital admission and support reintegration into the community, when someone no longer requires inpatient treatment. However it is acknowledged that inpatient treatment may be the most suitable option in keeping with assessed need. A stepped care approach linked to community and inpatient provision for adults with learning disability is being considered in respect of a regional Learning Disability Inpatient Model, being progressed by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any progress made in improving the transition from children’s services for those with a learning disability.
answer_text: My Department’s strategic response to the increasing level of need for Children with Disability across Northern Ireland has been set out through a regional Framework for Children with Disability. Focused Work is ongoing to finalise the Children with Disability Framework, which will better enable Trusts and independent sector providers to respond to the pressure points across Health and Social Care, specifically: the increasing demand for early and community-based support; the increasing demand for respite care/short breaks; the increasing demand for more bespoke residential placements; andbetter planning and management of the transition’s pathway from children to adult services.In terms of Adult Learning Disability Service - my officials are currently finalising a draft service model for Learning Disability, which also includes an action to improve Transitions for young people with Learning Disability in Northern Ireland.  My officials in both Children with Disability and Adult Learning Disability have worked together and collaboratively with a range of key stakeholders to develop a Draft Regional Transitions Protocol for Children and Young People with a Learning Disability, including Children and Young People with a Learning Disability and Co-occurring Autism. The primary aim is that this protocol will facilitate integrated service delivery and transition planning across Health, Social Care and Education.The outputs of both the Children with Disability Framework and the Learning Disability Service Model will feed into costed proposals which will seek to improve Transitions for children with learning disabilities. However I do need to caution that the current financial challenges my department faces are considerable

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action being taken to eradicate illegal dental consultations, examinations or procedures, particularly by organisations or individuals from outside the UK.
answer_text: To offer private dental consultations in Northern Ireland, the individual practitioner providing the service must be registered with the General Dental Council (GDC); and the “establishment” used must be registered with RQIA in accordance with Article 12 of the HPSS (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 and the Independent Healthcare Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005. These Regulations also require the practitioners involved to be registered with the GDC, as a condition of registration of the establishment.  My Department is aware that there has been an increase across the UK of dental companies from outside of the UK offering a “consultation” at a venue in Northern Ireland or GB prior to patients travelling abroad for dental treatment. If the “consultation” were to fall into the “practise of dentistry” then this is illegal unless the dental professional is registered with the GDC and the premises are registered with RQIA.Both RQIA and the GDC have recently updated their information for patients considering dental treatment abroad and attending a dental pop-up event in the UK firstly.  This is available through their respective websites.  This includes advice for patients on questions that they should ask to ensure they are aware of the risks of travelling abroad for care.Any intelligence received by my Department in relation to potentially illegal dental examinations, procedures or consultations is shared with the relevant regulatory bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration given to providing local NHS dental practices that are deemed hospitals by the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority, the same relief from National Insurance contributions being offered to hospitals in England.
answer_text: Dental Practices which provide an element of private care are required to register with the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority under the category of Independent Hospitals – Dental Treatment. Any dental practice which provides exclusively Health Service Care is excluded from this requirement. All General Dental Services practices, whether registered as Independent Hospitals or not, are private businesses; as such, they would not be currently eligible for any support to be targeted at public sector organisations. Some may be eligible, however, for reduced contributions through the Employment Allowance, depending on their level of Health Service work.I recognise that the increase in employer’s National Insurance contributions announced in the Autumn Budget will significantly increase the cost-base for Family Health Service and Social Care providers at a time when these services are fragile.GPs, community pharmacies, dentists, optometrists, care homes and hospices have all raised concerns about the impact this will have on them.While my department does not hold the detailed information to make absolute estimates, we have assessed that the projected increased costs will be some £36.5 million.Currently I am not anticipating additional funding will be provided to cover these additional costs, however I have written to the Secretary of State for Health and Social Care, to urge him to work to ensure the treatment of these providers faced with these cost pressures is equal to our other providers of health and social care.I have also written to the Finance Minister asking that he also bring his influence to bear on HM Treasury in this regard and I am aware that his predecessor has written to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury in similar terms.Following the announcement of the Draft Budget allocation my department has assessed that we will be facing a significant funding gap in 2025/2026 as a result of both inflationary pressures and increased demand.In recognition of the valuable role played by those providers affected, I will be considering these funding pressures alongside other priorities before I make any final funding decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the conclusion in the March 2024 Northern Ireland Audit Office report Access to General Practice in Northern Ireland that, such was the importance of progressing multi-disciplinary teams in primary care, that his Department needed to find ways to fund further implementation regardless of whether additional funding was likely to be forthcoming from the Department of Finance; and (ii) to detail any actions his Department has taken as a result.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the duration in weeks of the longest current wait for (i) a day case; and (ii) inpatient admission, broken down by speciality.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of paragraph 50 of the May 2024 Elective Care Framework Report which states, surgeons – one of the most expensive resources in the Health and Social Care system – spend less time in theatre than surgeons in other jurisdictions; and (ii) to detail any actions he is taking to address this.
answer_text: The time individual surgeons spend in operating theatres will be related to a number of factors including the consultant’s surgical area, the type of surgery provided at the hospital they are working in and funded theatre sessions available to the consultant. Currently, surgeons’ time is divided between different activities, including operating theatres, outpatient sessions, ward rounds, multi-disciplinary meetings and on call sessions.The Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan, published on 30 May 2025, gives a commitment to increase the time a consultant surgeon spends in theatre.  It is the intention that, as capacity is built, the consultant surgeon’s time in theatre will increase to at least two days a week. It is recognised that this will take time and there is a need for promotion and implementation of new ways of working and the development of new multidisciplinary roles to free up consultants to spend time in theatres. In addition, additional theatre capacity will be required, which is expected through expanding operating days and weekend working.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the duration in weeks of the longest current wait for a first outpatient appointment, broken down by specialty.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the spend on short breaks for (i) children; and (ii) adults in each of the last six years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the planning and delivery of respite/Short Break services, aligned to assessed needs. My Department has commissioned short breaks amounting to circa £154.6m in the last six years for children and adults. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the introduction of referral to treatment waiting time targets.
answer_text: My Department fully supports the development of a referral to treatment target. This target will require patients to be tracked from when they are referred from their GP and onwards through all subsequent assessment, diagnostic and treatment episodes. Such tracking will require a clear and succinct set of rules and well-defined definitions from referral to treatment. From a technical perspective, when the new regional encompass system is fully rolled out later this year, Northern Ireland would be well placed to be able to measure the full patient journey.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the performance level of the screeners and the management of the service at the Belfast cervical screening laboratory from 2013 to 2022; and (ii) to detail the cervical screener sensitivity for each of the four local screening laboratories for each year it is available since 2013.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s performance has been reviewed by Public Health Agency and the assessment of the Quality Assurance team was that the laboratory performance operated within expected quality parameters and in line with national standards for performance.  Regular review of performance data of the four screening cytology laboratories, which were operational from 2013 to 2022, was undertaken by the quality assurance team in PHA. This included an annual review of overall laboratory performance and individual screener and consultant performance against national standards and best practice indicators which were applicable at that time. The table below details the sensitivity of primary screening for all abnormalities for each of the four local screening laboratories for each year since 2013.Please note that this measure was only introduced as a new national standard in 2018 with a performance threshold of =90% applied. It was endorsed for application in Northern Ireland by the Chief Medical Officer in July 2019 and applied to annual data in Northern Ireland from 2020/21. Sensitivity is defined as the percentage of samples with abnormal cells which are correctly identified at primary screening before the rapid review process. The data below is calculated in line with the national definition. This data is subject to variation according to internal laboratory processes and has not been validated or quality assured prior to 2020/21. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the estimated volume of water used; and (ii) the costs involved in flushing the water systems at the new Maternity Hospital in Belfast since the discovery of Pseudomonas.
answer_text: During the construction of the new maternity hospital, the contractor reported high levels of pseudomonas aeruginosa (PSA) contamination within the domestic water systems. Following this the contractor carried out works on the water systems.The contractor continued to test the water and provided a set of samples immediately prior to handover. The results of these samples were reviewed, and they met contractual obligations. The building was handed over on 29 March 2024.Following handover, the Trust put the water systems within the building into ‘operational mode’ and ran a period of ‘steady state’. During this period, circa mid-May 2024 the Trust took sampling from all water outlets within the building, and PSA was detected in a significant number of outlets.  The flushing regime volumes of water since handover per month are contained in the following table:MonthVolume of Water Used (m3)Cost April 2024668£2,474.27May 2024352£1,303.81June 2024413£1,529.75July 2024520£1,926.08August 2024407£1,507.53September 2024401£1,485.30October 2024475£1,759.40November 2024429£1,589.02December 2024685£2,537.24January 20251,065£3,944.76February 20252,496£9,245.18March 20251,588£5,881.95April 20251,585£6,282.94Total11,084m3£41,467.24Between handover on 29 March 2024 and 30 April 2025 11,084m3 of water has been used within the new maternity hospital.  The NI Water charge for the supply of this water volume is £41,467.24.  The labour, plant and material costs incurred by the Trust in carrying out the flushing for the same period is circa £240,000.00.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail who signed off on behalf of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to accept the handover of the new Maternity Hospital from the contractor; and (ii) for his assessment of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust apology to the Committee for Health for mistakenly advising that it was the former Chief Executive that signed off to accept the handover.
answer_text: The Belfast Health & Social Care Trust have advised that Maureen Edwards, in her role as Senior Responsible Owner, accepted the handover of the new Maternity Hospital from the contractor based on assurances received from the Consultant Design team and DoH Health Estates.I welcome the fact that the Trust wrote to the Health Committee to correct the record having mistakenly advised the Committee verbally on 5 December 2024 that it was the former Chief Executive who had accepted handover.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any dates on which the Organisational Governance Steering Group of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust was notified of concerns of the Water Safety and Usage Group about the new Maternity Hospital project.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust's Water Safety and Usage Group notified the Trust's Organisational Governance Steering Group of issues concerning the new Maternity Hospital project on the following dates:January 2023, March 2023, May 2023, September 2023, January 2024, April 2024 and October 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the effectiveness of the operation of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s governance and assurance arrangements in the context of difficulties with the new Maternity Hospital and its handover from the contractor.
answer_text: Following the difficulties with the new Maternity Hospital after its handover from the contractor I have sought assurance that the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s governance and assurance arrangements are effective. I can confirm that Belfast Trust’s structures and governance arrangements for the delivery of major capital projects are broadly aligned with industry best practice and the structures recommended by the Department.  This includes oversight from the Trust Board, and relevant Programme/ Project Boards of the ongoing delivery through suitably resourced Project Teams. I can confirm that in reflection of the scale, complexity of the capital projects currently being delivered, the Trust have strengthened their structures and at Trust Board / Executive Team level through the creation of the post of Director of Strategic Capital Development. The Trust have also strengthened oversight at Programme Board level through the addition of “critical friend” roles on Boards (independent, experienced senior executives with knowledge of the industry and similar projects). These structures mark an enhancement to the typical structures of a major capital project and are considered necessary and appropriate for the scale and complexity of the projects being undertaken.  In reflection of lessons learned from the recent major NHS hospital builds, the project structures for the Children’s and Maternity Hospital projects are currently being enhanced through the creation of Project Safety Groups.  This structure is in alignment with emerging NHS guidance for construction projects. The Project Safety Groups will support the Project Team and will when necessary report and escalate issues to Programme Boards. All projects will be managed on a day-to-day basis through a Project Team comprising representatives from BHSCT, Health Estates, the consultant design team and the contractor. In summary I am content that the aforementioned enhancements to the Belfast Trust’s existing governance and assurance arrangements allow me to be satisfied that these are and will continue to be effective.

answer_text: Since 2017, my Department has commissioned a total of 234 Advanced Nurse Practitioner (ANP) training places, 164 in HSC Trusts and 70 in Primary Care. The breakdown of training places each year from 2017 to date is set out in the table below.YearANP Commissioned Places in HSC TrustsANP Commissioned Places in Primary Care2017 – 2018052018 – 20191662019 – 202023142020 – 20212472021 – 20223172022 – 202316122023 – 20242992024 – 20252510Total16470Advanced Nurse Practitioner training places have been funded from the Department of Health’s Nursing and Midwifery Post-Registration Education Commissioning budget and from GP Federations.The number of Advanced Nurse Practitioner training places commissioned by the Department of Health to meet population health needs is informed through operational workforce planning and annual training needs analysis, which is the remit of the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts and Primary Care.  There is a substantial body of evidence as to the impact and benefit of advanced practice roles, including increasing access to services, cost-effectiveness, workforce efficiencies and patient outcomes, and decreasing waiting times, hospital admissions, mortality, and overall costs. The Department is committed to growing the advanced practice workforce, subject to available budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of haematology staffing levels, including in the provision of chemotherapy.
answer_text: The haematological workforce is diverse and multidisciplinary. Disciplines include medical, nursing, pharmacy and pathology (including biomedical scientists) and support staff. Haematology services in NI have been under increasing staffing pressure for some time with several trusts reporting consultant and middle tier vacancies, and vacancies in pathology services.  There is a growing gap between demand for haematology services and the current workforce capacity. The British Society of Haematology workforce report highlights many vacancies in the haematology workforces across all disciplines and across the UK.  Consequently, NI is competing with the rest of the UK and ROI for a limited pool of professionals.Medical WorkforceThe Public Health Agency (PHA), on behalf of the Department, is conducting a Haematology Workforce Review. This report will evaluate the need for specialist trainees by considering various factors, including current and future consultant requirements, demographic trends, regional strategies, professional standards, and work-related factors.Specialist and Advanced Nursing WorkforceBased on the projected haematology nurse workforce a 5-year demand and capacity gap has been developed identifying a need for additional resources in the areas of Clinical Nurse Specialists (6.6wte), Haematology Nurse Practitioners (4.0 wte) and Advanced Nurse Practitioners (5wte). Pathology A recent Pathology Workforce Review Report (June 2024), examined combined haematology and transfusion pathology staffing levels.  A Regional Pathology Workforce Implementation Board has been established and is working in partnership with DoH Workforce Policy Directorate to align recommendations to the overall HSC Workforce Strategy 2026 ‘Delivering for our people’ Action plan 3. Skill mixIn 2020 in recognition of the demand capacity pressures and consultant recruitment issues in both oncology and haematology cancer services, a detailed Oncology Haematology Stabilisation Plan set out the requirements in terms of whole-time equivalents and associated costs for the cancer workforce in Northern Ireland. This plan focused largely on the development of sustainable teams that will create the capacity for skills mix and innovation whilst simultaneously reducing clinical risk and improving the patient experience of care. To date £3.24 million has been invested into haematology services staffing across a range of roles during 22/23 and 24/25 as part of NI cancer strategy 2022-2032 funding.My department currently facilitates a Regional Haematology Services Pressures group to address services pressures and explore opportunities for a greater skill mix. Investing in Clinical Nurse Specialists and Advanced Nurse Practitioners (ANPs) will help alleviate the consultant capacity crisis and enhance the skill mix in the cancer care pathway and treatment journey for blood cancer patients. Investment in the nursing workforce is also Action 39 of the NI Cancer Strategy. However, addres

To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether a cytology slide from smear samples continues to be available for review by a biomedical scientist as back-up to HPV testing; and (ii) how this compares with the position in other parts of the UK.
answer_text: Following the introduction of primary HPV testing into the cervical screening pathway in Northern Ireland, where a woman tests positive for high risk types of HPV, a cytology test is then performed to check for any abnormal cells in the sample by a cytology screener. The rest of the UK follows this process as recommended by the UK National Screening Committee.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the legal costs incurred by (i) his Department; and (ii) each of its arm's-length bodies, in each of the last five years.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 10676/22-27, to detail the information on ambulance handover times and other metrics that has been made available to the public on his Department’s website since 1 November 2024, including the dates on which the information was provided.
answer_text: Since 19 November 2024, the Department has maintained a Service Pressures Webpage on the HSC online website at Service Pressures - DOH/HSCNI Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) (https://online.hscni.net/our-work/service-pressures/) to provide a regional overview of the current pressures and challenges across the HSC System in Northern Ireland. This webpage is updated daily from Monday-Friday with the following data: A 12-noon snapshot of waiting times across Emergency Departments;The Number of Patients with a Decision to Admit and waiting for a hospital bed at 12 noon;The Number of Emergency Department attendances in the previous 24 hours.A weekly summary of key indicators of service pressures in the latest week has also been provided on the website covering:Number of ED AttendancesNumber of ED waits >12 hoursThe % of waits < 4hoursFrom Monday 13 January 2025, the HSC online website has also published data on the number of patients declared medically optimised for discharge with complex discharge needs. This is a snapshot of the position each morning at 10.30am.  Data on Ambulance Handover Delays is routinely monitored but is not currently published on the HSC online website nor the Department of Health’s website.However, reducing ambulance handover delays outside emergency departments remains a key priority due to the impact this has on the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service’s (NIAS) capacity to respond to calls in the community, particularly Category 1 and Category 2 emergency calls.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the impact of additional costs, including National Insurance and National Minimum Wage rises, on general practice, dental practices, hospices and care homes following the Autumn Budget 2024; and (ii) to detail any assistance he is offering.
answer_text: I recognise that the increase in employer’s National Insurance contributions announced in the Autumn Budget as well as the increase in National Minimum wage will significantly increase the cost-base for Family Health Service and Social Care providers.  Currently I am not anticipating additional funding will be provided to cover these additional costs, however I have written to the Secretary of State for Health and Social Care on the issue of National Insurance, to urge him to work to ensure the treatment of these providers faced with these cost pressures is equal to our other providers of health and social care.I’ve also written to the Finance Minister asking that she also bring her influence to bear on HM Treasury in this regard and I am aware that she has written to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury in similar terms.Following the announcement of the 2025-26 Draft Budget allocation my department has assessed that we will be facing a significant funding gap in 2025-26 as a result of both inflationary pressures and increased demand.In recognition of the valuable role played by those providers affected, I will be considering these funding pressures alongside other priorities before I make any final funding decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 18886/22-27, to confirm that no concerns were raised about the medical gas system at any point prior to October 2023.
answer_text: I cannot confirm that no concerns were raised about the medical gas system at any point prior to October 2023.  As part of the design, construction and commissioning process for a complex hospital project like the new Maternity Hospital it is considered common practice for project team members to raise concerns and points of challenge throughout the life cycle of the project to provide assurance that the system is fit for purpose and value for money

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 17487/22-27, to detail any occasions his Department used the Central Procurement Directorate previously for construction works and design.
answer_text: My Department has not used the Central Procurement Directorate for construction works and design.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures he plans to take to ensure that any contract for the new Children’s Hospital would not be signed by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust in advance of clarification on whether a significant redesign or addendum to the business case is required.
answer_text: A contract has not yet been awarded for the new Children’s Hospital though contract award is imminent.  Departmental officials and Belfast Health and Social Care Trust staff are working tirelessly to ensure that the contract for the new Children’s Hospital reflects any lessons learnt from the new Maternity Hospital.There is no requirement for a significant redesign currently.  If there is a reduction in the number of water outlets or a recommendation becomes apparent when the final report into the Water System at the Maternity Hospital is completed any change to the Scope will be handled in accordance with the contract conditions.An addendum is not currently required.  Departmental officials and Trust staff have reviewed the budget required and are content that the project can be successfully delivered for this amount which is within the 10% permitted variance to the approved FBC amount.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether laboratory accreditation formed any part of the scoring matrix in the process for determining the location of the new regional cervical screening service.
answer_text: Expression of Interest applications for a single regional laboratory were scored across 13 elements, of which UKAS accreditation was one.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 17606/22-27, for his assessment of the effectiveness of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s Integrated Governance and Assurance Framework in respect of the new regional Maternity Hospital project.
answer_text: I can confirm that Belfast Trust’s structures and governance arrangements for the new regional Maternity Hospital project were broadly aligned with industry best practice and the structures recommended by the Department.  This includes oversight from the Trust Board, and relevant Programme/ Project Boards of the ongoing delivery through suitably resourced Project Teams.In recognition of the of the scale and complexity of the capital projects currently being delivered by the Trust and the issues that have arisen on a number of capital projects undertaken by the Trust, including on the regional Maternity Hospital project, a decision was taken by the Trust in conjunction with my Department to strengthen the structures at Trust Board / Executive Team level through the creation of the post of Director of Strategic Capital Development.In the latter stages of the Maternity Hospital project the Trust strengthened oversight at Programme Board level through the addition of “critical friend” roles on the Board (independent, experienced senior executives with knowledge of the industry and similar projects). These enhancements are considered necessary and appropriate for the scale and complexity of the projects being undertaken. The regional Maternity Hospital project was overseen and managed on a day-to-day basis through a Project Team comprising representatives from BHSCT, Health Estates, the consultant design team and the contractor. In summary I am content that the enhancements to the Belfast Trust’s existing governance and assurance arrangements reflect the fact that the Trust, in conjunction with my Department, have taken corrective action following the issues that have arisen on a number of capital projects.  I am keeping these projects under continual review and will take further action where this becomes necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 17609/22-27, following the difficulties at the new Maternity Hospital, whether Belfast Health and Social Care Trust and departmental officials have given consideration to (i) a reduced number of water outlets; and (ii) the ability to isolate off specific sections of the overall water system, in the new Children’s Hospital.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust will be reviewing a number of areas of the design and commissioning of the water systems in the new Children’s Hospital in light of emerging lessons learned from the new Maternity Hospital. Areas to be considered will include but not be limited to a review of the quantity of water outlets and potentially isolating sections of the overall water system. It should be noted that any proposal to amend the water system in the new Children’s Hospital will need to be reviewed by a multi-disciplinary team and any implementation of proposed changes will be subject to an assessment of clinical impact along with costs and programme impacts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assurances has he sought personally to ensure lessons have been learned from the new Maternity Hospital project, prior to signing contracts for the new Children's Hospital.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail sickness absence rates for (i) his Department; and (ii) each of its arm's-length bodies, in each of the last five years.

answer_text: The total number of patients in emergency departments who were waiting for admission to a hospital bed is provided in Table 1.  This information is taken from a live dashboard and shows the position at noon each day.  Information is not recorded at weekends and bank holidays and therefore is not available (N/A) on those dates.Table 1 - Number of Patients Waiting in Emergency Departments for Admission to a Hospital Bed (noon position) from 1/12/2024 to 17/1/2025DateNumber of Patients01/12/2024N/A 02/12/202437103/12/202438804/12/202433805/12/202433006/12/202433307/12/2024 N/A08/12/2024 N/A09/12/202436010/12/202437611/12/2024 N/A12/12/202431113/12/202432414/12/2024 N/A15/12/2024 N/A16/12/202440318/12/202436219/12/202438720/12/202434723/12/202433624/12/202426527/12/202432930/12/202446231/12/202442102/01/202540503/01/202540604/01/2025 N/A05/01/2025 N/A06/01/202542607/01/202541308/01/202538309/01/202534810/01/202527811/01/2025 N/A12/01/2025 N/A13/01/202532214/01/202534415/01/202535116/01/202535517/01/2025322My Department recognises the potential risk of harm for patients who experience long waiting times in the emergency department.  Following a review of Emergency Medicine in NI, my Department is taking forward a number of actions to improve pressures on the wider unscheduled care system to reduce overcrowding and delays in emergency departments.  Trusts were required to develop winter preparedness plans for 2024/25 in conjunction with NIAS to improve patient flow through urgent and emergency care services.  This included specific actions to reduce ambulance delays, improve patient flow through emergency departments and the wider hospital system, and to ensure timely discharge for patients who are medically fit for discharge and no longer require hospital care.  In addition, Trusts have been asked to develop alternative pathways with access to NIAS as an alternative to conveyance to emergency departments. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 17703/22-27 , why theatre run time has decreased in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust hospitals while increasing in other Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Whilst the overall run time for theatres in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust has decreased from 93% in 2021/22 to 90% in 2023/24, the Trust has met the regional run time target of 90% consistently throughout this period. There are a wide range of reasons for the variation in run times, both within the Southern Trust and across all Trusts. Inefficiencies and delays within elective theatre processes, such as late starts and early finishes, impact on the ability to maximise theatre utilisation. Factors which contribute to these include:patients not attending for their procedure and not giving adequate notice to allow for a suitable alternative patient to be given that slotpatients having to be cancelled on the day if they are unfit for surgeryother hospital cancellations necessary, e.g. due to staff shortages, bed pressures, etc.Southern Trust has also advised that in recent years it is operating on less low-risk, routine patients. With a greater focus on the more complex patients, this leaves less room for ‘filler’ cases in the in-between, therefore resulting in reduced utilisation. The Trust accepts that it is less than ideal to see these decreases in the run time given the high demand for theatre access across all sites. Theatre utilisation as a whole is a priority focus for the Trust and there are multiple ongoing quality improvement projects aligned to minimising these issues.  These issues are continually monitored and are frequent points of discussion within the Trust and across regional and indeed national advisory groups. The Southern Trust is continuing in its efforts to make more elective sessions available and therefore anticipate that the run time will reflect these increases in a positive correlation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how much assistance he is providing towards general practice indemnity costs this year; and (ii) how this position compares with other parts of the UK.
answer_text: As part of the 2024/25 General Medical Services contract agreed with the Northern Ireland General Practitioners Committee in May this year, £38.9m funding was released for re-purposing as a result of the incorporation of the Quality and Outcomes Framework and specified Enhanced Services into the core contract, and with the inclusion of funding for clinical waste also moving into the core.  Of this, £5m was allocated to GP practices on a per capita basis towards indemnity costs as an interim measure pending identification of the long-term model for indemnity.A package separate to the winter pressures package was agreed with NIGPC in December 2024. As part of the package an additional £1.5million to provide additional support for indemnity costs associated with increased activity through the winter was provided.It is important to recognise that clinical negligence indemnity for GPs varies across the UK.  Local arrangements for clinical negligence indemnity differ from the state-backed schemes’ provision in England and Wales.  In Scotland, as like Northern Ireland, GPs secure in-hours clinical negligence indemnity cover via subscription to a Medical Defence Organisation.  The rates quoted by each of the Medical Defence Organisations reflect the position in each of the jurisdictions.

Hospital AttendedTotal AttendancesTotal Handovers Over 15minsTotal Handovers over 60minsAverage Handover Time (Minutes)Average Handover Time (Hours)Mater368331119711hr 12 minsRoyal Group1,7891,6879121001hr 42 minsAntrim Area1,3451,2926591412hrs 18minsCauseway4924753141562 hrs 36minsUlster1,1161,0526241803hrsCraigavon Area 1,1201,0305041312hrs 12 minsDaisy hill486461152621 hr 2 minsAltnagelvin1,1711,123474681 hr 6 minsSouth West623587205601 hrType 1 ED Total8,5108,0383,9631081 hr 48 minsTable 2Table 3, shows the longest and average hospital handover times, per acute site for the period 1st December 2024 to 13th January 2025.  Table 3HospitalAverage Handover TimeLongest Handover TimeBelfast City00:31:3704:05:43Mater01:21:5016:09:28RBSC00:22:0801:17:34Royal Group01:54:5414:48:38Antrim Area02:17:1325:15:05Causeway02:47:5418:59:39Downe00:20:3500:42:53Lagan Valley00:29:4803:18:46Ulster03:14:1623:36:16Craigavon Area02:11:0318:42:00Daisy hill01:03:0106:01:11Altnagelvin01:14:0512:41:07South West00:58:0309:34:00Reducing ambulance handover delays outside emergency departments has been a key priority due to the impact this has on NIAS’s capacity to respond to calls in the community.  These risks were also articulated in the ‘Getting It Right First Time’ (GIRFT) Review of Emergency Medicine for Northern Ireland commissioned by the Department.Ambulance handover delays are monitored on a daily basis, including average delays and longest delays at each hospital.  This information is circulated widely within the HSC system including to the Trust Chief Executives.  NIAS has implemented a range of initiatives to improve ambulance response times. The introduction of an Integrated Clinical Hub has placed senior clinicians within NIAS’s control centre who are able to provide clinical triage to improve safety and refer patients to alternative pathways through the use of ‘Hear & Treat’ and ‘See & Treat’ pathways, effectively reducing unnecessary hospital conveyances. Additionally, the introduction of mental health practitioners within the control room has resulted in a reduction in ED attendances for patients presenting with mental health crises.  Collaboration with HSC Trusts, including the Hospital at Home initiative, have further supported community-based care by minimising hospital admissions and ensuring high-quality care delivery closer to patients' homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he has taken to reduce the number of patients that are considered medically fit to leave hospital but are having their discharge delayed.
answer_text: My Department has established a Social Care Collaborative Forum to provide vision, along with strategic advice and guidance on social care and to take forward agreed actions to support improvement and transformation.  Workstreams have been established to deliver short, medium and longer-term actions.  Care Home Contract A series of workshops to consider various facets of the Regional Care Home Contract were held during September to December 2024 and this review is now at an advanced stage.  This will contribute towards addressing hospital delays associated with the cohort of individuals considered to have enhanced and complex needs. Regional Home Care ContractI have committed to introducing the “Real Living Wage” for social care within this calendar year which will go some way to assist with challenges in increasing capacity in home care services.  However, it is recognised that as with other sectors of Health and Social Care, different rates of pay will be required to create incentives and capacity in what otherwise may be challenging areas to service and staff.  A revised regional contract is key to ensuring equity and consistency. 		My Department will work collaboratively with independent sector providers to develop a business structure that allows for the demographics of the population, including rural home care packages, those requiring assistance of one carer and those with a weekly total of less than 10 hours.  Work on the regional home care contract is due to commence in 2025/2026.Early Review TeamA pilot model (the Early Review Team) was developed in October 2023. This model is to ensure early and timely review of new and increased home care packages for individuals aged over 65 years, within 2-8 weeks of the support being implemented.  The home care hours saved can then be recycled back into the system for those awaiting care either at home, in a care home or in hospital.  Between October 2023 and September 2024, a total of 2884 hours per week were recycled.  This equates to approximately 50 – 300 new home care packages, therefore supporting those in hospital experiencing a delayed discharge.  There will be a focus in 2025/2026 to roll out this service across all programmes of care in trusts.  Care Line LiveCare Line Live is a digital system which houses schedules for trust-led home care. It provides oversight of the capacity on schedules, therefore optimising the number of hours that can be delivered within existing staffing levels.  The system can identify areas that are at capacity and where staff recruitment needs to be focused.  Three trusts have now implemented this system, at varying levels.  By month 9 in 2024/2025, one trust had recycled 4138 hours of home care provision.  This equates to 100 - 400 new individuals being able to avail of a home care package that otherwise may have been on a waiting list.  A second trust commenced roll out of this service in January 2024, with full roll out expected by April 2025.  A third trust

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an estimate of how current flu levels compare with the last five years.
answer_text: Seasonal influenza occurs every year, and there is variation year-to-year on which type of influenza circulates, when the season starts and how many cases there are. The COVID-19 pandemic and the behaviour change associated with it interrupted influenza circulation in March 2020 with the first “normal” influenza season not being seen again until autumn 2022. This year, the season behaved most like that seen in the winter of 2022-23, starting early and reaching a peak around Christmas week. The predominant strain of influenza circulating in 2022-23, and also what is being seen this season to date is influenza A (H1). The number of people hospitalised during this season so far is higher than in 2022-23. The 2023-24 influenza season started later and did not reach such high levels at this stage in the season. Episodes of influenzaFrom week commencing 30th September 2024 (week 40) to week ending 12th January 2025 (week 2), there were 5,130 unique episodes of influenza identified in Northern Ireland. The predominant strain circulating to date is influenza A (H1).In the same period in 2023 (week 40) to week 2 in 2024 there were 695 unique episodes of influenza identified in Northern Ireland. The predominant strain circulating to date was influenza A (H3).In the same period in 2022/2023, there were 1,607 unique episodes of influenza identified in Northern Ireland. The predominant strain circulating to date was influenza A (H1).

To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the steps he has taken to increase awareness amongst vulnerable groups of online scams.
answer_text: Question 15 – AQO 1391/22-27Trading Standards officials raise awareness of scams through media appearances, press releases and social media alerts. They deliver educational talks to local community groups, while working with banks, charities and law enforcement agencies to identify victims of fraud. Officers visited hundreds of scam victims last year, helping many take cases to the Financial Ombudsman Service, resulting in over £1million being returned to victims. Trading Standards continues to educate victims on how to spot scams and protect themselves from financial losses.The Consumer Council supports consumers and vulnerable groups through outreach programmes ensuring they are better equipped to identify and avoid scams. It works with partners to promote awareness of scams and agree referral mechanisms to ensure vulnerable consumers are supported.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will place a copy of any risk assessment carried out by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust prior to accepting handover of the new Maternity Hospital from the contractor in the Assembly library.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust undertake many risk assessments on a project of the scale and complexity of the new Maternity Hospital.  There are risk assessments associated with the design, construction, commissioning, operation and management of the building, its engineering services and the clinical services being delivered.  These risk assessments are often carried out in conjunction with other organisations including the consultant design team, the main building contractor, etc and can contain names and qualifications of operatives working on specialist systems.  These risk assessments would need to be reviewed for commercial sensitivity and data protection issues before being released.  Taking the above factors into account it is not practicable for these risk assessments carried out by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust prior to accepting handover of the new Maternity Hospital from the contractor to be placed in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the individuals the chairman of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust wrote to to relay the thanks of the Trust's board for bringing the Maternity Hospital project to the stage of accepting handover from the contractor.
answer_text: No record has been found of the Chairman of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust writing to individuals to relay the thanks of the Trust’s Board for bringing the Maternity Hospital project to the stage of accepting handover from the contractor

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust had considered sufficient information by March 2024 to take a well-informed decision to accept handover of the new Maternity Hospital.
answer_text: The contractual responsibility for accepting the new Maternity building as having met all the standards and requirements of the construction contract lay with the private sector multi-disciplinary Consultant Design Team.  At the time of handover the Trust’s Senior Responsible Officer for the project recommended acceptance of the new building on behalf of the Trust on the basis of assurances received from the Consultant Design Team and DoH Health Estates.   These contractual relationships and the associated chain of assurance are common to all health projects, and to many other major capital projects across the Northern Ireland public sector.   It should be noted that while the contract documentation requires an individual signature for the certificate of completion, there is no single individual with the skills and experience to assess every element of a new building of the scale and complexity of the new Maternity Hospital. The Project Manager for the Consultant Design Team relies upon assurances given from a variety of subject matter experts within the multi-disciplinary team.  This team includes architects, engineers, surveyors, fire consultants, and others.   In advance of handover, considerable liaison also took place between the Consultant Design Team and subject matter experts within the Trust and the Department of Health for additional advice and challenge.   This involved liaising with Trust representatives from the Capital Redevelopment team, the Trust’s Estates teams, DoH Health Estates and the Trust’s Water Safety & Usage Group. Based on the above it is my assessment that the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust had considered sufficient information by March 2024 to take a well-informed decision to accept handover of the new Maternity Hospital.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the effectiveness of the process of arriving at contracts for major capital projects in his Department, given that the new Maternity Hospital was declared unsafe within weeks of meeting contractual requirements.
answer_text: DoH Health Estates is the formally accredited Department of Health Centre of Procurement Expertise (CoPE) for construction works and design services.The Health Estates Directorate Governance Framework for Engagement with Health and Social Care Trusts and ALBs can be accessed online at  https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/health-estates-statement-governance.  Section 2.3 of this document states that as CoPE, Health Estates, are responsible for the development, promotion and implementation of procurement methodologies for use by the ALB, including the delivery of sustainable procurement.  In addition the CoPE is responsible for the development and maintenance of health specific construction procurement policy and best practice guidance for application by the ALBs, including the Health Estates Procurement Manual. I can confirm that Health Estates as CoPE fulfil these responsibilities on an ongoing basis and this is evidenced by the number of successful major capital projects successfully delivered across the health and social care sector within Northern Ireland over a sustained period.  Recent examples include the delivery of the Ulster Hospital Inpatient Ward Block, the Ulster Hospital Acute Services Block and the NIFRS Learning and Development Centre.Your question refers to the new Maternity Hospital.  At the time of writing the cause of the issues being experienced has not been definitively established so it is not reasonable to suggest that the process of arriving at contracts for major capital projects is a contributory factor.  Taking all of the above into account I am satisfied that the process determined by Health Estates in arriving at contracts for major capital projects has been and will continue to be effective.  Like all processes it will evolve and be refined over time by Health Estates in their role as CoPE to ensure that it continues to be fit for purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of the tests for Pseudomonas carried out by the contractor prior to handover of the new Maternity Hospital to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust had been witnessed.
answer_text: Records held by the Design Team of testing by the contractor for pseudomonas prior to handover include 148 individual sample sets broken down as follows:   129 Sample sets taken by the contractor and test results provided by the laboratory.  The majority of these were witnessed by the Design Team. 18 Sample sets taken by the contractor and test results provided by the laboratory.  Approximately half of these were witnessed by the Design Team. 1 final Sample set taken by the contractor at the end of February 2024 as part of the handover methodology. Fully witnessed by Design Team. This sample set required two outlets to be remediated following positive pseudomonas results.  The retesting of these remediated outlets was witnessed by Design Team.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the longest period that a child has been awaiting percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery.
answer_text: As of the 18th December there were 66 children on the waiting list for PEG insertions.  This has fallen from 92 in April.  This is a fluid list and children will be added to and come off the list regularly.  My officials remain in contact with Belfast Health and Social Services Trust (BHSCT) officers to get updates on the number of patients waiting for surgery.The longest wait is 150 weeks.There are three Paediatric Surgeons and two Interventional Radiologists who are trained to carry out this surgery.  This is the current complement.BHSCT are unable to outline the number of staff who have carried out these procedures in the past five years.
