This is the current complement.BHSCT are unable to outline the number of staff who have carried out these procedures in the past five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of items that were on the snag list for the new Maternity Hospital.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the testing that has taken place of the fire alarm system in the new Maternity Hospital, including the results of these tests.
answer_text: The Fire Alarm system was tested during scheduled system demonstrations in November 2022, June 2023, October 2023 and a demonstration of Cause and Effect took place in early 2024. These were witnessed by the Design Team.  Any issues identified in early phases of testing were rectified and inspected during the later phases of testing.The certificate of acceptance for the fire detection and fire alarm system was provided to the Trust by the Design Team in March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the effectiveness of the type of nurse call system installed in the new Maternity Hospital.
answer_text: I have been advised by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust and Health Estates colleagues that the nurse call system installed in the new Maternity Hospital was deemed to have met the contractual requirements for handover.Given the above, caveated by the fact that the hospital is not currently occupied by staff or patients, I have no reason to doubt the effectiveness of the nurse call system installed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the effectiveness of (i) the heating system; and (ii) temperature regulation in the new Maternity Hospital.
answer_text: I have been advised by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust that there are defects within the heating system of the new Maternity Hospital.  These defects are currently being addressed under the normal contractual procedures.I am assured that once the defects are remedied the heating system will be effective and the temperature regulation issue in the new Maternity Hospital will be resolved/addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the proposed design of the new Children’s Hospital will be compliant with the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The New Children’s Hospital design was commenced during 2014 with the construction works tender being issued in 2021 prior to the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 coming into force.   The proposed New Children’s Hospital sits within the Royal Victoria Hospital site and as such is served from the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s existing network of heat, power, transport and waste management.  The energy supply and distribution for the Royal Victoria Hospital site will need to transition to net zero to meet the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 targets.  The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust are reviewing how systems will need to be upgraded in coming years to meet the net zero requirements of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.  This work will need to be coordinated with the Energy Strategy for NI to ensure that there is a robust and resilient energy supply infrastructure that is net zero. To minimise energy consumption and help mitigate emissions the New Children’s Hospital has achieved design-stage BREEAM certification of ‘very good’ and there is a photovoltaic system in the design.The New Children’s Hospital design enables the project to contribute to overall compliance with the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 upon completion of the transition of the existing energy supply and distribution systems for the Royal Victoria Hospital site to net zero.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the meetings his Permanent Secretary has had with senior management in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust when the new Maternity Hospital or Children’s Hospital projects were discussed.
answer_text: The Department of Health Permanent Secretary held regular stocktake meetings with the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust senior management in relation to progress on the new regional Maternity Hospital and Children’s Hospital on the following dates:16 May 202231 October 202224 January 202318 April 202320 July 202326 October 202318 January 202415 March 202423 April 202422 July 20245 August 202423 September 20242 December 202416 December 2024These meetings were to discuss progress on the projects and to allow Belfast Trust to raise any issues.  They were not decision meetings and were in addition to the normal governance arrangements for a project of this nature.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of medical staff in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust that are (i) fully trained to perform; and (ii) fully trained and up to date to perform, paediatric percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery.
answer_text: As of the 18th December there were 66 children on the waiting list for PEG insertions.  This has fallen from 92 in April.  This is a fluid list and children will be added to and come off the list regularly.  My officials remain in contact with Belfast Health and Social Services Trust (BHSCT) officers to get updates on the number of patients waiting for surgery.The longest wait is 150 weeks.There are three Paediatric Surgeons and two Interventional Radiologists who are trained to carry out this surgery.  This is the current complement.BHSCT are unable to outline the number of staff who have carried out these procedures in the past five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of individuals that have performed paediatric percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust in each of the last five years.
answer_text: As of the 18th December there were 66 children on the waiting list for PEG insertions.  This has fallen from 92 in April.  This is a fluid list and children will be added to and come off the list regularly.  My officials remain in contact with Belfast Health and Social Services Trust (BHSCT) officers to get updates on the number of patients waiting for surgery.The longest wait is 150 weeks.There are three Paediatric Surgeons and two Interventional Radiologists who are trained to carry out this surgery.  This is the current complement.BHSCT are unable to outline the number of staff who have carried out these procedures in the past five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the process by which the same independent consultants who had advised on the handover of the new Maternity Hospital to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust were commissioned to carry out a review examining this; and (ii) any subsequent remedial work that is required.
answer_text: In advance of handover of the new Maternity Hospital, Belfast Health and Social Care Trust instructed BSO Legal Services (DLS) in relation to concerns on the project.  As part of that instruction DLS appointed independent water consultancy experts to advise on issues surrounding water safety.  The independent consultant did not provide direct input into the design or construction of the hospital. When the post-handover sampling on the building showed the increased levels of pseudomonas aeruginosa (PSA) within the system, the Trust commissioned an independent report through DLS from the same consultant on the water issues within the building.  At the time of commissioning the independent report, the Trust’s legal advisors confirmed that they were satisfied that the work previously undertaken by the consultant did not materially impact on their independence under the Terms of Reference for the report. Regarding the remedial work that may be required on the project following pseudomonas contamination, Belfast Health and Social Care Trust are not yet in a position to confirm the extent of remedial works which may be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the date on which the defects liability period for the new Maternity Hospital in Belfast expires.
answer_text: The defects liability period for the new Maternity Hospital in Belfast expires on 29th March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail all courier costs incurred by (i) his Department; and (ii) each of its arm's-length bodies in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Courier costs for (i) the Department of Health and (ii) each of its arm's-length bodies, in each of the last three years, are set out in the table below. Department of Health2021/22£’0002022/23£’0002023/24£’0000.55.70.3Arms-Length Bodies2021/22£’0002022/23£’0002023/24£’000Belfast Health and Social Care Trust399.6584.9522.5Northern Ireland Ambulance Service-0.30.1NI Practice & Education Council For Nursing & Midwifery---Northern Ireland Medical & Dental Training Agency --0.8Business Services Organisation (BSO)481.0448.2572.4The Children’s Court Guardian Agency -0.2-Patient & Client Council0.1--Public Health Agency8.410.413.8The Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority0.20.2-Northern Ireland Blood Transfusion Service14.127.15.3Northern Ireland Social Care Council---Northern Ireland Fire & Rescue Service (NIFRS)1.81.20.9TOTAL905.21,072.31,115.8Table totals may not add exactly due to roundingsUnfortunately courier costs for Southern, Northern, Western, and South East Health and Social Care Trusts can only be provided at disproportionate cost and these figures therefore do not include any costs in relation to these organisations.Couriers were utilised for various reasons. Belfast Health and Social Care Trust utilised courier services for the transportation of blood specimens, pharmacy items, documents and equipment. The Business Services Organisation utilised couriers for the transportation of medical records, the delivery and return of testing kits under the Bowel Screening Programme and the delivery of lateral flow kits to Pharmacies.The Northern Ireland Blood Transfusion Service’s main use of couriers was to transfer blood and samples to specialist labs in Manchester and Bristol and also to transport specialist temperature probes to mainland UK for calibration and maintenance.Additionally, the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service utilised couriers for the transportation of PPE and equipment to specialist contractors, (primarily in Great Britain).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of (i) the effectiveness; and (ii) the medium to long-term viability, of the boiler plant at the Royal Group of Hospitals site.
answer_text: I have been advised by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust of the following: -The existing main boiler house at the Royal Group of Hospitals site contains five boilers which are serviced and maintained via a maintenance contract.   Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s Estates team maintain the fabric of the building and all mechanical and electrical services to continue to ensure that the boilers are effective. The medium to long term viability of the boiler plant is currently under review.  The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust are developing proposals to ensure the resilience of the plant for the wider Royal Group of Hospitals site.  Further works are also planned to support the new Children’s Hospital.  Considering the above I am satisfied that the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust are best placed to monitor the effectiveness and the medium/long term viability of the boiler plant at the Royal Group of Hospitals Site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the total costs incurred from the proposed energy centre project in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) the reasons for the project being abandoned.
answer_text: Total spend to date (10th December 2024) on the Energy Centre is £9,514,155.00.The preferred tenderer price for the energy scheme, received in April 2023, was four times the approved OBC cost and more than double the pre-tender estimate.  The pre tender estimate was provided by the consultant design team to reflect the extant conditions in the construction and supply landscape.  The construction period determined by the tenderer as part of their submission for tender was 260 weeks, almost two times longer than the estimate provided at OBC stage. A Gateway Review carried out for the Energy Centre in January 2024 received a delivery confidence rating of red.  The review panel recommended that the project should not proceed to the next phase until several issues are managed to an acceptable level of risk and the viability of the project/programme has been re-confirmed.  The Trust accepted the findings and recommendations of the draft Gateway Review and acknowledged the shortcomings in terms of assurances around strategic need, options appraisal and procurement. Following the concerns raised by the Gateway Team the Trust sought to develop an interim energy solution which would ensure that the Trust were able to continue to deliver sufficient and reliable electrical and heating supplies to existing facilities on the Royal Hospitals site.  This interim solution aims to provide an energy solution for the site for circa 10 years, whilst also accommodating the additional requirements of the New Children’s Hospital.  The project SRO, with the support of the Trust’s Executive Team and Trust Board, agreed to proceed with an interim solution developed in conjunction with the Trust Estates team, on the basis that this will ensure sufficient energy capacity for the RVH site for circa 10 years, including the supply of energy to the New Children’s Hospital.  Furthermore, the interim plan allows the Trust to introduce a significant pause to the energy centre project which would result in a number of benefits for the organisation as set out below: The renewable technology market is going through a period of rapid change and a pause period would allow these technologies to be embedded before the Trust commits to a major project.  DoH are currently producing their response to the ‘Path to Net Zero’.  A pause in the project would allow us to ensure that we deliver a project which is better aligned with future Departmental policy.  Should the market not change significantly in the short to medium term, an alternative procurement approach could be adopted which might deliver better value for money.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of occasions that any Health and Social Care Trust has been placed in special measures in each of the last five years, including the basis for placing each in special measures.
answer_text: Historically, the Department of Health has not used a specific definition of what degree of intervention or oversight constitutes “special measures”. However, proportionate action is taken by each relevant business area within the Department when the performance of one of the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts has been of concern, including in respect of corporate governance, financial management, clinical standards and operational delivery. The Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) has the power under Article 39 of The Health and Personal Social Services (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 to issue Improvement Notices on a particular HSC Trust where failings in minimum standards related to the provision of a service have been found.  RQIA will report to the Department of Health under Article 35(5) of the 2003 Order and may further recommend that the Department takes “special measures” in relation to the body or service provider in question. The only “special measures” intervention in place in an HSC Trust in the period 2019/20-2023/24 on this basis was in respect of the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service in relation to hygiene, cleanliness and infection prevention and control, early in the period. A new Health and Social Care Support and Intervention Framework was issued in October 2024.  The new Framework sets out the Department’s approach for gaining assurance from HSC organisations in relation to the delivery of ministerial priorities and other key deliverables and the approach to support and intervention where there are matters of concern that need to be addressed.  It promotes a ‘no surprises’ approach and supports early identification of emerging issues and concerns, so that they can be addressed before they have a material impact or performance deteriorates further. The framework includes five levels of escalation that provide a model for support and intervention from Level 1 (Area of Concern Identified) to Level 5 (Intervention at HSC Board level).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any special measures the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust was placed under by his Department in relation to the new regional Maternity Hospital.
answer_text: Whilst there is no framework of special measures associated with major capital projects, my Department has taken a number of steps in relation to the Belfast Trust. Senior Officials within my Department have been holding quarterly meetings in relation to the Maternity and Children’s Hospitals – an Executive Flagship Project, with the Chief Executive and the Senior Responsible Officer within the Trust.My Department recommended the appointment of a dedicated Senior Responsible Officer for these projects and this recommendation has been implemented with the appointment of a Director of Strategic Development within the Trust since 1st November 2024.Finally, senior departmental officials are now holding a weekly meeting with the BHSCT Director of Strategic Development focusing solely on the new Maternity Hospital to ensure that all necessary actions are being taken by the Trust to enable the hospital to open as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 16683/22-27, and given the Water Safety and Usage Group had no role in the contractual handover of the building, why a senior official from his Department advised the Committee for Health on 26 September 2024, in response to queries over the expertise of those signing off the handover, that the Trust have their own Water Safety Group with microbiologists and, as part of that, there are a number of people involved in that process that would be qualified.
answer_text: The response given by the Departmental Senior Official to the Health Committee on 26th September 2024 and the response to AQW 16683/22-27 answered on 14th November 2024 do not conflict.  The Trust’s Water Safety & Usage Group do not have a contractual role in the handover of the building.  The Trust’s Water Safety & Usage Group did however, given the presence of pseudomonas prior to handover, contribute to the process of determining the water system acceptance criteria which had to be met by the Contractor prior to the Trust accepting handover.Contractual responsibility for accepting the new Maternity building as having met all the standards and requirements of the construction contract, including the water system acceptance criteria, lay with the private sector multi-disciplinary Consultant Design Team.The Project Manager for the Consultant Design Team, in accepting the new Maternity building, relied upon assurances given from a variety of subject matter experts within the multi-disciplinary team.  This team includes architects, engineers, surveyors, fire consultants, and others.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any independent consultants commissioned to advise on remedial works required at the new Maternity Hospital following pseudomonas contamination.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust have commissioned an independent report on the water systems within the new Maternity Hospital through BSO Legal Services (DLS).  This review is being led by Hydrop.  Hydrop are also supported in the review by the UK Health Security Agency at Porton Down and Andersen Caledonia, an independent UKAS accredited laboratory.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any regular discussions that have taken place between the Permanent Secretary in his Department and the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust senior management in relation to the new regional Maternity Hospital project.
answer_text: The Department of Health Permanent Secretary held regular stocktake meetings with the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust senior management in relation to progress on the new regional Maternity Hospital and Children’s Hospital on the following dates:16 May 202231 October 202224 January 202318 April 202320 July 202326 October 202318 January 202415 March 202423 April 202422 July 20245 August 202423 September 20242 December 202416 December 2024These meetings were to discuss progress on the projects and to allow Belfast Trust to raise any issues.  They were not decision meetings and were in addition to the normal governance arrangements for a project of this nature.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 16742/22-27, whether it would be considered normal practice for those who advised on the handover from the contractor to be involved in (i) conducting an independent review examining the handover; and (ii) advising on remedial works required.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust Trust engaged legal advice at the time of the appointment of the independent expert to undertake the review of the water systems on the new Maternity Hospital.   The extent of advice provided by the independent expert prior to the handover of the building was known and declared at that time.  The Trust’s legal representatives advised that the advice provided by the independent expert prior to handover had no material impact on the terms of reference of the independent report.The independent expert has successfully commissioned water systems in several healthcare facilities across the United Kingdom and is therefore well placed to advise on any remedial works required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the interim report into the water systems at the new Maternity Hospital, which was co-led by independent consultants, as referenced in the Assembly on 26 November 2024.
answer_text: Having received a copy of this interim report on 26 November 2024 I asked Belfast Health and Social Care Trust officials and Departmental officials located within Health Estates for their assessment of same.Based on input from these officials my assessment is as follows:The interim report based on independent sampling shows that the number of outlets in which pseudomonas aeruginosa (PsA) or pseudomonas was detected has reduced by approximately 50%.Based on the sample results and a number of observations from site visits to and inspections of the new building, the interim report made several recommendations that the Trust are progressing in conjunction with Hydrop, with the expectation being that the implementation of these recommendations will reduce the level of pseudomonas in the system further.Whilst I can’t be absolutely definitive given the interim nature of the report both sets of officials are of the view that the worst-case scenario of full replacement of pipework is looking much less likely.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust first became aware of difficulties with the medical gases infrastructure in the new maternity hospital.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust first became aware of the issue within the Medical Gas systems in October 2023.  At the time of handover in March 2024, the issue had not yet been resolved, however the Trust received assurances from the Design Team and the Contractor that the issue would be resolved and it was included in the list of actions for remediation as part of the handover. On Friday 22 November 2024 the Trust was notified by DoH Health Estates of a report from the Design Team which outlined several options to remedy the medical gas issue.  This report noted for the first time the potential that replacement of a section of pipework may be required to rectify the issues.

To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the new build for Banbridge Library.

Musgrave Park hospital activity is also not included.Table 1: Run time for elective theatre activity by hospital and TrustTrustHospital2021-222022-232023-24*% run time% run time% run timeBelfastBelfast City98%99%99%Royal Victoria99%99%96%Children's88%86%85%Mater60%80%83%School of Dentistry54%49%59%Belfast Trust 93%94%93%NorthernAntrim98%95%101%Causeway90%90%93%Mid Ulster84%81%87%Whiteabbey78%74%81%Northern Trust91%89%94%South Eastern*Ulster85%86%88%*Downe77%80%78%*Lagan Valley72%76%80%*South Eastern Trust81%83%85%*SouthernCraigavon96%93%94%Daisy Hill93%91%86%South Tyrone88%90%84%Southern Trust93%92%90%WesternAltnagelvin83%85%90%Omagh68%69%71%SWAH86%93%85%Western Trust79%82%85%* Please note that data for the South Eastern Trust is only available from April to October 2023 due to their move to the new Encompass systemRun time is measured from when the anaesthetist starts dealing with the first patient (or surgical process begins if no anaesthetic is required) until the OP exit time from the theatre, i.e. the time at which the last patient is ready to move from the operating / procedure area. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the percentage reduction in suicide rate that has been achieved over the course of the Protect Life 2 Suicide Prevention Strategy.
answer_text: The 10% reduction target in the Protect Life 2 Strategy is indexed against a baseline, calculated from the 3 year rolling average annual rate of suicide for the period 2015-2017 (16.5 deaths per 100,000 of population). That 10% reduction equated to a target of 14.9 deaths per 100,000 to be measured for the period 2021-23. The most recent 3 year figures relate to the period 2020-22 and show a suicide rate of 11.5 deaths per 100,000.However, this reduction is largely due to NISRA publishing a revised suicide series following the completion of a review of suicide statistics in Northern Ireland. This review has reduced the number of deaths classed as undetermined with these more likely to be found as either intentional self-harm or as accidental. The number of deaths classified as suicide has therefore reduced in recent years. This has made the original target inaccurate.With the NISRA review now complete a more appropriate baseline will be set as part of the new Protect Life 2 Action Plan which is currently in development. The 10% reduction target will then apply to that revised baseline. I would stress that sustained reductions in suicide will only be possible with full implementation and delivery of all Protect Life 2 actions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many operating theatres there are at each hospital in Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail what medical equipment is stored on the ground floor of the new Maternity Hospital.
answer_text: The following medical equipment is currently stored on the ground floor of the new Maternity Hospital:Patient Monitoring equipment Ultra sound scanners x2 Neonatal resuscitaires  Omnicell drug cabinets x2

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the bacteria found in the new Maternity Hospital, for his assessment of the appropriateness of the proposed (i) design of the water system; and (ii) number of water outlets at the new Children’s Hospital.
answer_text: The following guidance documents have been used in the design of the hot and cold domestic water systems for the New Children’s Hospital:-   a)       HTM 04-01: 2016 Safe water in healthcare premises, Part A: Design, installation and commissioning b)       HSE’s (2103) Approved Code of Practice Legionnaires disease: The Control of legionella bacteria in water system (L8) c)       HSE’s Legionnaires Disease: Technical Guidance (HSG 274) d)       Water Supply (Water Fittings) Regulations 1999 e)       Water Regulations Advisory Scheme’s (WRAS) Water Regulations Guide f)       BS EN 806 (multi-part document) – Specifications for installations inside buildings conveying water for human consumption (Parts 2 & 3 include design and pipe sizing) In addition the contents of the following Health Building Notes (HBNs) have been considered in the design of the water systems for the New Children’s Hospital:-  	HBN 00-01 -	General design guidance for healthcare buildings.  		HBN 00-02 - 	Sanitary spaces 	HBN 00-03 -   Clinical & support spaces 	HBN 00-09 -   Infection control in the built environment 	HBN 00-10 -   Part C - Sanitary Assemblies 	HBN 04-01 -   Adult in-patient facilities 	HBN 04-01 -   Supplement 1 - Isolation facilities for infectious patients in     acute settings 	HBN 04-02 -   Critical Care Units 	HBN 07-02 -   Main renal unit 	HBN 12 -        Out-patients department 	HBN 15-01 -   Accident & Emergency Departments Planning and Design Guidance 	HBN 23 -        Hospital accommodation for children and young people 	HBN 26 -        Facilities for surgical procedures The following is the number of water outlets within the New Children’s Hospital:-   •              294 WC’s •              853 Wash hand basins •              127 Sinks •              190 Showers •              23 Slop hoppers The appropriateness or otherwise of the design of the water systems and the number of water outlets at the proposed New Children’s Hospital is reviewed by the Belfast Health & Social Care Trust in conjunction with Health Estates officials and the appointed Design Team. Given the above I am content that the proposed design of the water system and the number of water outlets at the new Children’s Hospital is appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the estimated costs of reparative works, including replacing pipework, at the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre at Belfast City Hospital.
answer_text: The initial estimate of costs for the reparatory works associated with the pipework issues within the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre is £4 million.  This estimate includes the costs of replacing pipework, and the associated refurbishment works.A ‘pilot scheme’ to complete works in two rooms is planned to commence week commencing 11 November 2024.  Once areas are ‘opened up’, it will better inform the actual scope of work required.  This information will be used to develop a more detailed cost estimate and breakdown for the remainder of the building and enable the development of a full programme in conjunction with service management.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how concerns of Assurance Groups within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust are escalated through its governance arrangements to senior management and the Trust Board.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has an Integrated Governance and Assurance Framework which sets out the Trust Board’s arrangements for integrated governance and details the organisational structure and accountability arrangements by which the responsibilities of the Trust Board are fulfilled. The Trust Board committees currently include the Assurance, Audit, Charitable Funds and Remuneration Committees.  A series of groups report to these committees.  Concerns are escalated within this structure by the Chairs of the respective groups and committees, including to the Trust’s Executive Team and Trust Board if considered appropriate. Further details are available on the BHSCT website at:  BHSCT Integrated-Governance–Assurance-Framework-2022-23.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the rationale for health procurements occurring through organisations other than the Central Procurement Directorate.
answer_text: The extant Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy refers to the various Centres of Procurement Expertise (CoPEs) which were established in 2002.These CoPEs contain specialist procurement expertise and they undertake procurement activities which are heavily interlinked with the area of responsibility they support.My Department uses two formally accredited CoPEs.  DoH Health Estates is responsible for procurement of construction works and design services.  BSO Procurement and Logistics Service is responsible for the procurement of Health and Social Care goods and services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the replacement of pipework at the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre at Belfast City Hospital.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust advised that the replacement of the potable water pipework in the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre commenced in November 2024.This work is being completed while the building remains operational and occupied.  The works are taking place on a phased basis with two rooms being refurbished at a time.  This has been agreed with clinical representatives to ensure that clinical services can be maintained.  The programme for completion of these works will be dictated by clinical needs within the facility.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 16227/22-27, (i) how many outlets tested positive for pseudomonas aeruginosa since handover of the new Maternity Hospital in Belfast from the contractor; and (ii) how many positive tests have been detected overall.
answer_text: As per the previous responses, Belfast HSC Trust can confirm that following the steady-state period after the handover of the new Maternity Hospital, 459 (36%) of outlets tested positive for Pseudomonas Aeruginosa.The Trust's independent expert is carrying out a review of the new Maternity Hospital, including outlet sampling.  The review is ongoing,  therefore, the Trust cannot answer the questions submitted at this time as the they do not wish to prejudice the outcome of an independent investigation. The Trust have advised that they will endeavour to get an updated time frame for the review from the Independent expert.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health who served as contract administrator for the handover of the new Belfast Trust Maternity Hospital from the contractor.
answer_text: The Contract Administrator for the New Maternity project is the Lead Consultant, Aecom and Isherwood & Ellis. This party served as the Contract Administrator for the handover of the Maternity Hospital from the contractor.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how the numbers of (i) outpatient appointments; (ii) inpatient admissions; (iii) Emergency Department attendances; and (iv) community care packages have changed in the six years from 2018 to 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of theatre sessions scheduled at each hospital in each quarter of the last three years.
answer_text: Whilst Trust could provide relative information on this request, due to the varying level of detail involved, this information could only be compiled in a suitable format at a disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service has given to the likelihood that the restriction of the maximum annual mileage of the Voluntary Car Service will (i) reduce the service provided; and (ii) require more expensive transport options.
answer_text: The introduction of a mileage cap for volunteer drivers is to ensure patient and driver safety, with the annual limit based on benchmarking with other ambulance services across the UK. The mileage cap has impacted on just 4 volunteers who undertook in excess of 50,000 miles per year. There will, however, be no impact on patients, as the journeys will be covered through other resources within the NIAS Patient Care Service, including other Volunteer Car Service drivers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the transportation of patients by the Voluntary Car Service assists to limit health service expenditure on taxis.
answer_text: Four of the six Health and Social Care Trusts have advised they use a Voluntary Car Service, when available and appropriate to meet patient needs. They advised this volunteer service assists to limit health service expenditure on taxis. Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) and Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) have advised they do not use/ or there is no supply of volunteer drivers in their area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how the known risks relating to pseudomonas aeruginosa were considered by the Belfast Health and Social Trust prior to agreeing handover of the new  Regional Maternity Hospital from the contractor.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has responded as follows: At the time the tender documentation for the Maternity project was being issued in May 2016, the documents demanded that the completed water systems should be safe.  The tender documentation at that time did not specify quantifiable criteria or thresholds regarding levels of bacteria within the water, nor did they specify the percentage of sampled outlets which must be free from bacteria.  While the more recent guidance documents would require this to be considered, this was in line with extant guidance at the time of the Maternity tender. Following the initial discovery by the contractor of pseudomonas aeruginosa (PSA) within the system, the Trust arranged a series of review meetings to consider how the contractual deliverables should be amended in advance of accepting handover in light of the known risks associated with PSA.  Discussions at that time included representatives of the private sector multi-disciplinary Consultant Design Team, Trust Capital Planning & Redevelopment, Trust Estates, the Trust’s Water Safety & Usage Group and the Trust’s independent expert (Hydrop). The discussion regarding safety criteria focussed on the percentage of sampled outlets which must be free from bacteria in order to determine that the water and the systems were safe.   The Trust’s independent expert noted at that time that the 2% “failure rate” for sampled outlets being sought by Trust representatives was a higher standard than they had witnessed in similar build projects in in other NHS Trusts.  They advised that 5% would be considered to be an industry norm.  After extensive review, the threshold of 2% failure rate for water sampling was agreed.  Through their sampling prior to handover, the contractor was able to demonstrate that they had met the criteria. The Trust has never referred to a singular risk assessment being carried out for the building.  Numerous risk assessments were carried out prior to completion covering areas of design, construction, clinical, etc. We are unclear what risk assessment is being referred to in this question.In the latest sampling outlets were tested across all departments within the building. Positive results were not confined to the Neo-natal area.Several Project Manager’s Instructions were issued over the course of the construction works.  We are unclear what risks or Project Manager’s Instructions are being referred to in this question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department raised any concerns with the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust in relation to the handover of the new Maternity Hospital from the contractor.
answer_text: At the date of handover there were no concerns raised by my Department in relation to the handover of the new Maternity Hospital from the contractor. The contractor had achieved the water quality acceptance criteria required under the contract and this was substantiated by laboratory tests of the water samples taken.The detection of Pseudomonas Aeruginosa within the domestic water systems of the new Regional Maternity Hospital was first reported to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust and Health Estates by the contractor in January 2023.Under the terms of the contract, the contractor was responsible for remediating the water systems contamination.Following detection of Pseudomonas Aeruginosa, the following remediations actions were implemented by the contractor prior to handover; Specialist chemical treatment sub- contractors were engaged to analyse and treat the pipework contamination.An additional chemical treatment system was installed on the pipework system.Sections of pipework were replaced.Valves and pipeline components were replaced, installed and chemically cleaned.Pipework circuits were re-balanced.Water flushing, water circulation, sampling and laboratory analysis procedures were employed to address the contamination.The contractors procedures were independently reviewed by the Trust appointed independent water specialist.Reporting in relation to the handover of the new Maternity Hospital included management of the design team, regular Project Board Meetings attended by officials from my Department and direct reporting from the Belfast Trust to my Department.My Department is providing oversight, professional and technical support to ensure that the matter is resolved at the earliest opportunity and that the new Hospital will be safely operational soon as possible.I can also advise that there have been regular discussions between the Department of Health Permanent Secretary and Belfast Health and Social Care Trust senior management in relation to the new regional Maternity Hospital project since May 2022. These meetings were to discuss progress on the project and to allow Belfast Trust to raise any issues.  They were not decision meetings and were in addition to the normal governance arrangements for a project of this nature.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale for the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service introducing a maximum mileage limit for Voluntary Care Service drivers.
answer_text: The rationale for the introduction of a mileage cap for volunteer drivers is to ensure patient and driver safety, with the annual limit based on benchmarking with other ambulance services across the UK.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) who has held the roles of Senior Responsible Officer in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust for (a) the Critical Care Building project; (b) the new Maternity Hospital project; and (c) the new Children’s Hospital project; and (ii) the dates in which these roles were held between 2010 and 2024.
answer_text: (i)	The Director of Planning and Redevelopment held the role of SRO for the Critical Care Building project from 2010 -2011. The Director of Finance held the role from 2011 until project completion in September 2020. (ii)	The Director of Finance has held the role of SRO for the Maternity Hospital and Children’s Hospital projects since inception to 3 November 2024.  The Trust has appointed a Director of Strategic Development who assumed the role of SRO for the Maternity and Children’s projects on 4 November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of nursing leadership in the Public Health Agency over the next ten years.
answer_text: The recommendations in the Nursing & Midwifery Task Group (NMTG) report of 2020 reflected a new vision and ambition to maximise the contribution of nursing and midwifery. Building on that, in 2023, my Department's Chief Nursing Officer (CNO) published her “Shaping Our Future” Vision for the future of nursing and midwifery here for the next five years.  This Vision recognises that leadership is the responsibility of all and seeks to deliver the right evidence-based care, with the right numbers, at the right time, in right place, by the right person with the right knowledge.  I also recently launched a new Collective Leadership Framework for Nursing and Midwifery which is aimed at growing leadership capabilities at every level of the nursing and midwifery workforce. This Framework is designed to empower individuals, providing the guidance and tools necessary to foster collaborative and effective leadership across all settings.I am aware that a reshape and refresh change process is ongoing within the Public Health Agency (PHA), and my Department is liaising with the PHA where appropriate as this proceeds. However the implementation of the CNO Vision, supported by the Collective Leadership Framework, will ensure that nursing leadership across the HSC system, including within the PHA, is well placed to meet service demands, now and into the future, while also ensuring patient safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Improvement Institute, as referenced in Health and Wellbeing 2026 Delivering Together.
answer_text: The improvement institute as referenced in Health and Wellbeing 2026 Delivering Together is the Health and Social Care Quality Improvement (HSCQI), which is a network of Quality Improvement experts and enthusiasts drawn from across many parts of the HSC.   It was established by the Department of Health in 2019 to support transformation within the HSC system and follows on from the recommendations set out in the 2014 Donaldson Report “The Right Time, The Right Place” and in the 2016 Strategy “Health and Wellbeing 2026 - Delivering Together”. HSCQI aims to empower clinicians, professionals, support staff and service users, to use a Quality Improvement approach to improve service design and service delivery. The work of the HSCQI network is currently mandated and overseen by the HSCQI Leadership Alliance which has membership from across the HSC.On the 24th October 2024, the Permanent Secretary of my Department wrote to all HSC Trust Chairs and Chief Executives advising that HSCQI will officially transfer from the Public Health Agency (PHA) to the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) on 1st November 2024.This transfer marks a significant and strategic move to align RQIA’s statutory duties with HSCQI’s vision “To inspire and influence Northern Ireland’s Health and Social Care Community to become a global leader in Quality Improvement and Innovation by working together and focusing on person-centred care”.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration the Southern Health and Social Care Trust has given to increasing car parking charges at its sites.
answer_text: The Department’s policy on Car Parking Provision and Management in the Health and Social Care sector for Northern Ireland was published in 2012 and sets the direction for car park management.  The policy states that it is for each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) to determine the way that the car parking policy, including charging, is applied to each of their sites, to enable them to take into account local issues.The Southern HSCT has recently approved an increase in car parking charging rates to meet the rising costs, due to inflation, associated with the management, maintenance and resourcing of their car parksThey are currently working on a timeline to implement the revised car parking charges in the coming weeks / months. Increased parking charges were identified as part of the Trust’s Delivering Value / Savings Plan Proposals. You will be aware of the position that the wholly inadequate 2024/25 funding allocation, that was regrettably supported by a majority of MLAs,  placed our health and social care system in. Yet even with these often very difficult savings plans, our health service is still facing a funding shortfall.  In line with the Hospital Parking Charges Act 2024, the trust is working at pace to introduce ANPR technology to enable free car parking for all users by May 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how suicide prevention services and support have improved in deprived areas over the course of Protect Life 2 strategy.
answer_text: In recognition of the fact that suicide rates are higher in areas of deprivation, the Protect Life 2 Strategy aims to ensure suicide prevention services and support are delivered appropriately in deprived areas where suicide and self-harm rates are highest.A summary of work in self-harm and suicide prevention that has taken place across Northern Ireland is detailed in the 2023/2024 progress report which can be located at Protect Life 2 A Strategy for Preventing Suicide and Self Harm in Northern Ireland 2019-2024.  This document sets out an overview of the collective departmental activities with many programmes being prioritised in deprived areas.Public Health Agency also fund a small grant programme for community and voluntary organisations to promote positive mental health and emotional wellbeing and tackle the contributing factors of self-harm and suicide through the Take 5 Steps to Wellbeing. The aim of this grant programme is to reduce health inequalities by increasing the delivery of resources, programmes and/or services to the top 20% most disadvantaged neighbourhoods. This programme issues each year and in the 2024 – 2025 financial year there have been 491 grants awarded for an investment of around £1.6 million.The Health Inequalities Annual Report 2024 shows consistent decreases in self-harm presentations in deprived areas over recent years. The Self Harm Intervention Programme has recently been commissioned across all HSC Trust areas to continue to provide support for those who self harm and their families and carers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the progress of his sign language Bill.

To ask the Minister of Health for an estimate of the additional costs for repairs in the Regional Maternity Hospital following the detection of pseudomonas aeruginosa after the handover from the contractor.
answer_text: The costs for remedial works to the new Maternity Hospital following the detection of Pseudomonas Aeruginosa are yet to be established and will be informed by ongoing investigations.The contractor detected Pseudomonas Aeruginosa within the domestic water systems of the new Regional Maternity Hospital in October 2022.  In January 2023 the contractor advised the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust and Health Estates that they continued to investigate the issues and possible solutions.  The tests carried out by the contractor prior to handover of the Regional Maternity Hospital were carried during the contractor’s pre-handover steady state period.Hydrop independent consultants provided advice to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust in relation to the handover of the new Regional Maternity Hospital from the contractor. Hydrop, Andersen Caledonia and Porton Down are the parties involved in conducting the independent review regarding water into the handover of the new Regional Maternity Hospital to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the disciplines that comprise the membership of the Water Safety and Usage Group in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Belfast Health & Social Care Trust has advised as follows:COMMITTEETrust Water Safety & Usage GroupUnder the Approved Code of Practice L8 the Trust has appointed four persons to perform the duty of theResponsible Person namely:	Director Responsible for Water Safety (Director ofFinance, Estates & Capital Development)	Director Responsible for Augmented Care Settings(Director of Nursing & User Experience)	Consultant Microbiologist (Chair of Water Safety &Usage Group)	Responsible Engineer for Water Safety (Head ofEstates Risk and Environment)See appendix 1 for contact details.PURPOSETo ensure as far as reasonably practical that potable and non-potable water is of the highest quality at the point of use.To ensure water is safe for all uses and users.To ensure that the usage of water across all TrustDirectorates complies with all relevant guidance and best practice.MEMBERSHIPChair:	Consultant MicrobiologistMembership:	Director of Finance, Estates & Capital Development	Director of Nursing & User Experience	Head of Estates Risk & Environment	Co Director Head of Estates	Head of Estates Operations	Infection Prevention and Control Doctor	Service Group/Sub-group Representatives – AugmentedCare Sub-group, Renal, Decontamination, Estates, Physiotherapy, PCSS, Pool Water Sub-group	Senior Nurse Infection Prevention and Control	Independent Specialist Advisor on water safety and hygieneIn attendance:	Any Director, Senior Professional or SeniorManager of the Trust will, where appropriate, be invited to attend.Secretary:	Estates DepartmentMember Appointments: The Group can co-opt as necessary other members when the Group is discussing areas of operation that are within their remit of responsibility.DUTIES	To set strategic direction with regard to water quality/safety and usage.	To provide assurance to the OrganisationalGovernance Steering Group that adequate control measures are in place within Service and CorporateGroups to manage water quality/safety and usage inline with legislation, standards and best practice.	To provide guidance, direction and recommendations with regard to all adverse incidents relating to water quality and usage. Participate in any Outbreak ControlGroup as and when required.	To co-ordinate communication with all statutory agencies and external stakeholders as and when required, ensuring good communication with Service and Corporate groups.	To ensure that membership of the group is reflective of the competencies required across all service and corporate groups with regard to water quality and usage.	Membership of the group will work collaboratively to achieve the stated purposes of the group.	To keep up to date with current guidance and applicable learning.	To review annually the Terms of Reference and membership of the group.AUTHORITYThe Water Safety & Usage Group is authorised by theOrganisational Governance Steering Group to review any activity within its terms of reference. In doing so, the Gro

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current pathway for treatment of venous leg ulcers, including how frequently compression or treating the underlying venous insufficiency is pursued.
answer_text: The National Wound Care Strategy programme provide leg ulcer management recommendations for the UK, these were updated in July 2024 https://www.nationalwoundcarestrategy.net/.These recommendations are followed regionally by all HSC Trusts and the pathway is described below:Identification and immediate/necessary care within initial 2 weeks. Apply mild graduated compression.Comprehensive and holistic assessment, diagnosis and initiate treatment-2 weeks (max), this should include Ankle Brachial Pressure Index (ABPI) which is a reading to assess peripheral arterial perfusion on the lower limb/other vascular assessment if appropriate. If the ulcers are venous in origin then the use of compression therapy to reverse venous hypertension, is the mainstay of treatment (Wounds UK, 2019).Ongoing care & management of leg ulceration checking for acute infection, ischaemia, skin cancer etc. Dressing frequency is dependent on individual factors such as exudate/infection/pain.Review of healing at 4 weekly intervals and refer to Tissue Viability Nurses as required. Some generalised healing should be noted after 12 weeks however complete leg ulcer healing is very individualised depending on patients’ health & wellbeing, co-morbidities, motivation and compliance with treatment etc.Care following healing-Advice should be given on how to reduce the risk of re-ulceration. This should be tailored to the individual but should consider skin care, footwear, healthy eating, and exercise (and if appropriate, smoking cessation).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of hours of health professionals' time that is required to manage a venous leg ulcer for a patient in the community.
answer_text: Treatment of leg ulceration should only be decided upon after a holistic individualised assessment of the patient, considering their past medical history and an accurate history of the leg ulceration.  There should be comprehensive recording and planning combined with continuous evaluation using evidence-based care and safe clinical judgement. Immediate and necessary care-Patient assessment, first aid management and early compression 20mmhg following assessment for red flags such as acute infection.  This could involve 30-minute daily visits from a registered nurse initially if ulcer is infected/exudating or painful, reducing in frequency based on clinical evaluation.Within 2 weeks a full Doppler and Ankle Brachial Pressure Index (APBI) should be completed by a registered nurse and compression increased as/if appropriate, this is directed by assessment outcome. This generally takes 60 mins. Ongoing leg ulcer clinical management may involve a visit for 45-60 minutes per leg possibly 2-3 times a week initially and then less frequently based on clinical evaluation of current treatment.Some partial generalised healing is expected to be noted within 12 weeks however most are unlikely to have completely healed in this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the target waiting time for varicose vein treatment; and (ii) how long it will take to reach the target at current activity levels.
answer_text: There are approximately 600 patients awaiting varicose vein treatment. These patients are held on a regional waiting list by the South Eastern Trust and the surgery  carried out in the Day Procedure Centres (DPCs) in  Lagan Valley Hospital and Omagh Hospital.  There are a total of nine vascular consultants who treat varicose veins, eight of which are employed by the Belfast Trust and one in the Western Trust.The waiting time target for inpatient and day case treatments, including varicose veins, is that 55% of patients should wait no longer than 13 weeks for treatment; with no patient waiting longer than 52 weeks. It is not possible to detail how long it will take to reach the target at current activity levels, however reducing waiting times for elective care remains a key priority.   Additional recurrent investment is required to address the current capacity demand gap for vascular services, and the scale at which this can be delivered will be subject to funding and the ability to recruit staff.  Improvement in service provision requires a long-term collective effort requiring sustained and substantial recurrent investment, workforce development and system wide transformation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether an estimate has been made on the percentage of those on waiting lists in Northern Ireland who are working-age adults.
answer_text: Reducing waiting times for elective care remains a key priority.  Within the constraints of existing budgets, I’m committed to taking whatever actions I can to tackle waiting times and ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes. This demands a long-term collective effort requiring sustained and substantial recurrent investment through multi-year budgets, workforce development and system wide transformation.The Department publishes quarterly Official Statistics and Accredited Official Statistics for waiting times on its website at the following location.  The published statistics are not broken down by age: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/topics/doh-statistics-and-research/hospital-waiting-times-statistics

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail Northern Ireland’s share of UK inward investment manufacturing projects which have been announced in each year since 2020.
answer_text: According to FDI Markets between 2020 and 2023, 4% of all new greenfield FDI manufacturing projects in the UK were located in the North.  While figures are subject to fluctuation on a year to year basis, this recent performance is in line with the historical average. Between 2003-2023, NI also had a 4% share of all new greenfield FDI manufacturing projects into the UK.In both 2020 and 2021 NI had 9% of New Greenfield FDI manufacturing projects in the UK. In 2022 and 2023 the NI share was zero.

answer_text: Community Dentists are those directly employed by HSC Trusts to provide dental services to those with special care needs who are unable to attend a general dental practice because of their particular care needs.Information on the whole-time equivalent number of HSC Community Dentists employed in each of the last 10 years is detailed in the table below.HSC Community Dentists Employed (Whole-Time Equivalent) at 30 June, 2015-2024Position at 30th June:Whole-time Equivalent202465.83202366.21202264.78202165.29202069.55201965.19201863.36201763.15201661.05201562.07Source: Information & Analysis Directorate, DoH

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of active dental practices in Northern Ireland; and (ii) how this compares with the figure in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The Business Services Organisation (BSO) holds information on the number of Health Service dental practices, whose dentists have made arrangements with the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) of the Department of Health (previously Health and Social Care Board) to provide health service dentistry in Northern Ireland. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the share of the costs to mitigate the trading arrangements between Great Britain and Northern Ireland that is borne by (i) the Executive; (ii) local councils; and (iii) the private sector.
answer_text: My department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the percentage of dental practices (a) that treat NHS patients; and (b) currently accepting new NHS patients; and (ii) how these figures compare with the respective percentages in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: My Department only holds information on those dental practices whose dentists have made arrangements with the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) to provide Health Service dentistry in Northern Ireland. It does not hold any information in relation to dental practices that are wholly private. Therefore, as we do not have a denominator, it is not possible to provide either of the percentage figures requested.General Dental Practitioners (GDPs) are independent contractors and, under their existing contract, are under no obligation to register new Health Service patients and are able to decide which patients they will treat under Health Service arrangements. Dental practices frequently open and close their patient lists so it is not feasible for the SPPG to hold and maintain a list of dental practices currently accepting new health service patients. I have included a table outlining the number of dental practices who have made arrangements with the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) to provide Health Service dentistry over the past 10 years: Dental Practices(Health Trust)20142015201620172018201920202021202220232024Belfast9190929393929090898788Northern8990909088888787878787South Eastern6970696768686767666666Southern7070706870696967676768Western6161595858595957565655Northern Ireland380381380376377376372368365363364Source: Business Service Organisation, Information & Analysis Directorate, Department of Health, NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how much (i) the manufacturing sector; and (ii) the service sector has grown in each year since 2020.
answer_text: Starting from the pre-Covid baseline of Q4 2019, local Manufacturing output, as measured within the Index of Production, increased by +2.4% to Q4 2020, +1.3% to Q4 2021, and +0.3% to Q4 2022, before falling by -3.9% to Q4 2023. The latest figures indicate that from Q4 2023 to Q2 2024 Manufacturing output declined by -1.2%.  According to Northern Ireland Economic Trade Statistics (NIETS) produced by NISRA, goods exports from the North increased by 8.6% between 2020 and 2021 to £8.8 billion. They increased another 12.5% to £9.9 billion in 2022.HMRC’s Regional Trade Statistics (RTS) report that goods exports from the North increased by 1.3% between 2020-2021, 17.0% for 2021-2022 and by 17.6% for 2022-2023.  In the latest figures for year ending June 2024 goods exports increased by 6.9% to £11.1 billion, compared with the 12 months to June 2023.Starting from the pre-Covid baseline of Q4 2019, local Services output, as measured by the Index of Services, decreased by -4.8% to Q4 2020, before increasing by +9.8% to Q4 2021, +0.8% to Q4 2022 and +2.4% to Q4 2023. The latest figures indicate that from Q4 2023 to Q2 2024, Services output increased by +2.6%. 	According to NIETS, services exports from the North grew by 20.8% from 2020 to £2.9 billion in 2021, and increased by a further 17.6% to £3.4 billion in 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the proportion of the movement of goods into Northern Ireland from Great Britain are deemed at risk and thus subject to full EU compliance requirements.
answer_text: My department has no role in monitoring goods moving from Britain.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the amount of investment from companies based in the United States of America that has been (i) announced; and (ii) committed for Northern Ireland in each year since 2019.
answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with the year ending 31st March 2024 being the most recent available.  The information supplied relates to the period 1st April 2019 to the 31st March 2024.  Total planned investment is the combination of company investment and assistance offered by Invest NI in support of a company’s project.It is important to note that there is a time lag between a business committing to invest in an economic development project and the public announcement of that project; therefore, a business may have committed to a project in one financial year and announced that project in a later financial year.  It is therefore not possible to directly compare the two tables.Table 1 details the amount of Invest NI supported investment in Northern Ireland by US owned businesses that has been publicly announced. It is  important to note that not all investment commitments are announced by Invest NI.Table 1 –Announced Invest NI supported investment by US businesses (2019-20 to 2023-24)YearTotal Planned Investment £m2019-20115.782020-2146.672021-2234.442022-2357.192023-2428.71Total282.79Table 2 details the amount of Invest NI supported investment in Northern Ireland committed by US owned businesses. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the process of handover of the new Regional Maternity Hospital from the contractor to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, including who was involved in the process and the decision-making.
answer_text: The contractual obligations for the new Maternity Hospital were detailed within the Mechanical Engineering Services Specification which formed part of the contract tender documents.The handover process, the persons involved and decision makers for the new Maternity Hospital are set out below:Completion of contracted construction worksThe construction of the water systems by the Contractor, completion of commissioning and testing was managed through to completion by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (Trust)’s Design Team, consisting of Architects and Lead Consultant and Consultant Engineers.Input was provided from the Trust’s independent advisor at Commissioning Stage regarding water quality.Practical Completion CertificationThe Contractual requirements for certifying that the construction works were practically complete in accordance with the Contract were completed by the Design Team.Acceptance of the Completed Building.The Trust accepted the completed building from the Contractor upon the Certification of the Design Team that it was practically complete to the standards of the Contract Documentation and acceptance criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the testing carried out by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust for pseudomonas aeruginosa at the new Regional Maternity Hospital subsequent to handover from the contractor.
answer_text: Following the handover of the new Regional Maternity Hospital building, Belfast Health and Social Care Trust ran the new maternity hospital in steady-state mode for four weeks. On the completion of steady state mode, the Trust tested all the water outlets in the building. This testing was carried out in line with Trust protocols. Trained staff carried out the testing between 29/04/2024 to 06/06/2024. The Public Health Laboratory at Belfast City Hospital reviewed and reported on samples. A significant number of outlets tested positive for pseudomonas aeruginosa.  As part of the independent review into the maternity water systems, the review team is sampling the water system to establish its current health in relation to pseudomonas aeruginosa. The sampling protocol  is in line with  current guidance. The results will form part of the review team's report and inform the Trust's decision-making on the next steps

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how the knowledge that pseudomonas aeruginosa contamination in the domestic water systems had been detected in a significant number of outlets, and that additional works had been carried out by the contractor, contributed to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s approach to the handover of the new Regional Maternity Hospital.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (Trust) was made aware in October 2022 that pseudomonas aeruginosa was detected during the construction stage when the Contractor advised the Trust via the Design Team.  The contractor took remediation measures to address the issue.  The Trust’s approach to handover was to achieve the Contractual requirements including acceptance criteria for water quality.The construction of the water systems by the Contractor, completion of commissioning and testing was managed through to completion by the Trust’s Design Team, consisting of Architects and Lead Consultant and Consultant Engineers.The Trust’s independent expert reviewed the Contractor's sampling methodology to ensure water sampling was carried out in line with best practice.The Trust accepted the completed building from the Contractor upon the certification of the Design Team that it was practically complete to the standards of the contract documentation and acceptance criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any contractual obligations on the contractor in relation to water quality and potential contamination in advance of handover of the new Regional Maternity Hospital to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The contractual obligations for the new Maternity Hospital were detailed within the Mechanical Engineering Services Specification which formed part of the contract tender documents.The relevant extracts of the contract tender documents are copied below:“DESIGN PARAMETERS • BS EN 806-2, BS EN 806-3 and supplementary guidance in BS 8558, CIBSE Public Health Engineering Guide G, CIBSE TM 13, HSE Approved Code of Practice & Guidance L8 2013 (The Control of Legionella Bacteria in Water Systems), HSG274 Legionella technical guidance, the requirements of the Water Supply (Water Fittings) Regulations 1999. • Comply with appropriate WRAS guidance. • On healthcare projects comply with the requirements of HTM 04-01. SYSTEM DESCRIPTION • It should be noted that the HTMs are to be treated as a minimum requirement throughout this specification, other more onerous requirements are noted where relevant but as an absolute minimum the relevant HTM shall be adhered to. The system left by the contracting team must be demonstrably free of infection including but not limited to Legionella. Testing of this point shall be at the clients discretion and all necessary rectifications shall be deemed to be included within the contract.• Water Quality All water systems in the building must be designed/installed in compliance with the measures outlined in the Health and Safety Executive’s “Legionnaires' disease - The control of legionella bacteria in water systems” Approved Code of Practice and Guidance, 2013 and, where relevant, other industry/sector best practice guidance - CIBSE TM13: Minimising the risk of Legionnaires disease 2002. Complimentary to the HSE ACoP, the water systems in healthcare buildings must be designed in compliance with the measures outlined in HTM 04-01 The Control of Legionella, hygiene, “Safe” hot water, cold water and drinking water systems.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the mileage allowance rate for Voluntary Care Service drivers in the health service in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The mileage allowance rates for the Voluntary Car Service (VCS) in each of the last five years can be found in the table below:20202021202220232024£0.38 per mile£0.38 per mile£0.38 per mile£0.45 per mile plus passenger miles at £0.05per patient£0.45 per mile plus passenger miles at £0.05per patientNIAS reimburses reasonable expenses for volunteers within VCS, with any mileage incurred while volunteering for the Trust reimbursed as per current NIAS guidelines. NIAS increased the mileage rates for VCS drivers from 1st July 2023 to £0.45 per mile, with an additional £0.05 per mile available for each passenger/patient carried. The mileage expense rate will be kept under review in line with national best practice across the Ambulance Sector in the UK.To ensure patient and driver safety, NIAS has introduced safe driving limits for VCS drivers through the introduction of a mileage limit per volunteer per annum, implemented in line with current benchmarking from ambulance services across the UK. The limit for 2023/24 was set at 60,000 miles, decreasing to 50,000 miles for 2024/25. While NIAS has lowered the maximum mileage per year, to ease the transition a tolerance of 10% has been applied, which will be removed on 31st March 2025. NIAS will not allocate patients to volunteers who reach 50,000 miles travelled per annum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any impact on drivers from the Voluntary Care Service as a result of the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service's review of non-ambulance transport arrangements.
answer_text: The Voluntary Car Service (VCS) is an important element of the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service’s (NIAS) non-emergency ambulance transport service, known as the Patient Care Service (PCS). NIAS has reviewed the provision of non-emergency ambulance transport and is now engaged in a programme of improvements.VCS is an invaluable element of the PCS resource, offering bespoke, high-quality transport for patients attending outpatient appointments for treatment and care. To expand current VCS resources, NIAS is implementing various recruitment initiatives and improvements focused on VCS volunteering. These efforts aim to enhance and broaden the services on offer. Additionally, NIAS is considering further relevant training packages and has developed a comprehensive VCS handbook.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there is a maximum annual mileage beyond which drivers with the Voluntary Care Service are not recompensed.
answer_text: The mileage allowance rates for the Voluntary Car Service (VCS) in each of the last five years can be found in the table below:20202021202220232024£0.38 per mile£0.38 per mile£0.38 per mile£0.45 per mile plus passenger miles at £0.05per patient£0.45 per mile plus passenger miles at £0.05per patientNIAS reimburses reasonable expenses for volunteers within VCS, with any mileage incurred while volunteering for the Trust reimbursed as per current NIAS guidelines.
