question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the role provided by the Voluntary Care Service in transporting hospital patients.
answer_text: The Volunteer Car Service (VCS) within the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) is a pivotal element of NIAS’s Patient Care Service. It provides valuable, personal and timely transport to patients across Northern Ireland, primarily for renal and oncology services. NIAS is currently conducting a recruitment drive to enlist more volunteers across the region and I would encourage anyone with an interest in this area and some free time on their hands to consider signing up. The impact of the VCS on both patients and volunteers is extremely positive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on her negotiations with the UK Government on the Mid South West and Causeway Coast and Glens Growth Deals.
To ask the Minister of Education how many additional classrooms and units have been provided for children with special educational needs in Upper Bann, in the last two years.
answer_text: In the last two years, the Education Authority (EA) has established 21 additional classes in the Upper Bann area for children with a statement of special educational needs (SEN), creating a total of 163 new places.This includes seven new specialist provision classes in mainstream primary schools, four in post-primary and six early years and foundation classes for pupils with a profile of severe learning difficulties (SLD) and four additional classes established in Donard School.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the testing for pseudomonas aeruginosa and other potential contamination in the water systems that was carried out by the contractor prior to the handover of the new maternity hospital to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Prior to handover of the new Maternity hospital, the Contractor conducted water sampling and microbiological laboratory analysis, to demonstrate compliance with the requirements of the Contract documents, as copied below. Note, ‘HTM’ refers to NHS England’s Health Technical Memorandum:"All water systems in the building must be designed/installed in compliance with the measures outlined in the Health and Safety Executive’s (HSE) “Legionnaires' disease - The control of legionella bacteria in water systems” Approved Code of Practice and Guidance (ACoP), 2013 and, where relevant, other industry/sector best practice guidance - CIBSE TM13: Minimising the risk of Legionnaires disease 2002. Complimentary to the HSE ACoP, the water systems in healthcare buildings must be designed in compliance with the measures outlined in HTM 04-01 The Control of Legionella, hygiene, “Safe” hot water, cold water and drinking water systems.It should be noted that the HTMs are to be treated as a minimum requirement throughout this specification, other more onerous requirements are noted where relevant but as an absolute minimum the relevant HTM shall be adhered to. The system left by the contracting team must be demonstrably free of infection including but not limited to Legionella. Testing of this point shall be at the client's discretion and all necessary rectifications shall be deemed to be included within the contract."Water sampling locations throughout the building and sampling procedures were agreed in advance, with input from the Trust's Water Quality Management Team.The tests completed by the Contractor included Total Viable Counts/TVCs, Total Coliforms, E.coli, Legionella and Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Samples taken were analysed by a United Kingdom Accreditation Service (UKAS) accredited laboratory.The Trust employed an independent expert to review the results of the Contractor’s testing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the area within his Department that is responsible for quality, safety and improvement.
answer_text: My Department’s Quality, Safety & Improvement Directorate (QSID) lead on a range of strategic and policy issues. However, many areas of my Department have elements of quality, safety and improvement embedded within their remit, and for service specific issues in this area, Departmental responsibility resides with the respective area. In addition, the HPSS (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (NI) Order 2003 places a statutory duty of quality on those HSC organisations which are responsible for the delivery of health and social care, such as HSC Trusts, and the PHA. It is my Department’s policy that responsibility for the quality of care sits primarily with the provider, then the commissioner and then the regulators.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to increase access to clinical psychology for general paediatric patients.
answer_text: Currently general paediatric patients are able to access psychological support in Trusts.  This service was further supported regionally for children and young people by Transformation funding since 2019.  This was specifically for children with a range of chronic illnesses, namely asthma, diabetes and epilepsy.  Improving access to clinical psychology is an important priority for the service but it must be noted that there are significant budgetary constraints and exceptional demands for limited funds and resources within the service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of times the Regional Clinical Ethics Forum has met in each year since it was constituted.
answer_text: The first meeting of the HSC Regional Clinical Ethics Forum took place on 8 July 2020.  The Forum met a further five times in 2020, nine times in 2021, 5 times in 2022, four times in 2023 and to date, four times in 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the actions taken by (i) the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) his Department following the February 2024 Northern Ireland Audit Office Follow-Up Report on Major Capital Projects.
answer_text: (i) The Belfast Trust continues to implement the actions contained in DAO (DoF) 05/03 – Revised Guidance on Engagement with the Gateway Assurance Review Process and the Appointment of a Senior Responsible Owner and regularly engages with Commercial Delivery Group in DoF to determine appropriate assurance activity in respect of major capital expenditure decisions. The Belfast Trust is currently in the process of appointing a Director of Strategic Development who will deliver and lead on the BHSCT Flagship projects as well as other major capital development projects in the pipeline. The postholder is expected to take up this role in November 2024. (ii) The DoH P3O (Programme, Project, Portfolio Office) has introduced more stringent controls around business case approvals.  Senior Responsible Owners (SROs) of Major Projects and Programmes are now being asked to provide project progress reports at appropriate intervals to ensure DoH officials are abreast of developments in a timelier manner and can take corrective action as required. The bi-annual reporting to the Departmental Board on high-risk projects and programmes also offers the opportunity to hold SROs accountable for time delays and cost overruns. DoH Health Estates is engaging with the ISNI Committee and other Centres of Procurement Expertise across government Departments to address the recommendations made in the February 2024 Northern Ireland Audit Office Follow-Up Report on Major Capital Projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total number of theatre sessions in each of the last five years, broken down by hospital.
answer_text: Information available from Hospital Statistic publications on www.health-ni.gov.ukTheatre usage by Trust, Hospital and Year from 2019/20Trust, Hospital2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24Belfast4064120820260463339438338Belfast City94764102625969516895Mater487212059327684853Musgrave Park65341582247359287266NI Cancer Centre522452442439437RBHSC34723209361537113803Royal Maternity20471728182417101878Royal Victoria137189627108401188713206Northern11850593578111137411968Antrim62943335404256086299Causeway19981719199119511735Mid Ulster191629691321702169Whiteabbey164258586516451765South Eastern173619934179561765117508Downe23812204542027382585Lagan Valley37803017344036324079Ulster11200471390961128110844Southern2192710946153431825319511Craigavon1203151817510951110508Daisy Hill70874223511055416173South Tyrone28091542272332012830Western1882612127161131747818597Altnagelvin12208729896701174512636Omagh Hospital & Primary Care Complex40222947428742344010South West Acute25961882215614991951Grand Total110605597628326998150105922Source: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/hs-theatres-tables-23-24.csvUse of Operating Theatres DataData users should note that ‘Use of Operating Theatres’ statistics are not designated as Accredited Official Statistics.Please note, figures on use of operating theatres were able to be extracted and validated from Encompass, and therefore South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust figures are included in this section. Figures sourced from Encompass are considered to be “official statistics in development.”Use of operating theatres for Northern Ireland Cancer Centre have not been validated, and users should therefore treat these with caution.Information in relation to the use of operating theatres is presented by session type, hospital and HSC Trust. The use of operating theatres relates to the number of cases operated on by National Confidential Enquiry into Patient Outcome and Death (NCEPOD) classification.In 2023/24, there were 105,922 theatre cases across the five HSC Trust hospitals in Northern Ireland; this was an increase of 7.9% (7,772) compared with 98,150 theatre cases in 2022/23.Across all Programmes of Care, the number of cases operated on was highest in Belfast HSC Trust, with 36.2% (38,338) of all theatre usage. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 14166/22-27, to detail any assurance he has received from each Health and Social Care Trust that the annual number of theatre sessions they are providing is being maximised.
answer_text: My Department monitors theatre utilisation each month, across all Trusts and across all surgical specialties. A range of theatre utilisation data is collated and monitored, including the overall theatre run time for the surgical sessions which are scheduled each month.  The ongoing focus to maximise theatre utilisation has resulted in an improved theatre run time position from 87% in 2019/20 to 90% in 2023/24. This is in line with best practice. Theatre performance is also discussed regularly at the SPPG performance meetings with each Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the mild to moderate depression service in primary care; and (ii) to detail the amount of funding his Department has provided for this service in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Bamford Mental Health Review highlighted that Primary Care has a crucial role to play in early identification and treatment of anxiety, depression and phobias as well as a wider role in promoting Mental Health and Well-Being. NICE guidance ‘Depression in Adults: recognition and management’ states that all interventions for depression should be delivered by competent practitioners and that for mild to moderate depression low intensity psychosocial interventions such as individual guided self-help based on the principles of cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) should be considered.My Department endeavours to make Primary Care services more responsive and accessible with a wider range of services in the community. We help practices to provide effective, person-centred services for people with Mental Health needs in line with the Primary Care Strategy.My Department has provided funding for a Local Enhanced Service (LES) for Mild to Moderate Depression for those GP Practices that are contracted to provide this service.  This service enables all patients aged 18 years and above with mild to moderate depression to be offered a regular counselling service where all therapies can be considered including stepped care models.(ii) The following table outlines the funding on the Mild to Moderate Depression Enhanced Service over the last 5 years:YearFunding2019/20 £1,821k2020/21 £1,979k 2021/22£2,058k2022/23 £1,801k2023/24 £1,721k

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 10384/22-27, for his assessment of (i) the Hidden Hero video posted by the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust on 9 April 2024; and (ii) whether this a resource priority given the pressures on front line care.
answer_text: The Hidden Heroes campaign was devised and rolled out by the South Eastern Trust in November 2021 to celebrate colleagues who carry out vital roles not always viewed as being ‘on the front line’ and indeed therefore more hidden from public view.  Appreciating staff at all levels is an important HSC value and the Trust has been recognised as a silver accredited Investors in People organisation for efforts in this regard.  The videos and other materials for the Hidden Heroes campaign are produced in-house, meaning no additional costs are incurred for the Trust. Trust staff regularly nominate fellow colleagues including porters, electricians, domestic and catering staff, occupational health staff, the postal team, those working in laboratories, and chaplains. The Hidden Heroes campaign celebrates staff efforts and serves to inform the public about the work that goes on throughout the Trust by teams who deserve to be applauded for the important public service roles they play. Two and half years after the campaign began, the Communications Team was nominated and featured for its work to inform, educate and empower patients and service users about changes to services and developments in the Trust.  The Communications Team plays a vital role in raising awareness of important health campaigns and vaccination programmes including being pivotal to the work of the Trust’s Mass Vaccination Centre based at the SSE Arena during 2021.  In more recent times, the Communication Team has led the creation of educational and impactful videos explaining how to use the new My Care patient portal. The Department very much values the contribution made by the award-winning SE Trust Communications Team.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Review of the Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care, including (i) a timeline for the launch of an initial public consultation in relation to ratios and health declarations; (ii) progress on the establishment of a review reference group and task and finish group; and (iii) his plans for a full public consultation on a revised set of Minimum Standards.
answer_text: The first stage of the review of the Minimum Standards, a public consultation on adult-child ratios and the requirement for all prospective childcare workers to provide a health declaration countersigned by their GP, was launched on Monday 24 March 2025.Following the close of the consultation on 20 June 2025, all responses will be collated for review by the Department, and a consultation report will be produced.  The Review Reference Group will also be established following the close of the consultation to consider the wider Minimum Standards in detail and assist with the drafting of a redesigned set of Standards for consultation early in 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients waiting long-term who were transferred to another Health and Social Care Trust (i) for an appointment; (ii) for admission; and (iii) to get a procedure more quickly, in each of the last five years, broken down by speciality.
answer_text: There is no standardised way on HSC Trusts' ICT systems to capture the information requested and it is not routinely collected by the Trusts.Some Trusts have been able to identify transfers from information recorded as free-text or other manual records. This free-text does not form part of any regional data standards and therefore is not applied uniformly across Trusts.The Trust responses are as follows;Belfast HSC TrustThe number of patients on the waiting list on 30 June in each of the last five years, i.e. 2020 to 2024, who had been waiting for longer than 52 weeks within an acute specialty, who were transferred to Belfast Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust for an Outpatient appointment, is provided in Table 1 which follows.Table 1: Number of Outpatients Transferred to Belfast HSC Trust, Waiting Over 52 Weeks, on 30 June each Year from 2020 to 2023SpecialtyQuarter EndJun-20Jun-21Jun-22Jun-23Dermatology0 260 0 Endocrinology0 10 0 Gastroenterology1 00 106General Medicine 010 0 General Surgery 0280 Neurology340 0 0 Respiratory Medicine 07132Rheumatology622 00 Urology 0 010 Source: Belfast HSC Trust Patient Administration SystemBelfast HSC Trust is unable to provide information for June 2024, due to the rollout of the new encompass electronic patient record system there on 6 June 2024, and its ongoing work to quality assure and validate the information in the new system.The Trust has advised that it is unable to answer parts (ii) and (iii) of the question as the information is not recorded and it would not be possible to identify if a person was transferred out to get a procedure done more quickly.Northern HSC TrustNorthern HSC Trust has advised that it has no specific coding to definitively identify patients transferred from other HSC Trusts in answer to this question. On 1 July 2024, there were 7 people identified on the Inpatients Waiting List who had transferred in who had been waiting longer than 52 weeks. No Outpatients were identified who had transferred and waited longer than 52 weeks across the requested period.South Eastern HSC TrustThe South Eastern HSC Trust has advised that it is not possible to provide detail regarding the number of patients who have been transferred to this Trust from another Trust who had been waiting longer than 52 weeks, as its waiting lists are not designed to be interrogated in this way. A manual validation of all waiting lists would be required instead, which is not feasible.Southern HSC TrustThe Southern HSC Trust provided a nil return.Western HSC TrustThe Western HSC Trust provided a nil return and confirmed that it has not placed any patient from any HSC Trust on an Inpatient, Day Case or Outpatient waiting list for (i) an appointment, (ii) admission or (iii) to get a procedure more quickly in any of the last five years as at June of each of the years 2020 to 2024, outside of any regional centres or regionally agreed pathways.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what discussions he has had in relation to replacing the Apprenticeship Levy.
To ask the Minister of Health how his Department reached the estimates provided to the Executive of (i) £807,000 start-up costs; and (ii) £821,000 annual costs to implement the Adult Protection Bill.
answer_text: The start up and annual costs to implement the Adult Protection Bill were first provided to the Executive in September 2021.  The figures at that stage had been estimated from advice received from the relevant service areas within my Department, the Health and Social Care Trusts and the Department of Justice on financial implications of implementing the provisions proposed for the draft Adult Protection Bill.  Since then significant work has been undertaken by my Department to better understand the full financial impact of the Bill.  Figures were refined with updated costs from the relevant parties above as well as statistical information provided by Scottish Government counterparts. The Health Committee were provided with an update on progress of the Adult Protection Bill on 16 May 2024 and this included an overview of the revised costs. Current estimates have indicated that the implementation of the draft Bill would cost approximately £11.9 million in the first year and £113.9 million over 10 years.  The breakdown of these costs is provided in the table below:Adult Protection Bill Estimated Costs of ImplementationYear 1Over 10 yearsStaffing Costs (gradual rise anticipated in the following years due to incremental recruitment)£7.3m£86.3mPowers of Entry and associated protection orders, the provision of medical assessments and the provision of Independent Advocates£338k£3.9mThe creation and implementation of an Adult Protection Board for Northern Ireland (APBNI) as well as funding for the Serious Case Reviews which the APBNI will be responsible for£522k £6.1mThe cost of offences going through the courts processes.  Offences will range from failure to comply with a Banning Order or Temporary Banning Order; obstructing a practitioner from carrying out their duties; and offences relating to ill-treatment and wilful neglect – care worker and care provider offences.£66k£664kAdditional Trust Accommodation and IT changes.£727k£1.5mTraining costs will be provided to Adult Protection Social Workers, as well as general training throughout the wider Health and Social Care Trusts and other organisations which the draft Adult Protection Bill will have impact on such as NI Ambulance Service, NI Probation Board, PSNI and NI Courts and Tribunals Service.  £2.45m£10.3mRQIA staffing costs for carrying out the role of inspection in terms of the use of CCTV.£444k£5.2mTotal Cost£11.847m (rounded to 11.9 million£113.9 millionIt should be noted that these costs are still subject to further clarification as the Business Case has not yet been approved.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how the tariff of charges for the recovery of health service charges is determined.
answer_text: The NHS Injury Cost Recovery (ICR) scheme was launched in 1999 based on the fundamental principle that those responsible for causing injury to others should meet the cost of NHS treatment. NHS costs are recovered only where personal injury compensation is paid - for example, after a road traffic accident. Funds recovered come primarily from a third-party compensator or insurer. The ICR scheme is based on a simple average cost tariff system to allow the efficient and cost-effective recovery of monies while also keeping running costs down in order to maximise the benefits for NHS services. My Department does not determine the Cost Recovery Tariffs but rather obtains the tariff uplifts from colleagues in Department of Health and Social Care in Great Britain. The tariff uplifts are then updated within a N.I. Statutory Rule to apply these revised cost recovery tariffs in Northern Ireland. The tariff and ceiling on charges payable by compensators for the recovery of NHS charges under the NHS injury costs recovery (ICR) scheme increased on 1 October 2024. This uplift in the level of charges reflects Hospital and Community Health Services inflation and applies only to injuries sustained on or after that date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the total volume of untreated sewage discharged into Belfast Lough.
To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the budget allocated to part-time PSNI Reserve Officers, for each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any specific measures being taken to avoid staff leaving the health service for posts in the Irish Republic.
answer_text: With high demand for skilled healthcare workers globally, the movement of staff across jurisdictions has been a long-standing feature of the health and social care workforce.My Department published the Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for our People in May 2018.  This outlines a programme of work aimed at making the HSC a fulfilling and rewarding place to work and train where our people feel valued and supported.  A summary report on progress towards implementation of the Strategy’s actions (to the end of March 2024) is published on the Department’s website: HSC Workforce Strategy 2026 Summary Report on Progress to 31 March 2024.pdf (health-ni.gov.uk)The introduction of the Sláintecare programme in the Republic of Ireland has brought renewed focus to the potential attractiveness of employment in the Republic of Ireland for HSC staff.My Department has commissioned a study into the overall benefits of HSC employment, including those over and above gross salary, compared to similar employment in the Republic of Ireland.  The findings of this study, which will initially consider contracts for medical consultants, will more fully inform understanding of the terms and conditions and overall benefits of employment in the respective jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the actions being taken to ensure that the administration of electroconvulsive therapy is compliant with the ethical principle of informed consent.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Clinical Guidelines are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here.  Where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. NG222 - Depression in adults: treatment and management was published by NICE in June 2022. Section 1.13 of the guidance includes recommendations on the administration of electroconvulsive therapy including informed consent. NG222 was endorsed by my Department in October 2022 for implementation in Northern Ireland and Trusts have provided assurance to the Department that the relevant recommendations have been implemented within mental health services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the measures being implemented to maximise the number of staff benefiting from funded training in Northern Ireland who go on to work and establish their careers here.
answer_text: Over 95% of newly graduated nurses, and nearly all midwives, opt to commence their careers in HSC and can subsequently access post registration education and training to support their ongoing professional development and career advancement.As a way of supporting the retention of resident doctors, my Department in collaboration with HSC Trusts, is taking forward a range of measures to enhance the quality of medical education. Key measures are HSC employers mandating protected educational development time; a commitment from HSC Trusts to “fair and timely” rota design; and HSC Trusts working to improve the provision of rest and on-call facilities for resident doctors.I have also asked my officials for advice on issues associated with the development of policy options on a Return to Service Obligation, and to bring forward substantive advice for my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 10383/22-27, to detail the full communication-related spend, including staffing costs, for each arm's-length body.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he and his Department remain committed to fully implementing safe staffing levels.
answer_text: To clarify, a commitment was made in 2020 to consider a case for safe nurse staffing legislation as part of the Framework Agreement that led to the suspension of industrial action by Health & Social Care trade unions from late 2019. Since then, the scope of the proposed legislation was broadened to include all professional disciplines within HSC. Expanding the scope has broadened the focus from “safe nurse staffing” to “safe and effective staffing”.I remain fully committed to the introduction of safe and effective staffing legislation within the current Assembly mandate.  On 22 July 2024, I launched a 12-week public consultation on the Introduction of Safe and Effective Staffing legislation, which closes on 14 October. The consultation sets out a number of legislative proposals relating to - Guiding Principles of Safe and Effective Staffing; Workforce Planning; Common Staffing Methods and Staffing Calculation Tools; and Reporting and Monitoring Arrangements.Please note, the consultation is not specifically about implementing safe staffing levels, instead safe and effective staffing is about giving organisations enough support and evidence to allow them to make balanced staffing decisions and have adequate numbers of appropriately skilled staff in the right place, at the right time, to provide an effective service. Following consultation closure, the responses will be analysed and considered before I publish a Government Response, setting out my decisions on the way forward. I would optimistically hope, contingent on Executive agreement, that drafting of the Bill will begin within the first quarter of 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department's resurfacing programme for the 2024-25 financial year in the Upper Bann constituency.
answer_text: The 2023/24 resurfacing programme for the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council area was presented to Council members in October 2023 and can be found at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-council-report-armagh-banbridge-craigavon-2023.pdf .The resurfacing programme along with other programmes for 2024/25 is currently being prepared and will be presented to Council in October 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assurance he can provide in relation to the ongoing prescribing of gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogues in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to children for gender incongruence or gender dysphoria.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust provide regional Gender Identity services and have provided assurance that treatments provided by the Trust to children are in accordance with the requirements set out in a letter issued to all Trusts on 27 August 2024 by the Chief Medical Officer (DOH Ref: HSS(MD) 30/2024), which advised that it will become a criminal offence for a doctor, pharmacist, or any other individual in Northern Ireland to sell or supply these drugs to patients under the age of 18.There are a number of exemptions to the prohibition, such as where the child or young person is prescribed these medicines on an NHS prescription (for example, from the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s Knowing Our Identity Service).  All NHS prescriptions and directions are outside of this ban.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the number of children continuing to be prescribed gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogues for gender incongruence or gender dysphoria on a private prescription.
answer_text: My officials have advised that we don’t have any access to private prescription data.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of children that are currently prescribed gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogues for gender incongruence or gender dysphoria in the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Less than 5 patients under the age of 18 currently receive prescribed gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogues for gender incongruence or gender dysphoria via the Knowing Our Identity (KOI) service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how mental capacity legislation in Northern Ireland fulfils Article 5 obligations of the European Convention on Human Rights.
answer_text: The Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (MCA) received Royal Assent in May 2016 and has been implemented in a phased approach. Phase one was introduced between October 2019 and December 2019, with the most notable provisions commenced being those relating to the Deprivation of Liberty (DoL) Safeguards, a new statutory process for deprivation of liberty. Provisions relating to research, money and valuables were also introduced in Phase one.  Article 5 of the European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR) provides that "Everyone has the right to liberty and security of person. No one shall be deprived of his liberty [unless he or she is of an unsound mind] and in accordance with a procedure prescribed by law."The DoL Safeguards provide that legal framework, as required by Article 5(1) of the ECHR. The system of safeguards, which must be in place, requires:- that there is a reasonable belief of lack of capacity; - that it is in the person's best interests;- a formal assessment of capacity to be carried out;- a consultation with the person's nominated person to be undertaken;- the 'prevention of serious harm' (POSH) condition to be met; and- an authorisation.The inclusion of the POSH condition is to ensure that the person who lacks capacity relating to their care which meets the criteria for a DoL, is not deprived of his or her liberty arbitrarily and that the detention is proportionate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of any safety risks associated with gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogues prescribed to children for gender incongruence or gender dysphoria.
answer_text: Gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogues are generally a safe drug but primary effects are noted on bone health and fertility as detailed in the Guidelines on Gender-Dysphoric/Gender-Incongruent Persons.The primary risks of pubertal suppression in Gender Dysphoric/Gender-Incongruent adolescents may include adverse effects on bone mineralisation (which can theoretically be reversed with sex hormone treatment), compromised fertility if the person subsequently is treated with sex hormones, and unknown effects on brain development.  Little data is available on the effect of Gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogues on Bone Mineral Density in Gender Dysphoric/Gender Incongruent adolescents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how mental capacity legislation and practice in Northern Ireland differs from other parts of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: The Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (MCA) received Royal Assent in May 2016 and provides a statutory framework for people who lack capacity to make a decision for themselves and for those who now have capacity but wish to make preparations for a time in the future when they lack capacity.To date, it has been implemented in a phased approach in Northern Ireland. Phase one saw the introduction of the Deprivation of Liberty (DoL) Safeguards and provisions relating to research, money and valuables, between October and December 2019. Implementation of the remainder of the MCA will include the commencement of provisions relating to care, treatment, personal welfare, Lasting Powers of Attorney, Independent Mental Capacity Advocates and will see the repeal of the Mental Health (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 for persons aged 16 and over. This will deliver on a recommendation from the 2007 Bamford Review of Mental Health and Learning Disability, fusing mental health and mental capacity legislation into one law and creating parity of esteem for mental health. There are no indicative timings at this stage for full MCA implementation.Much of the MCA in Northern Ireland will be aligned with the provisions within The Mental Capacity Act 2005, which applies in England and Wales and provides the legal framework for acting and making decisions on behalf of individuals who lack the mental capacity to make particular decisions for themselves. In relation to the commenced MCA provisions in Northern Ireland, there are some known differences between the two frameworks relating to the DoL Safeguards. The Department of Health and Social Care has legislated to introduce a new system for people who lack capacity to consent to arrangements which amount to a deprivation of their liberty, by way of new Liberty Protection Safeguards, which will replace the existing DoL Safeguards in England and Wales. These new safeguards, when brought into operation, will aim to put the rights and wishes of those people at the centre of all decision-making and it is believed that that the new system will be more in line with the existing MCA DoL Safeguards in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will work with the Minister for the Economy and the Minister of Health to ensure that young people with a statement of special educational needs have a legal right to education up to the age of 25, similar to other parts of the United Kingdom.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to address (i) increasing breast cancer waiting times; and (ii) the breast cancer waiting time inequalities between the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: I have commissioned an urgent review to consider how a regional breast cancer service can deliver timely and appropriate care for patients. A regional waiting list for breast assessment will be established in the coming months to ensure that women across the region are able to access the earliest appointment available to them.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what consideration has been given to supporting and promoting the agri-food sector, including the primary producer, in the forthcoming Programme for Government.
To ask the Minister of Health whether the powers of the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority in relation to dentistry are as extensive as those of the Care Quality Commission.
answer_text: The Health and Personal Social Services (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 established the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA), setting out its powers and duties. The Health and Social Care Act 2008 established the Care Quality Commission (CQC), setting out its powers and duties.The legislative framework in each of the UK jurisdictions that permits health and social care regulators to register and inspect health care providers, including dentists, is different.  For example, the RQIA will inspect every dental practice that provides private dental care and treatment once every 24-month period, whereas the CQC will inspect 10% of dentists in England each year.Whilst RQIA has established links with the health and social care regulators in each of the home nations, they have not compared and assessed their legislative powers with the other health and social care regulators, in relation to dentistry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether non-domiciled dental companies can offer consultations in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: To offer private dental consultations in Northern Ireland, the individual practitioner providing the service must be registered with the General Dental Council (GDC); and the “establishment” used must be registered with RQIA in accordance with Article 12 of the HPSS (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 and the Independent Healthcare Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005. These Regulations also require the practitioners involved to be registered with the GDC, as a condition of registration of the establishment.My Department is aware that there has been an increase across the UK of dental companies from outside of the UK offering a “consultation” at a venue in Northern Ireland or GB prior to patients travelling abroad for dental treatment. If the “consultation” were to fall into the “practise of dentistry” then this is illegal unless the dental professional is registered with the GDC and the premises are registered with RQIA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how the pay for pharmacists in primary care multi-disciplinary teams is determined; and (ii) how this compares with their colleagues and others in their profession.
answer_text: The General Practice Pharmacists (GPPs) are employed by the GP Federations, who are Community Interest Companies. There are 17 GP Federations and 4 Federation Support Units operating in Northern Ireland. As separate legal entities, GP Federations determine the pay for GPPs. In determining the pay for GPPs, the GP Federations will take into consideration the level of remuneration paid to pharmacists working in the Trusts and local Community Pharmacies. The Strategic Planning & Performance Group (SSPG) does not have access to the payroll records for either local GP Federations or Community Pharmacies. The salary paid to pharmacists working in the Trust is in accordance with Agenda for Change (AFC) pay scales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the development of ADHD diagnostic and treatment pathways.
answer_text: Department of Health officials recognise that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years. My officials are currently considering the commissioning of ADHD services, in discussion with relevant stakeholders. Subject to future budget availability, potential next steps include the commissioning of research/analysis of adult ADHD demand to inform options for future commissioning strategies, as well as a regional workshop to explore potential ways forward. My officials have recently completed an assessment of paediatric ASD and ADHD pathways. This work has identified a number of inconsistencies and gaps across the region. The development of consistent pathways will be taken forward through the Community Children’s Social Care Group (Children’s Disability MH/ASD/ND) jointly chaired by SPPG and PHA.  Pathways will take a broader neurodevelopmental approach, incorporating a regionally consistent Children and Young Peoples Referral Service. This will allow earlier intervention needs to be assessed and define capacity requirements. ASD and ADHD diagnostic pathways and interventions will be addressed through the implementation of the HSC Children’s EHWB Framework. Any decision to commission ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the extent to which the lack of availability of blister packs for an individual's medications has delayed discharges from hospital.
answer_text: Many patients require support to help them manage their medicines.  A “blister pack” is a device that separates out medicines based on the day and time of day that they should be taken and may be useful for some patients.  For many people in receipt of a Package of Care, assistance with medicines taking is a key component. Over time a reliance has built up by a number of domiciliary care providers on medicines being supplied in blister packs.There is no specific contractual requirement for community pharmacy to support this activity and they have been supplied by the goodwill of community pharmacists. Consequently, Trusts have been advised that they cannot assume that community pharmacies will be in a position to provide medicines in blister packs to facilitate administration by domiciliary care staff.  Trusts also have a statutory duty to provide their patients with an adequate supply of medicines on discharge, and in an appropriate form of delivery in line with meeting the patients’ needs.HSC Trusts have procedures in place to mitigate any delayed discharges due to the lack of availability of blister packs. Some Trusts do not record a discharged delays relating to Multi-Compartment Compliance Aids (blister packs).Hospital pharmacists liaise with community pharmacy colleagues under an SPPG process (June 23) to refer these patients onward for assessment of blister pack provision by community pharmacies. A summary of each Trusts position can be seen below.Belfast TrustThe Pharmacy Department is not aware of any specific issues relating to delayed discharges due to lack of availability of blister packs.Patients admitted to hospital using a blister packSome patients are admitted to hospital already using a blister pack for medicines, if these are in place, and assessed to continue to be needed these will continue at discharge. Arrangements are in place to provide new or changes to medicines. Communication with the patient, family/carers and GP/community pharmacy along with consideration of individual patient circumstances are used to determine if existing community blister pack supplies can be continued on discharge or if any changes are required and/or if hospital pharmacy need to supply any further medicines. These factors will be considered as part of ward discharge planning processes to minimise any potential delays.Communication with the patient, family/carers and community pharmacy along with consideration of individual patient circumstances are used to determine if required medicines can be administered in the short term using standard original packs and support structures or if an interim supply of medicines is required in blister pack format from the hospital pharmacy.Patients being discharged having been newly identified as requiring a blister packA number of patients discharging from hospital may have a newly identified need for a blister pack. This is most commonly to facilitate new/changing package of care arrangements in community. Hos

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the targets for reductions in the numbers on waiting lists that were contained in his Department's Elective Care Framework published in 2021.
answer_text: The Elective Care Framework, published in June 2021, set out a five-year plan with firm, time bound proposals for how the backlog of patients waiting would be systematically tackled, and how we will invest in and transform services to allow us to meet the population’s demands in future. The Framework sets two five-year targets, together with an underlying ambition to increase productivity and to improve performance:Subject to a commitment from the NI Executive that the necessary backlog funding will be made available, the Department’s clear aim is that, by March 2026, no-one should wait more than 52 weeks for a first outpatient appointment and inpatient/day case treatment; or, 26 weeks for a diagnostics appointment.Subject to a commitment from the NI Executive that the necessary capacity funding will be made available, the Department’s clear aim is that, by March 2026, we will have eradicated the gap between demand and capacity for elective care.Given the changes that have occurred in the HSC operating environment, and the level of funding that has been available to advance the Framework, a revised Elective Care Framework was subsequently published on 24 May 2024 – this sets the strategic direction for the development and transformation of effective and efficient delivery of elective care services in Northern Ireland over the next five years and details measures needed to address the unacceptable waiting lists here.This is not a new Framework, but an updated plan with specific actions for development and implementation of the changes necessary for delivery of effective sustainable elective care services over the next 5-year period. Long waiting times are unacceptable and addressing this issue is a key priority for me. I am, however, clear that making a real impact on waiting lists requires a twin track approach of reform and development of our elective services to meet demand and immediate investment in additional capacity to reduce current backlogs. Both these require levels of investment which the current health budget does not contain.My Department bid for non-recurrent funding of £135m for this financial year (above the £80m bid just to stand still for red-flag and time critical patients) to address the growing backlog.   In addition, there will be a requirement for recurrent funding of £80.5m per year when fully implemented to deliver the actions contained within the updated Elective Care Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the proportion of time surgeons spend in theatre compared with their counterparts in other jurisdictions.
answer_text: The time a consultant spends in theatre will depend on the consultant’s surgical area, the type of surgery the consultant carries out, the consultant’s job plan and on-call rota commitments, the funded theatre sessions available to the consultant and the ability to cross cover other surgical areas. There are ten recognised surgical specialties and multiple subspecialties, each surgical subspecialty will have a different theatre profile within the consultant’s job plan.Each consultant has a job plan which documents their sessional commitments throughout the week, this is their contract of employment  and is an agreed plan between the organisation and the consultant. Job Plans are reviewed annually.An effective job description is key to delivering the highest standards of surgical care. It benefits the consultant, the employing organisation and ultimately patients. Royal Colleges and Regional Professional Advisors consider and approve job descriptions and personal specifications for new or replacement posts at consultant level.Theatre sessions within job plans are part of consultant’s “direct care programmed activities” and there is some national variation in what would be considered a model contract.England and Northern Ireland have “7.5 programmed activities (PAs) for direct clinical care (DCC) duties and 2.5 PAs for supporting professional activities (SPA).”A similar split is set out in the Scottish consultant terms and conditions.Wales have 7 PAs for DCCs and 3 SPAs.Direct Clinical Care activity within a surgeon’s job plan will include activities such as theatres sessions (inpatients and daycases), outpatient sessions, ward rounds, multi-disciplinary meetings, on call sessions. The split of the proportion of activity within each DCC will have some local and national variation.  Belfast HSC and Northern HSC Trusts have confirmed that roughly 30% of their time is spent in theatres.South Eastern HSC Trust confirmed that on average, around 20% of a surgeon’s time is spent in theatre.Western HSC Trust advised that they could not provide a conclusive response given the various metrics involved in the request.Southern HSC Trust have noted that 20% of surgeons time is spent in operating theatres. However, this can vary depending on specialty, on utilisation and cross-cover within specialties, and in relation to each individual consultant job plan. By way of national comparison, in January 2024, the Royal College of Surgeons of England (RCS England) published its report Advancing Surgical Workforce, which found that 41% of consultants are only undertaking two scheduled operating sessions per week or 2 PAs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the targets for reductions in the numbers on waiting lists that are contained in his Department's Update Elective Care Framework published in May 2024.
answer_text: The Elective Care Framework, published in June 2021, set out a five-year plan with firm, time bound proposals for how the backlog of patients waiting would be systematically tackled, and how we will invest in and transform services to allow us to meet the population’s demands in future. Long waiting times are unacceptable and addressing this issue is a key priority for me. I am, however, clear that making a real impact on waiting lists requires a twin track approach of reform and development of our elective services to meet demand and immediate investment in additional capacity to reduce current backlogs. Both these require levels of investment which the current health budget does not contain.My Department bid for non-recurrent funding of £135m for this financial year (above the £80m bid just to stand still for red-flag and time critical patients) to address the growing backlog.   In addition, there will be a requirement for recurrent funding of £80.5m per year when fully implemented to deliver the actions contained within the updated Elective Care Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the actions he is taking to maximise the amount of time surgeons spend in theatre.
answer_text: The time a consultant spends in theatre will depend on the consultant’s surgical area, the type of surgery the consultant carries out, the consultant’s job plan and on-call rota commitments, the funded theatre sessions available to the consultant and the ability to cross cover other surgical areas. Each consultant has a job plan which documents their sessional commitments throughout the week, this is their contract of employment.  and is an agreed plan between the organisation and the consultant. Job Plans are reviewed annually. An effective job description is key to delivering the highest standards of surgical care. It benefits the consultant, the employing organisation and ultimately patients. Royal Colleges and Regional Professional Advisors consider and approve job descriptions and personal specifications for new or replacement posts at consultant level. Likewise, theatre utilisation is monitored and reviewed accordingly to identify any areas of under or over utilisation which is addressed with the individual.The Department of Health Elective Care Framework (ECF) June 21, update May 24 sets out the key steps on how the Department of Health would invest in and transform services to allow Northern Ireland to meet the needs of the population going forward. The workforce objective, ‘Transformation of the elective care workforce to meet future requirements and to create effective teams with the right skills mix.’ has a number of actions aimed at maximising the time surgeons spend in theatre.  This includes the development of other support roles aimed at ensuring surgeons are able to undertake the more complex activities.  There is an action included and commitment to work in conjunction with relevant professional bodies to develop proposals to transform consultant job plans to maximise time spent in theatres. This also includes recommendation to work with other professionals to enhance skills mix.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the underspend on the new Mother and Children’s Flagship hospital; and (ii) the reasons for this underspend.
answer_text: The Maternity and Children’s flagship project will provide a new Regional Children’s Hospital which will deliver integrated and contemporary paediatric healthcare services and a new Maternity Hospital which will provide modern facilities for enhanced maternity and obstetric services. The project also includes associated site and energy infrastructure.The spend to date on the Maternity and Children’s Flagship project has delivered the completed new Maternity building (Phase B1) and associated enabling works packages at c.£92.0m, the office and logistics building known as ‘Non-Clinical Support’ at c.£15.3m, Bostock House Demolition at c.£6.75m, and a series of demolitions and site clearance enabling contracts, ducts and complex services diversions including c.£8.1m duct, c. £2.2m Site Wide Infrastructure, c.£2.3m Spine Road Duct Crossing, c.£5.5m Energy Centre enabling works, c.£1.5m temporary decants, c.£6.3m West Access Road, on which the overall Maternity and Children's Flagship project is interdependent. The c.£44.05m balance of expenditure to date includes professional design works, equipment costs, statutory fees, management, and tendering processes.Design for the New Children’s Hospital is complete, tenders returned, and the project is due to start on site in early 2025.   The projects within the Maternity and Children’s flagship project are interdependent, with a delay on one enabling project having a consequent impact on subsequent projects. Working on a live, acute hospital site carries a high risk of contaminated land and complex services diversions, the nature of which is not known prior to relocation of function to permit detailed surveys. The breakdown of the annual underspend/ slippages on the Maternity and Children’s Flagship project and reasons for this follows. There are several factors that can affect the cost profile in a major capital programme and result in an underspend against the original forecast expenditure in a financial year. The underspent expenditure across each year was reprofiled into future years to align with more up to date information on delivery timescales.YearSlippage/underspend (£)Reason for slippage/ underspend 2011-121,000,000Business case approved March 2011. Design team appointed late 2012, resulting in slippage in originally projected expenditure in year.2012-130N/A2013-140N/A2014-155,787,030Maternity was delayed when tender prices exceeded the pre-tender estimate. Children’s: Slippage in design fees and non-works costs 2015-161,268,652Delays to enabling works and contaminated land removals required on the site for Maternity.2016-179,115,173Maternity: The Maternity hospital was re-tendered as the first tender result exceeded the pretender estimate. This had an impact on the planned construction start date.Children’s: delays to enabling works due to unforeseen ground conditions, delays on Non-Clinical Support (office and logistics building)  due to complex service diversions and knock on from enablin

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidance (i) NG133; and (ii) NG185 have been implemented in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Clinical Guidelines are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here.  Where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. DoH Circular HSC (SQSD) 13/22 sets out process for endorsement, implementation, monitoring and assurance of Clinical Guidelines in Northern Ireland. As there is no dedicated funding source to support the implementation of NICE Clinical Guidelines in Northern Ireland, any resources required are considered against other competing priorities.There are a significant number of recommendations contained within each guideline and the extent of implementation varies across Trusts. However all Trusts are broadly complaint with the guidelines, notwithstanding a number of highlighted challenges which the SPPG will continue to work with Trusts to address.Specific detail on NG133 and NG185 is as follows:NG133 - Hypertension in pregnancy: diagnosis and managementAll Trusts are reporting that the guidance has not been fully implemented. A number of Trusts have highlighted provision of placental growth factor (PlGF)-based testing as an issue and the Strategic Planning and Performance Group are currently engaged in considering options to enable future provision.NG185 - Acute coronary syndromesAll Trusts are reporting that the guidance has not been fully implemented however NHSCT and WHSCT are reporting 99% compliance. The remaining Trusts have highlighted capacity and recruitment issues regarding nursing, physiotherapy and clinical psychology resource which is impacting on waiting times for patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for breakdown of the annual spend to date on the Mother and Children's Flagship hospital.
answer_text: To date, £184.0m has been spent on the Maternity and Children’s Flagship project. This has delivered the new Maternity building (Phase B1) and associated enabling works packages at c.£92.0m, the office and logistics building known as ‘Non-Clinical Support’ at c.£15.3m, Bostock House Demolition at c.£6.75m, and a series of demolitions and site clearance enabling contracts, ducts and complex services diversions including c.£8.1m duct, c. £2.2m Site Wide Infrastructure, c.£2.3m Spine Road Duct Crossing, c.£5.5m Energy Centre enabling works, c.£1.5m temporary decants, c.£6.3m West Access Road, on which the overall Maternity and Children's Flagship project is interdependent. The c.£44.05m balance of expenditure to date includes professional design works, equipment costs, statutory fees, management and tendering processes.Full Business Case approval for the new Children’s Hospital was received on 5 July 2024.  The design phase is complete, with the main construction phase due to start on site in early 2025 and scheduled to run for a period of 4.5 years until summer 2029. A commissioning phase will follow, with the hospital expected to be operational by Q4 2029/30. A breakdown of the annual spend to date on the Maternity and Children’s Flagship project, including the above enabling projects, is provided in the table below.YearSpend (£)2011-1230,0002012-131,832,0002013-144,381,1002014-154,999,1922015-165,695,5142016-175,955,8282017-1817,086,8452018-1931,258,3662019-2036,045,6952020-2121,607,7092021-2228,429,3292022-2316,088,0852023-2410,527,9072024-2599,336Total to date184,036,906

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients currently considered medically fit to leave hospital who are having their discharge delayed, broken down by (i) hospital; and (ii) the reason for the delay.
answer_text: Discharge delays fall into two main categories, “Simple” and “Complex” and targets are in place against which all Trusts are monitored. Patients who do not need a package of care at home, or transfer to a nursing or residential home (‘Simple’ discharge) should be discharged from hospital within 4 hours of being assessed as ‘medically fit for discharge’.Patients requiring domiciliary, nursing or residential care (‘Complex’ discharge) should leave the hospital within 48 hours of being assessed as ‘medically fit for discharge’.Causes of complex discharge delays include ‘no domiciliary package available’ or ‘awaiting nursing home assessment/acceptance’.  For simple discharges, delays include internal hospital processes, for example pharmacy or discharge letters and also transport by family or ambulance.2023/24 Trust Winter plans included actions to ensure timely hospital discharge for patients who are medically fit.  Trusts increased domiciliary care and community care capacity and expanded discharge lounges.  This has remained a priority for Trust locality plans in 2024/25.SPPG have issued ‘Discharge Lounge’ guidance to Trusts to increase their use, monitored domiciliary care unmet need, established early review teams and engaged with care homes and RQIA to improve discharge processes.Service Delivery Plan targets for 2024/25 include a focus on weekend discharge rates, to ensure that simple and complex discharge rates help to maintain hospital flow.The responses detailed in tables overleaf have been provided by the Belfast, South Eastern, Northern and Southern Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts for the position on 2nd July 2024.Please note that the Western HSC Trust did not respond within the required timeframe.The reasons recorded for delayed discharges from hospitals in four of the five hospital Trusts on 2 July 2024 are categorised as detailed in the tables below.  Where Trusts have returned a number less than 5 (<5) to avoid patient identification, total and percentage calculations have assumed this to be 5.Belfast HSC Trust has reported approximately 213 patients in hospital with a delayed discharge across 4 hospital sites. The Royal Victoria Hospital has 117 patients (55%) and the Mater has 53 (25%) patients with Musgrave reporting 38 (18%) patients and Belfast City reporting less than 5 patients.The primary reason the Trust has reported delays is a Lack of Care home capacity.  72 patients (34%) of all delays are for this reason. The majority of these patents are in the Royal Victoria (45) and the Mater (16) with 11 at Musgrave and less than 5 patients at Belfast City Hospital.  The second highest reason for delays is ‘awaiting an internal assessment process’ which is primarily to assess patients care needs and can take a number of days to complete.  Up to 56 (26%) of patients are waiting for this reason.  29 patients at Royal Victoria, 14 patients at the Mater and 8 patients at Musgrave. Less than 5 patients at Belfast City are awaiting t

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there is a date by which the original planning permission secured for the new Mother and Children’s Flagship hospital will lapse.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s New Children’s Hospital planning approval will not lapse as the approved works have commenced.  The scope of the works included within the planning approval for the New Children’s Hospital project included the demolition of Bostock House.  Planning Service deemed that Bostock House demolitions commenced on 14 December 2020 and therefore the planning approval is not subject to expiry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the timeframe for the Mother and Children's Flagship hospital being fully operational.
answer_text: The Maternity and Children’s Flagship project will provide a new Regional Children’s Hospital which will deliver integrated and contemporary paediatric healthcare services and a new Maternity Hospital which will provide modern facilities for enhanced maternity and obstetric services. The project also includes associated site and energy infrastructure.The Maternity Hospital main building (Phase B1) reached construction completion in March 2024 and was handed over to the Belfast Trust to commence the clinical commissioning phase, with a target operational date of November 2024.  Unfortunately, testing of the water systems showed the presence of pseudomonas contamination which makes it unsafe to transfer clinical services to the new building until remedied. The Belfast Trust is now working to identify potential remediation options: however, this is likely to result in a delay in opening the hospital until it is clinically safe to do so. The remaining phases B2 and B3 of this project (demolition of part of the existing maternity building to accommodate the link bridge between the new maternity and critical care building) to facilitate the relocation of inpatient and outpatient maternity services from the existing maternity building to Floors 7-9 of the Critical Care Building, will be delayed until the new Maternity Hospital has opened, as the new building will provide the space required to decant services to enable these works to take place.The New Children’s Hospital is projected to commence on site at the beginning of 2025 following Full Business Case approval received in July 2024. The building works are programmed for a period of 4.5 years to complete in summer 2029. A commissioning phase will follow with the hospital expected to be operational by Q4 2029/30.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the mild to moderate depression service in primary care; and (ii) how much funding his Department has provided for it in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Local Enhanced Service (NI LES) for Mild to Moderate Depression enables practices to provide a regular counselling service and to provide administrative support for the counselling service for patients (aged 18 years and above) with mild to moderate depression. This is a vital service which ensures that patients receive care from competent practitioners for mild to moderate depression with low intensity psychosocial interventions such as individual guided self-help based on the principles of cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT).With regards to the query around funding provided in each of the last 5 years for the service, the information below details the amount of annual funding allocated to GP practices contracted to provide the Mild to Moderate Depression enhanced service:2023/24 – £1.7m2022/23 – £1.8m2021/22 – £2m2020/21 – £2m2019/20 - £1.8m

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether all necessary planning permissions for the Mother and Children's Flagship project are in place and up to date.
answer_text: The Maternity and Children’s Flagship project has planning permissions in place. The Maternity Hospital main building (Phase B1) is completed in accordance with planning approvals. Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s New Children’s Hospital planning approval is also in place and works have commenced.  The scope of the works included within the planning approval for the New Children’s Hospital project included the demolition of Bostock House which is already completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the percentage of patients with atrial fibrillation and at high risk of stroke that are being treated with direct oral anticoagulants.
answer_text: The number and the percentage of patients diagnosed with atrial fibrillation and at high risk of stroke who are currently being prescribed direct oral anticoagulant medication is not available.Atrial fibrillation is one of the disease registers in the Quality and Outcomes Framework (QOF), the purpose of which is to reward General Medical Service’s contractors for the provision of quality care.  The atrial fibrillation register contains the number of patients with atrial fibrillation.  QOF disease prevalence data is published annually and is available online at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/prevalence-statistics.  The number of people on the Quality and Outcomes Framework Atrial Fibrillation disease register at 31st March 2024 was 47,101, and the prevalence per 1,000 patients was 23.0. The HSC Business Services Organisation (BSO) holds data on prescriptions prescribed in primary care and dispensed by a community pharmacist, dispensing doctor or appliance contractor.  Information is not held on the indication for which drugs have been prescribed; oral anticoagulants can be used for the treatment of a range of conditions.  Table 1 shows the number of patients who were dispensed direct oral anticoagulant medicines in 2023/24.Table 1 The number of patients who were dispensed direct oral anticoagulant medicines, 2023/241, 2, 3, 4YearNumber of patients2023/2461,293Source: Family Practitioner Services Pharmacy Payment System, HSC Business Services Organisation1 All information is for prescriptions prescribed in Primary care and dispensed by a community pharmacist, dispensing doctor or appliance contractor. If a patient does not take a prescription to a dispenser and have it provided to them, the information will not be included in the dataset. Data on secondary care prescribing and private prescriptions are not held by the BSO.2 BSO dispensing data does not capture the indication for which the drug has been prescribed and subsequently dispensed.3 Information has been provided for medications listed under BNF Chapter 2.8.2.4 Patient numbers have been estimated based on unique patients whose dispensed prescriptions have been successfully matched against the central GP patient register in the given time period. There is a chance that some patients may have been missed if all of the prescriptions dispensed to them in the given time period were not successfully scanned.  However, because patients receiving these medications will generally have submitted multiple prescriptions in a year, there is a high probability they will have been picked up at least once. There is a risk of some patients who were newly prescribed these medications towards the end of the year being missed if the few prescriptions dispensed to them were not successfully scanned.5 If statistics are to be used for comparative purposes to other parts of the UK, differences in prescription dispensing and processing in Northern Ireland compared to other UK regions should be noted. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the support provided to adults with special needs for travel to and from day care centres; and (ii) whether such support is dependent on attending the nearest appropriate centre or available regardless of the centre attended.
answer_text: (i)	Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) may provide funded/free of charge transport for adults with a learning disability from the service user’s home address to Adult Buildings Based Day Centres, registered by the Regulation Quality and Improvement Agency (RQIA); where assessment indicates such transport is required to the Day Centre deemed most appropriate to meet the service user’s assessed need. The level and type of support regarding travel provided to adults with a learning disability may differ depending on the individual’s assessed needs. HSCT bus transport generally will have a driver and Day Care/Social Care staff. A guide help may also be provided to support service users on transport if the assessment undertaken indicates the need for same.As HSCTs should manage resources in the most efficient way, transport may only be provided in accordance with assessment, where the adult with a learning disability does not have the ability to safely use other private or public means of transport or cannot be transported by carers or relatives.  Adults with a learning disability who have been assessed as requiring transport but who are unable to avail of HSCT transport for any reason i.e. where the Trust bus is unable to gain access, may also be supported by HSCTs through a contracted taxi with escort/guide support.It should be noted that some families choose to transport individuals themselves and some adults with a learning disability wish to travel independently using local transport services.(ii)	Within HSCTs transport or travel support is generally provided to adults with a learning disability, attending day care/centres that are geographically local to the adult’s home address or deemed most appropriate to meet the adult’s assessed needs within available resources. Should an adult with a learning disability express a personal preference to attend another day centre, they may be required to make their own transport or support arrangements.My Department remains committed to the provision of high-quality buildings-based day care and will continue to work alongside Trusts to maintain and support service provision to include the provision of transport.
