question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the initial target date for completion of the Mother and Children's Flagship project.
answer_text: The Maternity and Children’s Flagship project will provide a new Regional Children’s Hospital which will deliver integrated and contemporary paediatric healthcare services and a new Maternity Hospital which will provide modern facilities for enhanced maternity and obstetric services. The project also includes associated site and energy infrastructure.  The Maternity Outline Business Case (OBC) approved in 2011 was programmed to complete in 2015. Full Business Case (FBC) approval in October 2017 was programmed to complete in late 2020/early 2021. The Maternity Hospital main building (Phase B1) reached construction completion in March 2024. The Trust is considering remediation works required to the water systems in advance of transfer of clinical services. Indications are that this could have a significant impact on the occupation of the main building and delivery of further construction phases. Two future phases will include demolitions and construction of a link bridge. The original OBC for the new Children’s Hospital was approved in Feb 2014 with a planned operational date for the new hospital of 2020. An Addendum to Children’s OBC was approved in 2018 with a planned completion date of March 2023.  The New Children’s Hospital is now projected to commence on site in early 2025 following FBC approval in July 2024. The building works are programmed for a period of 4.5 years to complete in summer 2029. A commissioning phase will follow with the hospital expected to be operational by Q4 2029/30. The projects within the Maternity and Children’s flagship project are interdependent, with a delay on one enabling project having a consequent impact on subsequent projects. Working on a live, acute hospital site carries a high risk of contaminated land and complex services diversions, the nature of which is not known prior to relocation of function to permit detailed surveys. The delay in the planned completion date was as a result of factors including planning approvals, design updates due to changes in technical requirements (Health Building Notes and Health Technical Memoranda), policy and care standards, business case approvals, unforeseen ground conditions, underground services, changes to legislation, third party issues and site wide infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the bids he has made to fund the Road Safety Strategy to 2030.
answer_text: The Road Safety Strategy received Executive approval on the 13 June.  My officials are working alongside those in other Executive Departments, and from emergency services, to ensure that we have a single, agreed, and collaborative approach to road safety.	The initial budget process is ongoing and allocations are being finalised, so it is too early to make bids. Once the outworkings of the budget are finalised officials will then work with road safety partners to agree a forward action plan – covering engineering, enforcement and education.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to outline what support is available to Members in dealing with online abuse, especially on social media platforms.
answer_text: It is unacceptable that anyone is subjected to online abuse, and the Assembly Commission takes very seriously its responsibility for the provision of support to Members in dealing with such behaviour.Social media and online security training were delivered to Members and their staff in 2021 and 2022. In June 2021, a social media session was offered which included two specific elements on how to deal with negative online comments, and minimising risks whilst using social media platforms. In January 2023, the PSNI hosted a one-day seminar and conference at Parliament Buildings on Member personal security and safety, with a particular focus on in-person and online safety of female Members.In addition, the Assembly Commission’s Parliamentary Excellence Programme provides sessions aimed at providing support to Members and their staff. Several sessions have been delivered this year including: Awareness of Personal Security;Building Personal Resilience; andCyber Security. As much of the abuse Members encounter online goes unreported, immediately after summer recess the Clerking and Member Support Office will undertake a survey of all Assembly Members to gauge their experience of online abuse. This will inform a broader programme of activity that the Assembly Commission will take forward to support Members in dealing with the unacceptable scourge of online abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the impact of defective septic tanks on water quality in the Lough Neagh catchment area.
answer_text: NIEA regulate discharges to the water environment through the issue of Consents to Discharge under the Water (NI) Order, 1999. Consents include conditions which prevent the discharge of any pollutant which would cause harm to the aquatic environment, including sewage. There are approximately 9000 domestic discharges within the Lough Neagh catchment, of which a significant proportion are septic tanks.Septic tanks are an efficient and low carbon means of treating small volumes of sewage effluent, particularly in remote locations where connection to the mains sewerage network is not possible.  However, they are not intended to remove nutrients (Nitrogen and Phosphorous) which are the main contributors to development of the blue- green algal blooms which developed in Lough Neagh, and other waterways, last year.  Phosphorous levels in domestic discharges can be reduced by choosing low P detergents, reducing the amount of food waste that is flushed down the drain and ensuring the system is maintained and de-sludged regularly.  NIEA applies a risk-based approach to regulation of discharges to the aquatic environment. For single dwelling discharges and those below 5 cubic metres per day, where a pollution incident has been reported or detected by NIEA which identifies the single dwelling discharge as the source, the Department’s Enforcement Policy is applied.  There were 27 confirmed cases of water pollution related to septic tanks in 2023, within the Lough Neagh catchment.  This represents some 0.3% of the private treatment systems estimated to be within the catchment area. I have allocated additional resources within my Departmental budget to tackle a range of factors impacting water quality in Lough Neagh. For example, I wish to see an awareness campaign developed to give householders information to promote best practice management of septic tanks and analysis of data on domestic waste water treatment system discharges in the Lough Neagh catchment in order to identify the most effective regulatory approach to reduce pollution from septic tanks.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of the amount of funding his Department has provided for promoting the agri-food sector in (i) Northern Ireland; (ii) the UK; and (iii) the rest of the world, in the last five years.
answer_text: My Department, through Invest NI, works with agri-food and drink processing businesses to help them grow their sales, with a focus on growth of sales in markets external to the north of Ireland.Including sponsorship activity, PR, inward buyer visits and international trade activity, for the five financial years from 2019-20 to 2023-24, Invest NI provided £3.4 million of funding towards activities to promote the sector. This can be broken down as:£138,499 in the north of Ireland£1,347,607 in Britain	£327,718 in activities spanning the north and south of Ireland and Britain.£1,561,665 in the rest of the world.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of the amount of funding his Department has provided in supporting the agri-food processing sector in the last five years.
answer_text: The total financial assistance offered by Invest NI to the agri-food and drink processing sector in the five-year period from 2018-19 to 2022-23 was £43.04m. The breakdown of assistance was:2018-2019 - £9.1m2019-2020 - £9.34m2020-2021 – £12.26m2021-2022 – £6.36m2022-2023 – £5.98m.Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with 2022-23 (i.e. the financial year ending 31st March 2023) being the most recent year available.  Information relating to 2023-24 is provisionally planned for release in July 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the timeframe in which the consideration of queries about the safety of patients should be concluded; and (ii) whether this timeframe is contained in a formal policy.
answer_text: Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts have supporting internal procedures and structures in place to identify and address patient safety concerns. This includes arrangements for the management of patient safety concerns raised, for example, under the regional HSC Complaints Procedure and the current Serious Adverse Incidents Procedure.HSC Complaints Procedure Guidance in relation to the HSC Complaints Procedure is available at Microsoft Word - Guidance in relation to the Health and Social Care Complaints Procedure - April 2023 (health-ni.gov.uk). In line with the Guidance, a complaint should be acknowledged in writing within 2 working days of receipt, or 3 working days for Family Practitioner Services, and a full response provided within 20 working days or 10 working days for Family Practitioner Services. If the response timescale is not achievable, the HSC organisation must provide the complainant with an explanation and then keep them updated every 20 working days thereafter regarding progress.   Serious Adverse Incidents Procedure The Procedure for the Reporting and Follow up of Serious Adverse Incidents (SAIs) is available at Procedure-for-the-reporting-and-follow-up-of-SAIs-2016 - DOH/HSCNI Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG)In line with the current procedure, SAI reviews should be conducted at a level appropriate and proportionate to the complexity of the incident under review. The levels of SAI reviews and associated timescales for completion are:Level One (Significant Event Audit): within 8 weeks of notification;Level Two (Root Cause Analysis): sections 2 and 3 of the report (outlining the Terms of Reference for the review and membership of the review team) within 4 weeks of notification, and the full review report and associated action plan within 12 weeks of notification; andLevel Three (Independent Review): to be agreed between the reporting organisation and the Designated Review Officer from the Department’s Strategic Planning & Performance Group or the Public Health Agency.  The Department is currently leading a programme of work to redesign the extant SAI Procedure. There is strong evidence underpinning the need to review the current procedure including that learning reviews can often take too long to complete which can be difficult for those involved including patients, families and staff. A key aim of the review is to design a new process which is streamlined and simplified in order to help conclude reviews in a timelier manner - which is key to support all involved - and to embed learning more quickly in order to improve services and help prevent reoccurrence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when will he bring forward plans for transformation of the health service.
answer_text: Reform of our health and social care service is one of my key priorities. There already is an existing overarching plan for reforming our health service. The ‘Health and Wellbeing 2026: Delivering Together’ report was launched in 2016 as the Department’s 10 year plan in response to the recommendations of the Bengoa Report, Systems not Structures – Changing Health and Social Care. Of course, we need to continue to review and refine this 10 year ‘Delivering Together’ plan. 	Progress has made across a broad range of critical areas, including social care, mental health, acute care, elective care and primary care. 		For example, in primary care we have rolled out the multi-disciplinary team model, which is not only providing a better service for patients but also helps to stabilise GP practices. 		In elective care we have established Day Procedure Centres at Lagan Valley and Omagh Hospitals, and Overnight Stay Centres at the Mater, Daisy Hill and South West Acute Hospitals. This is beginning deliver some welcome results. The latest inpatient and daycase statistics show a reduction for the seventh successive quarter, the longest sustained reduction since at least 2008. These are important examples of reform in action. Reform continues to be progressed through co-production and collaborative working with a wide range of stakeholders. My Department has in the past published a number of ‘Delivering Together’ update reports highlighting the progress that has been made on various reform projects. The Delivering Together 2024 progress report, which summarises all the current health and social care reform strategies and plans, will shortly be available for publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 7126/22-27, whether he plans to raise the review of tax arrangements for conacre-let land with the Minister of Finance.
answer_text: I am aware of representations made by the previous DAERA Minister to the UK Government to introduce a tax incentive to encourage longer term leasing on which the then Minister of Finance was consulted.  I plan to make further representations to the UK Government after the General Election.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of primary schools that provide wraparound care for pupils; and (ii) the annual cost to his Department and Education Authority of this wraparound care.
answer_text: Each individual primary school decides on their offer of before and after school provision including wraparound care services in response to the needs of their pupils and their families. The Education Authority does not retain a central register of before and after school provision (including wraparound services) nor does it hold information on the costs of such provision.The Extended Schools programme provides funding for schools serving disadvantaged areas to offer a range of services which often include before and after school provision.  Schools taking part in the programme in 2022/23, which included 304 primary schools, provided over 2,500 services of which 61% took place after school and 9% before school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on progress with the Peatland Strategy.
answer_text: The draft Peatland Strategy is being finalised and I intend to bring this to the Executive with a view to publication in due course.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the policy in place in each Health and Social Care Trust to ensure confidentiality of employees who raise concerns about patient safety.
answer_text: On the 4th March 2024 my predecessor, Minister Swann, announced the launch of the HSC Raising a Concern in the Public Interest (Whistleblowing) Framework and Model Policy. In addition to the other safeguards set out, the issue of confidentiality is dealt with within the Framework and stipulates that if an organisation is asked not to disclose an individual’s identity, it may not do so without the individual’s consent unless required by law (for example, by the police).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the safeguards in place in each Health and Social Care Trust to ensure that employees are protected from any detrimental actions should they raise patient safety concerns.
answer_text: Such safeguards are set out in the HSC Raising a Concern in the Public Interest (Whistleblowing) Framework and Model Policy, launched by my predecessor on the 4th March 2024. The policy is clear in that, where a member of staff raises a concern in the public interest, then they have legal protection from any detrimental treatment because of having raised that concern. All HSC managers and policy advocates should be trained by their organisation on how to protect staff who raise a concern in the public interest and where staff can get help or refer a concern.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the cost impact of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 on new school buildings.
answer_text: My Department does not have any data on the cost impact of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 on the new school building projects at this stage. Most of these projects are still at a very early stage of development. The costs will be monitored as the projects develop.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the policies and procedures in place in each Health and Social Care Trust to facilitate patient safety concerns.
answer_text: There is significant priority given to ensuring the quality of care provided and to addressing patient safety issues across the whole Health and Social Care (HSC) system. The Health and Personal Social Services (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 imposes a Statutory Duty of Quality on Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts for the quality of care they provide or commission.  This includes the requirement to put and keep in place arrangements for the purpose of monitoring and improving the quality of: (a) the health and personal social care which it provides to individuals;   (b) the environment in which it provides them.   To assist in discharging these duties, HSC Trusts are required to have in place adequate and effective organisational management, clinical governance, quality and patient safety systems, and to be assured that these systems are operating robustly in practice, in order to identify, address and act on patient safety concerns and to drive improvements. Organisational arrangements – and supporting internal policies, procedures and structures – will vary across HSC Trusts. HSC Trusts are also required to have in place arrangements for supporting the effective operation of regional policies set by my Department. This includes processes for the management of patient safety concerns raised, for example, under the HSC Complaints Procedure and the current Serious Adverse Incident (SAI) procedure (the latter is currently under review by my Department).

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what schemes are being made available to assist businesses to meet climate change targets.
To ask the Minister of Education whether he will include the School Enhancement Programme within his capital budget programme.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how GPs will be evaluated in relation to their adherence to patient cardiovascular targets, following the discontinuation of the Quality and Outcomes Framework; and (ii) the mechanisms or frameworks that will be implemented to ensure continued monitoring and assessment of GPs in this aspect of patient care.
answer_text: In 2024/25, the Clinical Care Domain within the Northern Ireland Contract Assurance Framework covers areas previously included in the Quality Outcomes Framework. Practices are required to continue to code appropriately to record that good clinical care is being maintained.  It is expected that care for all conditions will be delivered as clinically appropriate in accordance with national guidance issued by bodies such as National Institute for Clinical Excellence and using regional or local care pathways where available.  SPPG will continue to monitor outcomes through the GP clinical systems.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether state-registered care home operators in Northern Ireland can recover VAT on supplies and other expenditure related to care services.
answer_text: The Care Act 2014 (which sets out in one place, local authorities’ duties in relation to assessing people’s needs and their eligibility for publicly funded care and support in England), does not apply in Northern Ireland..Currently, Independent Sector registered care homes in Northern Ireland are VAT exempt but related expenditure is not recoverable.HSC Trusts are part of a single VAT registration for NI government, including the Department of Health, called the ‘NI Divisional VAT registration’ which covers all of the care services they provide including those of Statutory Care Homes. HSC Trusts are therefore able to reclaim VAT on non-business activities, under the provisions of the Value Added Tax Act 1994.  However, the actual percentage Trusts can reclaim (i.e. their partial exemption) will be determined on an individual Trust basis.  This partial exemption percentage is applied across all Trust services and will not be for a specific service they provide.HMRC’s VAT legislation sets out conditions which permit reclaims to be considered, the matter for my Department to consider is how to allow the management structure to exist contractually without weakening the key relationships between the Trust and the new care home management company, or indeed weaken the Regulation and Inspection elements undertaken by RQIA.  It is of paramount importance that any changes to these contractual and inspection regimes does not dilute the strong relationships between the Trust and the care provider, which have been relied on in the past to support the quality of care being provided to residents.Significant work has been undertaken to consider the nature of the change, potential operational, legal and regulatory implications, and associated resources that may be required to allow the recovery of VAT by independent care homes in Northern Ireland. It is paramount that any changes to the contractual arrangements between Trusts and providers are reflected in the HSC Care Homes contract.  The current HSC Care Homes contract that sets out the terms and conditions under which Trusts commission services from care home providers has been in place for several years and requires substantial revision. Work to deliver the review of the contract is being taken forward by the Commissioning and Contracting Workstream of the Social Care Collaborative Forum. This review will now include consideration of the structure and content of the contractual documents that would allow providers to restructure in a manner which would permit reclaims to be considered by them under VAT legislation.  The Workstream has a completion date of July 2025 for this work however, the issues pertaining to the change to the contract which would allow providers to reclaim VAT has been prioritised to be considered within the coming months

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact the wording of Health and Social Care Trust contracts has on the ability of care home operators being able to recover VAT.
answer_text: The current Regional Residential and Nursing Home Specification and Contract does not contain any reference to care home operators’ ability to recover VAT.		The contract was last issued to Trusts in 2015 and has been rolled forward for the last 8 years without annual review. Since the contract was last issued there have been many legislative, policy and strategy changes which need to be considered as part of any review and updating. A review of the current contract is necessary for the safe and effective use of care home beds and to ensure contract is fit for purpose for the future.				Workstream 8 of the Social Care Collaborative Forum has been established to provide a formal mechanism to review and update the current Regional Residential and Nursing Home Specification and Contract. This Workstream has taken forward significant preparatory work for the review of the care home contract, with input from key stakeholders across all sectors. 				Of note and in relation to this query, there has been focused analysis of the gaps, deficits and issues with the current contract. As part of these discussions Workstream 8 have noted stakeholders’ views in relation to Care Home restructuring. Overall analysis will inform the review process and the formulation of the new Regional Residential and Nursing Home Specification and Contract.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider amending legislation to enable families to claim benefits from the date their child receives a cancer diagnosis.
answer_text: Families with children who receive a cancer diagnosis can claim Disability Living Allowance (DLA) for children.  DLA for children is paid as a contribution towards the extra costs faced by children under age sixteen as a result of a long term health condition or disability. Entitlement is based on the effect that the health condition or disability has on the child’s need for help with care and/or mobility and not on any specific diagnosis. In order to establish that the health condition is long term, the child is expected to have had the care and/or mobility needs for at least three months and be expected to have them for at least another six months.This helps to ensure that resources are best targeted towards those with the greatest needs.It is important to note that the three-month qualifying period does not always start from the date of claim so a child may be paid straightaway where it can be shown that the qualifying period has already been satisfied prior to the date of claim. There are no current plans to make changes to the qualifying period. There are special rules already in place in the benefit where a child sadly receives a terminal diagnosis and has a life expectancy of twelve months or less.  In such circumstances the three-month qualifying period is waived, and access is provided to fast tracked financial support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on progress towards a new contract model for health service dentistry.
answer_text: The need for dental reform has been acknowledged for a number of years, however, the pandemic, followed by the absence of an Executive has impacted progress in this area.Most recently, efforts have been focused on addressing the immediate access issues patients are experiencing and establishing options to support the sustainability of dental practices while reform is progressed. This has resulted in the £9.2 million additional investment for 2024/25, which I announced on 27 March 2024.I am conscious of the developments in the rest of the UK in recent months, with Scotland, England and Wales adopting diverging models of remuneration. Officials are liaising with their counterparts in each region to monitor the impact of these models and assess the suitability of adopting similar approaches in Northern Ireland.  In addition, officials have held a number of engagement events with practitioners from across NI, the results of which will help inform decision making on the future of General Dental Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the locum; and (ii) agency staff costs across the different professional groups in 2023-24, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the importance of dental amalgam in the current practice of dentistry.
answer_text: Dental amalgam has been used for restoring teeth over more than 150 years making it the worlds most used restorative material.Although the use of dental amalgam is decreasing globally for clinical, aesthetic and environmental reasons, its many advantages continue to make it a restorative material of choice in many patients and for specific procedures including the replacement of existing amalgam fillings in complex cavities and in deep cavities where moisture control can be challenging. Current evidence indicates that amalgam fillings are effective, long-lasting and safe, and that resin-based composite restorations on posterior (molar and premolar) teeth are more likely to fail and need replacement due to secondary tooth decay. While I acknowledge that improvements in composite materials are helping to improve their longevity, clinical complexities of placement and the need for explicit moisture control mean that  composite and glass ionomer restorations may be more difficult to place, require more clinical time and are less predictable among patients with a high treatment need and vulnerable patient groups including  those with learning disabilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the oral health of the population in Northern Ireland in comparison with other parts of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: Across all areas of the UK, there have been marked improvements in the oral health of the population. The most recent available data that compared the oral health of adults in Northern Ireland to adults in England and Wales is sourced from the 2009 Adult Dental Health Survey. In 2009, 93% of Northern Ireland adults had at least one remaining tooth in their mouth compared to 94% of adults in England and 90% of adults in Wales. Over a quarter (28%) of adults in Northern Ireland had at least one decayed tooth in 2009 compared to 30% of adults in England and 47% of adults in Wales. Regarding the oral health of children, the National Dental Epidemiology Oral Health Survey of 5-year-old children in Northern Ireland conducted in 2018-19 revealed that 31.6% of 5-year-old children who took part in the survey had experience of tooth decay, with an average number of 1.2 decayed, missing, or filled teeth. By comparison, in Wales in 2015-16 (Picture of Oral Health 2017), 34.2% of 5-year-old children had experience of tooth decay, with an average number of decayed, missing, or filled teeth of 1.2 amongst all sampled children. In Scotland, the 2020 National Dental Inspection Programme reported that 36.5% of Primary 1 children had experience of tooth decay, with an average number of decayed, missing, or filled teeth of 1.0 amongst all sampled children. Finally, in England in 2019 (National Dental Epidemiology Programme), 23.4% of 5-year-old children had experience of tooth decay, with an average number of decayed, missing, or filled teeth of 0.8 amongst all sampled children.Regarding mouth cancers, the incidence rates of oropharyngeal cancer are shown to be rising rapidly in all four UK nations. The State of Mouth Cancer UK report 2022 shows that incidence rates for mouth cancer are higher in England and Wales compared to other parts of the United Kingdom (UK). Tooth decay, gum disease and mouth cancer rates are highest among the most deprived in the UK population.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the cost of carrying out bovine Tuberculosis testing in Upper Bann; and (ii) the amount paid to farmers for the removal of animals as a result of this testing, in the last three calendar years.
answer_text: Following the coming into force on 12 April 2024 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the requirements of Animal Health Law that are placed on DAERA, including as they relate to transmissible diseases and disease eradication, no longer sat within my ministerial responsibilities and were subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.However, from 17 May 2024, the Defra Secretary of state has exercised his discretion in relation to disease awareness, disease preparedness and disease control in relation to animal and plant health and these areas are now back under my ministerial responsibility.Information relating to bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) is not held on a constituency basis, rather, it is held on the basis of the geographical regions covered by DAERA Direct Offices. The Upper Bann constituency that you have requested information on falls mostly within the Armagh DAERA Direct Office, but a small proportion falls within the Newry DAERA Direct Office boundary.The cost of carrying out bTB testing in the Upper Bann area is based on the total figures paid over three calendar years to the 25 Private Veterinary Practices that are registered within the Armagh and Newry DAERA Direct office boundaries. This equates to £8,447,202.33. This figure must however be caveated as it does not:take into account deductions that practices may have incurred due to contractual breaches; account for the fact that the 25 practices concerned may have conducted bTB testing within the boundaries of other DAERA Direct Offices; andaccount for the fact that practices that are registered in DAERA Direct Offices other than Armagh and Newry may have conducted bTB testing within the bounds of Armagh and Newry DAERA Direct Offices.The amount paid to farmers for the removal of animals as a result of bTB testing in the Armagh and Newry DAERA Direct Offices over the last three years equates to £28,319,623.90. However, as above, this is the total amount paid to all farmers within the Armagh and Newry DAERA Direct Office boundaries, within which the Upper Bann constituency is located.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of bovine Tuberculosis tests, both individual or herd, which have been postponed or cancelled due to a lack of (i) budget; and (ii) staffing availability, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: Following the coming into force on 12 April 2024 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the requirements of Animal Health Law that are placed on DAERA, including as they relate to transmissible diseases and disease eradication, no longer sat within my ministerial responsibilities and were subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.However, from 17 May 2024, the Defra Secretary of state has exercised his discretion in relation to disease awareness, disease preparedness and disease control in relation to animal and plant health and these areas are now under my ministerial responsibility.My officials are unaware of any issues with bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) tests being cancelled due to resource or cost issues over the past year. It is routine that over 90% of herd tests are carried out by Private Veterinary Practitioners (PVPs). Herd tests may be cancelled or postponed, and it is usual that most changes are made as the original time/date does not suit the herd keeper. PVPs must report any staffing difficulties in resourcing the testing allocated to them, and no such difficulties have been reported over the last year. The remainder of herd testing is carried out by DAERA staff, primarily dedicated DAERA Testing Veterinary Officers (TVOTs), and their testing allocation is monitored by the local Divisional Veterinary Officer to ensure that they can cover the allocation given to them. Tests will be reallocated to PVPs in time to allow arrangement before the due date if DAERA TVOTs are oversubscribed. Traditionally DAERA vets in local offices have carried out much of the testing of individual animals considered at risk. These are primarily forward trace animals (animals that left a bTB breakdown herd before infection was disclosed), or animals that tested inconclusive at a herd test. Due to the pressures on veterinary resource in local offices it has been necessary to make amendments to ensure that the highest priority work can be completed. Over the past year, DAERA vets in local offices have been instructed to ensure that inconclusive animals are tested when due but that only the highest risk forward trace animals are to be tested by them. The default position is now that forward trace animal testing, unless in a high-risk situation, is to be completed as part of the next herd test due on the herd. Forward trace animals are restricted from movement off farm other than directly to slaughter. If a herd keeper contacts the local office and asks to have the animal/s tested prior to the herd test my staff try to accommodate this where possible, and if not possible, the herd keeper can arrange to have the animal privately tested by their PVP. Provisional figures show that for 9,354 forward trace tests were set with due dates from 01/05/2023 to 30/04/2024, 74% of these have already been completed within 90 days of the due date.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what steps his Department is taking to address the prevalence of sheep worrying; and (ii) for his assessment of whether legislation, similar to the Dogs (Protection of Livestock) Act 1953 in Scotland, would help reduce the prevalence of sheep worrying.
answer_text: I am satisfied that the Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 sets out robust provisions regarding livestock worrying.  Where a dog attacks, kills, or chases livestock, including sheep, in a way that might reasonably be expected to cause death, injury or suffering to the livestock or financial loss to the livestock owner, an offence has been committed by the keeper or person in charge of the dog.Councils enforce the legislation and can impose control conditions on the licence for the dog in question.  Control conditions include requirements to muzzle the dog when in public and keep the dog under control when in public.  Councils are also empowered to make recommendations for prosecution where they consider it appropriate, and the Court has the power to order the destruction of the dog.My Department continues to highlight the need for owners to ensure that control is exerted over their dogs at all times, including when around livestock.  The Department’s website provides guidance on livestock worrying emphasising the harm that it can cause and the steps that dog owners and landowners can take to prevent it.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) his Department's plans to have a presence at the National Ploughing Championships in 2024; (ii) his Department's indictive budget for this event; and (iii) his Department's budget for hospitality at this event.
answer_text: With the National Ploughing Championships being the Republic of Ireland’s largest agricultural event I recognise the unique opportunities it offers to promote Northern Ireland’s agri-food and rural sectors to a large audience.   The event also provides the basis for the building and enhancement of working relationships between Ministers and officials in addition to agri-food and rural commerce stakeholders right across the island. Arrangements are yet to be confirmed, however, I have intention of visiting the 2024 National Ploughing Championships for one day and will be accompanied by the Permanent Secretary and relevant officials.  I look forward to meeting with some of the Northern Irish exhibitors and my ministerial counterparts.  	Standard travel and subsistence costs will constitute the only financial outlay to the department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the progress of the new beef facilities at AFBI; (ii) the proposed cost of the project; and (iii) the target completion date.
answer_text: The AFBI Beef Facilities Project is currently at RIBA Stage 3 (Royal Institute of British Architects) of the design and construction process. This stage will be completed by March 2025 and at that point a detailed design and cost will be available.  It is planned to have all construction phases at AFBI completed by 2029, however this will be contingent upon successful planning approval, procurement and budget availability.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount of underspend in his Department for (i) 2022-23; and (ii) 2023-24.
answer_text: The amount of underspend in my Department for the 2022-23 financial year was £1.3m (0.2%) Non Ringfenced Resource DEL, £32k (0.1%) Ringfenced Resource DEL and £0.2m (0.2%) Capital DEL against the respective final budgets for that year.In relation to 2023-24, Final Outturn figures will not be available until the audit of the Annual Report and Accounts for the year ended 31 March 2024 has concluded and the associated returns submitted to the Department of Finance.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 8044/22-27, for an update following the publication of the Budget 2024-25.
answer_text: Following Budget 2024-2025 receiving Executive approval on the 25 April 2024, I am currently considering how best to allocate the funding available, given the competing statutory obligations and contractual pressures facing my Department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 9139/22-27, why a business case has not been taken forward in respect of support for agriculture shows.
answer_text: The Independent Resilience Review of Agricultural Shows (IRRAS)in Northern Ireland, published in June 2023, brought forward recommendations for both industry and government aimed at maximising the sustainability of agricultural shows in the years ahead including future funding options.Progress in evaluating the options proffered by the IRRAS will involve completion of a business case and Economic Impact Assessment which can demonstrate feasibility, regularity, propriety, and value for money.In the context of budgetary constraints in 2023 and there being no statutory obligation to participate in/fund shows, a decision was taken under the Northern Ireland (Executive Formation etc.) Act 2022 that no sponsorship payments would be administered to shows in that year.  Further, due to resourcing constraints, it has not been possible to progress work to evaluate the options set out in the Review. The Northern Ireland Executive agreed the 2024/25 budget on 25 April.  Funding for agricultural shows will be considered as part of the 2024/25 budget process. Statutory obligations and contractual commitments will require funding first before it can be determined if there is scope to fund discretionary expenditure such as support for agricultural shows.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on when the Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme will be open for new applicants.
answer_text: My officials have completed an evaluation of the 2022 Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme. The findings, lessons learned and recommendations from the 2022 evaluation as well as the independent evaluation of the schemes delivered between 2016 and 2021 have been considered and a Business Case proposing to launch the Scheme in 2024/2025 is at an advanced stage.Subject to my consideration and in conjunction with wider budget discussions I hope to be in a position to make an announcement regarding the future of the Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme in coming weeks.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 8045/22-27, for an update following the publication of the Budget 2024-25.
answer_text: As you are aware, the 2024/25 budget was agreed by the Executive on 25 April. I will shortly consider that budget cognisant in the first instance of my department’s statutory obligations and contractual commitments. Subsequent consideration will follow in respect of discretionary expenditure, such as funding for local agricultural shows.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to the NICS Hybrid Working Policy, (i) whether there is a mandatory number of days staff should be physically present at their base location; (ii) what procedures are in place to monitor and enforce this policy; and (iii) to detail the compliance rate for this policy, broken down by Department.
answer_text: The NICS Hybrid Working policy does not prescribe the number of days staff should physically be present in the workplace.  The Hybrid Working policy allows staff to avail of a mix of workplace, home and remote working.  The option of hybrid working is not mandatory as the ability to offer this flexibility depends on business need and the nature of the job role. Individuals requesting hybrid working do so by completing a workstyle agreement which is subject to line manager approval.  The work style agreement sets out the agreed split between workplace and home or remote working.   The Department of Finance does not hold information centrally on the total number of staff availing of hybrid working in the NICS.  As at end of February 2024, 90.7% of Department of Finance permanent/fixed term staff have a hybrid workstyle agreement in place.

answer_text: Following the coming into force on 12 April 2024 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the requirements of Animal Health Law that are placed on DAERA, including as they relate to transmissible diseases and disease eradication, no longer sit within my ministerial responsibilities and are now subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 7488/22-27, (i) what other regions of the United Kingdom are implementing the 0.08 per cent threshold for the purpose of ecological risk assessments for air pollution impacts; and (ii) to provide a link to the specific policy or guidance documents which refer to its implementation.
answer_text: The evidence underpinning the air pollution Decision-Making Threshold (DMT) project is applicable across all regions of the UK.  The other conservation and regulatory agencies in the UK are considering implementation of the DMT report’s findings, taking into account the specific background levels of air pollution and development pressures in each region.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his plans to provide further support to isolated communities through the Rural Halls Refurbishment Scheme.
answer_text: The Rural Halls Refurbishment Scheme pilot has been evaluated and the outcomes, lessons learned, and recommendations are now being considered in full as I consider the future of rural policy.  It is important to note that responsibility for addressing social and economic issues affecting rural communities rests primarily with the departments which have the statutory remit.  It is my intention to engage with my Executive colleagues to help inform and set the direction of future rural policy, to ensure a better outcome for rural communities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the progress made to implement the recommendations outlined in the Independent Strategic Review of the NI Agri-Food Sector, including the budget allocated to meet these priorities.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of the agri-food sector to our economy and the contribution it will make to delivering my economic vision.Since the Independent Review of the NI Agri-Food Sector (ISRAF) was published in 2021 my Department has worked with DAERA to progress and implement a number of recommendations, including the establishment of the Northern Ireland Diamond, proposals for a new investment initiative for the agri-food and drink processing sector and the establishment of a new Sustainability Body.    The restoration of the Executive provides further opportunity to work collaboratively with colleagues from DEARA, to continue to take forward the ISRAF recommendations.In addition, many of the recommendations from the ISRAF report have informed the Agri-tech Action plan which is being developed as part of DfE's Technologies and Cluster policy, to unlock the growth within the Agri-tech sector.Whilst the Executive has agreed an overall budget, I am currently working through what it means for my Department so it is not possible at this time to confirm how much funding will be allocated to meet the ISRAF recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the advice available to farmers to reduce the transmission of bovine TB between badgers and cattle, including the risks of water infected by wildlife with TB.
answer_text: My Department makes available to farmers a range of material, including advice and guidance, on the role wildlife plays in the spread of bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) and how to mitigate against it.  Measures to prevent a bTB breakdown can include raising the height of drinking and feeding troughs. This information is publicly available and can be found on the DAERA website at:	https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/dard/tb-wildlife-biosecurity-leaflet.pdf and https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/biosecurity-measures-which-help-protect-your-herd-against-tb .	In the event of a bTB breakdown, a wildlife biosecurity leaflet; a general advisory leaflet on biosecurity measures to help protect the herd against bTB and a BT95 document are issued. 		A BT95 document lists the precautions advised by the department to control and eliminate the disease, such as cleansing and disinfection and prevention of further spread. It contains advice to avoid rivers or streams for watering purposes if possible and to raise the height of drinking and feeding troughs. This measure is designed to prevent the contamination of food or water by wildlife species which may otherwise access this equipment.  		In addition, local Animal Health and Welfare Inspectors carry out an on-site visit for most bTB breakdowns where the risk posed by wildlife contact will be discussed, and advice given on how to minimise that risk.		The UK Government TB Hub also contains recommendations on the practical measures that farmers can take to reduce the risk of introducing bovine TB onto their farm. 		Detailed advice, factsheets and recommendations on preventing bTB breakdowns, such as the use of barriers to restrict badger access to cattle and the limiting of access by cattle to badger latrines and setts is available at: https://tbhub.co.uk/preventing-tb-breakdowns/protect-your-herd-from-bovine-tb/
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department is meeting its obligations under the Autism (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The Autism (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 places a responsibility on the Department of Health to prepare a cross-departmental autism strategy in collaboration with all government departments which sets out key priorities and actions to enable autistic people and their families to be supported throughout their lives.  The Autism Strategy 2023 to 2028 has been developed to reflect the methodology, content and timeframes set out in the Autism (Amendment) Act (NI) 2022 legislation.  The Autism Strategy 2023-2028 was published on 13 December 23 with a two year Delivery Plan for 2023-2025 which will be reviewed on an annual basis. The Delivery Plan sets out the actions to be achieved against commitments, timeframes associated outcomes and the delivery partners involved.   Work on implementation of the strategy is underway across government departments and public bodies and progress will be assessed through the annual monitoring report, which officials have commenced working on. This will be shared with Executive Colleagues and published on the Department’s website once collated and approved.The appointment of an Autism Reviewer remains an outstanding action, which I will consider alongside the 24/25 budget for Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when regional endoscopic services at (i) Lagan Valley Hospital; and (ii) Omagh Hospital, will be fully operational.
answer_text: Lagan Valley Hospital is fully operational and delivering its funded capacity of 20 endoscopy sessions per week. Omagh Hospital is currently delivering six sessions per week based on the latest schedule. It is anticipated that Omagh Hospital will be fully operational by October 2024, pending successful completion of the recruitment process for consultant staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact of the role of the Independent Autism Reviewer.
answer_text: The role of the Independent Autism Reviewer is a significant one that will be aligned to the functions outlined in the Autism (Amendment) Act (NI) 2022.  I fully recognise the importance of this role to monitor the implementation of the Autism Strategy 2023-28 and to review the wider system of legislation, funding, practice, services, and research.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the measurable targets contained in the Autism Strategy 2023-2028 and the accompanying delivery plan.
answer_text: At the time of the development of the Autism Strategy 2023-2028, Northern Ireland government departments were experiencing unprecedented budgetary challenges including planning within singular year budget allocations that placed some constraints upon initial development and implementation of this strategy.  In consideration of these constraints, and to ensure that provision of support and services could continue to be delivered the initial two-year Delivery Plan 2023-2025 commenced implementation of this strategy.  The Delivery Plan, which was published on the Department’s website, sets out the actions to be achieved against commitments, timeframes associated outcomes and the delivery partners involved. A monitoring framework has been established to drive implementation. The monitoring framework will collect information on how outcomes have been measured and I will ensure that the Assembly are provided with an update on the first-year delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what assessment his Department has made of the extent of the risk to (i) road users; and (ii) the broader population associated with delays in MOT tests being carried out.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) appreciates that in some cases customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires and has taken steps to ensure that motorists are still able to use their vehicles when they are unable to secure an MOT appointment before their current MOT expires. The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at A guide to booking your MOT/ vehicle test | nidirect and is also included as an advice note with every MOT reminder letter issued to customers.The PSNI has agreed not to penalise drivers whose MOT has expired so long as:•	their vehicle is in a roadworthy and safe condition and does not have a statutory off-road notification (SORN) declaration.•	their vehicle is properly insured. •	they can prove (by showing an MOT appointment notification/ reminder, either in electronic or paper form) that a test appointment has been booked for their vehicle.All other vehicle roadworthiness and insurance offences will be dealt with in the usual way.It is the vehicle owner’s legal responsibility to make sure their vehicle is in a roadworthy condition to be used on a public road.  This is the expectation of the Police and Insurers and does not change, whether there is an MOT in place or not.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) what welfare assistance is available to parents of children with a terminal illness; and (ii) how this compares with other parts of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: I am committed to supporting people who are nearing the end of their life by providing financial support quickly and compassionately.Special rules for terminal illness allow my Department to provide fast-track access to the appropriate Disability Benefit. Disability Living Allowance for children is paid as a contribution towards the extra costs faced by severely disabled children under 16 as a result of their disabilities.  A child qualifying under special rules is not required to undergo face to face assessments, serve qualifying periods, or demonstrate care or supervision needs.  For those aged 16 or over, Personal Independence Payment is available to assist with the extra costs arising from having a long-term health condition or disability.  If conditions of entitlement are satisfied a Universal Credit claimant who is responsible for a terminally ill child will be entitled to additional financial support in the form of a Disabled Child Addition (£487.58 per calendar month) and a Carer’s Element (£198.31 per calendar month) of Universal Credit. The claimant may also agree a new claimant commitment to take account of the additional caring responsibilities associated with a terminal diagnosis.Carer’s Allowance may also be available for those regularly and substantially caring for a terminally ill child for at least 35 hours a week.In addition, the Department’s Make the Call service can carry out an entitlement check to ensure a person is accessing all the benefits, supports and services available to them. It can be contacted via Freephone number 0800 232 1271 or by emailing makethecall@dfcni.gov.ukSocial security policy and legislation in Northern Ireland is generally maintained in parity with that put in place by the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) in Great Britain. Scotland maintain a separate social security system for disability benefits and a child diagnosed with a terminal illness can apply for Child Disability Payment under special rules for terminal illness.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the planned model for hyper-acute stroke care in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Policy work to consider Hyperacute Stroke Care has recently recommenced. A new process has been agreed to identify a preferred option for Hyperacute Stroke Care. This three-phase process will include an updated and extended activity analysis and the development of evaluation criteria which will be applied to all potential options. In addition, detailed financial, feasibility and equality analyses will be undertaken of all short-listed options.  It is estimated that the process will take 18 months, with a preferred option identified in Summer 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he has taken to improve ambulance handover times.
answer_text: My Department has taken a number of steps to improve ambulance waiting times.Reducing ambulance handover delays outside emergency departments has been a key priority due to the impact this has on NIAS’s capacity to respond to calls in the community, particularly Category 1 and Category 2 emergency calls.A 3-hour ambulance handover backstop was introduced in December 2022 and amended to a 2-hour backstop in February 2023 to reduce long waits outside emergency departments and release ambulances more quickly.In 2023 HSC Trusts were required to develop plans in conjunction with NIAS to include specific actions to improve ambulance handover times, including the introduction of additional alternative care pathways as alternatives to conveying patients to the Emergency Department.  The Department has allocated funding to NIAS for additional Hospital Ambulance Liaison Officers at main hospital sites.Learning has been shared from the Association of Ambulance Chief Executives on improvements to ambulance handover delays at regional unscheduled care meetings with Trust Directors to support local adaptation.A regional Ambulance Cohorting Standard was issued to Trusts setting out escalation processes to release waiting ambulances in the event of NIAS having an outstanding Cat 1 call, or Cat 2 response times exceeding 45 minutes.  Daily monitoring of average delays and longest delays at each hospital is circulated widely within the HSC system including to the Trust Chief Executives.   Individual Trust performance against this standard is a standing agenda item at monthly unscheduled care performance meetings with all Trusts.  As part of Winter planning arrangements, ambulance handover information is one of four matrices made available to the public on a fortnightly basis via the Departmental website.Key performance targets for NIAS Category 1 and Category 2 Ambulance Response rates have been set as part of the Department’s Service Delivery Plans and performance against these targets is monitored on a monthly basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how long parents are entitled to receive Personal Independence Payments for a terminally ill child.
answer_text: Parents of a child with a terminal illness may make a claim to Disability Living Allowance up to the age of 16 years. For Personal Independence Payment, a person aged 16 or over is treated as a young adult.  DLA awards for children and PIP awards for young adults with a terminal illness vary in length. Where a child or young adult is terminally ill and has a life expectancy of under 12 months, an award may be made under the Special Rules for End-of-Life criteria, for an initial period of three years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how long parents are entitled to receive Disability Living Allowance for a terminally ill child.
answer_text: Disability Living Allowance awards for children with a terminal illness vary in length and will remain in payment while the child continues to meet the eligibility criteria of the benefit. Where a child is terminally ill and has a life expectancy of under 12 months, a faster award with a higher rate of Disability Living Allowance may be made under the Special Rules for End-of-Life criteria, for an initial period of three years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) average; and (ii) longest ambulance handover times at each Type 1 Emergency Department, in each of the last six months.
answer_text: Information on the average and longest ambulance handover times at each Type 1 Emergency Department (ED) is not held centrally. However, the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) has provided a response in relation to the average and longest ambulance handover times at each Type 1 Emergency Department, in each of the last six months.Tables 1 and 2, overleaf, show the average and longest patient handover times at each Type 1 Emergency Department in each of the last six months from October 2023 to March 2024. answer_text: In Northern Ireland the number of under-18s that have attended adult gender services are as follows:202028202118202292023122024<5NB: Please note that we  cannot provide patient numbers, where the response is less than or equal to five (=5) as it would potentially allow identification of the individual patient and would therefore be personal data.The Service does not hold data for elsewhere in the United Kingdom or outside of the United KingdomThe Service does not hold data for elsewhere in the United Kingdom or outside of the United Kingdom

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) adults; and (ii) under-18s from Northern Ireland that have attended the Tavistock Clinic, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The information is not held centrally in my department and has therefore been requested from the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts. Belfast and Northern HSC Trust advised that they do not readily hold this information and a manual trawl would be required to provide this information, for which the time taken to extract the information would be at a disproportionate cost. The South Eastern, Southern, and Western HSC Trusts advised that they have not made any referrals to the Tavistock Clinic in the last 5 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions he has taken to minimise under-18s accessing puberty blockers from providers not registered in the United Kingdom.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, puberty blockers are only available on NHS prescription for under 18s, for those young people who were accepted and remain on the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services Gender Identity Service endocrine pathway prior to March 2020. As with all medicines it is particularly important to take great care when buying medicines online. Many websites selling medicines online are based overseas and are not regulated by UK authorities. Sourcing prescription medicines from unauthorised sources significantly increases the risk of getting substandard or even fake medicines. My Department’s Medicines Regulatory Group (MRG) works with partners across government and policing to combat the unlawful trade in medicines. MRG monitors online channels for evidence of illegal activity and takes proportionate enforcement action. This includes using the full range of powers to investigate and prosecute offenders, where necessary and appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are restrictions in place on puberty blockers being prescribed to under-18s in Northern Ireland through private clinics.
answer_text: Triptorelin is licensed by the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) for the treatment of various conditions and has also been used outside the terms of the MHRA licence (‘off-label’) to suppress puberty in gender dysphoric adolescents. There may be clinical situations where the use of unlicensed medicines, or medicines outside the terms of the MHRA licence (‘off-label’) may be judged by a prescriber to be in the best interest of patients on the basis of available evidence, where the patient's needs cannot be met by an appropriately licensed medicine. The responsibility that falls on healthcare professionals when prescribing an unlicensed medicine or an ‘off-label’ medicine may be greater than when prescribing a licensed medicine within the terms of its licence. Prescribers operating in private healthcare providers considering prescribing Triptoretlin on an ‘off-label’ basis to suppress puberty in gender dysphoric adolescents are still required to operate within relevant legislative frameworks as well as professional guidance, such as the General Medical Council’s 'Good practice in prescribing and managing medicines and devices'.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the bills he intends to introduce during this Assembly mandate.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the role and objectives of communications teams in (i) Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) other health bodies.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts and the Department’s ALBs are required to discharge a range of responsibilities in relation to health and social care and public safety in Northern Ireland. Subsequently, communications activities and output will vary across the different bodies according to their remit and operational need. Communicating with patients, local communities, staff and other stakeholders including the media is of vital importance to the HSC system. Key functions of HSC Communications teams include: managing internal communications, media handling, social media management and stakeholder communication. They have responsibility for communicating important messages on service delivery, public health and wider issues to the public. They are also key to ensuring transparency and openness in the delivery of public services. Departmental ALBs are responsible for ensuring that there are appropriate mechanisms in place to ensure that services are delivered appropriately and effectively. Communications, like any other operational function, will be subject to these same review mechanisms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on progress towards meeting safe staffing recommendations in health and social care.
answer_text: I made a commitment to consider and develop safe nurse staffing legislation as part of the January 2020 Framework Agreement.  In October 2022, I approved the expansion of the scope beyond nursing, to include all professions within the Health and Social Care Trusts. My officials are continuing to work in partnership with key stakeholders, including trade unions to develop a consultation on legislative proposals, which I intend to publish in the coming weeks.
