question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what discussions his officials had with their Department of Health and Social Care counterparts in advance of the Ministerial Statement by Secretary of State Atkins in the House of Commons on 15 April 2024; and (ii) to detail the actions his Department is taking to implement the recommendations of the Cass Review.
answer_text: My officials did not have any discussions with their colleagues in the Department of Health and Social Care in advance of the Ministerial Statement by the Secretary of State, Rt Hon Victoria Atkins MP, on 15 April 2024. However, my officials have previously liaised with professional colleagues in NHS England and the other nations to discuss a range of issues, some of which were highlighted in the Interim Cass Review report of 2022,  not least the significant increase in referrals into Gender Identity Services in recent years which has contributed to long waiting lists and growing concern about how the NHS should most appropriately assess, diagnose, and care for this population of children and young people.(ii)	My Department notes the publication of the Cass Review, which was commissioned by NHS England. The Cass Review includes 32 recommendations for England, encompassing a wide range of subject areas including medical interventions, requirements for further research and data, and safeguarding measures. My Department will carefully consider the Cass Review recommendations and any implications for those services provided in Northern Ireland.  My officials will meet with clinicians and stakeholders in the coming weeks to consider the Review recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) adults; (ii) individuals aged 16-17; and (iii) under-16s, prescribed testosterone in (a) 2021; (b) 2022; (c) 2023; and (d) 2024.
answer_text: The number of people prescribed testosterone is not available; if a patient does not take a prescription to a dispenser and get it dispensed, then the data cannot be collected. The table overleaf shows the total number of patients in each age band dispensed testosterone products in primary care for 2021, 2022 and 2023, the latest period for which information is publicly available.Number of patients dispensed testosterone in primary care, 2021-20231,2,3,4Age band20212022202315 and under23202016 and 1718231618 and over2,5223,1233,311Total2,5633,1663,347Source: Family Practitioner Services Pharmacy Payment System, HSC Business Services Organisation. 1All information is for prescriptions prescribed in Primary care and dispensed by a community pharmacist, dispensing doctor or appliance contractor. Data on secondary care prescribing and private prescriptions are not held by the Business Services Organisation.2 The number of patients is the number of estimated unique patients whose dispensed scripts have been successfully matched against the central GP patient register in each time period. If a patient has one or more prescriptions, the patient will be counted once.  While there is a chance that some patients may have been missed completely if all of the scripts dispensed to them were not successfully scanned, because patients will generally have submitted multiple prescriptions over the course of a year, there is a high chance they will have been picked up at least once in any given year, the exception being those newly prescribed the drug towards year end.3 To avoid duplication, the age of the patient when the last prescription was issued in each year has been used.4 Information has been provided for the chemical names Testosterone, Testosterone Undecanoate, Testosterone Enantate and Testosterone esters (testosterone decanoate, testosterone isocaproate, testosterone phenylpropionate and testosterone propionate).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his Department’s assessment of Dr Hilary Cass’s report, Independent Review of Gender Identity Services for Children and Young People.
answer_text: The Department of Health notes the publication of the Cass Review, which was commissioned by NHS England.  The Cass Review has put forward 32 recommendations for England, encompassing a wide range of subject areas including medical interventions, requirements for further research and data, and safeguarding measures. My Department will carefully consider the CASS Review recommendations and any implications for those services provided in Northern Ireland.  My officials will meet with clinicians and stakeholders in the coming weeks to consider the Review recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) adults; (ii) individuals aged 16-17; and (iii) under-16s, prescribed oestrogen in (a) 2021; (b) 2022; (c) 2023; and (d) 2024.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) what consideration his Department has given to the February 2022 Independent Review of Gender Identity Services for Children and Young People: Interim Report of the Cass Review; and (ii) to detail the actions taken as a result.
answer_text: (i) My Department noted the publication and contents of the February 2022 Independent Review of Gender Identity Services for Children and Young People: Interim Report of the Cass Review. The interim report to NHS England set out their work to date, what had been learned so far and the approach going forward. The report did not set out final recommendations.(ii) My officials have previously liaised  with professional colleagues in NHS England and the other nations to discuss a range of issues, some of which were highlighted in the Interim Cass Review Report of 2022  not least the significant increase in referrals into Gender Identity Services in recent years which has contributed to long waiting lists and growing concern about how the NHS should most appropriately assess, diagnose and care for this population of children and young people.  The key points raised in the report were:The rapid increase in the number of children requiring support and the complex case-mix means that the current clinical model, with a single national provider, is not sustainable in the longer term. We need to know more about the population being referred and outcomes. There has not been routine and consistent data collection, which means it is not possible to accurately track the outcomes and pathways that children and young people take through the service.  There is lack of consensus and open discussion about the nature of gender dysphoria and therefore about the appropriate clinical response.  Because the specialist service has evolved rapidly and organically in response to demand, the clinical approach and overall service design has not been subjected to some of the normal quality controls that are typically applied when new or innovative treatments are introduced.Whilst a regional review of Gender Identity services began in Northern Ireland in September 2019, the  significant  challenges posed by the COVID-19 pandemic resulted in that work having to be paused. My officials, working with the Gender Identity Services Pathway Review Group and the Gender Identity Services Liaison Panel, have now developed a solution which meets the immediate needs of service users in Northern Ireland and enables the service to expand  and build capacity over a longer period of time.  This solution will  also address the long waiting times for this service. However, given the very significant funding challenges faced by my Department it has not been possible to progress this work as we would have liked.  The implications of the Cass Review will of course be taken into account by my officials and clinical experts as part of any service development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) adults; (ii) individuals aged 16-17; and (iii) under-16s, prescribed puberty blockers in (a) 2021; (b) 2022; (c) 2023; and (d) 2024.
answer_text: The information requested is not available.  Drugs typically prescribed to suppress the release of sex hormones in individuals can be for the treatment of a range of conditions and the HSC Business Services Organisation does not hold information on the indication for which drugs have been prescribed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the amount of financial support provided to the faith-based sector in the last five years.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline (i) the details required for companies in the UK to access the UK Internal Market; and (ii) whether customs information is required in accordance with Article 9(2) of the Joint Committee Decision 1/2023.
answer_text: It is important to note that these matters do not fall within my official responsibilities as Economy Minister. 	However, I would direct you to the details published by the British government on the trading arrangements set out in the Windsor Framework.  These provide, in full, the requirements for different types of goods and movement arrangements in line with the British governments agreement with the EU and is regularly updated. The published advice to businesses can be accessed at https://www.gov.uk/guidance/trading-and-moving-goods-in-and-out-of-northern-ireland

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail his Department’s contribution in terms of staff and costs to the (a) Trader Support Service; and (b) Movement Assistance Scheme; and (ii) what estimate his Department has made regarding staff and costs for future years.
answer_text: The UK Government is solely responsible for the management of both the Trader Support Service and the Movement Assistance Scheme.  The deployment of staff and liability for associated costs of these programmes are therefore matters for the UK Government, both now and in the future.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail his Department’s contribution in terms of staff and costs to the (a) Trader Support Service; and (b) Movement Assistance Scheme; and (ii) what estimate his Department has made regarding staff and costs for future years.
answer_text: My department does not make any contribution in staff or costs to either the Trader Support Service or the Movements Assistance Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the current negotiations that are taking place between the UK and the EU on the issue of veterinary medicines moving from Great Britain to Northern Ireland in light of the end of the grace period in December 2025; and (ii) the extent to which his Department is involved in these negotiations.
answer_text: The current grace period was granted on condition that the Veterinary Medicines Directorate (VMD), an agency of DEFRA and the relevant UK competent authority, complies with measures in a joint EU/UK Action plan. This action plan is a matter for the UK Government. DAERA Officials meet with VMD and DEFRA officials on a regular basis to highlight the relevant impacts and risks involved with any change to the supply of Veterinary Medicines to Northern Ireland.The movement of veterinary medicines from Great Britain to Northern Ireland is now subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State as provided for in regulation 3 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the current process for the movement of seed potatoes from professional operators in Great Britain to consumers in Northern Ireland; and (ii) how this differs from the process in place prior to January 2021.
answer_text: Following the coming into force on 12 April 2024 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the movement of seed potatoes is a matter which is now subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) whether the EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism will apply in Northern Ireland; (ii) whether he will publish his Department’s analysis of the impact on the Northern Ireland economy; and (iii) whether he will publish his Department’s analysis of the impact on trade between Great Britain and Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism  does not fall within my official responsibilities as Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the compliance and documentary requirements for sanitary and phytosanitary goods to move from Great Britain to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Following the coming into force on 12 April 2024 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the movement of sanitary and phytosanitary goods from Great Britain to Northern Ireland is a matter which is now subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what action he will take to ensure that schoolchildren in Northern Ireland can benefit from the Woodland Trust School Scheme in the same way as other children in the UK.
answer_text: The Woodland Trust’s free tree pack scheme, operating in GB, includes tree species listed as high-risk plants.The movement of high risk plants is now subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State as provided for in regulation 3 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) a list of all categories and species of trees that are allowed into Northern Ireland from Great Britain; (ii) a list of all categories and species of trees that are not allowed into Northern Ireland from Great Britain; and (iii) whether his Department is involved in the working group set up under Command Paper 1021 to consider these issues.
answer_text: Matters relating to the movement of trees from Great Britain to Northern Ireland are now subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State as provided for in regulation 3 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024. 	I understand that the Horticultural Working Group has been established by the UK Government.  To date my Department has not received an invitation to attend.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action he will take to ensure that schoolchildren in Northern Ireland can benefit from the Woodland Trust School Scheme in the same way as other children in the UK.
answer_text: The Woodland Trust provides free resources that promote understanding and the value of woodlands and wildlife. Outdoor learning has so many benefits, both for pupils and for schools. The work of the Woodland Trust and Forest Schools complement and support “The World Around Us” aspect of the Northern Ireland Curriculum and I understand that many schools have already engaged in these programmes. Whether or not schools choose to take part in the Woodland Trust’s School Scheme is a matter for individual schools to determine, in line with their curriculum offer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how many dossiers requesting the lifting of a ban on trees being moved from Great Britain to Northern Ireland have been sent to the EU; (ii) what progress has been made on each one; and (iii) what level of consideration his Department has made to each dossier.
answer_text: UK Government has submitted technical dossiers for 42 plant and tree species to the EU, for consideration of lifting prohibitions on their entry to the EU, including Northern Ireland.		The EU has approved 11 of these dossiers, lifting the prohibitions for these species to move from GB to EU, including NI. A further 31 dossiers remain under consideration by the EU, with 5 of these having progressed to commodity risk assessment stage.		The list of prohibited plant and tree species can be found at the link below:	High Risk Plants (HRPs) prohibited from export from GB to EU		DAERA, as part of the UK Plant Health Service, provides information where required to contribute to the dossiers submitted to the EU.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) what progress has been made to date with the implementation of the Zero Emission Vehicle mandate; (ii) the timeframe for implementation; and (iii) his assessment of what action is required by (a) car manufacturers; (b) car garages; and (c) customers to progress the Zero Emission Vehicle mandate.
answer_text: The Zero Emissions Vehicle Mandate was laid by the British Government, Scottish Government, and Welsh Senedd as the Vehicle Emissions Trading Schemes Order (VETS) in England, Scotland, and Wales in October 2023 and came into force in those jurisdictions from 03 January 2024. Unfortunately, due to the absence of the Assembly, we were not able to join at that time. I am minded to pursue the introduction of the VETS Scheme here and am working with my officials to understand the process that will need to be followed for implementation of this legislation.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their Department's plans to support isolated minority communities living along the border.
To ask the Minister of Health how the specialist therapy previously provided by Nexus will be delivered as part of his Department's mental health provision.
answer_text: (i) On 4 March 2024 in my statement to the Assembly, I advised that funding for this specialist counselling service for victims of sexual abuse would be extended for a further 12 months, under contract to Department of Health. The intention has always been to use this funding to continue to support people who are victims and survivors of sexual abuse in line with the draft Domestic and Sexual Violence Strategy 2023-2030.(ii) My Department has been in communication with Nexus over many months in connection with the contract and has worked extensively with Nexus to improve waiting list times and the standard of service being provided.There is a commissioning lead assigned to the contract.  As part of the contract management arrangements, regular performance meetings are held with the service provider based on submitted monthly monitoring reports. Monitoring meetings provide a two-way challenge function, whereby Departmental Officials ensure service delivery, performance and value for money and providers can discuss service or cost pressures as required, this process will continue for the life of the contract.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to the extension of the Nexus NI contract, what measures he has put in place to monitor performance and operational issues.
answer_text: (i) On 4 March 2024 in my statement to the Assembly, I advised that funding for this specialist counselling service for victims of sexual abuse would be extended for a further 12 months, under contract to Department of Health. The intention has always been to use this funding to continue to support people who are victims and survivors of sexual abuse in line with the draft Domestic and Sexual Violence Strategy 2023-2030.(ii) My Department has been in communication with Nexus over many months in connection with the contract and has worked extensively with Nexus to improve waiting list times and the standard of service being provided.There is a commissioning lead assigned to the contract.  As part of the contract management arrangements, regular performance meetings are held with the service provider based on submitted monthly monitoring reports. Monitoring meetings provide a two-way challenge function, whereby Departmental Officials ensure service delivery, performance and value for money and providers can discuss service or cost pressures as required, this process will continue for the life of the contract.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the sustainability of General Practice in its current form.
answer_text: Since returning as Health Minister, my immediate priority has been to progress the 2023/24 Doctors and Dentist Review Board recommendations award for GPs.  I recently approved a 6% funding uplift for GPs and their staff. 	I am aware that over the past two years, 24 contracts have been returned however despite this high level of contract hand-back, through working with key stakeholders including HSC Trusts and GP Federations, it has been possible to ensure that no practice has closed and that patients can continue to access GP services in their areas.		Officials have been taking forward work with relevant NICS Departments and public bodies to explore ways to reduce the administrative burden on GP practices of completing non-statutory administrative tasks and the unnecessary directing of patients to GPs.		My Department has been engaging with HSC Trusts and GP representatives to explore how GP services can be sustained both in the short and medium term.  This includes work to facilitate earlier involvement of HSC Trusts in instances where GP practices are experiencing difficulty.  Other work has also been progressed to develop guidance that will improve management of the interface between primary and secondary care.  		My Department has provided significant financial support to help stabilise primary care services.  In October 2023, my Department published its Winter Pressures Plan, providing £3.4m in funding to General Medical Services and Out of Hours Services to support the capacity of GP practices to meet the anticipated increase in demand over the winter period.  My Department also made £4.3m funding available to assist GP practices to deliver proactive support and care to those in nursing and residential care homes this winter. In addition to this, the Department reallocated £2.7m as outlined above to boost capacity.  		Against the background of a challenging financial climate, my Department continues its focus on building the GP workforce which includes funding the Attract, Recruit and Retain scheme, the GP Retainer Scheme and the GP Induction and Refresher Scheme, and maintaining the increased level of 121 GP training places.  		My Department remains committed to the further implementation of the Multi-Disciplinary Team model across NI, and is developing a plan for rollout over the next number of years, subject to the availability of the necessary recurrent funding. 	While a budget for 2024/25 has not yet been set, it is expected that the overall financial position for all NI departments will remain very challenging.  My expectation therefore is that we will not be able to do all that we want or need to do and the main focus will be on preserving and protecting existing services, with all the limitations that they currently have.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the business cases taken forward in the absence of an Executive and which relate to spend in the 2023-24 budgetary period; and (ii) how many of these business cases have been progressed without a fully-funded or guaranteed budget.
answer_text: My Department’s Casework Committee considered 17 business cases with a value in excess of £5 million from February 2022 to January 2024 which related to spend in the 2023-24 budgetary period.  These are listed in the table below.Business Case TitleUK Ireland Collaborative Research Centre ProgrammePrivate Veterinary Practitioner FeesEnvironmental Fund 2023-28 Collaborative Research Funding ProgrammeCentre for Environmental Data and RecordingCarbon Benchmarking ProgrammeWhitespots Country ParkAnimal Health and Science Building AFBI Green Growth CAFRE Redevelopment - Loughry Campus AddendumCAFRE Redevelopment - Greenmount AddendumBeef Carbon Reduction ProgrammeBovine Genetics ProgrammeFruit and Vegetable Aid SchemeFuture Implementation Programme Phase B Delivery Future Farm Support and Development Programme A Replacement for the Marine Research Vessel ‘Corystes’ 	In line with Executive funding for all departments, no budgets have been confirmed beyond 31 March 2024.  However, in each of these business cases an affordability assessment has been made in terms of Non Ringfenced Resource DEL, Ringfenced Resource DEL and Capital DEL as appropriate across the relevant years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of (i) the peer-reviewed scientific analysis entitled difference in differences analysis evaluates the effects of the badger control policy on bovine tuberculosis in England; and (ii) the effectiveness of badger culls on the incidences of bovine tuberculosis in herds.
answer_text: I am aware of this paper in which the authors, Birch et al, state they have analysed data from 52 badger culling areas in England to estimate the change in bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) incidence rate in cattle herds due to badger intervention.  My officials have advised that the authors suggest in their analysis that badger culling can reduce the transmission of bTB from badgers to cattle, with the authors noting that vaccination of badgers, fertility control and on farm biosecurity may also achieve this effect.   I have asked my officials to carefully assess the paper to ensure we have a thorough understanding of its findings.It is my intention to take account of all relevant peer reviewed scientific papers and evidence when making any decision on the way forward for wildlife intervention.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what discussions he has had with the profession in relation to the long-term future of General Practice.
answer_text: General Practice is the bedrock of health and social care provision and I am committed to ensuring that General Practice is sustainable for the long term.Since returning as Minister of Health in February, I have met with GP representatives a number of times to discuss the pressures facing primary care and the actions that we can take to support service sustainability. I will be meeting again with GP representatives in the coming weeks.  I believe that these discussions are a vital part of working together collaboratively to address the pressures facing general practice and support its long-term sustainability.In addition to these ongoing discussions, since returning as Health Minister, I have also moved quickly to approve the DDRB uplifts for 23/24, resulting in a 7.33% uplift to the Global Sum Equivalent for general practice locally.  Moving forward, I also want to ensure that a General Medical Services contract is in place that is fit for future purpose and can better meet the changing environment and context in primary care.  This is the subject of ongoing discussions between my officials and representatives from the profession.  While a budget for 2024/25 has not yet been set, it is expected that the overall financial position for all NI departments will remain very challenging.  My expectation is that we will not be able to do all that we want or need to do.  The main focus, therefore, will be on preserving and protecting existing services, with all the limitations that they currently have.  It will be essential in this challenging environment that we continue to collaborate positively to support the resilience and sustainability of General Practice locally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he is taking to improve access to diagnosis and treatment for people with autism.
answer_text: My Department has recently published the cross-departmental Autism Strategy 2023-2028 including an action plan for implementation. The Department is aware of the extensive waiting lists for autism assessment and the challenges that this presents to those people who need support. All HSC Trusts are commissioned to provide paediatric assessment and diagnosis for ASD. Rather than treatment, children and young people receive post diagnostic intervention to support and meet their identified needs.Over the last six months, my staff have held meetings with all Trusts to chart the existing referral and assessment routes for paediatric ASD. After completing this mapping exercise, officials are collaborating with colleagues from the Public Health Agency and Community Child Health to establish a standardised pathway across the region that aligns with NICE guidelines. The development of this pathway will incorporate best practices, emerging evidence, and the understanding that effective workforce planning is essential in meeting the demands.The Autism Strategy 2023 – 2028 Delivery Plan (2023-2025) includes an action to consult on, and finalise, the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework which will guide future development of services. The strategy and the Framework embrace a move towards understanding Autism within a broader neurodevelopmental approach and incorporate a regionally consistent single point of referral and diagnostic pathway. It will allow for earlier intervention and support without diagnosis. In relation to Adult Autism provision a review across the region has been undertaken and the recommendations were presented to the Permanent Secretary.  One of the recommendations is the appointment of a Lead for Adult Autism provision - recruitment of this post is currently underway. This role will be responsible for taking forward the recommendations emerging from the review; this will include the management of diagnosis, assessment, treatment and monitoring waiting lists. Department of Health officials are keeping the situation regarding the commissioning of Autism services under review, recognising that the demand for assessment and diagnosis has risen in recent years.  In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability and Ministerial priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of how many hospital beds are currently occupied by individuals deemed fit for discharge; and (ii) whether he plans to establish a dedicated team in his Department to work on resolving this issue.
answer_text: for his assessment of how many hospital beds are currently occupied by individuals deemed fit for discharge.Information on hospital beds occupied by individuals deemed fit for discharge is not held centrally and therefore the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts were asked for data, on 15th March 2024. The data is tabulated overleaf.Number of individuals deemed fit for discharge per HSC Trust on 15th March 2024HSC TrustIndividualsBelfast150Northern1114South Eastern67Southern2149Western3148Source: Health and Social Care Trusts1Northern HSC Trust figures are only within acute hospital sites, and of these 114 inpatients, 42 were still within discharge target timeframes. On 12th March 2024, there were 15 delayed discharges across Mental Health within Holywell and the Ross Thompson Unit.2Southern HSC Trust indicated that of these 149, 25 were discharged that day. Of the 149, 14 were Mental Health inpatients, and 3 were Learning Disability.3Western HSC Trust figures are only within acute sites. Of these, 29 were waiting on domiciliary care, with many in hard to source areas of Fermanagh & Omagh Council Area. There were also 43 patients waiting on a nursing home bed, with 27 of these awaiting Elderly Mentally Infirm nursing. Many of these will require enhanced care at least initially, and Western HSC Trust has the lowest provision of these community beds in Northern Ireland.whether he plans to establish a dedicated team in his Department to work on resolving this issue.My officials have established a Team within the Strategic Planning and Performance Group of the Department of Health, who performance manage the Trusts on their delayed hospital discharges through a range of regionally agreed measures.  An unscheduled care forum has been established to actively address the issues which cause delayed hospital discharges, and provide support to Trusts.  The forum is attended by acute and community senior Trust staff, SPPG, PHA and NIAS to encourage whole system thinking.  It provides an opportunity to progress key issues pertaining to unscheduled care patient care and patient flow.As you will be aware, a number of delayed discharges are due to a shortage of community provision to support the person when they return home.  So, as well as the work being in done in SPPG to performance manage the delayed discharges, I have recently announced a £70m support package for social care providers and hospices.  This funding will help independent sector organisations deal with rising costs from the start of the new financial year, particularly forthcoming minimum wage increases. This £70m package includes an increase in the regional funding rate for domiciliary care providers to £20.01 per hour.  It is hoped that this increase in payment will stabilise the market as it is recognised that domiciliary care services are  critical to supporting patient flow out of our hospitals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many dental practices have ceased NHS work in the last 12 months.
answer_text: Of those General Dental Practices carrying out Health Service dental work on 31st December 2022, four were not carrying out Health Service dental work on 31st December 2023, the reference date of the latest publicly available quarterly Accredited Official Statistics. Although these practices have ceased HSC work, they may have merged with other practices or transferred their patient lists to other dentists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions he is taking to encourage GPs who have left or retired from the profession back to work, even on a part-time or sessional basis.
answer_text: Since returning as Health Minister, my immediate priority has been to progress the 2023/24 pay award for GPs.  I recently approved a 6% funding uplift for GPs and their staff in response to the recommendations of the 51st report of the Review Body on Doctors’ and Dentists’ Remuneration (DDRB) published in July 2023.  This was an important step in recognising the important role of GPs and their staff and to acknowledging the increasing pressures and demands faced by General Practice. 	Stabilisation is a key concern and one which my Department is pro-actively taking steps to address, including a continued focus on building the GP workforce.  The GP Retainer Scheme is designed to assist in the retention of GPs in primary care in Northern Ireland.  It also aims to attract those doctors who are approaching the end of their career and may be considering reducing their sessional commitment to general practice and /or retirement.   The GP Induction and Refresher Scheme provides an opportunity for GPs who meet the required criteria to safely return to general practice following a career break or time spent working abroad.  It also supports the safe introduction of overseas GPs and provides support to GPs returning to or entering clinical practice in Northern Ireland.  The Attract, Recruit and Retain Scheme promotes recruitment in hard to recruit areas. It aims to support practices in attracting and recruiting GPs as well as retaining those who join their team.  GP practices can apply for a number of packages under the Scheme including a Golden Hello, relocation costs, recruitment costs, management costs, reimbursement costs for applying to the NI Performers List and funding for international GPs.The Scheme was launched on 4th January 2023 and, as of March 2024, 48 GP Practices have applied to the Scheme and received approval in principle for funding.  23 GP practices have applied to the Scheme and received final approval for payment in respect of a recruited GP.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of access to NHS dentistry.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on waiting times for an autism spectrum disorder assessment.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the extensive waiting lists for autism assessment and the challenges that this presents to those people who need support. At 31 December there was 11,013 children on the paediatric ASD diagnostic assessment waiting list, the longest wait was 4 years and 6 months. On the same date there was 3,117 adults awaiting ASD diagnostic assessment, 1,935 of these were waiting longer than 52weeks.The Department has recently published the cross-departmental Autism Strategy 2023-2028 including an action plan for implementation.Over the last six months, my staff have held meetings with all Trusts to chart the existing referral and assessment routes for paediatric ASD. After completing this mapping exercise, officials are collaborating with colleagues from the Public Health Agency and Community Child Health to establish a standardised pathway across the region that aligns with NICE guidelines. The development of this pathway will incorporate best practices, emerging evidence, and the understanding that effective workforce planning is essential in meeting the demands.The Autism Strategy 2023 – 2028 Delivery Plan (2023-2025) includes an action to consult on, and finalise, the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework which will guide future development of services. The strategy and the Framework embrace a move towards understanding Autism within a broader neurodevelopmental approach and incorporate a regionally consistent single point of referral and diagnostic pathway. A Review of Adult Autism provision across the region has been undertaken and the recommendations were presented to the Permanent Secretary.  One of the recommendations is the appointment of a Lead for Adult Autism provision - recruitment of this post is currently underway. This role will be responsible for taking forward the recommendations emerging from the review; this will include the management of waiting lists for adult autism assessment. Department of Health officials are keeping the situation regarding the commissioning of Autism services under review, recognising that the demand for assessment and diagnosis has risen in recent years.  In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability and Ministerial priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of qualified GPs not working in general practice.
answer_text: The requested information is not readily available as there are a number of reasons for qualified GPs not working in general practice.  For example, there are other roles within Health & Social Care for qualified GPs, and some will carry out these roles in conjunction with general practice. In addition, some qualified GPs may be dual trained and have another medical specialty in which they work.  Qualified GPs could also be employed in advisory roles, academic roles, research, private sector roles, or may not have a current GMC registration or licence to practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on a pathway for ADHD assessment and intervention.
answer_text: Provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) assessment and diagnosis has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals there is currently no specifically commissioned services. The Department is aware that this has led to inconsistencies in provision between Trusts and inconsistent pathways and service gaps. The limited capacity available in Trusts in terms of meeting demand and the level of prevalence of child and adult ADHD is leading to individuals waiting longer for assessment and support than described in NICE guidelines.The Department of Health are committed to work closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet NICE compliant pathways for ADHD diagnosis and support.Department of Health officials are considering how a future ADHD service might be delivered to meet needs, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years.  In line with the usual process for commissioning services, any decision to commission ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the agreement with Kingsbridge Healthcare for 2,000 red flag endoscopies and 1,000 urology procedures, to be carried out by the end of March 2024.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he will consider multinational healthcare providers from outside Northern Ireland to assist with our capacity in addressing waiting list backlogs; and (ii) what discussions his officials have had with these healthcare providers about assisting in Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister of Health for an estimate of the number of people living with ADHD.
answer_text: There is no regional register or requirement for Health and Social Care Trusts to record if a person has ADHD. Therefore, my department does not hold information on the number of people in Northern Ireland living with ADHD.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an estimate of the number of endoscopies that will not take place between January 2024 and the end of the current financial year, that would have taken place had his Department not instructed the independent sector to cease providing endoscopies for the NHS in January 2024; (ii), using previous statistics, for an estimate of the proportion of that figure that would result in a cancer diagnosis; (iii) for his assessment of the implications of resulting late diagnosis for those patients on their long-term prognosis; (iv) for his assessment of the implication
answer_text: Waiting List Initiatives money for additional assessment, diagnosis and treatment has been provided in each quarter in 2024. There has been no instructions to stop using the Independent Sector for endoscopies.Each year over the last few years, the Department has allocated non-recurrent funding for Waiting List Initiatives to provide extra capacity for assessments, diagnostics and treatment. This extra capacity is delivered both by the Health and Social Care Trusts through additionality using own staff and by using the Independent Sector.The majority of Waiting List Initiatives funding has been used for red flag (cancer) and time critical patients. This includes endoscopies for cancer pathways.In addition to the continued use of Independent Sector capacity, the Department has been working to expand core Health and Social Care endoscope capacity. New endoscope capacity in now operational in the regional centres at Lagan Valley and Omagh Hospitals. This expansion of core capacity has helped reduce the total volume of patients awaiting endoscopy. From March 2023 to October 2023 (prior to South Eastern HSC Trust Encompass / Epic computer system go live) the number of patients waiting across all urgency types reduced 10% (3,867 patients). Since October 2023, and excluding South Eastern HSC Trust, there has been a further reduction of 10% (2,372 patients). (Due to the go live of Encompass / Epic computer system, data is not yet available for end of December 2023 from South Eastern HSC Trust.)The decision on which provider to use, and how to spend the money, are taken in line with procurement policies to ensure best value for public money.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of controlled schools to have transformed into an integrated school in the last five years; and (ii) the change in income as a result of this in each of these schools.
answer_text: In the last five years from 2019 to 2024, six schools, three primary, one post primary and two nursery schools have transformed from controlled schools to controlled integrated schools, four in 2021 and one each in 2022 and 2023.All grant aided schools are funded under the Common Funding Scheme.  The Common Funding Formula (CFF) is the Department’s mechanism for allocating budgets to schools funded through Local Management of Schools arrangements. A school’s CFF allocation is determined by both the quantum of funding available to the Aggregated Schools Budget and a combination of the school’s overall enrolment, the differentiated needs of its pupils and the school’s characteristics.  The funding arrangements for controlled schools and controlled integrated schools are the same.  For this reason, no additional funding has been allocated to the controlled integrated schools referenced above over what they would have received had they not transformed to controlled integrated status.The Irish Medium and Integrated Education Team has a small budget (£20k) to support schools with ballot costs and post transformation costs (for a period of up to 5 years). Eligible schools are invited to submit bids and allocations are made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any outstanding court judgements regarding police officers' pay and conditions which have not been met.
answer_text: The Department of Justice has not been notified of any outstanding court judgements regarding police officers’ pay and conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of road projects which have been (i) amended; (ii) postponed; and (iii) cancelled, as a result of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: Following the introduction of the Act, work is ongoing to shape the policies and proposals that will need to be progressed. While seventeen road projects have been paused primarily due to financial pressures, no road projects have been cancelled as a result of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of patients allocated to each GP practice; and  (ii) the number of patients allocated to each full-time equivalent GP in each of these practices.
answer_text: The number of patients registered at each GP practice is published quarterly by the Business Services Organisation and is available at the following link:https://bso.hscni.net/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/fps_gms_quarterly_tables_q3_23-24.xlsxFull-time equivalent data is not available for GPs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to fund agricultural shows, given their importance to the agri-food industry and the sustainability of rural communities.
answer_text: I would like to set out my appreciation of the vital role that local agricultural shows play in our communities.  I recognise the importance of shows in boosting rural economies, attracting visitors and showcasing our excellent agri-food sector. They are an integral part of the fabric of rural life, building cohesion and providing our rural communities with a focal point.		As you are aware the Northern Ireland Executive is not yet in a position to agree a budget for 2024-25. Until this is known, I am not in a position to consider any proposals in relation to funding for shows which will be considered as part of the 2024/25 budget process.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount of new funding allocated for rural development projects in financial year (i) 2023-24; and (ii) 2024-25.
answer_text: In 2023/24, £1.99m was allocated to rural development projects plus an additional £921k was baselined in the 2023/2024 budgets of other NICS Departments. The projects were: Rural Community Development Support Service - £925k Rural Support Charity - £252kSPRING Rural Enhanced Social Prescribing Project - £70kAccess and Inclusion Grant Aid Scheme - £300kFarm Families Health Checks Programme - £20k. Rural Social Enterprise Investment Scheme - £423kAssisted Rural Travel Scheme (DfI) - £560kCommunity Development (DfC) - £211kFarm Families Health Checks Programme (DoH) - £147k         Village Catalyst Pilot Scheme (DfC) - £3kAllocations for 2024/2025 will be determined once the Executive agree the budget for 2024/2025 for all departments.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what stage the business case for providing support to agricultural shows is at, following the publication of the Independent Resilience Review of Agricultural Shows in Northern Ireland in August 2022.
answer_text: The Independent Resilience Review of Agricultural Shows in Northern Ireland, published in June 2023, brought forward recommendations for both industry and government aimed at maximising the sustainability of agricultural shows in the years ahead including future funding options.  In the absence of ministers and due to the financial constraints placed upon the Department by the 2023/24 Northern Ireland budget, it has not been possible to progress work to evaluate the options proffered by the Review.  The Northern Ireland Executive is not yet in a position to agree a budget for 2024-25. Until this is known, I am not in a position to consider any proposals inrelation to funding for shows which will be considered as part of the 2024/25 budget process.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has any plans to reconsult on the call for evidence on impacts of air pollution on the natural environment.
answer_text: Following the Call for Evidence last year, my Department is developing the revised Operational Protocol and is considering its approach to stakeholder engagement and consultation as part of this process.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what stakeholder engagement (i) has taken place; (ii) is planned, in respect of the call for evidence on impacts of air pollution on the natural environment.
answer_text: Both during and after the Call for Evidence, my Department met with representative organisations from the agriculture and environment sectors and responded to individual stakeholder enquiries. My Department is considering its approach to further stakeholder engagement and consultation as the revised Operational Protocol continues to be developed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will commit to promote the agri-food industry on a UK and global stage to increase the profile of our local produce.
answer_text: Our agri-food industry is recognised across these islands and beyond for its high-quality produce.  As an industry with a strong sub-regional footprint, it has an important role to play in delivering against my economic priorities, not least through the adoption of innovative processes and global sales.  Moreover, my Department and its agencies will support our agri-food businesses to make full use of our market access to the EU and Britain in growing local exports.  	Invest NI has a dedicated Business Development team based in London and Belfast working with local businesses to promote our food to retailers, wholesalers and food service customers in Britian and Ireland.  Their international teams are actively engaging one-to-one with companies looking to enter or grow export sales in those regions.  Invest NI also partners with The Department for Business and Trade, to ensure our businesses receive as much support as possible to grow export sales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme, to detail (i) a timeline of the business case approval for the scheme; and (ii) for an update as to when the scheme will open for applications.
answer_text: Officials are at an advanced stage of finalising a Business Case for the Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme. Subject to my consideration and budget availability I anticipate being in a position to make an announcement regarding the Scheme early in the 2024/2025 financial year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) how many sewerage and water treatment outlets enter into Lough Neagh; (ii) how many have real time monitoring, including emission loads; and (iii) what mechanisms are available in other areas of the UK to carry out real time monitoring.
answer_text: The issues you raised are operational matters for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what engagement he has had with counterparts in other regions of the United Kingdom in relation to using the 0.08 per cent threshold to determine ammonia planning advice.
answer_text: The 0.08% threshold is part of a range used to determine when a project’s emissions could be considered “nugatory” for the purpose of ecological risk assessments for air pollution impacts, depending on site-specific considerations including development pressures.  It was developed as part of the Decision-Making Threshold (DMT) project, led by JNCC, on behalf of Defra and the UK conservation and regulatory agencies.DAERA officials have had extensive engagement with Defra and the other UK agencies regarding the DMT approach and its application via the Inter-agency Air Pollution Group and the UK Air Quality Technical Advisory Group.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the steps taken to protect the agri-food industry from the effects of the bluetongue virus; and (ii) what financial support is available to farmers if their flock is impacted by the virus.
answer_text: Since the first confirmed case of Bluetongue (BTV) in Kent, England on 10 November 2023 DAERA has been taking all actions necessary to assure ourselves, and industry, that the disease has not spread to Northern Ireland including carrying out enhanced post-import surveillance.  To date tracings have been completed on all movements of susceptible species from Great Britian to Northern Ireland from 1 September 2023, resulting in over 1700 samples all testing negative for BTV.		In addition, as a result of the BTV confirmation in Great Britain, live ruminant animals cannot move to Northern Ireland, as the relevant Export Health Certificates (EHCs) cannot be signed by Official Veterinarians in Great Britain.  The impacted EHCs have been removed by the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra).		Veterinary officials are reviewing the BTV Veterinary Risk Assessment (VRA) on an ongoing basis in light of the recent developments in Great Britian. The most recent draft of the VRA highlights that the movement of an infected animal is the most likely route of BTV introduction to Northern Ireland.		Furthermore, officials have been in regular contact with key industry stakeholders to advise on the developing situation.  Weekly updates have been issued via email and the DAERA website has been continually amended to reflect the timeline of events in Great Britain.  Social media posts have also been issued to highlight the risk of BTV advising keepers to be vigilant for clinical signs of the disease.With regard to financial support to farmers if their flock or herd is impacted by BTV, compensation will be payable if the Department considers it necessary to cull any animals for disease control measures. BTV positive animals that are culled are paid at 50% of their market value while negative in contact animals that are removed are paid at 100%. It is important to note that any imported animals that are found to be infected with BTV will be culled with no compensation payable.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the business case for a Northern Ireland veterinary school; and (ii) to detail the dates when discussions last took place with stakeholders.
answer_text: From a Department of Agriculture Environment & Rural Affairs (DAERA) perspective, former Minister Poots agreed that DAERA could facilitate and chair a steering group made up of representatives from DAERA, Department for the Economy (DfE) and the universities (subsequently joined by a representative from Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council) to consider options for a veterinary school. As part of those considerations the Strategic Investment Board (SIB) was commissioned to undertake a situational analysis and baseline report to inform any future strategic business case produced. SIB produced reports on both, and these have been published on the DAERA website. It was recognised by the steering group that the situational analysis and baseline report was only a small element of the overall consideration of the need for a veterinary school and it was an area that should be taken forward to Strategic Business case by the main 3rd level educational institutes.The steering group last met in November 2022 and considered that the production of the reports was a natural breakpoint for the current group which was then stood down. There has been no further discussions with the Steering Group from November 2022. It is ultimately now down to the key 3rd level educational establishments in conjunction with the Veterinary Industry to drive this project forward to business case stage and we await further detail on their proposals as this will help inform whether there is any future role for DAERA.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will commit to promote the agri-food sector on a UK and global stage to increase the profile of our local produce.
answer_text: Our agri-food industry is recognised across these islands and beyond for its high-quality produce.  As an industry with a strong sub-regional footprint, it has an important role to play in delivering against my economic priorities, not least through the adoption of innovative processes and global sales.  Moreover, my Department and its agencies will support our agri-food businesses to make full use of our market access to the EU and Britain in growing local exports.  	Invest NI has a dedicated Business Development team based in London and Belfast working with local businesses to promote our food to retailers, wholesalers and food service customers in Britian and Ireland.  Their international teams are actively engaging one-to-one with companies looking to enter or grow export sales in those regions.  Invest NI also partners with The Department for Business and Trade, to ensure our businesses receive as much support as possible to grow export sales.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will commit to providing direct road infrastructure access to the Balmoral Park site as a matter of urgency.
answer_text: A new or improved road connection will be essential for future development of the entire MLK site in whichever form that takes.  We recognise there would also be an immediate benefit for the Balmoral Park site.  This will be a substantial project in its own right, and the Maze/Long Kesh Development Corporation are considering options.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any planned maintenance by the Housing Executive to their housing stock in the Upper Bann constituency in 2024.
answer_text: Any planned maintenance by the Housing Executive to their housing stock in the Upper Bann constituency is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the progress that has been made to deliver A Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland 2022-2032, published in March 2022.
To ask the Minister for the Economy what new support his Department will allocate in order to drive efficiencies within the agri-food sector.
answer_text: My Department and Invest NI continue to work together to identify how we can best deliver upon public policy objectives and incentivise businesses in the sector to become more productive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the advice available for women in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust area who are a blood donor and awaiting for a smear review.
answer_text: Women remain eligible to donate blood whilst awaiting their slide review outcome, providing they fulfil all other criteria on the day of donation.  Prior to donation of blood, all potential donors complete a Health Check Questionnaire (HCQ) with the Northern Ireland Blood Transfusion Service (NIBTS).  This is designed to screen donors and identify any potential issues that would prevent a donor from donating.  These questions help the NIBTS donation staff to identify those donors affected by the review of cervical smear tests in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) area.Advice in relation to women who donate blood in the SHSCT and are affected by the cervical cytology review, is available on both the Trust’s website https://southerntrust.hscni.net/news/cervical-screening-review/#and the NIBTS website https://nibts.hscni.net/donating-blood/can-i-donate/  then go to ‘waiting for test results or investigations’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she will examine the proposals suggested by the land mobility programme to review tax arrangements for conacre.
answer_text: The tax treatment of conacre-let land is an Excepted Matter for the Treasury to determine.The Minister for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs has policy responsibility for agriculture locally, and the examination of any such proposals would be a matter for him to consider in the first instance. Should Minister Muir consider that there is a robust case to present on the tax treatment of conacre-let land, then I would be happy to consider whether an approach to Treasury would be appropriate given my role in leading engagement on Excepted tax matters locally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) how many new police officers will be recruited by the end 2024; and (ii) what will be the total number of police officers by the end of 2024.
answer_text: Police officer numbers and recruitment decisions are a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board. You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) how many part-time police officers, formerly police reserve, are currently available for duty; and (ii) whether there are plans to recruit more officers.
answer_text: PSNI has advised that there are currently 193 PSNI Part-Time Reserve Officers, of whom 177 are currently available for duty.Recruitment Decisions are a matter for the Chief Constable.  The PSNI is also operationally independent from my Department and is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  It is important that I respect the operational independence of both organisations.  You may wish to direct your enquiries to these organisations directly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what representation has been made to the UK government in pursuit of reviewing Northern Ireland's Barnett consequentials allocation to ensure needs are being adequately met.
answer_text: ANSWERThe financial package which accompanied the return of the Executive, while welcome, does not put the Executive’s finances on a sustainable footing.Concerns have been raised with the British Government about the financial package, including the need for fair funding with a fiscal floor, as well as the conditions attached to the write-off of the £559 million provided in the package for ‘debts’, and the timescale for a sustainability plan.The Executive is committed to delivering sustainable public services. A pre-requisite of that must be that the right level of funding is provided. Therefore, a fiscal floor which reflects need is vital. I am meeting the Chief Secretary to the Treasury on 28 February to discuss these concerns and the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps will be taken to address the high levels of Bovine TB in our cattle population.
answer_text: Bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) is one of the most challenging issues faced by the cattle farmers in Northern Ireland.  Herd incidence currently stands at 9.97% for the 12 months to the end of January 2024 and last year total spend on the control of bTB in cattle was £53million, with a similar amount expected this year.  The high cost of controlling this disease is not sustainable for either the public purse or for hard working farm businesses. 	To address the high levels of bTB in our cattle, my Department delivers a bTB eradication programme in cattle in addition to progressing a long-term Strategy to reduce, and ultimately eradicate the disease in Northern Ireland.  This bTB Eradication Strategy for Northern Ireland was launched in March 2022 and contains 21 actions across six thematic areas. These are measures designed to tackle the different causes that facilitate the transmission of bTB.  As you may be aware, the wildlife intervention element of the Strategy was overturned by a judicial review decision in October 2023. 		Tackling this disease and all the factors which contribute to its spread and endurance will be a top priority for me in my new role.  I will be working closely with my veterinary and policy officials in the coming weeks on next steps for the bTB Eradication Strategy, in addition to engaging with stakeholders to examine the best ways to help lower the rate of bTB in our herds.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans there are to reinstate the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: Reducing waiting times for elective access is a key priority for me. Current waiting times are unacceptable, no one should have to wait longer than necessary for the surgery they need.  While good progress has been made in tackling waiting times in some areas, there remains much more to be done. Within the constraints of existing budgets, I’m committed to taking whatever actions I can to tackle these waiting times and ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes.As you will be aware, I introduced the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS) on 30 June 2021 as part of the Elective Care Framework to help reduce NI waiting lists. The scheme was a limited 12-month administrative version of the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive (CBHD) which ceased to apply to NI following the UK’s exit from the EU. In June 2022, I announced that the RoIRS would be extended beyond the planned 30 June 2022 deadline, until an additional £5m of new funding had been committed. The RoIRS closed to new applications on Wednesday 21 September 2022 when the additional £5m funding had been exhausted. Given the pressing demands for funding across many areas of health and social care no further funding could be assigned to this scheme.Any future funding allocation to a similar scheme to help reduce waiting lists will now need to be considered as part of a broader review of resource investment in Waiting List Initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail the role of the Office of Environmental Protection; and (ii) whether it is independent from (a) his Department; and (b) the Northern Ireland Environment Agency.
answer_text: The overarching role of the Office for Environmental Protection (OEP) is to protect and improve the environment by holding government and other public authorities to account. It does so by: scrutinising Environmental Improvement Plans and environmental law; advising government departments on environmental law; and, where necessary, taking enforcement action on serious breaches of environmental law. The OEP is independent of DAERA, including NIEA, with a statutory duty placed on my department to have due regard to its independence.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the evidence he has considered as part of his exploratory work to establish an independent environment agency to strengthen environmental governance.
answer_text: We need to have the optimal structures and processes in place to enable us to achieve the best environmental outcomes. All relevant evidence will be considered as part of the exploratory work on options for strengthening environmental governance, including identification of the benefits that could accrue from, and the costs of establishing, an independent Environment Agency.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the powers that the Office of Environmental Protection has to review and sanction (i) his Department; and (ii) the Northern Ireland Environment Agency.
answer_text: The Office for Environmental Protection (OEP) has powers to review and report on DAERA’s Environmental Improvement Plan and monitor and report on implementation of Northern Ireland environmental law. The OEP also has extensive enforcement powers to deal with serious breaches of environmental law, including the power to seek an environmental review by the Courts. These powers apply in respect of all relevant public authorities including DAERA and its agency, the NIEA.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what progress has been made to deliver the A1 Junctions Phase 2 road improvement scheme.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times increase reliability.and improve road safety.I remain fully committed to delivering this important road safety project.  The statutory Orders for the scheme were made in summer 2021 and work on the project is continuing through the development of contract documents and Business Case. In recognition of current and future potential funding challenges, my Department is exploring options to deliver the project in smaller phased packages.  Details of the current status and timescales for of the A1 Junctions Phase 2 project will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/a1-junctions-phase-2-overview

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the gross hourly rate of an agency nurse, broken down by pay band; and (ii) the gross hourly rate of a Health and Social Care Trust nurse, broken down by pay band.
answer_text: Trust expenditure on agency staff has been incurred to ensure that safe and effective services are sustained and contributes importantly to maintaining service provision to patients and clients. HSC Trusts employ locum staff for several reasons, for example, cover for sickness and maternity/paternity leave; cover for existing vacancies; and when demand increases over the winter months.With costs increasing substantially over the last number of years I am concerned of the impact they are having on the HSC budget and recognise that these costs are not sustainable, particularly at a time of serious financial pressures right across the public sector. My Department has therefore undertaken significant work to try and alleviate the workforce pressures across HSC, which includes addressing the issues of rising agency and locum expenditure.  Work has been ongoing regionally across HSC, under the Regional Agency Reduction Implementation Group with significant progress already made towards the cessation of ‘Off-Framework’ agency use in Nursing and Midwifery and Social Workers across NI’s health service. Alongside this work has also been focused on Recruitment, Retention and Training of staff, as well as, the reform of the Bank system.Data for all professional groups shows that in 22/23 the total agency spend was £394m – of which £196m related to off-framework agency/locum spend.   Current estimates, based on grossing up April to December figures to full year estimates on a straight line basis indicate projected spend for 23/24 to be in the region of £373m with total off-framework spend projected to be in the region of £110m.  On this basis, the overall net reduction on agency spend is projected, on a straight line basis, to be in the region of £20m.  It should be noted these figures are estimates and should be treated with extreme caution at this time.  Transformation of health and social care in Northern Ireland is a priority.(i) the gross hourly rate of an agency nurse, broken down by pay bandThe current total hourly charge to the Trust for a standard day rate on Framework - Agency Workers - Nurses is within the ranges below;  basic pay hourly rate £Band 5£31.20 - £39.39Band 6£34.94 - £48.75Band 7£40.16 - £57.25 **These charges include the pay to the worker, holiday pay, employers national insurance contributions, pension contribution, government apprenticeship levy and agency fee.(ii) the gross hourly rate of a Health and Social Care Trust nurse, broken down by pay band.This information is publicly available and can be accessed through the following linkhttps://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-afc-pay-arrangements-2223.PDF

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many consultants are currently employed by Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) how many consultants also carry out private health care roles.
answer_text: At 31 December 2023, there were 2,061 consultants (1,942.5 whole-time equivalent) directly employed by HSC Trusts.  A breakdown of consultants employed by each HSC Trust is provided in the table below.Consultants Directly Employed by HSC Trusts, at 31 December 2023HSC TrustHeadcount*WTEBelfast895842.6Northern275259.3South Eastern347324.5Southern283267.1Western264249.1Total2,0611,942.5Source: Human Resources, Payroll, Travel & Subsistence (HRPTS) system* Where individuals hold more than one active post, they are counted once in each HSC Trust they hold an active post but counted only once in the overall total.  Therefore the headcount total may not sum.Information on the number of consultants who carry out private health care roles is not held by the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many patients have been treated by the Health Service in Northern Ireland whose main place of residence is the Republic of Ireland, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The table below shows the number of individuals living in the Republic of Ireland who have been admitted to hospitals in Northern Ireland in each of the last three years.Individuals1 living in the Republic of Ireland admitted to hospitals in Northern Ireland from 2020/21 to 2022/23YearIndividuals2020/215102021/225402022/23554Source: Hospital Inpatient System, Hospital Information Branch, Information & Analysis Directorate, DoH, NI.1Admissions are approximated using deaths and discharges. Admissions to the learning disability and mental health programme of care are not included. Numbers across years cannot be summed as an individual may have been admitted in more than one year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many procedures have been carried using Health and Social Care Trust (i) staff; and (ii) facilities, for private patients, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Information on the number of procedures carried out on private patients using Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) staff and facilities is not held centrally within the Department, therefore each of the HSCTs were asked for input.The figures for Belfast, South Eastern and Southern HSCTs have been tabulated overleaf. Number of procedures by HSCT, carried out using HSCT staff and facilities to treat private patients in each of the last three years.YearBelfast HSCT1South Eastern HSCT2Southern HSCT32020/2199702021/22206902022/2320970Source: Belfast, South Eastern and Southern HSCTs1Belfast HSCT figures exclude radiotherapy patients.2South Eastern HSCT figures exclude outpatient attendances as these would be overridden if the patient attended a subsequent NHS appointment. South Eastern HSCT figures are coded as paying patients.3Southern HSCT figures are identified as private patients using Category on Admission Code “PPG (PRIVATE)” or “PP2 (PRIVATE format pt II). This includes inpatient admissions (elective & non-elective), day cases and outpatients for Cancer and Clinical Services Directorate, and day cases and outpatients for Medicine & Unscheduled Care Directorate. The two remaining HSCTs were unable to provide a response in the timeframe provided.

Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts employ agency staff for several reasons, for example, cover for sickness and maternity/paternity leave; cover for existing vacancies; and when demand increases over the winter months.With costs increasing substantially over the last number of years I am concerned of the impact they are having on the HSC budget and recognise that these costs are not sustainable, particularly at a time of serious financial pressures right across the public sector. My Department has therefore undertaken significant work to try and alleviate the workforce pressures across HSC, which includes addressing the issues of rising agency and locum expenditure.
