question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will bring forward legislation in this mandate to establish a separate body to look after the interests of controlled schools.
answer_text: The Panel for the Independent Review of Education recommended in their Report that the new education landscape in Northern Ireland, as they envisaged it, should include a single authority, responsible for employment and other transactional services, for schools.They recommended that in the immediate future (prior to the creation of the single authority, which will require legislation), a directorate should be set up within the EA with responsibility for Controlled schools, but subsequently, the functions of the EA should be split to form (a) a body for school management and employment and (b) an early intervention and learner support service.My Department is currently considering these recommendations as part of a wider consideration of the Report and is carrying out internal consultation to inform a way forward. This consultation is currently ongoing and is expected to finish by the end of March 2024. Officials will then require time to consider the outcome of the consultation before producing a paper for my consideration setting out advice on the various recommendations in the report.  This will include the possibility of bringing forward legislation to establish a separate body to look after the interests of controlled schools.It is anticipated that this paper will be completed by the end of April 2024. I will be better informed to make a decision at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how much her Department received as a Barnett consequential following the announcement of the extension of childcare in England.
answer_text: The Executive received £57.2 million of Barnett consequentials for 2024-25, following the announcement in the Spring Budget of the expansion of the 30 free hours of childcare scheme in England. Barnett consequentials are unhypothecated, meaning they do not have to be used for the purposes for which they were issued.  It will be for the Executive to allocate this funding as part of its Budget 2024-25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will reverse the decision taken by the Department to no longer rely on published Ammonia Standing Advice to facilitate a period of consultation and analysis.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what is the current unfunded inescapable pressure her Department faces against its indicative base line.
answer_text: Education is facing extensive unavoidable cost pressures and rising service demands associated with delivering statutory and policy obligations.Taking into consideration an indicative baseline of £2,269.6m and only those pressures which are deemed to be inescapable or pre-committed, the Department reported an initial estimated Resource funding gap of £366m for 2022-23, with the gap increasing in subsequent years.  Following a recent review, these pressures are now estimated to be c£530m. This covers a range of pressures including teaching and non-teaching pay, the rising costs of funding Special Education Needs, resourcing pressures in schools, School Holiday Food Grant payments, increased energy costs and the Engage programme. There are also a number of other general Resource education pressures assessed as High Priority or Desirable estimated at £14m for 2022-23, £47m for 2023-24 and £48m for 2024-25.The Department has been advised that the contingency planning envelope for capital spend is £184.2m, which was based on estimates of tails of existing projects, contractual commitments at 4 February 2022, routine programmes and maintenance, and programmes and projects where there is previous Executive agreement.  This includes £34.5m of ring-fenced funding for Fresh Start projects. This is only sufficient to meet the Department’s contractual commitments.In advance of a Final Budget being agreed and in line with Department of Finance guidance, I have made a number of risk-based Resource expenditure planning decisions to ensure continuity of service in the 2022-23 financial year.  I have given a commitment to fund a number of pressures over and above the indicative baseline for Education, such as: maintaining the Aggregated Schools Budget at the same level as in 2021-22, providing funding to continue the Healthy Happy Minds pilot and the Engage programmes until December 2022, extending the School Holiday Food Grant payments to cover the full financial year, and the funding of a number of other time sensitive critical areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how many EU acts, containing provision which rests within the devolved competence of his Department, were included in the Annexes to the Northern Ireland Protocol on (i) 1 January 2021; and (ii) 1 September 2022.
answer_text: As at January 2021, the number of EU acts, containing provisions which rest within, or potentially impact on, the devolved competence of my Department was 109.  None have been added since then.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to confirm how many EU acts, containing provision which rests within devolved competence of his Department, were included in the Annexes to the Northern Ireland Protocol on (i) 1 January 2021; and (ii) 1 September 2022.
answer_text: My Department’s internal assessment of provisions resting within the devolved competence of my Department at 1 January 2021 is 52 of which 49 are devolved and three are partially devolved.    The EU legislation Information Tracking System (EULITS) which will track and monitor all legislation that falls within the scope of the Protocol is not yet operational.  Once the system is active, we will be in a position to provide numbers of EU laws in force as at 1st September 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many EU acts, containing provision which rests within the devolved competence of her Department, were included in the Annexes to the Northern Ireland Protocol on (i) 1 January 2021; and (ii) 1 September 2022.

Once the system is active, I will be in a position to provide the numbers of EU laws in force as at 1 September 2022.

answer_text: No EU acts, containing provision which rests within the devolved competence of my Department were included in the Annexes to the Northern Ireland Protocol on (i) 1 January 2021 and (ii) 1 September 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many EU acts, containing provision which rests within the devolved competence of her Department, were included in the Annexes to the Northern Ireland Protocol on (i) 1 January 2021; and (ii) 1 September 2022.
answer_text: There have been no EU acts containing provision that rests within the devolved competence of my Department included in the annexes to the Protocol since 1 January 2021 and 1 September 2022. No acts have been approved by the Joint Committee and therefore no new EU acts have been added to the Protocol under 13(4).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many occasions, since 1 January 2021, has she or her officials been consulted on new EU acts that have taken legal effect in Northern Ireland via Article 13 (4) of the Northern Ireland Protocol and which rest within the devolved competence of their Department.
answer_text: Since 1 January 2021, no new Acts resting within the devolved competence of my Department have been added to the Protocol by virtue of Article 13(4).

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many EU acts containing provisions which rest within the devolved competence of his Department were included in the Annexes to the Northern Ireland Protocol on (i) 1 January 2021; and (ii) 1 September 2022.
answer_text: Some EU acts included in the Annexes to the Northern Ireland Protocol are complex, cross-cutting and may cover a blend of devolved and reserved policy. The best assessment of those which contain provisions resting within the devolved competence of my Department on 1 January 2021 was 20.No provisions have been added to or deleted from the Annexes to the Protocol between 1 January 2021 and 1 September 2022.  Therefore the best assessment at 1 September 2022 is unchanged.

answer_text: Since 01 January 2021, neither I nor my officials have been consulted as there have been no new EU laws that have taken legal effect in Northern Ireland via Article 13 (4) of the Northern Ireland Protocol which rest within the devolved competence of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 1444/22-27, when the last formal review of the rate of legal aid payments was undertaken in accordance with The Magistrates’ Courts and County Court Appeals (Criminal Legal Aid) (Costs) Rules (Northern Ireland) 2009 and The Legal Aid for Crown Court Proceedings (Costs) Rules (Northern Ireland) 2005.
answer_text: The second statutory review of the Magistrates' Courts and County Court Appeals (Criminal Legal Aid) (Costs) Rules (Northern Ireland) 2009 (“the 2009 Rules”) has recently concluded, with amending Rules coming into operation on 6 January 2022.  The second statutory review of the Legal Aid for Crown Court Proceedings (Costs) Rules (Northern Ireland) 2005 (“the 2005 Rules”) was concluded in May 2015.  However, the resulting amending legislation was challenged by the Law Society and Bar of NI by way of judicial review.  In addition, defence representatives refused to provide representation for legally aided Crown Court defendants under the amended legislation.  This resulted in further amendments being made to the 2005 Rules which came into operation in April 2016.  The third review of the 2005 Rules is ongoing.  A consultation on the initial findings of this review is due to close on 1 August 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 1276/22-27, of the 6,400 cases that are due to be prosecuted, how many have been waiting (i) six months or more; (ii) twelve months or more; (iii) eighteen months or more; and (iv) two years or more.
answer_text: The figures for the period of time for cases where the Public Prosecution Service (PPS) had taken a decision that a case should be prosecuted but where the court date scheduled for the case to be heard had not yet been reached are provided in the table below.Length of time for cases in the justice system awaiting first court date after PPS decision to prosecute, at 1 June 2022Time from PPS decision date to 1 June 2022DefendantsUnder 6 months3,4696 months to under 12 months1,14612 months to under 18 months69118 months to under 2 years5462 years and over519Note:1. Figures relate to cases brought on behalf of the PSNI, NCA, Harbour, Airport police and other Governmental bodies in Northern Ireland.2. Figures exclude cases for adult and youth magistrates' courts that had more than five summons issued.3. Figures relate to the number of defendants whose cases are at this stage in the justice process.4. Figures include cases where there was a summons not served or where a bench warrant had been issued.5. Figures are taken from management information systems and should be considered as approximate, as they have not been thoroughly validated.Based on these figures, over half of the approximately 6,400 cases have been waiting less than 6 months and nearly three quarters of cases have been waiting under a year for their scheduled court date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following the opening of the Game of Thrones exhibition, how his Department intends to increase public transport links between Belfast Port and Banbridge.
answer_text: Increasing public transport links between Belfast Port and Banbridge is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.ukHowever, it is worth noting that if this request is to connect cruise ships to an exhibition in Banbridge, these forms of services would usually be contracted by either party and would not form part of the public transport network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department has plans to address the backlog in waiting list times for a Housing Executive home in Banbridge.
answer_text: I have set out an ambitious and long-term plan to increase the supply of social and affordable housing, and reduce housing stress. Crucial to this is the protection of the social homes we have; ensuring they can be maintained, and ultimately through the Housing Executive, in a revitalised form, being able to access borrowing to sustain itself and to build again. One of my priorities has been to enhance investment and increase new social home starts. The Housing Executive is committed to working with the Housing Association sector to bring forward new social housing proposals to address identified need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of passengers regularly travelling via Translink services from Belfast to Banbridge.
answer_text: Details on passenger number travelling regularly on Translink services from Belfast to Banbridge is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she has considered introducing a carers supplement for full-time carers.
answer_text: I appointed an Independent Advisory Panel to complete a comprehensive review of welfare mitigations and to consider other social security measures, including in relation to Carer’s Allowance. The panel engaged with a wide range of people who access the social security system and with key stakeholders (including carers) so that their voices are heard on these important issues. The panel has provided its final report to my Department and this will be published at the earliest opportunity.The implementation of any new payment schemes will require Executive agreement on the allocation of necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many mental health patients are awaiting (i) referral to a mental health consultant; and (ii) a mental health inpatient appointment, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information is not available in the format requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how her Department intends to ensure fair rates of pay for barristers undertaking public defence work.
answer_text: The Magistrates' Courts and County Court Appeals (Criminal Legal Aid) (Costs) Rules (Northern Ireland) 2009 (“the 2009 Rules”) and The Legal Aid for Crown Court Proceedings (Costs) Rules (Northern Ireland) 2005 (“the 2005 Rules”) prescribe the remuneration for solicitors and counsel representing legally aided defendants in criminal cases.Both pieces of legislation require the Department to keep the general operation of the Rules under review and to conduct a formal review of the levels of the prescribed fees and the rates of payment at least once in every three-year review period. Article 37 of the Legal Aid, Advice and Assistance (Northern Ireland) Order 1981 requires the Department to have regard to the following criteria when setting rates of payment in the 2005 and 2009 Rules:the time and skill which work of the description to which the rules relate requires;the number and general level of competence of persons undertaking work of that description;the cost to public funds of any provision made by the rules; andthe need to secure value for money.When conducting a review, the Department has regard to any representations from key stakeholders including the General Council of the Bar of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health up to how many weeks an abortion can be accessed in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Abortion (NI) (No.2) Regulations 2020 are being addressed for N. Ireland women and girls as follows.In respect of Regulation 3 for those pregnancies which are not exceeding 12 weeks:Belfast HSC Trust is providing an Early Medical Abortion (EMA) service for women and girls for gestations up to 9 weeks + 6 days. Northern HSC Trust is providing an EMA service for women and girls for gestations up to 11 weeks + 6 days.  Southern HSC Trust is providing an EMA service for women and girls for gestations up to 9 weeks + 6 days.South Eastern HSC Trust is providing an EMA service for women and girls for gestations up to 9 weeks + 6 days.At present, an Early Medical Abortion (EMA) service is not available in the Western HSC Trust.  As an interim measure, until such times as Western HSC Trust is in a position to deliver an EMA service, NHSC Trust is providing an EMA service to Western HSC Trust women and girls who reside in the Northern sector of the Western HSC Trust geographical catchment area.  Southern HSC Trust is providing an EMA service to Western HSC Trust women and girls who reside in the Southern sector of Western HSC Trust area until Western HSC Trust services are in place. The current situation with regard to Regulations 4, 5, 6 and 7 is as follows.All HSC Trusts are providing abortions under Regulation 5 where there is an immediate necessity.  The Department of Health and  the HSC Trusts are working closely together to progress the phased implementation of abortion services in all other circumstances under the 2020 Regulations, including abortions post 12 weeks’ gestation under Regulations 4 and 6 and abortions on the grounds of Fatal Fetal Abnormality (FFA) and Severe Fetal Impairment (Regulation 7). In the interim, women can continue to have funded access to abortion services in GB which are not currently available in NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Sub-Regional Stadia for Soccer Programme is a cross-cutting or significant issue.
answer_text: I have received legal advice in respect to the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer and this confirms that I have a responsibility to bring my proposals to the Executive for the necessary approval.My position has been clear and consistent on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many cases are currently awaiting hearing in the (i) civil; and (ii) criminal courts.
answer_text: Based on management information, at 1 June 2022, there were just under 6,400 cases where the Public Prosecution Service (PPS) had taken a decision that a case should be prosecuted but where the court date scheduled for the case to be heard had not yet been reached.  It is not possible to accurately determine the number of cases awaiting hearing for civil business in exactly the same manner as for criminal. Civil cases are lodged in court and may be inactive for several reasons including negotiations between parties, solicitors not informing the court office that cases have settled or because parties simply decide not to proceed with a case.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many abortions have been carried out in each Health and Social Care Trust since the introduction of the Northern Ireland (Executive Formation etc.) Act 2019.
answer_text: The Abortion (Northern Ireland) (No.2) Regulations 2020 (“the 2020 Regulations”) came into force on 31 March 2020, legalising abortion in Northern Ireland.  The Regulations require a notice of each termination to be sent to the Chief Medical Officer. As of 13 June 2022, the Chief Medical Officer has received 3,459 abortion notifications, broken down by HSC Trust as follows:Belfast Trust		1,215 Northern Trust		763South Eastern Trust	725Southern Trust 		518Western Trust		238

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many additional bids he has received for energy costs from each Executive department.
answer_text: My Department has written to other departments asking them to provide returns detailing the pressures that they are facing, including energy costs.  While this will help inform a budget process, the lack of an Executive means that it is not possible to agree a budget or to consider formal bids.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure on an update on the A1 Junctions Phase 2 Road Improvement Scheme.
answer_text: I am fully committed to delivering this important road safety project.  The statutory orders for the scheme were made in summer 2021 and work on the development of the business case is nearing completion. The next stage is to prepare the procurement and contract documents. This work will take approximately 12 months and be funded as part of the financial contingency arrangements. However, the continued refusal of the DUP to form an Executive and an agreed 3-year Budget causes uncertainty in terms of timescales for commencing the tender process and moving to construction.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many civil servants are currently working from home.
answer_text: I can only respond to this question in respect of my own Department and would refer you to my answer to AQW 534/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities why she has not released the funding allocated for the Sub Regional Stadia for Soccer Programme.
answer_text: The challenges created by not having a functioning Executive mean that I cannot bring proposals for endorsement and agreement as required for the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how much additional funding for energy costs has her Department bid for in the current financial year.
answer_text: At this stage my Department has submitted a bid to the Department of Finance for additional funding for energy costs of £13.1m in 2022-23, however this will need to be kept under review given the evolving situation around energy costs
